experiments.title: How increasing the urgency impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the urgency and need for rural education donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2025 - 07/08/2025
During the Make Summer Fair campaign, we wanted to test whether increasing the urgency of the need on the donation form value prop copy, specifically tied to rural education, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether shifting rural education messaging and imagery to emphasize need — rather than happiness — would motivate more donors to give, and to give higher amounts.
Key Results:
Overall conversion dropped by 20% at 97% confidence — a statistically significant decline. RPV decreased by 6%, though that result wasn't statistically significant. The average gift, however, increased by $13.
The decrease in conversions outweighed the increase in average gift size, resulting in a net negative impact on overall performance.
Performance by Segment:
On desktop, conversion fell 23% while RPV nudged up 16% — suggesting the more urgent messaging resonated with a subset of desktop donors willing to give more, even as it turned others away. Mobile told a similar story: conversion dropped 15%, and RPV fell 21%, indicating the imagery may have been particularly off-putting on smaller screens where it's more prominent.
One-time giving saw a 19% drop in conversion at the 93% confidence level. Recurring giving declined across the board — conversion down 24% and RPV down 46% — though the sample wasn't large enough to draw firm conclusions there.
What the Results Suggest
The messaging didn't connect with most donors, but for those it did reach, it encouraged higher gifts. This suggests a segmentation opportunity rather than a broad messaging shift — the tone of urgency may work for a specific audience but feels less effective for the majority.
Next Steps:
Rather than abandoning the approach, we'll continue iterating to find a version that captures the average gift lift without the conversion drop. For example, we'll test a softer framing of need that feels compelling without being off-putting.

experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donate button on mobile impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for mobile donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 10/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button for mobile users. We hypothesized that showing the donation amount at the point of conversion would reduce friction by reinforcing the user’s selection and thus would increase conversion rate.
We had run this test earlier in the year with different results. For this experiment, we ran it on a Gaza-specific donation form that was getting traffic due to recent news in the area.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test assessed whether showing the donation amount on the "Donate" button increased mobile donor confidence and completion rates.
Key Results:
The conversion lift was statistically significant — the overall conversion rate increased by 37% at the 99% confidence level. Revenue Per Visitor (RPV) also trended upward by 12%; however, this change was not statistically significant, as it did not reach the 95% confidence threshold.
What the Results Suggest:
Displaying the amount on the button helps mobile donors commit with more confidence.

experiments.title: Can Highlighting the Monthly Impact Boost Recurring Giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly giving callout above the donation form on the main donation page will increase monthly donor conversions, as framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations. Framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/26/2025
The organization’s main donation page serves both committed donors and new visitors, playing a central role in overall giving. Unlike acquisition-focused pages meant to convert new audiences, this page often attracts high-value donors and returning supporters. As such, it presents a high-opportunity but high-risk space for testing.
In previous tests on acquisition donation pages, the organization found that adding a monthly gift callout that highlights the recurring impact and donor benefits increased the number of donors who chose to give monthly. Those tests indicated that, with the right framing, some page visitors can be persuaded to commit to becoming long-term donors.
This experiment asked whether that insight would transfer to the main donation page (without cannibalizing revenue from larger one-time gifts). Would prompting donors to “consider a monthly gift” increase monthly conversions? Or would it cause high-capacity donors to downshift from large one-time gifts to smaller recurring ones?
The Control used the standard donation page layout, which defaults to a one-time gift and includes no additional prompts about monthly giving.
The Treatment introduced a soft callout above the form that read: “Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $15 ensures families in spiritual crisis can find trusted help the moment they need it—and receive biblical support year-round. Plus, you’ll get to choose a new free resource every month for your family.”
No other changes were made to the page layout or the form’s functionality. We randomly split traffic 50/50 between both versions and ran this test for 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that a soft monthly giving prompt above the donation form can significantly increase recurring donor conversions—without suppressing large gifts and with potential upside for smaller one-time donations.
The Treatment produced a 125% increase in monthly donor conversions (2.1% vs. 0.9%), with a statistically significant 99.3% level of confidence.
 	Recurring donor volume more than doubled (33 vs. 15), while the average monthly gift remained steady ($30 on Treatment vs. $31 on Control, after removing an outlier)
 	While the Treatment had fewer gifts over $1,000 (35 vs. 41), this difference was not statistically significant (44% level of confidence), indicating no reliable evidence that large one-time gifts were cannibalized.
smaller one-time gifts (under $1,000)
The Treatment saw a 9.5% lift in volume, with an 86.4% level of confidence -- this is a strong directional signal, though not statistically conclusive
 	The average gift amount also increased, from $132 on the Control to $140 on the Treatment
Next Steps for Testing:
Explore alternate monthly ask amounts (e.g., $20, $25, or $30) to assess the revenue impact of shifting the default suggested amount.
 	Refine the callout language or visual design to test whether urgency, impact framing, or reward emphasis further improves performance.
 	Test this approach on other key donation pathways (e.g., campaign pages, seasonal asks) to evaluate if the effect carries over beyond the main donation page.

experiments.title: How defaulting a donation module to monthly giving impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to monthly for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2025 - 08/13/2025
Throughout the year, we tested a donation module as a replacement for a hero image and saw increases in conversions across the board. The next iteration of this test was to determine whether defaulting to monthly donations affected conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Defaulting to monthly donations led to a significant increase in conversions for potential donors.

2. The treatment group saw a 9.7% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.5%).

How to Use What Was Learned in Future Experimentation:

1. Consider defaulting to monthly donations in future donation modules to potentially improve conversion rates.

2. Continue to experiment with different variables such as donation amounts, payment methods, and messaging to further optimize conversion rates.

3. Monitor and analyze the results of future experiments to continuously improve the donation process and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visual call out for monthly giving for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2026 - 09/26/2026
With monthly giving a big focus for Save the Children, we want to test whether a graphic call-out highlighting the impact of monthly giving would increase conversion rates. We ran 3 different variants for this test, only for desktop traffic (since the buttons stacked on mobile):
Control
Variant 1- monthly call out text below the monthly/one-time array button
Variant 2- monthly call out text above the monthly/one-time array button
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding a visual monthly callout on the donation form. We hypothesized that this would strengthen the proposition for monthly giving.
Key Results:
This test showed that the visual monthly callout made the recurring option more salient and easier to process. By reducing cognitive friction and highlighting it as a “default-worthy” choice, users were nudged to see monthly as the standard, or even preferred, behavior.
Rather than deterring one-time donors, the callout acted as a commitment anchor. Donors compared themselves against the recurring option and felt reassured in choosing either. The result is a “lift all boats” effect: greater clarity increases confidence, which increases giving.
This suggests that visual emphasis on giving types does more than shift behavior between options. It can grow the whole pie by lowering decision friction and reinforcing giving as the normative action.

experiments.title: How adding a impact statement below the donate button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement below the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting the potential impact of their gift at the donate button would act as an extra layer of reinforcement and encourage the user to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2025
CaringBridge had a 2 day match. For this match, we wanted to test adding an impact statement below the donate button that highlighted that their gift would be doubled during this match. The statement read as follows –
Your $x gift goes twice as far, keeping this a free platform for patients, caregivers, and families to receive support and coordinate care every single day throughout a difficult health journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact statement below the donate button did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant 21.5% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.7% confidence level.

In future experimentation, it's important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of impact statements or any additional content on donation pages. It's possible that the impact statement used in this experiment was not compelling enough or may have caused confusion or distraction for potential donors.

There was a decrease in revenue as well. There was an 18% decrease in revenue with a 41% level of confidence

Control - $17,443

Treatment - $14,520

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different variations of impact statements, adjust the placement of the statement on the page, or even consider other strategies to emphasize the impact of donations. Additionally, gathering feedback from donors through surveys or qualitative research could provide valuable insights into what resonates with them and what motivates them to donate.

Overall, while this experiment did not yield the desired results, it provides valuable insights that can inform and improve future fundraising strategies for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: Does an educational slideout, with no ask, increase tribute giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that educational copy with no ask for users on a site profile will increase donor conversion rates because it provides context for the ask and greater clarity around tribute giving.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2026
CaringBridge’s platform is driven largely by tribute donations, gifts that are made to CaringBridge on a user’s page in honor of them, often with an accompanying message to the author. However, we hypothesized that users may not fully understand how tribute giving works: with questions about where their gift goes or why it’s impactful to an author’s page. We ran an A/B/C test in which both treatments contained no additional ask—but simply provided educational copy and context—believing this would better support the existing donation widgets.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that providing educational copy with no direct ask on users' profiles significantly increased donation conversions by 63.8%. This indicates that users are more likely to donate when they better understand the purpose and impact of tribute donations on CaringBridge. In future experiments, we should continue testing the effectiveness of educational copy and messaging in driving donation conversions. We can further refine the messaging to see if specific language or framing yields even higher conversion rates, and whether there's a way to optimize the experience so the slideout isn't intrusive in the long term.

experiments.title: How adding value prop to the donate button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop to the donate button on Care Net’s main donation page will achieve more donations because it will encourage users to click the donate button. It will remind them why they are giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2025 - 12/08/2025
Care Net’s donation pages typically have a donate button that says “Give Now” under that it says “Amount: $XX.” We were interested in seeing what would happen if we replaced the “Amount: $XX” with additional value prop. We replaced it with “I want to protect life.” We ran this experiment on their main donation page because that’s where they see the majority of their traffic and we knew this test would have a better chance of validating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated the powerful impact of mission-driven language on donor behavior. By replacing the default “Amount: $XX” label on Care Net’s highest-traffic donation page with a value-based statement — “I want to protect life” — the test reframed the giving decision from a financial transaction to a personal expression of identity and belief. The treatment produced a 44% lift in conversion rate, increasing from 18.7% to 27.0%, with 99.3% statistical confidence, confirming that the effect was both meaningful and reliable.
This result suggests that donors are more likely to complete a gift when the call to action connects directly to their values and sense of purpose, rather than focusing on dollar amounts or mechanics. In essence, emphasizing the “why” of giving at the point of decision appears to activate emotional motivation and a sense of alignment with the organization’s mission. For Care Net, this insight reinforces the importance of using identity-based and mission-oriented framing not just in broader messaging but in microcopy across key conversion points. Future tests could explore how different variations of purpose-driven language—tailored to distinct donor motivations—further influence giving behavior.

experiments.title: How Removing the $30 Premium Gift Threshold on a Marriage Offer decreases conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the $30 minimum gift requirement and offering the premium for a gift of any amount for donors visiting the “reconnected” donation page will achieve increased donation conversion rates because eliminating the minimum requirement lowers the psychological barrier to entry and makes it easier for more couples to say yes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2026 - 01/20/2026
Focus on the Family’s “Reconnected” content offer is designed to help married couples strengthen and restore their relationships. The donation page includes a premium incentive: a free resource written by the guest speaker featured in the video series.
Prior to this test, donors were required to give a gift of $30 or more to receive the book. While this threshold helped anchor the value of the premium, we questioned whether it might also discourage participation in any donation.
Because this offer often reaches people during a vulnerable or reflective moment in their marriage, we wanted to understand whether reducing the perceived cost of entry would invite more donors to give.
This ran as a split-url test on the donation page, with a 50 – 50 traffic split. The control noted that you’ll receive the book with a gift of $30 or more, while the treatment offered the premium with a gift of any amount. No imagery, giving form, or other copy was changed. Most visitors arrived to this page from meta advertising with strong messaging about the importance of reconnecting with your spouse today.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the $30 minimum gift requirement significantly decreased conversion rate, resulting in nearly half as many donors compared to the control. This result reached 99.1% statistical confidence, indicating a reliably negative effect. The treatment also predictably dropped average gift size, falling from $34.20 to $16.18.
For this particular audience, the $30 threshold acted as a signal of value and commitment. Rather than feeling like a barrier, the minimum gift amount may have:
Reinforced that the content and premium were valuable Framed the gift as an intentional act of support, rather than a casual transaction Helped donors feel confident about what gift size is appropriate
By removing that threshold, the page may have unintentionally weakened urgency and reframed the gift as being less impactful.
In future experimentation, its important to consider the perceived value of the premium incentive in relation to its audience. This test performed well on another offer named "Proof you Need to Believe in Jesus" about scientific proof for religious believers. And previous testing with that offer has indicated that those visitors are more motivated by curiosity and conviction, where the gift feels like access to strengthening their beliefs, with a low barrier to entry.
For emotionally resonant content like this offer, donors may respond better to clear expectations, ensuring them that this resource is trustworthy and meaningful. Lowering the premium requirements might have reduced both participation and revenue because of the loss of perceived value in this case.

experiments.title: How including a call-to-action in the headline of the donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that adding a clear call-to-action directly into the donation page headline would increase donor conversion rates because it would make the request immediate and emotionally explicit at the moment of decision, reinforcing the donor’s role and purpose in giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2025 - 12/30/2025
This experiment ran during The Heritage Foundation’s year-end fundraising campaign, when donors were already primed to give and donation page traffic was high. The campaign messaging emphasized urgency, high stakes, and the importance of acting before the calendar year closed. At this point in the funnel, visitors had already clicked through with intent, making the donation page headline a critical moment to either reinforce commitment or introduce friction.
Earlier research and experiments suggested Heritage supporters respond strongly to calls for action and personal responsibility, raising the question of whether explicitly placing the ask in the donation page headline would improve performance—or inadvertently overwhelm donors who had already decided to give.
The test ran on the donation page headline itself. All visitors arriving at the donation page were randomly assigned to see either the control headline or one of two CTA-embedded headline variations. This placement tested the impact of headline framing at the final decision point, immediately before donors selected a gift amount and completed their contribution.
We wanted to understand whether making the ask unavoidable at the top of the donation page would increase conversions—or whether it would feel redundant or overly aggressive for donors already in the act of giving. The two treatments tested distinct psychological levers: identity alignment (“stand with Heritage”) versus action-hero framing (“join the fight”), both rooted in prior survey insights about donor motivation, urgency, and self-perception.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced statistically valid results, and the outcome was clear: including a CTA directly in the donation page headline decreased performance. Both treatment variants underperformed the control in conversion rate, average gift, and revenue per visitor. Treatment A saw a significant drop in conversions (-19.7%) with high confidence, while Treatment B also underperformed, though less severely.
The control headline—descriptive but non-directive—performed best across all key metrics. This suggests that once donors reach the donation page, they do not need to be asked again. Introducing a fresh CTA at this stage likely created friction or pressure, disrupting donors who were already prepared to act.
Moving forward, we will avoid embedding overt calls-to-action in donation page headlines and instead use that space to reinforce context, stakes, and meaning rather than issuing another ask. CTAs appear to be more effective earlier in the funnel—on landing pages, emails, and homepage messaging—while donation pages perform better when they assume intent and reduce cognitive load.
This experiment reinforces a broader principle: clarity and restraint matter most at the point of conversion. For high-intent donors, the role of the donation page is to remove barriers, not escalate persuasion.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover (redirect) impacts conversion rates on Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a homepage takever for potential donors on Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2025 - 12/02/2025
For Save the Children US, Giving Tuesday has historically been one of the most important fundraising days of the year. This year, we wanted to test whether using a homepage takeover–where a user visiting the main homepage would instantly be redirected to a donation page featuring Giving Tuesday language–could increase conversion rates. The control had no takeover, and the treatment was set to show once per user per day, and featured a ‘continue to savethechildren.org’ link at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redirecting homepage traffic to a donation form on a high-volume giving day. We hypothesized that this would increase conversion by reducing friction for those who were looking to donate already, as well as help nudge those users on the fence of donating.
Key Results:
-       +14% statistically significant increase (96%) in overall conversion rate.
-       +12% non-statistically significant increase (84%) in overall RPV.
The results confirm that during peak giving times, homepage visitors include a high share of donation-intent users. Directing them straight to the donation form reduced friction and choice overload, elevating focus on the single task of giving and better aligning with user motivation.
Specific Results:
Overall performance when looking at transaction conversion rates had a 14% increase with a 96% level of confidence. In terms of revenue per visitor, the homepage takeover saw a 12% increase with an 83% level of confidence.
Desktop vs. Mobile:
Desktop:
Control: 19% conversion rate
Treatment: 22% conversion rate, which is a 15% increase with a 95% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 16% with an 87% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 13% conversion rate
Treatment: 14.2% conversion rate, which is an 8% increase in conversion rate with a 55% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor only increased by 2.3%, with a 54% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis for this big difference between desktop and mobile is that on mobile most potential donors are presented with a donation module at the top of the page, and therefore did not see a radically different experience like desktop users did.
Since Save is focusing on increasing recurring gifts, the emphasis on both control and treatment was pre-selecting monthly gifts. Therefore, it is interesting to look at whether the takeover had an impact on those:
One-time gifts vs. recurring
Recurring:
Control had a 1.2% conversion rate
Treatment: 1.1% conversion rate, which is a 9% decrease with a 29% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 28% with an 82% level of confidence
One-time:
Control had a 15% conversion rate.
Treatment: 17.6% conversion rate, which is a 16% increase with a 97% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 14% with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a more modernized donation form design impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more modern design for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2025 - 11/21/2025
Save the Children US has been using the same donation form design for a number of years, so we wanted to test whether a more modern look and improved visibility of key features would increase conversion rates by making the experience more appealing and accessible to potential donors. We ran this experiment on their evergreen donation form so as to not impact any campaign-specific results.
Instead of testing every single element to get to a ‘best practices’ form, we decided to test a major redesign first, and then in subsequent tests look at how changes to each element of the form impacted conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redesigning the main donation form. We hypothesized that a modernized design, combined with improved visibility of the form, would increase conversion by making the experience more appealing and accessible to donors.
Key Results:
-       47% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.
-       200% statistically significant increase (100%) in overall RPV.
The results show a directional increase in overall conversion and RPV, suggesting that the improved design and visibility of the donation form encouraged more users to complete a donation.
Overall results:
Control: 25% conversion rate
Treatment: 37% conversion rate, which is a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased dramatically, with a 200% increase and a 99% level of confidence.
Desktop versus mobile:
Since the biggest design changes were on desktop (the mobile version of the new form stacked similarly to the control) we expected the biggest changes on desktop:
Desktop:
Control: 27% conversion rate
Treatment: 41% conversion rate, which is a 48% increase with a 98% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 225% with a 99% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 21% conversion rate
Treatment: 26% conversion rate, which was a 22% increase with only a 43% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor on mobile also increased slightly, with an 8% increase, but only a 56% level of confidence.
One-time versus recurring:
Recurring:
Control: 5.9% conversion rate
Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 28% decrease with a 56% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased, however, by 91% with a 76% level of confidence (the donation sample size, however, was low, with a larger gift skewing this metric).
One-time:
Control: 19.5% conversion rate.
Treatment: 33% conversion rate, which is a 70% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 211% with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Today in the Word website visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Today in the Word’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Dear Reader.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for one and a half weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learning
The primary takeaway from this experiment is that adding a premium image to the Today in the Word website did not increase engagement or downstream donor metrics. Instead, the treatment featuring the premium image experienced a statistically valid 47% decrease in clicks compared to the control.
This decline extended to giving behavior. The control generated six gifts through the Dear Reader, while the treatment generated none—resulting in a 100% decrease in gifts at a 98% level of confidence.
Importantly, this outcome was consistent across devices. Clicks declined on mobile by 43% (valid), on desktop by 81% (valid), and on tablet by 21% (27% LOC). While desktop users responded most negatively to the image, the pattern indicates that users on all devices were less likely to click the CTA when the image was present.
Implications
These results suggest that the premium image may have distracted from the core message or failed to reinforce the value of the offer for this audience. Rather than increasing motivation, the visual element appears to have introduced friction at a critical decision point.
Next Steps
Future experimentation should approach visual elements with caution and intentionality. Continued testing of alternative imagery—or the removal of images altogether—will help identify what best supports clarity, engagement, and conversion. Ongoing analysis of engagement and donation data will remain essential to guiding optimizations that drive meaningful donor response.

experiments.title: Can Highlighting Monthly Impact Boost Recurring Giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly giving callout above the donation form on the main donation page will increase monthly donor conversions, as framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations. Because framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/26/2025
An organization’s main donation page serves both new and returning donors, including high-value supporters. Unlike acquisition-focused pages, this page represents a high-opportunity, high-risk environment for testing.
Previous tests on acquisition pages have shown that adding a monthly gift callout increases recurring gifts, suggesting that some visitors can be persuaded to become long-term donors.
This experiment aimed to determine if the prior finding would carry over to the main donation page without reducing revenue from larger one-time gifts. The core question: Would prompting donors to consider a monthly gift boost monthly conversions—without causing high-capacity donors to switch from making large one-time gifts to smaller recurring ones?
The Control used the regular donation page, defaulting to a one-time gift with no prompts for monthly giving.
The Treatment added a soft callout above the form: “Would you consider a monthly donation? Your $15 monthly gift helps families in spiritual crisis receive trusted help immediately and biblical support throughout the year. You’ll also receive a new free resource for your family each month.”
At the conclusion of the one-month test, the data revealed whether the monthly donation callout on the main donation page increased monthly conversions, had no effect, or caused a reduction in larger one-time gifts. This clarified the impact of the prompt on donor behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that a soft monthly giving prompt above the donation form can significantly increase recurring donor conversions—without suppressing large gifts and with potential upside for smaller one-time donations.
The Treatment produced a 125% increase in monthly donor conversions (2.1% vs. 0.9%), with a statistically significant 99.3% level of confidence
 	Recurring donor volume more than doubled (33 vs. 15), while the average monthly gift remained steady ($30 on Treatment vs. $31 on Control, after removing an outlier)
 	While the Treatment had fewer gifts over $1,000 (35 vs. 41), this difference was not statistically significant (44% level of confidence), indicating no reliable evidence that large one-time gifts were cannibalized
smaller one-time gifts (under $1,000)
The Treatment saw a 9.5% lift in volume, with an 86.4% level of confidence -- this is a strong directional signal, though not statistically conclusive
 	The average gift amount also increased, from $132 on the Control to $140 on the Treatment
Next Steps for Testing:
Explore alternate monthly ask amounts (e.g., $20, $25, or $30) to assess the revenue impact of shifting the default suggested amount.
 	Refine the callout language or visual design to test whether urgency, impact framing, or reward emphasis further improves performance.
 	Test this approach on other key donation pathways (e.g., campaign pages, seasonal asks) to evaluate if the effect carries over beyond the main donation page.

experiments.title: How does pre-selecting monthly on a tabbed form impact recurring gifts in paid media?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the tabbed form to monthly for paid media users on instant donation pages will lead to an increase in recurring giving, while not negatively impacting overall transactions, as it treats monthly giving as a priority, suggesting it as the primary way of giving, rather than an afterthought or add-on.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 08/05/2025
Time of Grace had recently tested and rolled out a valid increase in overall transactions when using a tabbed form for paid media traffic. We hypothesize that defaulting the tab to monthly would passively increase recurring giving without a negative impact on overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on this data, defaulting the tabbed form to monthly giving for paid media users on instant donation pages significantly increased recurring donations by 1,354.5%.
In addition, Overall Transactions were not negatively impacted; instead, there was a slight bump in giving overall to the treatment. Indicating that this experience is not misleading, the 300+ donors who participated were able to select the type of gift they wanted to give—whether that was monthly or one-time. Total transactions and validity of the lift can be seen below:
Overall Transactions:
Control: 3,452 Samples | 151 Donations |
Treatment: 3,652 Samples | 172 Donations | +10% Increase with 64% LOC.
Finally, within the time frame of this experiment, only one donor reached out to cancel their monthly gift. The impact of this experiment is that Time of Grace can now begin passively acquiring recurring donors from their paid media traffic, without any increase to budget or a separate monthly campaign.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout to a pre-selected monthly tab affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly tab, highlighting the impact of monthly giving for donation page visitors, will increase recurring donations because the callout will help users understand the potential impact they can have on the FUS mission and encourage them to make a recurring donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/08/2025
Franciscan University has content offers of their video series’ running on Facebook, which perform really well. They get a lot of donations from these offers, so we wanted to look into ways we could get more recurring donors from audiences coming to the donation pages.
For this test, we had the monthly tab pre-selected and we added a callout to hover above the tab that had language about how monthly giving was the most impactful way they could help FUS continue doing their mission.
This was tested on the donation page of the offers currently running for them in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding a callout on the monthly tab highlighting the impact of monthly giving led to a 1.5% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
2. However, this increase in recurring donations was accompanied by a 211% decrease in Transactions with a 96% level of confidence and a 411% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully weigh the benefits of increasing recurring donations against the significant decline observed in one-time donations and transactions. Further analysis of why one-time donations and transactions dropped could reveal ways to promote recurring giving without causing large declines in other donation types.
Test different callout messages or positions to optimize recurring donations without hurting other metrics. Segment the audience by behavior and preferences to tailor the approach and maximize total donations.
Continue experimenting with new strategies and closely monitor all donation metrics. Aim to increase recurring donations while maintaining overall donation levels.

experiments.title: How reinforcing security on a campaign donation page donor conversion and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security to the year end donation page will increase donor conversion and overall revenue, as it enhances clarity regarding how a potential donor’s data is being used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2025 - 04/30/2025
America Needs Fatima ran a calendar year-end campaign during December. With increasing concerns about privacy and user data, the team wanted to test whether adding a statement about the organization’s privacy commitment on the donation page would affect transactions and overall campaign revenue. The next step is to analyze the test results to determine whether the privacy statement affected donor behavior or revenue. The test revealed that including a privacy statement had a measurable effect on donation page conversions and total year-end campaign revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security on the donation page significantly increased donor conversion and overall revenue. The treatment group, which included the statement of security, saw a 50.5% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 98.5% confidence level.
This suggests that potential donors highly value transparency and clarity regarding the use of their data. By addressing their concerns about privacy and security, we were able to gain their trust and increase their willingness to make a donation.
In future experiments, consider adding a statement regarding security or privacy on donation pages. Use this on any page where user data is collected. This can help alleviate concerns and increase conversion rates. Further explore other ways to enhance transparency and build trust with donors. Regularly test and iterate on these strategies to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How a popup prompting homepage visitors who abandon the donation page during a matching challenge to complete their gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup reminding visitors who abandon the donation page to complete their gift to double their impact will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2022 - 01/23/2023
The Christian Broadcasting Network is a Christian news and media ministry that reaches millions of people around the world and provides humanitarian aid and practical resources to those in need. During their Giving Tuesday campaign, they tested a popup that would display for visitors to their homepage who started the process of making a gift but then abandoned the donation page before it was completed.
That experiment found that it increased donor revenue. They wanted to also see the impact it had on donations and, because of how the experiment had been set up, we weren’t able to determine the impact it had on donor conversion. So, we retested this popup during the last days of their December campaign to see the impact on donor conversion of this popup.
Because this popup only fires for those who abandon the donation page without making a gift, the control audience in this experiment shows only one sample in the results. Because the control audience, those who abandon the page but don’t get a popup, aren’t recorded in these results. Rather, we are looking to see the conversion of presenting that popup to abandons, so how many donors can we draw back to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In all, 53 people who abandoned the donation page and saw the popup clicked to return to the page. Of these, 26% completed their gift. So, the popup successfully brought back 14 donors who otherwise would have been lost and with them approximately $5K in additional revenue.

This tactic is one that we will continue to test with other value propositions. So, for example, would we reactivate more abandoned donors by presenting them with a lower appeal than what they were considering making? Or would we reactivate more by offering them an eBook or pledge that then pointed to a donation page? These are questions we will seek to answer in future testing.

experiments.title: Does concise donation form copy outperform long-form messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we shorten and clarify the donation form copy to present a concise value proposition, then more donors will complete their gifts (conversion rate increase) and potentially give at higher levels (revenue increase). This is because WUNC’s multichannel donors are already primed and don’t need long explanations—clear, streamlined copy will reduce friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2025 - 10/06/2025
This experiment was conducted on WUNC’s primary donation form, which receives the majority of donor traffic from email, direct navigation, and on-site promotions. The goal was to evaluate whether shorter, more action-oriented copy would improve donor conversion rates compared to WUNC’s traditional, longer-form messaging.
WUNC’s standard donation form historically emphasized emotional storytelling, a sense of community, and shared responsibility for supporting public radio in North Carolina. However, recent research in donation behavior and user experience has suggested that minimizing friction—especially in digital donation flows—can lead to higher conversion rates. Internally, there was interest in exploring whether this applied to WUNC’s audience, especially during high-engagement periods.
The team hypothesized that short, punchy copy emphasizing urgency and clarity might increase conversion by reducing cognitive load and decision-making time. However, there was concern that cutting too much might sacrifice the warmth, trust, and shared identity that WUNC’s donors often cite as core motivations for giving.

experiments.title: How defaulting a form to monthly & adding a monthly callout affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation form to Monthly giving and adding a clear, impact-focused callout for users on acquisition offer donation pages will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because default bias nudges people toward the pre-selected choice, while a tangible impact message increases perceived efficacy and reduces decision friction—making monthly giving feel both easier and more meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/29/2025
Focus on the Family (FOTF) uses free video series offers, promoted primarily on Facebook and Instagram, to acquire new subscribers and donors. These campaigns have recently become highly efficient at driving new support, as optimizations significantly lowered the gross cost per donor (GCPD) and increased the volume of one-time donors acquired.
With this success, the team sought to improve the quality of donor acquisition by increasing the number of recurring donors acquired through this same channel. The key question: could making monthly giving more visually and textually prominent on the donation form encourage more people to begin a recurring commitment at the point of acquisition?
The Control used the standard acquisition donation form, which defaulted to a $30 one-time gift and contained no additional monthly-specific messaging.
The Treatment donation form was identical in design and copy but defaulted to $30 monthly giving. It also added a callout above the form that read:
“Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $30 will enable 50 people every month to get signed up for this free video series.”
The test ran across all active video series acquisition donation pages for 10 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Defaulting the donation form to Monthly and adding an impact-focused callout led to a 187.7% increase in monthly gifts (99.9% confidence), showing that defaults and impact framing can strongly influence recurring donor acquisition. The average gift also increased slightly ($33 → $36), though not with statistical confidence.
At the same time, one-time gifts decreased by 34.2% (99.9% confidence). However, this is because many of the people who would have given a one-time donation chose to give a monthly gift instead.
The takeaway: monthly defaults can meaningfully grow recurring donors but risk suppressing one-time gifts. Whether this is a net positive depends on the lifetime value of new monthly donors compared to the lost one-time gifts.
Future tests could evaluate long-term revenue impact, try highlighting monthly giving without changing the default, or explore post-donation monthly upgrade prompts.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a high urgency campaign impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a monthly first ask for Fall Share giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving. because  more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2025 - 09/26/2025
Each year, Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign to inspire generosity among listeners.
This year, we made a strategic shift: instead of prioritizing one-time gifts, we focused on growing Moody’s recurring donor file. To accomplish this, we tested a monthly-first ask during the Fall Share campaign.
The goal was to build their recurring file while minimizing the negative impact on overall conversion on the giving page.
The test ran for 11 days, with traffic evenly split between each variant.
Note: The majority of participants came from known audiences. While a separate non-donor–specific donation page was available, many of the web assets directed to this general campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a monthly-first ask for Fall Share donation page visitors significantly increased recurring giving. The treatment group experienced a 168.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence in the results.
Furthermore, with this large increase in recurring giving, we wanted to be sure it wasn't at the expense of overall transactions. After further analysis, we discovered the treatment was resulting in a 5.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 42% level of confidence. Based on these results, the value of the more recurring donors outweighs the slight (and invalid) decrease in overall giving.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize the monthly-first ask strategy, as it proved to be highly effective in increasing recurring donor conversions without significantly reducing overall transactions. This approach capitalizes on the convenience and appeal of monthly giving for donors, leading to a higher likelihood of acceptance of the pre-set option and a perception of monthly giving as both the recommended and more manageable choice. By consistently incorporating this strategy in future campaigns and experiments, we can continue to grow our recurring donor file and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a Quick Comment box to posts affects comments on a post and overall tribute donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quick comment box for single post page visitors and profile visitors will increase comments because having that box would reduce the friction of having to think about what to comment on and encourage them to leave a positive comment on a post.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2025 - 09/12/2025
CaringBridge wanted to find ways to increase comments on posts. One test we wanted to do was to have a quick comment box on the first post on the profile feed and on single post pages. This box had some prompts where someone could click on any of the prompts, and that would populate in the comment.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop. We wanted to test how having an easy way to comment with some suggested prompts would affect comments.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a quick comment box with suggested prompts for single post page visitors and profile visitors led to a 5.8% increase in comments left for all traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence. This supports the hypothesis that reducing users' friction by providing prompts can encourage them to leave positive comments.

2. While there was a valid lift in comments, there was a decrease in tribute donations overall and on the single post page.

Overall Tribute Donations - 

Control : 311,312 samples | 633 Donations

Treatment : 309,625 | 565 Donations

10.3% Decrease with 93.9% level of confidence

Tribute Donations on Single Post Pages - 

Control : 311,312 samples | 178 Donations

Treatment : 309,625 samples | 158 Donations

10.8% decrease with 70% level of confidence

Future experimentation should build on these learnings by:

1. Continuing to explore the impact of reducing user friction in different platform aspects to increase engagement and interactions.

2. Conducting further research to understand the unexpected impact on donations and how it can be mitigated or leveraged in future experiments.

3.Considering additional factors that may influence user behavior when implementing new features or changes to the platform, it is essential to anticipate and address any potential unintended consequences.

experiments.title: How the addition of matching language on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the matching language for Non-Donors to the IMB will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because new donors will feel their impact is doubled, creating both urgency and greater perceived value for their donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2025 - 09/16/2025
IMB has been raising support this fall for the Dealympics project. Recently, we learned about a special matching challenge available for new donors to IMB. To maximize its impact, we wanted to test using this match as an incentive—positioning it as the perfect opportunity for someone’s very first gift to IMB.
For this test, the control followed our standard copy approach, while the treatment added language about the new donor match.
*This test was only shown to Non-Donors
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding language about a matching challenge for new donors actually led to a decrease in transactions, including a significant decrease in one-time donations. This goes against our hypothesis that highlighting the match would increase donor conversion metrics by creating urgency and perceived value for donations.

In future experimentation, we should be cautious about assuming that all incentives will lead to positive outcomes. It is important to test different strategies and incentives to see what resonates best with our audience. Additionally, we should consider the impact of potential matching challenges on different donor segments and tailor our messaging accordingly. It is also crucial to closely monitor the results of experiments to quickly identify any negative impacts and make adjustments as needed.

experiments.title: How does showing donors that their gift reaches all three Moody Bible Institute ministries influence conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the impact of a gift on all three MBI ministries for Moody Radio’s Book of Signs premium donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because by clearly demonstrating that their contribution reaches beyond a single ministry, we highlight the added value of their gift and inspire giving by showing it can go further and impact more lives.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2025 - 08/13/2025
Moody Bible Institute encompasses three distinct ministries, but their brands are often marketed separately. We wanted to test whether donors would see greater value in giving to one ministry while having an impact across all three. To explore this, we ran a test within Moody Radio’s Book of Signs premium ask funnel to measure donor response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including the impact of a gift on all three Moody Bible Institute ministries for Moody Radio's Book of Signs premium donation page visitors led to a 22.4% decrease in revenue for all traffic. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a clear difference between the control and treatment groups.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and framing of donation appeals. While the intention was to show the donors that their gift could have a wider impact across all three ministries, it inadvertently resulted in a decrease in revenue.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research to understand donor preferences and motivations before implementing changes to donation pages. Additionally, A/B testing with smaller sample sizes or conducting pilot studies can help in gauging the potential impact of changes before a full rollout. It's crucial to continually test and iterate on donation strategies to find the most effective approaches for engaging donors and driving revenue for Moody Radio and the Moody Bible Institute as a whole.

experiments.title: How does a premium focused vs impact-focused donation page impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impact-focused copy for The Pursuit of God donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates an emotional connection to Moody Bible Institute’s mission before presenting the premium offer, thereby increasing the perceived value of that offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2025 - 08/13/2025
The Moody Bible Institute recently started running acquisition ads. For one of their first offers, The Pursuit of God, we offered a free ebook of the resource but the donation page offered the physical copy with a gift of any amount.
We wanted to learn more about the motivations of EDU donors, so we tested framing the donation page copy around the premium vs around the impact of the gift.
We believe the treatment will outperform the control because it opens with a student-centered impact story that builds emotional connection and urgency before presenting the premium offer. This impact-first approach is likely to increase perceived value and inspire more donors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment conducted for the donation page for The Pursuit of God resource is that the control, which focused on the premium offer, outperformed the treatment, which focused on the impact of the gift. The treatment, despite its impact-focused approach, resulted in a significant 53.9% decrease in revenue for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
From this experiment, we have learned that, in this specific case, donors were more responsive to the premium offer than they were to the impact-focused copy. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing different messaging and approaches to understand donor motivations better. It is essential to tailor the messaging and offers based on the audience and context to maximize donation revenue.
Overall, this experiment emphasizes the importance of testing and understanding donor preferences, as well as the need to constantly iterate and refine strategies based on data and results.

experiments.title: How more concise and clear donation page copy impacts donor conversion metrics on a funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the donation page copy for Pray for a Missionary visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it reduces cognitive load and helps the users reach the call to action faster, while still preserving emotional and scriptural impact to keep them engaged.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 08/11/2025
To improve donor conversion rates on IMB’s Pray for a Missionary funnel, we tested donation page copy that was more concise while still clearly communicating the value proposition of the offer. The treatment was shown to 50% of traffic, and the test ran for approximately five weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
**Concise copy is effective**: The results demonstrate that condensing donation page copy for the Pray for a Missionary funnel led to a significant increase in revenue. This suggests that minimizing cognitive load and allowing users to reach the call to action faster can positively impact donor conversion metrics.
 	**Emotional and scriptural impact is crucial**: Despite the copy being more concise, it was still able to preserve the emotional and scriptural impact necessary to keep users engaged. This indicates that maintaining the core value proposition and emotional connection with the cause is essential for donor conversion.
 	**Statistical significance**: The 100.0% statistical level of confidence in the results strengthens the validity of the findings. This high level of confidence indicates that the observed increase in revenue is likely a direct result of the condensed copy treatment.
**Continuing to test copy variations**: Explore more concise ways to communicate the value proposition and emotional impact to donors. Test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the target audience.
 	**Segmenting the audience**: Consider segmenting the audience based on donor behavior, demographics, or other relevant factors to personalize the donation page copy further. This personalized approach could further enhance donor conversion rates.
 	**Iterating and optimizing**: Regularly review and optimize donation page copy based on user feedback, analytics, and ongoing experimentation. Continuously testing and refining copy will help maintain and improve donor conversion metrics over time.

experiments.title: Will positioning a premium offer as monthly first increase recurring transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directly asking for a monthly gift in the Killing Sin Premium Donation Page copy, combined with a donation form that defaults to monthly giving, will increase the number of recurring gifts by reducing decision ambiguity, framing monthly giving as the expected norm, and reinforcing the value of sustained impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2025 - 08/13/2025
Moody has several Premium offers that their audiences seems to respond to really well. For this test we wanted to see if we can position the donation page on one of their premiums to be monthly focused copy and defaulting the form to monthly, making it the standard choice for users who land on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Directly asking for a monthly gift in the Killing Sin Premium Donation Page copy, combined with a donation form that defaults to monthly giving, resulted in a significant decrease in one-time donations by 42.2%. While we did see an increase in recurring transactions, the sample size is small for recurring on both. While we would prefer recurring donors because their LTV is so much greater, the tradeoff for the loss of one time donors is not worth it here.
In conclusion, while the hypothesis did not yield the expected results in this experiment, the findings provide valuable insights that can be used to refine future fundraising strategies and optimize donation pages for better performance.

In future experimentation, we should consider incorporating more visual elements or animations into our CTAs to make them more engaging and attention-grabbing. It's important to continue testing different design elements and messaging to see what resonates best with our audience and drives the most donations. Additionally, we should apply the learnings from this experiment to other areas of our website or campaigns to optimize our efforts and increase overall revenue for our cause.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement below the donate button during a Challenge Grant affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy on the donate button and adding a statement highlighting an impact statement for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because highlighting the impact of their gift and reminding them that the impact of the gift will double would encourage users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2025 - 08/06/2025
Time of Grace has a challenge grant every year in August. This allows users to double the impact of their gift up to the goal amount that TOG sets, with each challenge grant highlighting a problem that TOG is trying to tackle. For this test, we wanted to test changing the copy on the donate button and adding a statement below the donate button highlighting the impact of the user’s gift. The statement was dynamic and would pull in the amount that the user was planning on donating. So for example, if the user were to donate $25, the statement would read as “Your $25 gift connects more people with the healing and freedom found only in Christ when we are equipped to confess and confront the struggles we don’t talk about.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy on the donate button and adding a personalized impact statement below the button significantly increased revenue and donations. The treatment group, which included the updated copy and impact statement, saw a substantial 187.7% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level.

It is also important to note that we saw a slight increase in transactions as well, making sure that this experiment did not cause a decrease.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue emphasizing the impact of the donation and the concept of doubling the impact through the challenge grant. Highlighting the tangible outcomes of a user's donation can motivate them to contribute more and feel a stronger connection to the cause they are supporting. Personalized impact statements based on the user's intended donation amount can also be a powerful way to make the donation feel more meaningful and relevant to the donor.

Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of effectively communicating the impact of a donation to potential donors and how small changes to the donation process can lead to significant increases in revenue. These learnings should be applied to future experiments and donation campaigns to continue optimizing donation processes and driving more support for Time of Grace Ministry.

experiments.title: How will donations be impacted if we add a step in the giving process?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading users to the landing page instead of the donation page for the 48 Hours of Impact hero image link will achieve less transactions because we will be adding more friction for the user to get to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
Moody Radio is running a 48 Hours of Impact high urgency campaign. Leading up to the event, we wanted to test and see how leading users from the homepage to the landing page instead of the giving page would impact overall transactions.
To test this on the homepage we updated the 48 Hours of Impact Hero Image to go to the landing page instead of the donation page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading users from the homepage to the landing page instead of the donation page for the 48 Hours of Impact hero image link resulted in a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group saw a 90.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence of 99.6%.

This suggests that adding an extra step or friction for users to get to the donation page can deter them from completing the donation process. In future experimentation, it is important to prioritize a seamless and direct path for users to make donations. It is also valuable to consider the user experience and minimize any unnecessary steps that could potentially hinder donation conversions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of streamlining the donation process and making it as easy and straightforward as possible for users to support the cause.

experiments.title: How not including the monthly donation program name and description on a donation page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Not adding the monthly donation name and description on a donation page will increase the conversion rate because the name of the program takes more description to explain than it does to say “become a monthly donor.”
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/15/2025
Mountain States Legal Foundation protects property rights and economic liberty, defends the right to keep and bear arms, champions free speech and association, and upholds the principle of equal protection.
We ran this test on a donation page form connected to a high-urgency email asking supporters to become monthly donors. Audience: MSLF email house file.
Our goal was to determine whether including the monthly donation program name and description would lead to more people becoming monthly donors, or if simply asking them to give monthly—without mentioning the program name—would be just as effective.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that not adding the monthly donation program name and description on a donation page led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group, which did not include the program name and description, saw a 366.7% increase in donations compared to the control group. This suggests that simplifying the donation ask to just "become a monthly donor" without additional information about the program may be more effective in encouraging supporters to donate.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of adding extra information or details to donation appeals. This experiment indicates that a more concise and straightforward donation ask could lead to higher conversion rates. It may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging approaches to see what resonates best with supporters and drives the most donations. Additionally, the results of this experiment can help guide future communication strategies and donation page designs to optimize for conversions and incentivize supporters to become monthly donors.

experiments.title: How clarifying the perception of the problem on a main donation page impacted revenue and donor conversions

rows.name: Treatment - Improved Perception of the Problem

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the perception of the problems that the organization is working to solve for visitors to the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because urgent, problem-oriented framing enhances perceived relevance and impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 07/09/2025
Focus on the Family’s main donation page presents a high-leverage opportunity for revenue growth—if we can identify changes that lift donor conversion. Despite this potential, nearly every prior test on the page has either validated a decrease or yielded no significant improvement.
This experiment aimed to break that pattern by testing a new value proposition. The control copy offered a general appeal to “empower families with biblical resources,” while the treatment made the problem more vivid and urgent. It introduced specific pain points families face—financial strain, broken communication, cultural confusion, and spiritual drift—and positioned the donor’s gift as a direct response to these needs.
The test ran on the primary donation page for users who clicked the main “Donate” button in site navigation. This was our first test in a planned series of experiments to refine how FOTF frames its impact—and to better understand what kind of value proposition most effectively motivates donor action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test showed that deepening the emotional urgency and specificity of the value proposition can meaningfully shift donor behavior—particularly in prompting recurring giving.
The treatment, which emphasized the family crisis and FOTF’s role in meeting those needs, lifted overall donor conversion by 11.9% at an 82.4% level of confidence. While not at the desired 95% validation threshold, these results show that the reframed value proposition resonates more strongly with site visitors.
The most notable shift came in recurring gifts, where the treatment produced a 248.7% lift at a 97.1% confidence level. This indicates that donors exposed to the new framing may not only be more likely to give—but more likely to commit long-term. This behavioral shift could reflect an increased sense of urgency, credibility, or alignment with the organization’s long-term mission.
Interestingly, we observed a -3.8% decrease in one-time gifts, statistically validated at 98.4% confidence. However, the higher average gift size ($56.42 vs. $47.03) and greater total revenue ($51.6k vs. $42k) from the treatment suggest a net positive financial outcome, despite the slight decline in single transactions.
What does this mean?
The results point toward the power of emotionally resonant, problem-focused messaging to increase commitment and revenue—even if it slightly narrows broad appeal. Future tests might explore how to preserve or recover one-time volume while building on the strong gains in recurring giving and total revenue.Next steps: We will test this winning treatment language against a new version of the donation page copy that approaches the value proposition from an identity-based angle, to see which version works best for converting page visitors into donors

experiments.title: How defaulting donation form to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors to recurring will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it reduces decision fatigue and makes it feel like the expected choice, encouraging donors to follow through with the default option.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2025 - 06/27/2025
In an effort to generate more recurring donors for IMB, we opted to default their Bible Direct Ask donation form to recurring transactions for 50% of traffic.
The only change made on the treatment was the form default.
This test ran for about 2 weeks.
We anticipated seeing some level of increase in recurring gifts, but our goal was to also not see a large drop off in overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are: Defaulting the donation page to recurring for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donations by 647.9% at a valid 99.8% level of confidence. These results indicate that defaulting to recurring on a donation form will, in fact, lead to more recurring gifts on this funnel.

We did see a 14.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 53% LOC, but ultimately, a recurring donor is much more valuable than an episodic donor; therefore, the overall trade-off is worth the cost.

We kept the copy and value proposition consistent between the control and treatment to test how the form itself impacted recurring gifts, but in future experimentation, we plan to test using recurring language on the donation page along with a recurring defaulted form.

experiments.title: How a $50 impact callout on a premium donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact callout for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue and average gift because highlighting what the $50 gift could do for someone would encourage users to give a higher gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2025 - 07/08/2025
For Time of Grace’s premium donation pages, we wanted to test some iterations of an impact callout to see how that would affect revenue and average gift. We did and A/B/C test here. For the first variant, the language was highlighting what the gift amount would do. The second variant was more about the impact of the gift. This test was done for Facebook audiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an impact callout to donation pages leads to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment, which highlighted what a $50 gift could do for someone, resulted in a 72.0% increase in revenue for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.
We also saw a significant increase in average gift for both variants, with variant 1 having the higher average gift.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of impact callouts on donation pages. This could involve further refining the messaging around the impact of donations and testing different ways to communicate the difference a gift could make. Additionally, expanding this test to other channels or audiences could help determine the broader impact of impact callouts on donation behavior. Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of emphasizing the impact of donations in fundraising efforts for Time of Grace Ministry.

experiments.title: How adding PayPal as a giving option on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of PayPal for Pro-Life Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics  because  it provides a smooth and effortless giving experience, eliminating any extra hassle while also offering an additional way to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2025 - 05/28/2025
For ACI’s Pro-Life Pledge donation page, we tested the integration of PayPal, leveraging Anedot’s native PayPal feature. Our goal was to assess whether adding PayPal would introduce any friction into the donation process before rolling it out to all users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding PayPal as a payment option to the Pro-Life Donation Page resulted in a notable 28% increase in transactions across all traffic. This outcome was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 96.5%, providing a solid foundation for future decision-making.
While we saw a general uptick in donations, we wanted to determine whether the increase was due to the availability of more payment options or if PayPal itself was driving the change.
Upon reviewing the data, we found that 30% of the donations on the test page were processed through PayPal. This suggests that PayPal is a trusted and credible giving method for ACI’s audience. Additionally, the donation lift closely aligns with the percentage of PayPal transactions, further supporting the impact of this payment option.
We plan to test this strategy in another ACI funnel to verify the results, and based on these findings, we’ll decide whether to implement PayPal across all ACI donation pages.
