experiments.title: How adding Express Checkout options to a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of Express Checkout options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) for pledge donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it provides a smooth and effortless giving experience, eliminating any extra hassle while also offering additional ways to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2025 - 06/13/2025
We wanted to test the addition of Express Checkout options, leveraging native Express Checkout features. Our goal was to assess whether these payment options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) would introduce any friction into the donation process before rolling it out to all users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment suggest that the addition of Express Checkout options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) for the pledge donation page did not have the intended positive impact on donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in transactions at 36.6% compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 99.9%.

Specifically, the one-time donation option saw a substantial 384% decrease in transactions within the treatment group. This indicates that offering Express Checkout options may have introduced friction into the donation process for visitors, leading to a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully evaluate the impact of introducing new payment options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct smaller-scale tests or surveys before rolling out changes to a larger audience. Additionally, analyzing donor preferences and behavior patterns can help in making informed decisions about optimizing the donation process.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of understanding the needs and preferences of donors when implementing changes to donation processes.

experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

2. The statistical significance of 97.2% gives us confidence that the results are reliable and not due to random chance.
3. The hypothesis that the callout with reinforcement language would reaffirm the reason for donating proved to be correct.
4. The use of animation, visual elements, and callout language can positively impact donation behavior.
It is important to note here that although there weren't many recurring donations, there was a valid increase in transactions.
How to Use Learnings in Future Experimentation:
1. Consider incorporating more visual and interactive elements into donation pages to capture and maintain donors' attention.
2. Continue to test different types of callout language and visual cues to see which ones resonate most with donors.
3. Conduct further experiments to understand the specific elements within the callout that were most effective in driving donations.
4. Apply these learnings to other areas of the website or marketing materials to see if similar tactics can be successful in different contexts.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of creative and strategic elements in donation pages to motivate and inspire donors to contribute to the cause.

experiments.title: How adding a donation ‘module’ to the hero image for a specific campaign page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates  because  of the reduction of friction..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2025 - 05/28/2025
With the escalation of conflict in Israel in May, Save the Children US was seeing an increase in traffic to their Gaza campaign page. We wanted to test whether adding a donation module to the hero of the page–reducing the amount of clicks donors had to take to make a gift–would lead to a change in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation module to a landing page on the website.
Key Results:
-       +12% statistically significant lift (96%) in overall conversion rate.
-       +60% statistically significant lift (96%) in recurring RPV.
This test proved that the donation module helps reduce friction and improves donor engagement across main pages of the website. The module also elevates the monthly option, making it more visible and more tangible with individual equivalencies, on a page/issue where we would typically see a majority of one-time donors.
Recommendation/Implementation:
We will be implementing the donation module on the Gaza page. We are planning to launch another test on other landing pages which evaluates the placement of the module on the page.
Desktop:
Control: 17% conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donation button impacted conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for potential donors will achieve change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2025 - 06/16/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that adding the amount to the button when people went to submit their donation would remove some of the anxiety when donating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
-       +31% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.
-       -25% non-statistically significant decline (85%) in overall RPV.
-       -70% statistically significant decline (99%) in recurring donor conversion rate.
-       -46% non-statistically significant decline (94%) in recurring RPV.
Although there was a lift in overall conversion, driven by mobile users, there was a significant decline in revenue. This test suggests that calling out the donation total on the last step causes friction for certain audiences and also gives them another opportunity to question their giving amount.
Recommendation/Implementation:
Given our priority for recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.

experiments.title: How relocating the tribute widget on single post pages affects tribute donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that relocating the tribute widget to below the post for single post page visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because the tribute widget wouldn’t be perceived as a paywall and the user would be able to focus on the messaging of the widget rather than see it as a distraction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2025 - 05/12/2025
On CaringBridge’s single post pages, the tribute widget is located above the post. For a regular user, it may be perceived as a paywall to have to read or interact with the post, and therefore may be off putting for the user. For this experiment, we wanted to see how moving the tribute widget to be below the post would affect donations to the tribute widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that relocating the tribute widget to below the post on single post pages led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 110.8% increase in Donations - Tribute (JEN) for All Traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence, indicating that the change was highly impactful.

Furthermore, there was a substantial 426% increase in Donations - Tribute overall, with a 96% level of confidence. This indicates that not only did moving the widget below the post lead to more donations specifically to the JEN tribute, but it also had a positive impact on overall tribute donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the placement of donation widgets and ensure that they are not perceived as barriers to accessing content. This experiment shows that small changes in design and placement can have a significant impact on user behavior and donation outcomes. It is essential to continue testing different placements and designs to optimize donation widgets for maximum impact and user engagement.

experiments.title: Will Adding 3rd Party Validation Images Strengthen Trust on the Donation Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding 3rd party validation images on the episodic donation page for users to see when they are about to click “donate” will achieve more transactions because the images will reinforce trust and credibility.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 04/21/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions on the main episodic donation page by adding 3rd party validator images. These included organizations that partners with the organization.
We added these partnership icons to the bottom of the page right under the donate button where they could reinforce trust right when the user is about to click submit.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding 3rd party validation images on the episodic donation page did not increase transactions as hypothesized. In fact, it resulted in a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence at 95.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changes to the donation page before implementing them. It is possible that the placement of the images may not have been optimal, as the user as to scroll past the donate button to even see the images, especially on mobile.

We also looked at these results from a new vs. returning user and found that the majority of traffic to this page was from new visitors. Our hypothesis would be that new visitors would be more moved by adding the 3rd party images that those who are returning. However, the data says new visitors are 19% less likely to give at a 98% LoC when the images are added. The majority of new visitors are coming in from a mobile or tablet device where the user would have to scroll past the point of decision, the donate button, to see the images.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Living with Contagious Joy, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for the donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2025 - 04/10/2025
To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for the mission.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a treatment page, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 17 days and tested the impact of defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page within the “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel.

While the treatment successfully increased recurring transactions by 313.8% with a 98.2% level of confidence, it also led to a 45.2% decrease in one-time donations, resulting in an overall 33% drop in total transactions. This indicates that although more donors opted for monthly giving when it was pre-selected, the change significantly discouraged one-time donations.

Given these results, the test highlights a valuable trade-off: optimizing for recurring giving can improve long-term donor value, but may reduce total donor acquisition in the short term. Future testing could explore ways to elevate the visibility and value of monthly giving —such as using stronger messaging, layout hierarchy, or design emphasis. Additionally, tracking the retention and lifetime value of these newly acquired monthly donors will be critical to understanding if the short-term loss in total transactions is outweighed by long-term donor value.

experiments.title: TW – Will changing the donation page to be focused on the donor impact affect conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the reader’s personal connection to the devotional and the fact that it is entirely donor-funded for individuals landing on the donation page will achieve more donations because it makes the impact of their gift feel immediate, essential, and personally meaningful—motivating them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/25/2025
Today in the Word is a ministry that offers daily devotionals. We have an acquisition funnel running all the time to get names into their daily email devotional.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the copy on the donation page for this acquisition funnel would increase the number of gifts.
The control version focuses on the organizational legacy and mission of Moody Bible Institute, emphasizing its long history and continued commitment to spreading the Gospel. The tone is formal, mission-driven, and emphasizes how the gift will be used for broad gospel impact.
The treatment version focuses more directly on the devotional itself and the personal impact it has on readers. It highlights that the devotional is donor-supported, the number of people being reached daily, and positions the donor as a direct enabler of that spiritual impact. The tone is more personal, emotionally resonant, and focused on the reader’s role in expanding access to this devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the reader's personal connection to the devotional and the fact that it is entirely donor-funded significantly increased donations and engagement. By highlighting the direct impact of their gift on expanding access to the devotional and making it feel immediate, essential, and personally meaningful, individuals were motivated to take action and donate.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize personalizing the donor experience and focusing on the individual's role in supporting the mission. By highlighting the immediate and tangible outcomes of their donation, we can continue to drive higher levels of engagement and donations. Additionally, we should consider testing different elements of personalization and emotional resonance to further optimize our acquisition funnels and maximize impact.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 03/14/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $25 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 233 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on Seeing Beautiful Again, one of FamilyLife’s best performing offers.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this retest, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option once again led to an increase in recurring donations. The Treatment group, which featured recurring giving as the default, generated 16 recurring gifts compared to 0 in the Control group. This resulted in a 0.92% recurring conversion rate, validated by a 99.9% level of confidence—providing strong evidence that this approach effectively encourages more donors to commit to ongoing support.
When looking at immediate revenue, there was little difference between the two groups. The Control group generated $902 in revenue, while the Treatment brought in $1,017—a modest increase that is not statistically significant. However, while short-term revenue remained stable, the shift toward recurring gifts has the potential to yield much greater long-term value.
Although overall transactions were slightly lower in the Treatment group (93 gifts) compared to the Control (105 gifts), this was observed at a 66.8% level of confidence. This outcome is to be expected, as introducing a default recurring gift option may cause some one-time donors to reconsider their gift in the moment. However, those who do proceed are more likely to become committed, long-term supporters.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Continuing to test and optimize recurring donation strategies: Based on the success of this experiment, it is essential to explore and refine different approaches to promoting recurring gifts. Testing variations in messaging, default options, and impact statements can help further increase recurring donations.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2025 - 03/05/2025
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
This experiment was previously tested from October 16 – November 12, with the original test defaulting to a $15 recurring gift. The results showed that defaulting to recurring significantly increased recurring donations—the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. Based on these results, we initially moved forward with defaulting other pages to recurring gifts. However, as we continued monitoring performance, we observed lower ROIs and encountered limitations in the donation form’s gift array, which restricted customization for one-time and monthly gift amounts. As a result, we ultimately reverted the default back to a one-time gift.
Towards the end of the year, we saw a noticeable increase in sustainer giving, prompting us to revisit this test.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
Control: Defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The gift array included “$30, $50, $100, and $200,” with no pre-selected amount. The copy specifically asked for $30, while $50 was highlighted as a “popular” gift option. Note: No changes were made to the current live pages for Proof You Need for the control group.
Treatment: Defaulted to a recurring gift, with a gift array of “$15, $30, $50, and $100,” with $30 pre-selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 17 more recurring transactions than the control, resulting in an 830.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 100% confidence level.
However, overall transactions decreased slightly, with the treatment group converting 122 donations versus 141 in the control group. Revenue for the control was $5,520, while the treatment generated $4,470. Despite this initial decline in immediate revenue, the additional 17 recurring donors in the treatment group, with an average gift of $30 and an 85% retention rate, project to generate an additional $5,202 in revenue over time—demonstrating the long-term value of sustaining donors.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While immediate revenue per visitor declined, this result aligns with previous findings that indicate the long-term value of recurring donors outweighs short-term losses.
Next Steps
Default to Recurring Gifts for This Offer: Given the strong lift in recurring transactions and projected long-term revenue, we will permanently default this offer to recurring gifts.Test on Another Offer (Living with Contagious Joy): To further validate these findings, we will run a similar test on another offer, "Living with Contagious Joy," to assess whether the impact holds across different campaigns.Refine Messaging: Continue optimizing the way we present the recurring gift option to further encourage commitment while minimizing any deterrent effect on one-time donations.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track retention and lifetime value of these new recurring donors to ensure the strategy continues to drive sustainable growth for Focus on the Family.

experiments.title: How “kill the negative” language on a post-survey donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing copy to “kill the negative” for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because people are more motivated to fight against negative forces.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 01/17/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a survey on President Trump’s Agenda. The post-survey donation page copy was framed in a positive light, outlining how the viewer’s gift could “help save the American Dream” and “ensure America’s best days are ahead.”
In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, they wanted to test if reframing the page’s copy to highlight how the viewers gift could “kill the negative” as opposed to “restoring the positive” would lead to this increase.
The “kill the negative” treatment changed the more positive copy to language like “take a stand against the forces destroying the American Dream,” and “fight together to secure America’s future,” and added more references to fighting the ideas and principles that threaten America.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reframing the donation page copy to focus on "killing the negative" rather than "restoring the positive" led to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment saw a 55.9% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 96.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating a clear win for the new messaging approach.

Moreover, Transactions - One-Time experienced a substantial 547% increase with a 95% level of confidence, highlighting the effectiveness of the "kill the negative" messaging in driving one-time donations.

These results suggest that people may be more motivated to take action when presented with the idea of fighting against negative forces rather than simply working towards positive outcomes. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different messaging approaches to see what resonates most with donors.

By incorporating the learnings from this experiment into future campaigns and donation page optimizations, The Heritage Foundation can potentially continue to increase donor conversion rates and support for their cause.

experiments.title: How removing specificity from a direct ask donation page affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing specificity of the gift designation for Bible direct ask donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate because donors may feel less emotionally connected or personally invested in a generalized ask, reducing their sense of urgency and clarity about the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2024 - 01/04/2025
The IMB is running a donation direct ask offer through paid Facebook advertising asking prospects to give to the IMB to fund Bibles being provided to different people groups around the world.
Due to issues of the gift designation, the IMB removed language about gift specificity to allow all donations to be designated where needed. Because of this, the “after” donation page did not have:
Gift handles specifying the donation amount
Specific language about what donating to this fund does
A goal thermometer
The results below show the conversion rates of donors during the “before the changes” time period compared to the “after the changes” time period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing specificity of the gift designation for Bible direct ask donors led to a significant decrease in donor conversion rate. The "after the changes" group experienced a 92.6% decrease in donations compared to the "before the changes" group. This difference was statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a clear impact of the changes made.

It is important to note, that our results from this direct ask offer went from the cheapest cost per donor ($206 GCPD) in the most expensive fundraising period ($17.33 CPM) to the most costly cost per donor ($384.17) in the least expensive fundraising period ($2.08). Meaning in the "before the changes" time period, it was costing the IMB $17.33 to show their ads to 1,000 people, receiving a 1.3% instant conversion rate, resulting in a $206 cost per donor. Whereas in the "after the changes" time period, it has cost the IMB only $2.08 to show their ads to 1,000 people. This should mean that our results are dramatically better! But with the new page, the conversion rate has tanked to 0.10%, resulting in an average cost per donor of $384.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the emotional connection and sense of urgency that donors feel when making a donation. Providing clarity about the impact of their gift and giving them a sense of ownership over where their donation is designated can lead to higher conversion rates. Therefore, it is important to communicate the specific goals and impact of the donation fund in order to motivate donors to contribute. Additionally, setting clear expectations and goals for fundraising campaigns, such as using a goal thermometer, can help create a sense of urgency and encourage donors to take action.

experiments.title: Will adding a testiony on the doantion page influence people to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimony for the campaign donation pages will achieve a higher number of transactions because the testimony will show social proof, that other people donate, and share the reasons for it.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase the number of transactions on the campaign giving page by adding in a donor testimonial. The goal of this test is to if a donor testimony is more distracting to viewers, or sparks the reason to give even more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages significantly increased the number of transactions. The treatment group, which included the donor testimonial, saw a 615.6% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 96.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of donor testimonials in increasing donations. This outcome suggests that donor testimonials can be a powerful tool in encouraging others to give, as they reinforce the reasons why someone should donate. It would be beneficial to further explore different types of testimonials, placement on the page, and messaging to optimize the impact on donation numbers.

Additionally, these findings highlight the importance of incorporating donor perspectives and stories into fundraising efforts. By showcasing the impact of donations through testimonials, nonprofits like KCBI can create a more compelling case for support and connect donors to the mission on a more personal level. This can ultimately lead to increased engagement, loyalty, and support for the organization's work.

experiments.title: How Adding A Progress Bar to the Moody Bible Donation Page Impacted Donor Conversion at Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a progress bar to the campaign donation page for the cye campaign will achieve a higher rate of donor conversion because  the thermometer would increase urgency during these final days of the year to reach our campaign goal with donors that would have a high motivation to give. because   the thermometer would increase urgency during these final days of the year to reach our campaign goal with donors that would have a high motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 12/31/2024
In this experiment, we aimed to test whether adding a progress bar to the donation page could communicate a sense of urgency more effectively than text alone. To do this, we created a countdown thermometer that tracked the days remaining in the campaign. As time passed, the thermometer gradually filled up, visually showing how close we were to the deadline. Our goal was to see if this dynamic progress bar could boost donor conversion during the critical final days of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the progress bar to the donation page significantly increased donor conversion rates by creating a sense of urgency. The treatment group, which had the thermometer feature, saw a 23.6% increase in completed transactions compared to the control group with this element present.
In future experimentation, it is vital to continue testing the impact of visual elements on donation pages to drive higher conversion rates during high-urgency campaigns. Experimenting with different visuals, such as countdown timers or progress bars, could further enhance the sense of urgency and motivate more donors to contribute during key campaign days. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze the impact of these elements on different audience segments to tailor the approach for maximum effectiveness.
Overall, the success of this experiment underscores the importance of incorporating visual cues to elicit a desired response from donors and encourages continued innovation and testing to optimize donation strategies.

experiments.title: How rotating a Ways to Help card in the Tribute Widget Impacts Clicks and Tribute Gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rotating Tribute Widget that displays a Ways to Help card 50% of the time for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in Send a Gift card traffic while having minimal impact on the tribute widget because the variety of calls to action will encourage users to explore other ways to give while also not presenting them with the same Tribute ask 100% of the time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2024 - 12/02/2024
On CaringBrige’s Tribute Widget, we hypothesized that having this card alternate (showing a Ways to Help gift card opportunity) 50% of the time would result in an increase of Send a Gift card traffic, while also having minimal impact on the Tribute Gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. **Increased Engagement:** The variation that displayed the Ways to Help gift card opportunity 50% of the time resulted in a significant increase in Send a Gift Card clicked for All Traffic by 41.8%. This indicates that offering a variety of calls to action can encourage users to explore different ways of giving.

2. **Positive Impact on Ways to Help Clicks:** The experiment also led to a 153% increase in the Ways to Help button in the menu clicked, a 3,457% increase in the Ways to Help widget CTA button clicked, and a 418% increase in Send a Gift Card clicked (src=widget). This demonstrates that featuring different giving options can drive higher engagement.

3. **Decrease in Tribute Donations:** The experiment resulted in a 398% decrease in Donations - Tribute clicked. While this may seem like a negative outcome, it indicates that users were more inclined to explore other giving opportunities beyond the Tribute widget.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of rotating calls to action on user engagement. By offering a variety of giving options and monitoring their performance, we can optimize the user experience and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts. Additionally, we should explore ways to further personalize the calls to action based on user behavior and preferences to drive even higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a survey affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a survey will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
because  the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2024 - 08/23/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a survey on Voter Fraud where people can take the survey and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight fraud in elections. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on Voter Fraud survey donation pages include the following:
1. Decreasing gift array amounts can increase donor conversion rate: The treatment, which presented lower gift amounts of $10, $25, and $50, saw an 18.0% increase in donations, with a valid 98% level of confidence, compared to the control with the $25, $50, and $100 amounts. This indicates that lowering the suggested gift amounts made donating feel more attainable to a larger number of people, leading to a higher conversion rate.
2. Increase in one-time transactions: The treatment group also showed a 16.8% increase in one-time donations at a valid 97% level of confidence, demonstrating that the lower gift array amounts not only attracted more donors, but also encouraged more one-time contributions.
3. Decrease in average gift amount: The treatment group also led to a 14.8% decrease in average gift, indicating that lower gift amounts may lead to people giving lower amounts, but at a 4% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.
Additional testing may include lower gift amounts on other survey pages as well as other types of offers or direct appeals.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During ACI’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. However, we hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current  experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that introducing a graphical, interactive slider on the donation page, allowing donors to dynamically select the level of their impact, can significantly increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign. The treatment group saw a 28.9% increase in revenue for all traffic with a 95.7% level of confidence.
Although we saw a valid increase in revenue, we actually saw a 19.5% decrease in transactions with an 86.6% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore interactive elements that provide donors with a clear and tangible understanding of the impact of their donations. This includes incorporating dynamic features that allow donors to see in real-time how their contributions will make a difference. Additionally, we should aim to reach the minimum sample size required for statistical significance to ensure the reliability of the results.
Overall, it is crucial to prioritize clarity, personalization, and interactivity in donation pages to engage donors effectively and drive increased contributions, especially during high-urgency campaigns like Giving Tuesday.

During this organization’s Fall recurring campaign, we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesize that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations will increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
Due to technical constraints, we set this up as a split URL test. Following our development team’s guidance, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic, while the original control received 0%. This configuration allowed us to filter out users blocking VWO redirects, ensuring they didn’t affect the main control group and were excluded from results, yielding more accurate data for redirected pages.
In this test, the control page defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The treatment page defaulted to a recurring monthly gift of $15, with the gift array adjusted to include $15, $30, $50, and $100, with $15 pre-selected. The treatment page was adjusted to ask for a monthly gift.
We ran a similar test on another acquisition donation page, “Reconnected,” as a separate experiment using the same setup to compare results across different offers to determine if the type of offer would impact the results. You can review the results of that concurrent experiment here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. However, overall transactions decreased by 15.1%, with the treatment group converting 157 donations versus 187 in the control group. Additionally, revenue per visitor dropped by 22.4%, but this result only reached a 5.3% level of confidence, indicating that the difference is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While the average gift size was slightly lower in the treatment group ($31.16 vs. $33.76), recurring donors provide more sustainable support over time. The long-term value of recurring donations, as seen in similar experiments, outweighs the short-term decrease in revenue.
Next Steps:
Default to Recurring Gifts: We recommend that Focus on the Family default to recurring gifts on all acquisition donation pages to drive long-term donor retention and sustainable support.Refine Messaging: Continue testing variations of messaging that emphasize the broader impact of recurring donations to further encourage donor commitment.Optimize Recurring Gift Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to determine which options are most attractive to donors and maximize recurring revenue.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their long-term retention and contribution to Focus on the Family’s mission and goals.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Reconnected, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Reconnected” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesize that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations will increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
Due to technical constraints, we set this up as a split URL test. Following our development team’s guidance, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic, while the original control received 0%. This configuration allowed us to filter out users blocking VWO redirects, ensuring they didn’t affect the main control group and were excluded from results, yielding more accurate data for redirected pages.
In this test, the control page defaulted to a one-time gift, with copy asking for a one-time donation. The treatment page defaulted to a recurring monthly gift of $15, with the gift array adjusted to include $15, $30, $50, and $100, with $15 pre-selected. The treatment page was adjusted to ask for a monthly gift.
We ran a similar test on another acquisition donation page, “Proof You Need,” as a separate experiment using the same setup to compare results across different offers. You can review the results of that concurrent experiment here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In a similar experiment conducted on a different donation page, we found that defaulting to a recurring gift option led to a significant increase in recurring donations. On this page, the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 13 conversions compared to just 3 in the control group, marking a 348.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. This result mirrors the impact seen in the "Proof You Need" test, reinforcing the effectiveness of defaulting to recurring gifts.
However, overall transactions decreased in the treatment group, with 67 conversions compared to 89 in the control group, resulting in a 22.1% decrease in conversion rate at an 89% confidence level. Revenue per visitor also dropped by 50.4%, from $2.37 to $1.50 per visitor. This result, though significant, had a low confidence level of 1.28%, suggesting that the drop in revenue is not as pronounced as the figures suggest. The average gift size also decreased in the treatment group ($27.99 vs. $34.42), which aligns with the expected shift toward smaller, recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in overall transactions and average gift size, the surge in recurring donations points to the long-term value of building a recurring donor base. The increase in recurring donors highlights a sustainable revenue stream that will contribute to Focus on the Family’s mission over time, even though immediate revenue was lower.
We believe that defaulting to recurring gifts, along with messaging that emphasizes their broader impact, made it easier for donors to commit to ongoing support. The resulting increase in recurring donations suggests that the strategy can help Focus on the Family build stronger, longer-term relationships with donors.
Next Steps:
Default to Recurring Gifts: We recommend continuing to default to recurring gifts on other donation pages to foster long-term donor engagement.Refine Messaging: Test additional messaging variations focused on the impact of recurring gifts to further motivate donors.Optimize Recurring Gift Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to find the most effective pricing structure for donors.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their retention and contribution to Focus on the Family’s mission.

experiments.title: Does lowering the Ask Amount Increase Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific ask for the slide up personalized ask will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less friction of the donor having to think of how much to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
During Fall Share there is enough traffic driven to the site where we can test outside of the donation page and the homepage. We have previously tested into having a slide up on the internal pages that leads to the donation page.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by having a specific ask without hurting the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
or this test, we observed a significant and positive impact on conversion rates when we either suggested a lower gift amount of $10 or did not specify a particular donation amount. This change appears to have influenced donor behavior in a beneficial way.

It is crucial to highlight that the overall revenue remained stable and was not negatively affected by asking for a lower donation amount. Despite initial concerns that a lower suggested gift might reduce the average contribution, the data suggests otherwise. Below are the observed average gift amounts across different versions of the donation ask:

 	Control (no specific ask): $182
 	$10 suggested gift: $213
 	$30 suggested gift: $167

These findings imply that suggesting a smaller initial gift, like $10, can actually lead to an increase in the average gift amount compared to higher or unspecified asks. This is likely because the lower ask amount feels more accessible to potential donors, potentially encouraging more significant or frequent contributions.

Moving forward, it will be essential to apply this insight strategically when determining how to frame donation asks. Tailoring the ask amount in a way that maximizes both conversion and average gift size could optimize overall fundraising outcomes.

experiments.title: Will expanding value proposition on the donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for the main episodic donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion because it will be able to better prime the user to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/01/2024
The main episodic donation page has always converted donors really well with little to no value proposition copy. Over the last several years we have proposed and executed sever tests on the page and still, nothing will perform better than the little to no copy that they have on the page natively.
For this test, we wanted to see if somebody who is not involved in the Focus on the Family account could come in with fresh eyes and see where this page could see improvements.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition copy to the main episodic donation page for Focus on the Family actually led to a significant decrease in donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which received the new value proposition copy, saw an 80.5% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, with a 100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, Transactions - One-Time experienced a 796% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that sometimes less is more when it comes to donation pages. In this case, the original page with minimal to no value proposition copy performed better in converting donors. It's crucial to continually test and iterate on different elements of the page to find the most effective combination for donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on refining other aspects of the page, such as design, layout, and call-to-action buttons, while keeping in mind the impact of adding additional copy.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.

experiments.title: How a Badge Icon paired with Tribute Widget Treatments Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget for users who are in the Tribute tab will achieve an increase in donor conversions and revenue because it will address the potential friction of not knowing what this widget is, who the gift is to, or why it has value to the profile.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/17/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile.
In a previous iteration, we are able to increase donations slightly by simply driving more traffic to this tab using an animated Badge Icon that showed the number of tribute widgets on the page. However, a large majority of what we saw was an increase in clicks that didn’t necessarily translate to more donations.
We hypothesized that by leveraging the increase in traffic brought on by the animated Badge while pairing it with a Social Proof and Education treatment copy on the widget itself would increase both clicks and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Incorporating social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget can significantly increase donor conversions and revenue**: The treatment that included social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget led to a substantial 204.4% increase in revenue from the widget. This indicates that providing users with more information about the widget and its value can positively impact their decision to donate.
Furthermore, the increase in revenue was statistically significant, with confidence levels of 98.0%. This reinforces the reliability of the results.
In future experimentation, we should continue to leverage the use of social proof and education copy on widgets to increase donor conversions and revenue. Additionally, we could explore other ways to drive traffic to key pages, such as using animated Badge Icons, to further enhance the effectiveness of our messaging. Conducting similar experiments on other high-traffic pages or widgets within the platform could also yield valuable insights and potentially increase overall donations. By continuously testing and optimizing our strategies based on learnings from experiments, we can continue to improve user engagement and drive more donations for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How a microgoal impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking for 25 donors to make gifts today for the housefile will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is a small achievable goal where a donor feels like they can make a difference.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/23/2024
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s fiscal year end campaign, we decided to test a microgoal of 25 donors.
We have seen success with this tactic for other organizations and wondered if the PTV audience would find it appealing as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment conducted for Pathway to Victory's fiscal year-end campaign is that setting a microgoal of 25 donors resulted in a 55.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This significant increase shows that the hypothesis was correct in predicting that by asking for a small, achievable goal, donors would be more likely to participate and make a gift.
We also saw a 122% increase in revenue for the treatment and 40% increase in average gift amount!
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of setting microgoals for donors. This strategy can be used in various campaigns and appeals to engage donors and drive a higher conversion rate. By leveraging the success of this experiment, Pathway to Victory can tailor future fundraising efforts to include similar microgoals, making it easier for donors to see the impact of their contribution and motivating them to support the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How a more positive tone in copy impacts donor conversion metrics on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the 2023 copy will perform better than the 2024 copy for Dr. Carson’s birthday card signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because we tend to use a more negative approach of the “what ifs” if you don’t make a gift which typically performs well for ACI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 08/29/2024
Every year, ACI runs a high urgency campaign for Dr. Carson’s birthday for people to sign his card.
This year, we wanted to test running this year’s donation page copy vs last year’s copy. The 2024 copy had more visual styling on the page in the form of headers and more easy to consume paragraphs and sections as well as a heavier focus on Dr. Carson’s values and a positive tone.
The 2023 copy had a more negative and fearful approach to acquiring more donations and used a much more simple page style.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the 2023 copy, with its negative and fearful approach, did not perform better than the 2024 copy, which had a more positive tone and focused on Dr. Carson's values. In fact, the 2024 copy outperformed the 2023 copy significantly, with a 60.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic in just 3 days, with a statistical level of confidence of 96.0%.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the tone and messaging used in donation pages. While a negative approach may have worked well in the past for ACI, it is essential to continuously test and iterate on different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with donors. Additionally, it is important to continue utilizing A/B testing to gather data and insights on donor behaviors and preferences, which can inform future decision-making and strategy.

Overall, the results of this experiment emphasize the importance of testing different approaches and being open to new ideas and strategies to effectively engage donors and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How a pop-up inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion on donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting one-time donors to convert their one-time donation to a recurring gift will have a more significant impact and will increase recurring giving by making the value proposition of a recurring gift more appealing and transparent to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 07/31/2024
This organization uses a tabbed donation form to enable donors to select either a one-time or a recurring donation. Last year, they tested adding a callout to the recurring tab to highlight the greater impact of becoming a recurring donor. This validated an increase in recurring giving and became a part of all their donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could further increase recurring giving on the page by firing a popup when someone clicks the donate button on the form. This popup would invite donors to give a smaller recurring gift to have a bigger impact than the higher one-time donation they had selected. The form would automatically ask for a gift that was 75% of the value of the one-time donation originally chosen.
We ran this experiment on instant donation pages and the donation pages of their one-time donor-focused July Campaign. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The popup inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to a recurring gift significantly increased recurring donations by 129.4% for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.6%). This indicates that emphasizing the value proposition of recurring donations can effectively incentivize donors to choose ongoing giving.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to highlight the benefits of recurring donations and nudge donors toward choosing this option. Additionally, leveraging popups strategically to interrupt the donation process and present compelling offers can be a powerful tool in increasing donor engagement and conversion rates.

Most importantly, we found that the popup had no statistically significant impact on overall donor conversion, so we were not sacrificing one-time donations by running the popup. Because of the significantly higher retention rate and lifetime value of recurring donors, this increase in recurring giving could have significant impacts on donor revenue for this organization.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy-supportive copy to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding autonomy-supportive copy for people who have signed up for a course will achieve more donations because donors will feel like they have the freedom to make their decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/27/2024
While sending visitors from a free course sign up page to an instant donation page, we noticed the copy on the page could be improved. The copy was rewritten to include things like: a simplified opening, removed redundancy, conversational tone, clarity in donation impact, and consistent gratitude. We hypothesized that this would encourage more donors to make a donations if they felt as if they had more freedom in their decision.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Autonomy-supportive copy significantly increased donations: By providing donors with a sense of freedom in their decision-making process, we were able to increase revenue for all traffic by 69.1% with a high level of statistical confidence. This shows that empowering donors to make their own choices can lead to more positive outcomes.

2. One-Time Donations and Transactions significantly increased: The treatment resulted in a 398% increase in One-Time Donations with a 96% level of confidence, as well as a 385% increase in Transactions with a 95% level of confidence. This indicates that the revised copy not only encouraged more donations but also led to larger contributions in terms of one-time donations.

Moving forward, we should use the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation by continuing to focus on autonomy-supportive messaging in our donation appeals. By emphasizing donors' freedom to choose and the impact of their contributions, we can potentially see continued increases in donations and revenue. Additionally, we should iterate on the successful elements of the revised copy to refine our approach and further optimize our donation pages for maximum impact.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on a premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for FamilyLife donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a more manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/29/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life, and fostering lasting, healthy relationships. In this experiment, we aimed to test whether defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing its impact would increase recurring donations.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version defaulted to a one-time gift of $50 and included a call-out stating, “Equip 39 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” Conversely, the Treatment defaulted to a $15 recurring gift and featured a more compelling call-out: “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and was tested on 5 premium donation pages, including two best-performing offers: Seeing Beautiful Again and 52 Weeks in the Word.
We hypothesize that by highlighting the broader impact of recurring gifts and making it the default option, we would see an increase in the number of donors choosing to make recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for FamilyLife’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift resulted in 20 conversions compared to 0 in the Control group, marking a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 100% level of confidence. Revenue was down by 29.4%, but this was at a 16.1% level of confidence, indicating that the change is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue. Overall transactions were lower, reflecting the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. However, we identified that FamilyLife's recurring donors have a significantly higher retention rate of 77% compared to 24% for episodic donors, an increase in retention of 247%, underscoring the value of focusing on recurring contributions for the organization's long-term sustainability.
The increase in recurring donations can be attributed to the effective use of a default recurring gift option, which likely made it easier for donors to commit to ongoing support. The enhanced messaging, which emphasized the broader impact of recurring gifts, probably resonated more with donors and encouraged them to choose the recurring option.
Next Steps:
We recommend that FamilyLife continue to default to a recurring gift on their donation pages. This should be a permanent update to their microsite.Refine Impact Messaging: Continue testing variations of impact messaging to determine the most effective approach for driving recurring donations.Optimize Recurring Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to find the most appealing option for donors.Monitor Long-Term Performance: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their impact on retention and overall organizational goals.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on instant donation pages impacted gift conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for donation pages following acquisition offers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is added need in that such a small amount of people that receive free resources donate and that there is appeal because of the match.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2023 - 12/20/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For calendar year end, we decided to test adding a sticky bar to donation pages for acquisition offers to see if it would increase conversions and revenue. The copy for the sticky bar mentioned that only 1% of people who request resources donate to PTV. We also mentioned the CYE match that is in place.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to donation pages following acquisition offers significantly increased the conversion rate and revenue. The treatment group saw a 50.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, indicating that the sticky bar was successful in encouraging more visitors to donate.

Furthermore, the statistical levels of confidence were high, with a 98.0% confidence level for the increase in Transactions for All Traffic, a 97% confidence level for the increase in Revenue, a 97% confidence level for the increase in Transactions, and a 100% confidence level for the increase in Revenue for All Traffic.

Based on these results, it is evident that the sticky bar effectively conveyed the message that only 1% of people who request resources donate to PTV, creating a sense of urgency and the desire to contribute. Additionally, the mention of the CYE match likely provided an added incentive for visitors to donate.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using sticky bars on donation pages following acquisition offers, as it has proven to be a successful strategy in increasing conversions and revenue. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to communicate the low donation rate and the impact of the CYE match to further motivate visitors to take action.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of targeted messaging and the potential impact of utilizing persuasive elements in fundraising campaigns.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the gift array for new names acquired will achieve an increase in transactions because we have decreased the perceived sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that has both physical and digital resources to acquire new names through Facebook and website traffic.
When a resource is requested, the individual completes a form to receive the item and is then directed to an instant donation page.
The gift array used on these instant donation pages was $25, $50, $75, $95.
As these are brand new people to Pathway to Victory, we wanted to see if reducing the array would increase motivation to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was conducted on both physical and digital resources, these results are a combination of those resource types.

A breakdown of physical resource results can be found here -

A breakdown of digital resource results can be found here -

By reducing the gift array down to $10, we saw a 106% increase in donations with a level of confidence of 99.9%.

 

Our hypothesis is that by dropping the gift array to begin at $10, we created an environment where the donation ask is less of a "risk" and "sacrifice" from the new potential donor. While someone may not want to make a gift of a larger amount, the ask of $10 is a "safe" level with minimal loss to the donor. For Pathway to Victory, they saw a 106% increase in people willing to make that gift so they were able to reap the increase in transactions which can translate to an increase in revenue. At the very least, Pathway to Victory acquired more new donors to build a relationship with and provide them opportunities to upgrade their gift or to give additional gifts in the future.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the small percentage of users who donate impacted donor conversion on a petition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out the low donor conversion rate for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because   creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity to highlight a lack of follow-through and consistency will motivate more individuals to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/18/2023
ACI is a conservative organization that is guided by the four cornerstones: life, liberty, faith and community. They were running a petition to speak out against anti-life ideology and were driving a lot of traffic toward this offer. Once users signed the petition, they would be brought to a donation page asking them to make a donation to support the pro-life cause.
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether using expanded value proposition language would increase donor conversion on the donation page. The control page uses nonspecific language while the treatment page uses new language that highlights how “Less than 4% of people who [signed the petition] were willing to take the next step and [make a donation].”
Our hypothesis is that the additional language will increase donor conversion on this landing page by giving readers more clarity into WHY they should take the next step and support ACI’s work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using expanded value proposition language around how little petition signers actually donate to support a cause they believe in can significantly increase donor conversion on a landing page. The treatment page, which highlighted the low conversion rate of petition signers, resulted in a 35.2% increase in Transactions and a 360% increase in One-Time Donations.
This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity, along with highlighting a lack of follow-through and consistency, can motivate more individuals to donate. The language used in the treatment page resonated with users and led to a higher conversion rate.
Based on these findings, in future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using expanded value proposition language and focus on creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity. This can be achieved by emphasizing the impact and importance of the cause, as well as highlighting the need for support. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of language to further optimize conversion rates.
Overall, this experiment demonstrates the power of well-crafted value proposition language in driving conversions for nonprofit fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How shortening the donation page copy for one of the most popular offers impacted instant donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy on a donation page for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in donation conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 07/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their longest-running offers, Dueling Populisms, we wanted to test whether shortening the copy on an instant donation page would impact donor conversion rates. The hypothesis was that a donor would have already read a long email and long landing page to get to the instant donation page, therefore we were reducing friction by limiting the amount of copy they needed to read.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Shortening the copy on a donation page for content offer downloaders led to a 41.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, indicating that the treatment was less effective than the control in terms of donor conversion rates.
2. Despite the overall decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, One-Time Donation experienced a 19% increase, showing that the shortened copy may have resonated better with donors looking to make a one-time contribution.
3. It is essential to consider the specific donor behavior and preferences when designing donation pages. Different segments of donors may respond differently to varying levels of copy and messaging.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test different approaches to optimize donation pages, taking into account the insights gained from this experiment. It may be beneficial to tailor the copy length based on the donor's intent (e.g., one-time donation vs. regular giving) and conduct further testing to refine our strategies for different donor segments. Additionally, we should explore other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as design elements, donation amounts, and calls to action, to further enhance the effectiveness of our donation pages.
