experiments.title: How humanized letter copy with photo alignment impacts revenue on main donate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanized letter copy with photo alignment for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion and revenue because it causes the main donation page to read like it’s from a real person, humanizing the experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2024 - 08/13/2024
For CaringBridge’s main donation page, we had tested a series of copy iteration on the main donation page that removed the photo background in favor of a cleaner design, with copy that was stylized to read like a humanized letter. This included a greeting, copy being updated so that it read like an address from Kelly, as well as her sign-off and photo a the bottom. Initially, this no difference when it was tested previously.
However, we hypothesized that by simply updating this letter copy treatment to also include Kelly’s photo aligned at the top of the letter versus at the bottom with her signature would help drive home the humanization of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Humanization of the donation page: By aligning Kelly's photo at the top of the humanized letter copy, the treatment created a more personal and engaging experience for users. This likely contributed to the significant increase in donor conversion and revenue.

2. Importance of personalized communication: Addressing users as if they were receiving a letter from Kelly added a personal touch to the donation page. This approach resonated well with users and led to a higher conversion rate.

3. Visual hierarchy matters: Placing Kelly's photo at the top of the letter helped to establish a visual hierarchy that drew users' attention to the humanized content. This likely played a role in driving the increased engagement and conversion.

4.Additionally, this also had the impact of acting as a social proof since in the copy Kelly also shares how she herself has been impacted by CaringBridge and donor generosity.

In conclusion, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of humanizing the donation experience and personalizing communication with donors. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to test and optimize the user experience, CaringBridge can further improve donor engagement and ultimately drive more significant impact for the nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a continuation headline for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because the continuation headline will provide more clarity on next steps for the visitors. because the continuation headline will provide more clarity on next steps for the visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2023 - 11/20/2023
In an effort to optimize the instant donor conversion rate on our Pro-Life Statement Donation Page, we evaluated treatments to enhance user engagement. As a result, we implemented a continuation headline: “[First Name], your name has been added. Before you go, would you consider making a gift to fight for the unborn?”
We believe the original headline was inadvertently being perceived as a mere “thank you” page, potentially leading visitors to prematurely exit. Recognizing the need to convey a compelling message that extends beyond gratitude, the revised headline is designed to convey a sense of purpose and entice visitors to explore further down the page.
By using the new headline, visitors will know that there is more waiting for them on the page. Therefore, increasing engagement on the donation page resulting in a higher number of transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a continuation headline significantly increased user engagement and donor conversion rate. The treatment saw a 31.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence (97.2%).

2. The revised headline, which conveyed a sense of purpose and enticed visitors to explore further down the page, was more effective than the original headline that seemed like a mere "thank you" page.

3. Revenue experienced an 1.1% increase, while One-Time Donations saw a 33.4% increase, both with a high level of statistical confidence (97%). This indicates that the treatment not only increased donor conversion but also resulted in slightly higher donation amounts.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of messaging and headlines on user engagement and conversion rates. This experiment showed that a clear and compelling continuation headline can significantly improve user engagement and lead to higher conversion rates. It would be beneficial to test continuation copy throughout the body of the donation page to push even more visitors to the donation form to increase our instant donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How detailing the benefits of donating any amount using a monthly giving premium and incentive language impacted recurring donor giving and overall page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that clarifying the benefits associated with giving any amount, while also presenting the benefits of a monthly donation, will increase the number of donors choosing the monthly donation option on the Superbook giving page. This will increase the overall donation revenue without negatively impacting the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/19/2024
CBN is a large Christian media organization that co-produces a popular animated series called Superbook. CBN Animation’s SuperBook series has been seen by millions of children worldwide and is one of CBN’s most effective ministries for children and families.
A few months ago we tested new language on the Superbook donation page that exists to acquire new donors who want to support upcoming seasons of Superbook. In that test we added new language to the page that detailed the benefits that a user would receive if they gave to CBN as a monthly donor instead of a one-time donor. The goal of that test was to increase recurring donor conversion on the page – and it was successful! We validated a 50% increase in recurring giving, but that lift came with a valid decrease in overall conversion on the page. The full test write-up can be found here: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/ewBd
In this test, we want to determine whether it’s possible to add language that clarifies the benefits of monthly giving and increase monthly donor conversion without negatively impacting overall conversion on the page. Our hypothesis is that, if we incentive people to make a recurring gift to support Superbook while also clarifying the benefits associated with giving a gift of any amount we can drive more monthly donor conversion without hurting one-time giving.
Both pages in this test featured the same value proposition copy explaining what a donor’s gift would do, but the Treatment included some additional changes:
Instead of saying “Your gift right now will…”, the Treatment said “Your gift of any amount will…”
The Treatment also added a paragraph that said “As a bonus gift, for a monthly donation of $20 or more, in addition to all Superbook episodes, you will receive streaming access to CBN’s Partner Exclusive library of teachings, devotionals and partner premiums to help your family grow in their faith.”
We set up both versions of the page in VWO and split the traffic 50/50. CBN used ads, emails, and site content to push traffic to the page and ran the experiment for roughly 2 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this test is that clarifying the benefits associated with giving a gift of any amount, while also presenting the benefits of a monthly donation, significantly increased the number of donors who chose to make a monthly gift on the Superbook giving page. This change in language resulted in a 138.4% increase in Recurring Donation for All Traffic with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence.

In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the benefits of monthly giving while also highlighting the impact of giving any amount. In our next experiment we also want to test whether setting the default gift amount in the giving array to $20 will increase conversion and average gift, since right now the page is asking for a gift of $20 but the giving array defaults to $0.

experiments.title: How adding a callout to the monthly tab on the donation form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly callout for for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it draws attention to the monthly option and adds value proposition of the impact of a monthly gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/14/2024
In an effort to boost donor conversion metrics on MSLF’s Searle Petition, we decided to test including a callout on the monthly tab of the form. We have tested this concept with other MSLF offers and seen positive results.
The callout was only shown to half of the traffic and it read: “By giving a monthly gift, you are not just supporting our legal team’s defense 365 days a year, but you are safeguarding your Constitutional rights, your family’s Constitutional rights, and the rights of countless others.”
We believe that adding a callout for recurring giving for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it draws the visitor’s attention to the monthly option as well as provides additional value proposition of the impact of a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly callout for potential donors actually decreased recurring donations by 79.3% compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 97.1% confidence level, indicating a strong likelihood that the decrease was not due to random chance.

After reviewing other donor conversion metrics, we saw some very interesting results. For one-time gifts, we actually saw a 70.7% lift in donations at an 87.5% LOC. While we did not anticipate that a monthly callout would result in an increase in one-time gifts, it does suggest that there is a psychological concept that we could tap into.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further explore donor motivations and preferences through qualitative research or surveys. Understanding donors' perspectives and how they perceive different messages can provide valuable insights for designing effective fundraising strategies. Additionally, continuing to test different variations and closely monitoring the results will help in optimizing donation forms for increased conversions in the future.

experiments.title: How an animated icon on the monthly tab of a donation page impacts monthly donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form, that defaults to monthly, and has an animated heart icon for donation page vistors will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the animated heart makes it more appealing to look at.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2024 - 04/23/2024
Care Net has “Donate” and “Give Monthly” buttons in the header of their website. They both lead to different donation pages. The “Give Monthly” button leads to a page that defaults to a monthly gift with a checkmark. We wanted to test that using a tabbed donation form, that defaults to monthly, and has an animated heart icon. We think it could increase monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the tabbed donation form with an animated heart icon, which defaulted to monthly giving, did not lead to the expected increase in monthly gifts. The treatment group experienced a 100.0% decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 96.0% confidence level. Even though the experiment is considered valid, we should retest this because there were only four conversions total.

Based on these findings, it is important to reconsider the hypothesis that the animated heart icon would make the donation form more appealing and lead to an increase in monthly gifts. Future experimentation should focus on testing different elements and factors that could genuinely appeal to donors and encourage recurring donations. It is crucial to continuously test and iterate on donation forms to find the most effective design and messaging for encouraging donors to give monthly.

experiments.title: How Adding A Donor Testimony on a Donation Page Impacts Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimony on the donation page for the sustainer campaign will achieve a higher number of monthly donor transactions because of the added social proof to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/22/2024
Focus on the Family runs a sustainer drive each year. In this campaign, there will be language all over their site that talks about the impact that a monthly donor makes and will have several CTA, including a countdown for the number of sustainers needed, wiki banners on article pages, etc.. All of those CTA are pointing to their main sustainer donation page for the campaign. We wanted to see if we could increase the number of conversions by adding donor testimony specifically from a monthly donor since that is who we are trying to acquire.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimony on the donation page for the sustainer campaign significantly increased the number of monthly donor transactions. The 61.0% increase in Transaction - Recurring for All Traffic with a 97.0% statistical level of confidence indicates that the treatment was successful in achieving its goal of driving more conversions.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue leveraging social proof, such as donor testimonials, to reinforce the value of becoming a monthly donor. This provides potential donors with reassurance and validation, ultimately motivating them to take action. Additionally, incorporating more donor testimonials or varied forms of social proof across different touchpoints in the donation journey could further improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using monthly giving language and defaulting to the recurring tab in the donation form affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using monthly giving value proposition and defaulting to the recurring tab in the form for lapsed Heritage members will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because we capitalize on the importance of monthly giving and the impact their monthly gift will have on the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2024 - 03/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation sends traffic to their membership renewal page through the main website menu. The goal of this page is to have previous members renew their membership to the Heritage Foundation through a one-time or monthly gift.
For this experiment, we wanted to test how adding value proposition language to the page about monthly giving would affect recurring donor conversion rates. In addition, we defaulted the tab in the donation form to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 179.1% lift in recurring donor conversion with a 96.6% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 24.2% decrease in one-time giving with only an 84.3% level of confidence.

The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Value Proposition Language Works**: Adding value proposition language about monthly giving effectively increased the conversion rate of recurring donors among lapsed Heritage Foundation members. This indicates that emphasizing the impact and importance of monthly giving can significantly influence donor behavior.

2. **Defaulting to Monthly Giving Tab Increases Conversion**: Defaulting the tab in the donation form to the monthly giving option also contributed to the increase in recurring donor conversions. By making the monthly option the default choice, it likely nudged more donors towards selecting that option.

In future experimentation, the Heritage Foundation should continue to test different strategies for increasing donations among lapsed members. They should also consider applying the learnings from this experiment, such as using value proposition language and defaulting to the desired option, in other donation campaigns to see if similar results can be achieved. Additionally, ongoing testing and optimization of donation pages and strategies will help the organization continue to improve donor conversion rates and overall fundraising success.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for renewal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action. because  the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2024 - 04/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a slate of experiments on their membership renewal page to try to increase conversion and get more members to renew as monthly donors. In their last experiment, they had tested the arrangement of the giving form tabs to prioritize their monthly ask first.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
The Heritage team wondered if this might be true for donations, and decided to test it on their renewal page. They added a small American flag to appeal to the patriotism of their donors to the “Join the Leaders Club” tab and to the donate button itself, and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The use of a small American flag on the membership renewal page significantly increased the conversion rate for renewing as monthly donors by 170.4% with a high level of confidence.
2. The treatment version with the flag image and patriotic messaging outperformed the control version by a large margin.
Moving forward, we should consider the following for future experimentation:
1. Including visual elements like the American flag that align with the organization's values can have a significant impact on conversion rates.
2. Personalized and motivational messaging can help create a sense of belonging and purpose, potentially leading to higher conversion rates.
3. It may be beneficial to continue testing different visual elements and messaging strategies to further optimize the membership renewal process and maximize conversions.
4. Consider incorporating similar elements in other areas of the website or marketing materials to maintain consistency in messaging and branding for better results.
Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of using visual cues and persuasive messaging to influence donor behavior positively.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to a news platform site’s header affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 04/10/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
In an effort to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we previously tested adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the category menu at the top of the page on the desktop version, and in the navigation menu of the mobile version. This experiment resulted in a 42.8% increase in donations compared to the control. However, with a 53.2% level of confidence, these results can’t be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
In this attempt to increase traffic to the donation page, we wanted to test whether adding the donate button to a more notable location at the very top of the page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. We also wanted to test whether adding a gift box icon to the donate button in order to match the “branded” style of the “Search” and “Menu” buttons next to it would lead to an increase in conversion rate.
The regular donate button and “branded” donate button appeared in the top header sitewide for both the desktop and mobile experiences. 50% of traffic saw the control with no button, and the two treatments received 25% of traffic each.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors led to a significant increase in traffic to the donation page. The first treatment saw a significant 2,694% increase in clicks to the donation page compared to the control group with no button, while the second treatment saw an even higher 2,866.2% increase in clicks at a 99.9% level of confidence. This result suggests that making the donation button more prominent and easily accessible can greatly impact the amount of traffic led to the donation page.
However, in terms of donations, the first treatment lead to a 9.3% decrease in donor conversion rate (at an invalid 14.5% level of confidence), and the second treatment led to a near-valid 64% decrease in donor conversion rate compared to the control.
While invalid, these results may suggest that by more prominently displaying the donate button and increasing traffic to the donation page, people who do click on the button may be less motivated to give than someone in the control experience who seeks out the donation page in order to support the organization.
Future experimentation is needed to understand what motivates visitors of The Daily Signal to make a gift to support their work. Experiment ideas include testing alternative copy on the donation page to motivate more viewers of the site to give, and testing an alternate icon that aligns more strongly with the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How adding a premium impacted donor conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a premium for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in average gift because there will be greater appeal to choose a higher donation amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2024 - 04/03/2024
When evaluating ways to increase average gift for instant donation page visitors, we wondered if adding a book premium with a minimum gift amount of $50 or more would motivate more people to choose a higher gift amount when donating. We split traffic between a page with no premium against a treatment that included an image of the book and added language that indicated the book was available for gifts of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a book premium with a minimum gift amount of $50 or more did not lead to an increase in average gift amount from instant donation page visitors. In fact, the treatment group saw a 22.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. The difference was statistically significant at a 96.6% confidence level, indicating that the control page without the premium performed better.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully consider the value proposition of the premium offered and the minimum donation amount required to receive it. It's possible that the book premium did not resonate with donors or that the $50 minimum gift amount was too high for many visitors.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different types of premiums, adjust the minimum donation amount, or explore other strategies to increase average gift amounts. Additionally, considering the preferences and motivations of the target audience when designing experiments can help ensure more successful outcomes.

experiments.title: How simplifying and removing organizational language from an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and removing organizational language from an instant donation page for survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will enhance clarity and immediacy, aligning with the fast-paced nature of online interactions..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2024 - 04/01/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey about election fraud through paid Facebook advertising. The instant donation page that follows the survey page had a lot of organizational and repetitive language that focused on the problem of election fraud and how Heritage is fighting back.
We decided to test simplifying the donation ask and removing the organizational language to see if we could increase the instant conversion rate. We hypothesize it will reduce the cognitive load and eliminate barriers to understanding.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that simplifying the message and removing organizational language from an instant donation page can significantly increase donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment saw an 88.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 99.1% level of confidence, indicating a clear impact on donor behavior.

Moreover, Transactions - One-Time experienced a remarkable 959% increase with a 99% level of confidence, demonstrating the effectiveness of streamlining the donation ask and making it more straightforward for potential donors.

It is crucial to apply these learnings in future experimentation by focusing on clarity, simplicity, and immediacy in donation requests. By removing unnecessary complexity and organizational language, nonprofit organizations can improve conversion rates and drive more donations. This experiment highlights the importance of catering to the fast-paced nature of online interactions and ensuring that donation pages are optimized for quick and easy contributions.

experiments.title: How eliminating the highest gift array option from a donation form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of gift array options for a non donor audience will achieve an increase in conversion because there is reduced friction in the decision making process. because  there is reduced friction in the decision making process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 02/23/2024
When sending non-donor traffic to a donation page after signing a petition, we wondered if having a $200 option in the gift array was necessary or if would harm conversion by making non-donors have more options to consider, and feeling that if they could not make a large gift amount, then they should not give. We wondered if by reducing the friction in the process by taking out the highest option—which we surmised would be the lowest likely option for a non-donor to choose as their first gift—we would see an increase in conversion from this audience. We split traffic 50/50 via email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array options for a non-donor audience led to a significant increase in conversions. By removing the highest option of $200 from the donation page, there was reduced friction in the decision-making process for potential donors, resulting in a 28.5% increase in transactions with 98% level of confidence for the non-donor traffic that qualified for entry in the test. This increase in conversions demonstrates that providing fewer options can help streamline the donation process and encourage more individuals to make a contribution.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of reducing the number of gift array options on conversion rates. This could involve further refining the donation page by eliminating additional high-end donation amounts or simplifying the overall layout to make it easier for potential donors to choose a gift amount. Additionally, it would be valuable to explore how this approach may impact donor retention and overall fundraising success in the long term. By incorporating the learnings from this experiment into future testing and optimization strategies, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation can continue to improve its donation process and drive greater support for its cause.

For the PTV audience we wanted to test to see if this feature caused friction in the donation process. Therefore we tested removing it.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of removing certain features or elements from the donation process. It is crucial to test such changes and analyze the results before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is essential to focus on providing a smooth and seamless donation experience for donors to maximize conversion rates.

experiments.title: How removing the fair market value for a premium on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing the fair market value for donation page viewers will achieve a higher average gift because the perceived value of the premium is not influenced by a low fair market value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/14/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV uses Anedot as its donation page platform. A feature of the donation page is to show the market value of a premium. When a donation is made, and a premium is given, the tax-deductible amount is the gift amount with the fair market value (FMV) of the premium removed.
PTV frequently self-publishes books as premiums, and therefore, the FMV is typically less than $5. We have observed that the average gift for these premiums is less than professionally published premiums and, therefore, has a more expensive FMV.
We hypothesize that having the fair market value for the premium on the donation is causing the average gift to be lower for self published books because the value of the premium appears to be less.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the fair market value (FMV) for premiums on the donation page significantly increased the average gift and the number of transactions. The treatment group, which had the FMV removed, experienced a 23.2% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 96.3%.
From these findings, it can be concluded that providing the FMV for premiums on the donation page can negatively impact the perceived value of the premium, leading to lower average gifts and fewer transactions.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue with the approach of not displaying the FMV for premiums. This can help maximize the average gift and increase the number of transactions, ultimately leading to higher overall donations. It may also be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the design and layout of the donation page, to further optimize the donation process.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar affects donor conversion rates on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized sticky bar on a CYE donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization. because  it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2023 - 12/31/2023
ACI ran their annual calendar year-end campaign. In the final four emails of the campaign, they wanted to test how an urgent, personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson would perform. Typically, they see higher conversion through funnels and campaigns when messaging is directly from Dr. Carson, so we expected an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version of the email had a 19.3% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version, with a 95.1% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version experienced a significant 237% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 98% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the personalized sticky bar did not create the urgency and special message that was expected. Instead, it may have had a negative impact on donor conversion.
To use this learning in future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in fundraising campaigns. While using a well-known figure like Dr. Ben Carson may normally result in higher conversion rates, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging aligns with the organization's goals and resonates with potential donors. Additionally, it is important to conduct thorough testing and analysis before implementing new strategies to minimize potential negative impacts on donor conversion.

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.

It is crucial to test any changes thoroughly before implementing them widely, and to always be prepared for unexpected outcomes.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to explore other strategies to increase average gift amounts, such as targeting specific donor segments with tailored messaging, offering different types of premiums, or improving the overall user experience of the donation process. experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small minority people donate after taking a survey affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for survey takers will achieve an increase in conversion rate because  it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”. because   it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a Voter Fraud survey for several months. The survey was incredibly cost-effecient at getting emails, but the donor conversion rate had been less than one percent.
They had seen other tests that highlighted the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take the survey are willing to take the next step and donate. This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Highlighting that only a small minority of people donate for survey takers can significantly increase the conversion rate. The treatment group, which included this language, experienced a 105.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
2. The use of language that appeals to the ideal donor, emphasizing the importance of doing the "right thing" rather than the "popular thing," can be an effective strategy to attract donations. This was evidenced by the significant increase of 91.7% in Transactions - One-Time for the treatment group.
This is an application of social identity theory. This theory suggests that people tend to categorize themselves and others into various social groups. Supporting the underdog can be related to group identity and solidarity with a particular group.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue using the language that highlights the small minority of people who donate in future experimentation. This messaging appears to resonate with the target audience and create a sense of exclusivity and righteousness in supporting the cause. However, it would also be beneficial to continuously test and optimize this messaging to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

				gift).

experiments.title: How increasing an ask from $30 to $60 impacts giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the ask amount for site visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because there is a larger initial ask that a site visitor is prompted by. because  there is a larger initial ask that a site visitor is prompted by.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2024 - 01/29/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, we tested using a $60 ask instead – knowing that we could potential lose donors but perhaps make up the difference in revenue by seeing a higher average gift. This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the ask amount for site visitors from $30 to $60 resulted in a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group, where the higher ask was used, saw a 16.2% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
This suggests that the higher initial ask of $60 may have deterred potential donors, leading to a decrease in the number of donations. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is 99.4%, indicating a high level of certainty in these results.
Additionally, because of the significant decrease in conversion rate, revenue did not increase as aspected. We saw a 9% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 8.38%. It's suggested we test a lower amount so as not to double the ask right away. Or, prompt a specific ask on the donation page itself.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small minority people donate affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for survey takers will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”. because  it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a Voter Fraud survey for several months. The survey was incredibly cost-effecient at getting emails, but the donor conversion rate had been less than one percent.
They had seen other tests that highlighted the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take the survey are willing to take the next step and donate. This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Highlighting that only a small minority of people donate for survey takers can significantly increase the conversion rate. The treatment group, which included this language, experienced a 105.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
2. The use of language that appeals to the ideal donor, emphasizing the importance of doing the "right thing" rather than the "popular thing," can be an effective strategy to attract donations. This was evidenced by the significant increase of 91.7% in Transactions - One-Time for the treatment group.
This is an application of social identity theory. This theory suggests that people tend to categorize themselves and others into various social groups. Supporting the underdog can be related to group identity and solidarity with a particular group.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue using the language that highlights the small minority of people who donate in future experimentation. This messaging appears to resonate with the target audience and create a sense of exclusivity and righteousness in supporting the cause. However, it would also be beneficial to continuously test and optimize this messaging to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

experiments.title: How removing a paragraph of language regarding an unsubscribe affects gifts given through a tribute widget
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the unsubscribe language for journal followers will achieve an increase in conversions from the journal entry page because removing the extraneous language increases clarity. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge drove a lot of emails from JEN notifications, which were sent when a user posted a new journal entry. There were two primary links in this email. The first linked to the journal entry, which users read the post and, at the end, were invited to give. The second was a direct ask to support CaringBridge.
At some point in the last few years, unsubscribe language was wedged in between these two links. CaringBridge wanted to remove this language, but wanted to understand how this had affected them in arrears.
Unfortunately, they were not able to A/B test this email as it was a transactional email. So, they decided to remove the language and look at the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days preceding the removal versus the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days after the removal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from the experiment is that removing extraneous language, such as unsubscribe language, can lead to an increase in conversions by increasing clarity for users. In this case, the treatment version without unsubscribe language saw a 16.4% increase in donations with a high level of statistical confidence, indicating that the removal of unsubscribe language had a significant impact.
In future experimentation, it may be worthwhile to consider removing extraneous language in other communications to increase clarity and potentially improve conversion rates. However, it is important to carefully track and measure the impact of any changes made to ensure that they are having the desired effect. Not all friction is bad friction!

experiments.title: How suggesting a gift amount on an instant donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a $25 donation in the copy letter from Dr. Ben Carson and by pre-selecting it on the gift array will result in an increase in average gift and revenue overall because we’re removing the decision friction for the donor of what amount to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/18/2023
ACI was running a petition to stand up against cancel culture. On the instant donation page following the petition page, users were asked to make a donation in support of ACI and their fight to find solutions against cancel culture. The control page had $25 pre-selected in the gift array, but there was no mention of this ask in the copy from Dr. Ben Carson.
Because the average gift on this page is $19, we thought asking the user to make a $25 gift in the copy would remove decision friction and increase overall average gift and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for a specific donation amount in the copy letter and pre-selecting it on the gift array led to a 68% decrease in conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence. There was no difference in revenue or average gift.
The control version, on the other hand, did not mention a specific amount in the copy and had a $25 pre-selected option in the gift array. This version performed better in terms of donation generation.
Based on these results, it is clear that the specific ask of a $25 donation created decision friction for the donors. By removing this ask from the copy and pre-selecting a default option in the gift array, the control version was able to provide a more frictionless giving experience and generate higher revenue.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of specific donation asks and pre-selected options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to test different donation amounts, language, and default options to optimize for higher revenue and average gift. Additionally, it would be prudent to carefully analyze the potential consequences of any changes in copy or design to ensure that they align with the objectives of the nonprofit and do not negatively impact donation performance.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Sticky Bar on a CYE Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar for prospective donors on the CYE donation page will achieve an increase in conversion rate because donors are more likely identify with and give to a person.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Donation Page, we hypothesized that a humanized sticky bar treatment featuring an image of RTB’s President Fazale Rana would increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized sticky bar treatment featuring an image of RTB's President Fazale Rana did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing conversion rate. Instead, it resulted in a significant decrease in donations.
This result was unexpected since RTB has used Humanized Sticky Bar of Fazale on the donation page to great success in the past. One change for consideration is in the language between the two sticky bars. In addition to having an image of Fuz, the treatment does change the statement from being one of "we" to "I."
It is possible that this ultimately led the mission to sound more indvidual/Fazale focused rather than broadening to greater impact.

experiments.title: How calling out a most popular gift option on a gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the inclusion of a “most popular” callout on a gift array  for the $50 option for potential donors will achieve a higher average gift because it leverages the psychological principle of social proof, creating a sense of trust and legitimacy among potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/23/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute is offering a book called “Why America Matters” for a gift of $25 or more through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to test adding a most popular callout to the $50 option on the gift array to see if donors would give a larger amount to receive the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a "most popular" callout on a gift array for a specific option can significantly increase donations and average gift. The treatment version, which included a "most popular" callout on the $50 option, resulted in a 27.5% increase in donations compared to the control version. Additionally, the treatment saw a $0.35 increase in gift size with a 95.46% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrates the power of social proof, where potential donors are influenced by the choices made by others. By highlighting a specific option as the "most popular," potential donors are more likely to trust and perceive it as a legitimate choice, leading to a higher average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of social proof in different ways. For example, we can test different callouts such as "best value" or "preferred choice" to see if they have a similar impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore the effect of social proof in other aspects of the donation process, such as the donation page layout or testimonials from previous donors.

experiments.title: How reducing decision friction in the gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing decision friction in the gift array for prospective donors coming from paid traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the cognitive momentum will be improved with fewer choices and more social proof as guidance when choosing an amount from the array.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/29/2023
When converting a housefile offer to acquisition traffic, we reviewed the donation page and wondered if the gift array for the housefile was too complex for traffic that did not have as close of an affinity for the organization or have a previous history of giving. We wanted to test tailoring the gift array to the type of visitors we would be sending to the page and we wondered if a 6 option gift array with the majority of options over $100 would cause friction in the process for these prospective donors. We split traffic between the existing control gift array ranging from $25 to $25,000 and lowered the barrier for entry by making all gift options amounts under $100 as well as giving guidance/social proof pointing to the most popular selection to guide people seamlessly through the selection process and give more context for their choice.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing decision friction in the gift array for prospective donors coming from paid traffic significantly increased donor conversion. By providing fewer choices and more social proof as guidance, the cognitive momentum of these visitors improved, leading to a higher likelihood of making a donation.
The treatment version, which included a simplified gift array with amounts under $100 and highlighted the most popular selection, resulted in a 26.1% increase in donations for all traffic, with a 96.2% level of confidence. Moreover, new visitors experienced a significant 261% increase in donations, and email visitors saw a remarkable 615% increase in donations, both with a 96% level of confidence.
To further leverage these learnings in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing and optimizing the gift array for different traffic sources. By tailoring the gift options based on the visitors' affinity and previous giving history, we can further reduce decision friction and increase donor conversion.
Additionally, it would be valuable to explore other forms of social proof beyond highlighting the most popular selection. Testing different types of social proof, such as testimonials or impact statements, may provide further guidance to prospective donors and enhance their decision-making process.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of optimizing the gift array to align with the preferences and needs of different traffic sources. By minimizing decision friction and providing guidance through social proof, we can maximize conversion rates and ultimately increase the number of donations for the Leadership Institute.

experiments.title: How authentic and impact-driven language on a premium offer donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial and increasing the authenticity and value impact language for people on the premium offer donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the increased value prop language will give readers more clarity into what the premium offer is about and how it will add value to their lives. because  the increased value prop language will give readers more clarity into what the premium offer is about and how it will add value to their lives.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/25/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry organization that creates and shares resources to help families grow the relationships that matter most. One of their best-performing premium offers is a book called Seeing Beautiful Again, and we decided to test new value prop language on this offer’s donation page to see if we could improve the donor conversion even more.
For the treatment version of this page, we wanted readers to understand what the Seeing Beautiful Again book is about and how the lessons of the book can impact their own lives. To do this, we added the following to the body copy on this page:
Increased clarity in the body copy to explain the purpose of the book and the problems it will help the reader solve
Bullet points with clear impact statements to tell readers what specific lessons/tools the book will give them
An emotional and authentic review from someone who read the book to provide social proof
Our hypothesis was that the social proof and  increased clarity would lead to a higher rate of donor donor conversion on the treatment version of the page. We created the new treatment and split the traffic evenly between the control and treatment. Since we were running acquisition ads for this offer, we knew we’d have enough traffic to validate the impact of the new disclaimer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a testimonial and increasing the authenticity and value impact language on the premium offer donation page significantly increased donor conversion. The treatment version of the page resulted in a 954.4% increase in donations with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence.
Based on this result, future experimentation should focus on incorporating social proof and increasing the clarity of the value proposition on donation pages. Testimonials from people who have been impacted by FamilyLife's content offers or premium offers can be used to help build trust and credibility, while clear language that explains the purpose of the offer and the problems it solves can help readers better understand the value they will receive.
Our next steps will focus on testing testimonials and impact language on FamilyLife's other offers to see if we can achieve this lift in donor conversion with other offers.

experiments.title: How a stripped down acquisition donation page impacts donor conversion compared to a newer, heavily designed donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a stripped-down acquisition donation page design will increase donation conversion by decreasing friction and increasing the strength of the value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2023 - 07/26/2023
Buckner International’s top donor acquisition campaign has been their Shoes for Orphan Souls campaign.  In this campaign, donors donated pairs of new shoes and socks to vulnerable children worldwide. The donation page for this offer had been tested and refined for several years. Recently, they launched a radically redesigned version of this page that featured a much more image-heavy and designed “look” than their original page that was stripped down and very text-heavy.
They had no data on how this new page was performing compared to their old page and were unable to test the design since Google Optimize ended. We hypothesized that the new page design may be harming donor conversion because it led with the ask and placed the value proposition for giving shoes beneath the appeal. We also feared that the new design could be distracting to users, increasing friction.
Since we couldn’t test in Optimize, we decided to test the new page against the old on Facebook. We created an AB test of their Shoes campaign and evenly split traffic and budget between both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the stripped-down acquisition donation page design was highly effective in increasing donation conversion.
In future experimentation, it is important to prioritize simplicity and clarity in the design of donation pages. The stripped-down design reduced friction and made the value proposition more prominent, leading to a higher conversion rate. This suggests that donors respond better to a clear and focused ask, rather than being overwhelmed with excessive imagery and design elements.
Our hypothesis is that the primary issue with the new design is the lack of a compelling value proposition before visitors are asked to make their gift. It is also possible that the competing CTAs of the new design are contributing to the decreased conversion. The original design has one call to action. The new page has multiple, including a subscription CTA in the footer. It is impossible to know from this limited test which elements of the new design were the most distracting to users and had the greatest negative impact on conversion. Once Buckner is able to launch tests on their donation pages again, we will be able to dig into each element's impact on the page.
Also, due to budget limitations in Facebook, we weren't able to get a large sample of gifts through the experiment. However, at no time did we see the new page result in a higher conversion rate than the old per Facebook's campaign data.

experiments.title: How stating a specific goal in the headline of a pop-up for CYE fundraising affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the goal in the pop-up headline for website visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate of visitors to donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/23/2022
Our CYE fundraising strategy included a pop-up on the Libertas homepage with the goal of delivering the Tuttle Twins series to 200 public schools by raising $50,000. Most visitors to the site want to ensure that we are teaching children the value of liberty so we appealed to this desire with the headline “Help teach Kids the Value of Liberty by Making a Year-End Donation” which is clear and direct. However, we also know that a tangible goal appeals to donors, so we wanted to see if we stated the goal in the headline if it would lift response.

The treatment pop-up saw a 4.3% lift in donor conversion rate with a 98.7% level of confidence. This experiment is only based on 6 total gifts, but due to this campaign being very time sensitive and the control pop-up receiving 0 gifts, we decided to roll out the treatment headline to 100% of traffic.

We believe the treatment headline won in this experiment because the goal gave donors something tangible to give to instead of a more broad topic like "teaching kids about liberty".

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA impacts donor conversion during a Fiscal Year End Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar notifying visitors of the threat of the federal abortion hotline for Care Net’s Pregnancy Decision Line will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and fear of loss.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/30/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
Historically, they’ve used a dear reader at the bottom of article pages with messaging around the urgent need in the pro-life space and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their fiscal year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. The control and the treatment both had the same copy, but the placements were different: the control being at the end of the article and the treatment being at the beginning. On the treatment, once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, the dear reader would then turn into a sticky bar with the same copy.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not have a federal, taxpayer-funded hotline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which consisted of an in-line appeal that became a sticky bar with the same copy when scrolling, performed significantly worse than the control. It resulted in a 70.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this design approach did not effectively convert visitors into donors.
2. Different visitor segments showed even larger decreases in donations. Returning visitors had an 828% decrease. These significant drops in donation rates suggest that the in-line appeal with the fear of loss did not resonate well with these specific segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the Wikipedia "before article appeal" approach used in the treatment did not effectively appeal to visitors to become donors. The results suggest that a different messaging strategy should be tested in future experimentation.
Instead of relying solely on the placement, other emotional appeals or value-driven messaging could be considered. For example, highlighting how many more people can be reached through the Care Net Pregnancy Decision Line might be a more compelling appeal for potential donors, or showcasing how this new federal hotline could impact people being able to find life-affirming care.
Additionally, it may be worth exploring different placements for the appeal. While the treatment placed the messaging at the beginning of the article, future experiments could test different placements such as the end of the article, in a sidebar, or in a pop-up overlay.

experiments.title: How including a photo of a premium impacted recurring gift conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a premium for recurring donor prospects will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because the clarity and appeal of the offer to give monthly would be enhanced for page visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 07/03/2023
We were running a monthly upgrade ask to existing donors on the housefile and wanted to offer a special premium for those that chose to convert. We know that sometimes adding images to a donation page can cause additional friction, but we also believed adding a specific image previewing the premium that monthly donors would receive might increase clarity and appeal for this offer. So we split traffic 50/50 between the control page with no image and the treatment page that added an image of the membership card that would be available to those who made a recurring gift on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of a premium for recurring donor prospects had a positive impact on conversion rates. The treatment page, which included an image of the membership card available to monthly donors, resulted in a 79.5% increase in recurring gifts compared to the control page at 80% level of confidence. We believe the visual aid highlighting the benefit they would receive if they said "yes" to the appeal on this donation page helped more people follow through with converting because they had more clarity around what a Servant Leader Membership Card means.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the inclusion of images or visual elements on donation pages. While this experiment showed a positive impact, further testing is needed to obtain statistically significant results. It is also important to consider other factors that may affect donor conversion, such as the placement and design of the image, the clarity of the premium offer, and any potential friction caused by the addition of the image.
It is recommended to conduct additional A/B tests with larger sample sizes and optimize the design and placement of the image to ensure that the results are valid and reliable. By testing different variations and analyzing the data at a higher level of statistical confidence, we can make more informed decisions about the inclusion of images on donation pages to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the principle of scarcity impacted donations from petition signers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that evoking the principle of scarcity for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because by stating that a limited number of people choose to donate (1%), we are creating a sense of scarcity or exclusivity which can potentially make the opportunity to donate seem more appealing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/05/2023
When running a petition, we wanted to find out if utilizing the principle of scarcity could motivate more petition signers to make a gift as the final step in the funnel. In the treatment donation page copy, we called their attention to the fact that out of the people who submitted the petition, only 1% would typically step up and actually make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. We ran this against a personal message on a donation page signed by an investigative journalist.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we learned after splitting traffic between the control and the treatment is that the treatment, which called attention to the fact that only 1% of petition signers typically make a donation, resulted in a 63.9% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that creating a sense of scarcity or exclusivity can make the opportunity to donate more appealing to individuals.
The treatment also had a substantial impact on revenue. It led to a 70.3% increase in revenue, indicating that not only did more people donate, but they also gave larger amounts. This result suggests that emphasizing scarcity can not only increase donor conversion but also encourage higher donations from those who do choose to donate.
The scarcity message had a significant impact on new visitors, with a 133.6% increase in donations. This suggests that highlighting scarcity can be particularly effective in capturing the attention and inspiring action from individuals who are interacting with the organization for the first time.
Based on these learnings, future experimentation should focus on further optimizing and targeting the scarcity message. Specifically, testing different variations of scarcity messaging, such as emphasizing limited time offers or exclusive access, can help identify the most effective strategy for driving donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a donation interruptor including a monthly upgrade ask affects recurring donation rates for previous donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly upgrade ask for one-time, returning donors during the donation process will achieve higher recurring donation rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 03/25/2023
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a public policy organization during a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor, both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits, and asking them to a) make a recurring monthly donation of $20 or b) proceed with their one-time gift.
This is a follow-up experiment to a previous donation interruptor test in order to test whether less body copy, and thus a better mobile viewing experience, would lead to an increase in recurring donations.
This A/B test targeted only the member donation page (previous donors of $25 or more).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking donors to become monthly supporters instead of making a one-time donation result in a decrease in donations for all traffic.
After running this experiment for 9 days, we saw a 64.6% increase in monthly giving with a 81.6% level of confidence. As a result, this experiment is invalid.
We also saw a 20.9% decrease in overall donations with a 99.9% level of confidence.
We believe that multiple factors contributed to producing this result:
Forcing donors to make another choice after submitting their one-time gift may produce friction. Asking donors to commit to a recurring gift in this case may be too strong of an ask and be perceived as pushy or invasive.Asking donors to join a Leadership Club may produce confusion at what membership entails, how it is different from "renewing" Heritage membership, and seem like too much of a commitment as opposed to a one-time gift to receive a Heritage 2023 member card.Results may be inaccurate due to the experiment recording all page viewers as part of the sample, even before they've seen either the control or donation interruptor experience. The sample size should be adjusted to apply only to those who have clicked the donation form submit button, excluding viewers who land on the page but navigate away before seeing either experience. This will isolate the experiment data only to those who donate or attempt to donate.
Based on these results and the data concerns, we will explore additional testing with added technical feature to record results only when the user clicks on the submit button and sees either experience at that step.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the need for donors to step up for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because visitors will have more clarity and visibility into the needs of the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 06/25/2023
We were seeing consistently low donor conversion for petition signers and we wondered if calling attention to the low conversion would cause more people to stop and consider helping. Our hypothesis was that if all petition signers assume that CTF is receiving plenty of donations they might feel less compelled to give because they believe that someone else will fill in the gap. We rewrote the donation page to draw the visitor’s attention to the fact that only a small portion of petition signers care enough to take the next step and make an impact on the issue they care about from the petition. In the treatment, we also kept one element from previous testing that shares a testimonial to help persuade visitors to give monthly. We split traffic 50/50 for all petitions.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for two weeks, the treatment resulted in a 54.8% increase in donations with a 100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, CTF received a significant increase in monthly donors on the treatment. We believe the increased callout to ask the donor to demonstrate if they truly believe in the issue they just signed the petition for by asking them to take the next step and make a donation would make them feel like they were taking a more exclusive action than the "average" petition signer. The treatment copy could have made the donors feel more trust in the organization because of the transparency about the status of the needs and low volume of donations, which may have come as surprising information to some visitors if they believed other petition signers were making gifts so there was not a true need for that individual to also make a gift of support. We also believe this increase could be partially due to the shorter length of copy that puts the donation form above the fold for visitors so they can see the next step more clearly without mistaking the test on the screen as a confirmation page. In future experimentation, CTF should continue to focus on creating a sense of urgency and emphasizing the importance of donor support for achieving its goals.

experiments.title: How adding a tab for recurring tribute giving affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tab for a recurring donation for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring gifts with no decrease in overall donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
Most of CaringBridge’s revenue came through their tribute donation page, which was driven by traffic to their journals. Because of the episodic nature of CaringBridge usage, donor retention was low — except for recurring donors. Since monthly recurring donors give smaller amounts regularly, they stick around a lot longer.
CaringBridge wanted to test including recurring giving on their donation page to see if it affected overall giving. They decided to allow it for 20% of traffic to mitigate any risk of decreasing overall giving. They did this via a “tabbed” form that gave the user a new “first choice” between a one-time and a recurring gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, the treatment produced no statistically significant change in giving, while producing 8 recurring gifts from a very small slice of the traffic. It is interesting that the treatment drove a directional 3.7% lift in overall giving, which might suggest that the tab decision (which is a "smaller decision" than the gift array, increased overall engagement with the donation form.
This shows CaringBridge that they can safely include a recurring option without affecting overall giving, and produce valuable recurring donors from this funnel.

experiments.title: How a multi-step donation process impacted donations for an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a multi-step donation process broken down into three short steps instead of one long step for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because donors will appreciate the easy flow of the multi-step donation process and will encounter less friction as they complete their donation one simple step at a time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/09/2023
ACI’s donation platform now has the functionality to set up a multiple-step donation process. In this instance, the standard one-step process would be divided into three steps, one to select the donation amount, one to provide contact/billing information, and one to add credit card information. We want to test the one-step vs. the three-step donation process for a popular content offer to see how donors respond. Would three shorter steps lead to more donation conversions than one long step?
We have set up this experiment for their Cancel Culture Pledge, and traffic will be split 50/50 between the one-step and multi-step process for the duration of the experiment. Let’s see how donors respond.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Donors did not respond to the multi-step donation process as we hypothesized. In fact, the treatment validated with a 51.9% decrease in donations after running for only 14 days. The treatment also produced a valid 72.3% decrease in revenue.
Possible explanations for the negative results could be that most donors are accustomed to the standard one-step donation process and this multi-step process led to increased anxiety and possibly confusion. There is also the possibility that the three-step process invited more friction instead of less, as we hypothesized. Instead of having all the necessary information directly in front of them, donors encountered an uncertain number of steps and also more opportunities to flee the donation opportunity. Perhaps adding clarity to the three-step process would improve the donor experience and save conversions. This could be communicated by noting that this is step 1 of 2 or 2 of 3 as they go through the process.
Some tweaks are clearly needed if we want to try testing this multi-step process once more with our audience.

experiments.title: How showing a recurring pop-up to users who are about to make a one-time donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring pop-up to the instant donation page for petition signers will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because the donor will be able to make more of an impact with a monthly gift at potentially a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 05/21/2023
MSLF wanted to increase its amount of recurring donors. To test into increasing recurring donors coming in from petitions, they decided to add a recurring pop-up on the donation page once someone hits “donate now” at the bottom of the giving form.
This recurring pop-up calculated the new recurring ask based on how much their one-time gift was:
It will check if the one-time amount they give is less than $1000 then take 60% of the original amount rounded to the nearest $5.
If that is less than $20 then it will set it to $20.
If it’s more than $100, then it will set it to $100.
After they ran this experiment for one month, we found that the pop-up increased recurring giving by .13% with a 84.1% level of confidence. But we also found that it decreased one-time giving by 37% with an 87% level of confidence.
Due to the average gift in that experiment being $26.89, we believed the calculations of the pop-up caused the recurring ask to not be much less or the same as their original one-time gift which deterred them from giving at all.
We decided to retest this recurring pop-up with different calculations to see if the results would change:
Anybody who gave $100 or less, ask for a recurring gift. If they gave more than $100, do not show the pop-up.
$20 – $100 = 60% of what their gift was
$20 or less = Just ask for a recurring gift of whatever their gift amount was
We believe these calculations will better reflect what a recurring gift would look like in comparison to what a donor would give one-time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring pop-up caused one one-time donor to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift. There were no recurring donors from the control donation page.

Unfortunately, the recurring pop-up caused a 43.8% decrease in the overall conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence.

We believe this means that most people who were about to make a one-time donation left the page when presented with the option to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift. The recurring pop-up added friction to the donation process.
