experiments.title: How adding value proposition to the monthly donation tab on the main donation page impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Value proposition on the monthly donate tab

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the monthly donation tab on the main donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2022 - 11/04/2022
An organization that uses online content and breaking-news stories to engage with visitors wanted to test ways of acquiring more monthly donors on their main donation page.
They decided to test the impact of adding a callout above the “monthly donation tab” on the form with an additional value proposition for making a monthly gift.
They tested this approach on the main donation page (this experiment) and also on a campaign donation page they used in the October recurring donor campaign. The results of that experiment are recorded separately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After one month, they achieved an 11% increase in donor conversion with an 88% Level of Confidence. They were able to validate a 57% increase in donor conversion for returning visitors to the site. Other key segments with only the exception of direct traffic all showed directional lifts in conversion.

Our hypothesis is that for most visitors to the page, the extra value propositional copy increases the perceived value of becoming a donor and thus increases donor conversion. Interestingly enough, we were not able to validate an impact on monthly giving from the callout. It seems that saying that a monthly gift "saves lives 365 days a year" reminded visitors that a onetime gift saves lives as well.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
All Traffic visitors had an 11.0% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 48.6% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 1,025.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 21.1% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 100.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How calling out a monthly gift on a main donation page affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly callout to the recurring option on the donation form for potential donors visiting the main donation page will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 10/21/2022
Mission Aviation Fellowship wanted to test adding a “callout” to the recurring giving option on their main donation form to see if it would increase the number of recurring donations instead of one-time donations.
We added the callout to their main donation page for 50% of the traffic. The callout was a short, one-sentence description on how monthly giving has the greatest impact year-round.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment saw a 455% lift in monthly donor conversion with a 98.9% level of confidence on mobile devices specifically. We believe this happened because the callout absorbs a lot of white space on the mobile view making the recurring donation option stand out more to the user on this page compared to the control donation page or to the treatment page on a desktop.

Other notable takeaways that we saw in this experiment were a 33.9% lift in conversion rate for the all-traffic segment and a 28% lift in revenue for the all-traffic segment. Although neither one of those segments was validated, we believe the lift in conversion for both all-traffic segments can be attributed to the extra boost of value proposition that the donor was given when they were in the process of making their gift. Even though the callout was for monthly giving, it served as an extra piece of confirmation that a gift to Mission Aviation Fellowship was helping isolated people around the world. Therefore we believe the extra reminder within the process convinced more people to make a recurring gift and/or increase the size of their gift.

The last notable takeaway from this experiment is that this callout did not negatively affect donations altogether. We did not want to make users believe that a one-time gift was less impactful than a recurring gift, and it seems from the data, the callout did not have this impact. Donations for all giving only had 3.5% lift in conversion rate with a 34% level of confidence.

To continue further testing, we need to run an altered experiment for desktop users only.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security and privacy of a donor’s payment information impacts conversion at point of transaction
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the perceived security and privacy for donors will achieve a positive lift in donor conversion because online security causes anxiety and friction to the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2023
We know that online security and privacy is highly important to people, especially when it comes to entering sensitive data like payment information online. But how important is online security and privacy really and what causes the greatest anxiety and friction around this topic in the giving process? To test into this, we developed an experiment on Save the Children’s main donation page that communicated how seriously we take data security.  Specifically the message said, “We take data and privacy seriously. Your payment information is secure and will be used only to process your gift.” This message was placed just under the donate button.
Additionally, in order to draw attention to the security statement below the donate button, we animated a lock icon to catch the eye of potential donors.
For this experiment, we split traffic evenly between the treatment and the control and ran it for a total of 33 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment that displayed a security statement and an animated lock icon resulted in an 11.1% increase in donations with a high level of statistical confidence. The key learning from this experiment is that donors DO have anxiety and friction when giving; even at the point of transacting, but when we can clearly communicate to them that their privacy and security is not only important to us, but tell them exactly what we will do with their payment information, we can remove some of that perceived cost in the donor's mind and increase their likelihood to give.
Other notable data points from the experiment were:
Overall mobile donor conversion was higher for the treatment (+16%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).Mobile recurring donor conversion was higher for the treatment (+56%) at a statistically valid LoC (99%). Mobile recurring revenue per visitor was higher for the variant (+83%) at a statistically valid LoC (99.8%).
As a whole, we believe that mobile users saw such a significant increase in conversion (more so than desktop) because the animated lock icon drew people's attention to the security statement. This animation and statement on mobile devices is much more prominent resulting in more people seeing it and ultimately converting at a higher rate.
Additionally, the dramatic lift in conversion and revenue for recurring donations only elevates the very real anxiety that people have around data security. For people considering making an ongoing gift to the organization, the weight of that anxiety is heavier as they consider and question what happens with their payment information every month as it gets charged for their recurring gift commitment.
This experiment resulted in an estimated revenue impact over seven figures for recurring donors.
Future experimentation could involve testing different variations of security statements and icons to find the most effective combination. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test the impact of security messaging on different types of donors or donation amounts to see if the effect is consistent across different segments.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
MAF was running a high-urgency campaign for Giving Tuesday. Due to the previous testing of this concept in other high-urgency campaigns, we hypothesized that adding a sticky bar on the donation page itself reiterates the goal and would increase donor conversion. However, we also wanted to humanize this sticky bar by using Aaron Bear’s (CAO and Vice President) image and name so that it seems like it’s a message from Aaron himself. The added layer of using an authority figure within the organization is expected to increase our conversion rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 49.8% lift in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence when adding the humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page. As hypothesized, we believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page reiterates the goal of the campaign and personalizes the ask since we are using the face of someone that works at MAF.

Moving forward, we will rollout a humanized sticky bar to all campaign donation pages.

experiments.title: How asking for personal information before payment information on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for personal information first for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because asking for personal information demonstrates a genuine interest in establishing a relationship beyond just receiving financial contributions, which can build trust and credibility with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2023 - 04/20/2023
Prison Fellowship International wanted to test asking for the donor’s personal information first on the donation page rather than asking for the credit card information first.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for personal information first on the donation page led to a significant decrease in donations for Prison Fellowship International. The control group, which had the credit card information form first, performed better than the treatment group by 64.3% with 99.5% statistical confidence. Furthermore, the treatment group had decreased revenue and donations across all types of visitors, such as returning, direct, and organic search visitors, as well as mobile and desktop visitors.

We believe the treatment page performed worse because it was easy for people to provide their personal information first, but by the time they got to the credit card information, they had too much time to think about the process which caused them to bounce from the page. By asking for the credit card information first, you're asking them to get the hard details out of the way and by the time they hit the donate button, it is less transactional.

Although we can pull assumptions from this experiment, we recommend testing this concept again since it only received 19 gifts.

experiments.title: How linking directly to a donation page in email impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directing an email resend for the email audience will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the friction of an extra step is removed from the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
PTV launched a new music CD title For God and Country, featuring patriotic songs performed by the First Dallas choir and orchestra. It is typical for PTV to resend emails to people that did not click on the link a couple of days following an initial send. For this CD we decided to test having people go directly to a donation page to make a gift and receive the CD vs the traditional approach of first going to a landing page and then clicking to go to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment is that directing an email resend directly to the donation page can significantly increase the donor conversion rate compared to the traditional approach of going to a landing page first. The treatment group resulted in a 43.9% increase in donations for all traffic, with a statistically significant level of confidence of 97.6%.
For revenue, after removing outlying gifts of $150 and $500, we saw a 32% increase with a level of confidence of 93.5%.

experiments.title: How a value driven express giving headline and giving options impacts donor conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing common express pay options with a value-driven headline will increase donor conversion and revenue because it will reduce the confusion and friction that users feel around the express giving options.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/06/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry that receives a lot of web traffic through its acquisition video series. We previously ran an A/B test on their video series donation pages to test into a new express-giving option that was added to their donation platform. In that test, half of the visitors saw a donation form without an express giving option. The other half of visitors saw the same form with an added express giving section where they could complete their donation using their Anedot login or Apple/Google Pay options.
The results of that test showed that the express giving version resulted in a significant increase in donations, revenue, and conversion rate. We do not believe this indicates that express giving options themselves are the problem, but rather the options used and the way they were presented to users. Simply saying “express checkout” on the form leaves the user with more questions than answers as to how these options benefit them. Moreover, the test used Anedot pay as an option, which was likely too unknown to provide value to the user.
The purpose of this experiment is to test whether presenting just Apple/Google Pay with a value-driven headline will increase the clarity and reduce donation friction for users as they fill out the form. Our hypothesis is that removing the Anedot option and providing extra value language around the express giving option will increase the rates of donor conversion for these acquisition donation pages. We also believe that mobile users will show a higher conversion rate because the express pay options will make it easier to pay while they are on their phones.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the Anedot option and presenting only Apple/Google Pay with a value-driven headline did not increase the rates of donor conversion for the Focus on the Family's video series donation pages. On the contrary, the express giving option still resulted in a significant decrease in donations, revenue, and conversion rate for all traffic when compared to the form without the express giving options. However, one interesting takeaway from this experiment is how Desktop and Mobile users responded to the express giving treatment:
Desktop users saw a 58.5% increase in donations (95.8% LOC), with 70.7% more revenue (98.4% LOC) from the express giving experienceHowever, mobile users made 27.7% fewer donations (99.9% LOC) and gave less money overall, which led to 26.6% less revenue (99.7% LOC)
In contrast to our expectations, the express giving options did not improve the experience for mobile users - it made them significantly less likely to donate. Our analysis is that the express giving option lengthened the form length for mobile users and did not provide enough clarity or value to their donation experience to lift donor conversion or revenue. However, the express giving option led to statistically significant lifts for desktop users, which could be a result of users feeling that the express pay options made it easier for them to donate while at their computer.
Because of these results, we cannot assume that the addition of these express giving options in any form will always lead to an increase in donations. We should carefully consider factors such as the options used, the audience targeted, and the value proposition presented. Further testing would be required to identify whether the placement of the express giving option is contributing to the perceived friction (ex: could placing the express giving option in the Step 2 or Step 3 sections of the form add clarity?). Future tests can also try different value-driven headlines to determine which statements will provide the most clarity and value to users. It would also be worthwhile to test express giving on other donor platforms to see if they have similar results.

experiments.title: How asking for less money on a faith premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
Focus on the Family is a global ministry that offers video series on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
This is the second test that we have done in this series of lowering the donation amount needed to receive the premium offer. The first test we did was with Cherish Your Spouse, you can find the write-up for it here. We saw a significant impact on donor conversion and revenue by lowering the donation ask to 30 from 50, we wanted to see if that held true across all verticals of Focus’s acquisition offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course results in a higher donor conversion rate. The treatment group had a 46.5% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.1% level of statistical confidence compared to the control group.
The experiment also revealed that mobile, new, and tablet visitors had significant increases in donations, indicating that the treatment was effective across different segments. Mobile visitors had a 408% increase in donations, new visitors had a 534% increase, and tablet visitors had a 2527% increase.
Based on this result and the results of the other offers where a lower appeal amount was tested, we will use a lower donation amount for the time being.

experiments.title: How removing images impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing images for main donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the content of the page is easier to read and understand.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/05/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversions on the Spiritual Direction donation page, we decided to remove the additional images associated with each giving level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Avila Foundation aimed to increase donor conversions on the Spiritual Direction donation page by removing the additional images associated with each giving level. However, the treatment group witnessed a 60.4% decrease in donations, which indicates that the control group worked better. Therefore, the hypothesis failed, and the key learning from this experiment is that images play a crucial role in donor conversion.
In future experimentation, Avila Foundation should focus on finding better images and ensure that they complement the content of the page. They should conduct more surveys and collect feedback from donors to understand their preferences and expectations better. Also, they should focus on A/B testing with small changes and daily iterations instead of significant changes, which may lead to adverse outcomes. Additionally, they should consider the seasonality, demographics, and location of donors while designing experiments to make them more relevant and personalized. Overall, the experiment highlights the significance of testing and experimentation in driving data-driven decision-making and improving donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a monthly pop up during a donation process impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the giving experience for those who give one time gifts of $200 or less will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because we are giving the user an opportunity to read through the value proposition for a monthly gift before making a final decision .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/16/2023
IMB has a multistep donation process within a widget. In an effort to increase monthly donations, we wanted to give people the opportunity to switch their one-time gift to a monthly gift. Our hypothesis is that if we give people the opportunity to switch their gift frequency before they submit their gift, we will see an increase in monthly gifts. The chart of standards used for this experiment is as follows:
If the one-time gift is $25 or less, the monthly ask is $5
If the one time gift is $26 – 50, the monthly ask is $10
If $50 – $100 , then $20 monthly ask
If $101 – $200 , then $25 monthly ask
If the gift is more than $200 then the pop up would not show up.
We ran this experiment on the main donation page which regularly receives a lot of organic traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that interrupting the giving experience for those who give one-time gifts of $200 or less with a pop-up prompting them to switch to monthly donations can potentially increase monthly donations. However, the results cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. This experiment resulted in a 25% increase in monthly donations with a level of confidence of 33%. We did not generate enough monthly gifts to validate the results of this experiment.

The donor conversion rate for all donations, however, did see a valid increase. It's unclear as to why this is the case considering that the pop up did not show up until the very last step of the giving process. The next iteration of this experiment will move the pop up after the first step of the giving experience as opposed to waiting until the end. That way, the interruption is done right after they have selected "one-time" as their gift frequency.

experiments.title: How value prop on a donation page impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding value proposition  for Avila Army web visitors will achieve a higher conversion of monthly donors because there is added clarity to what it is and the benefits of joining. because  there is added clarity to what it is and the benefits of joining.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversions on the Avila Army donation page, a monthly donation ask, we decided to add additional value prop around what the Avila Army is and clarity around the benefits of various levels of gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by the Avila Foundation showed that adding a clear value proposition around the Avila Army monthly donation ask resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment version, which clarified the benefits of joining the Avila Army, saw an 81.3% increase in donations for all traffic with a 96.5% statistical level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity and value proposition is essential for increasing donor conversions. In future experimentation, the Avila Foundation should consider testing different value propositions and messaging to determine what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, they should continue to prioritize the reinforcement of the benefits of joining the Avila Army and emphasizing the impact of their mission.

experiments.title: How leveraging specificity and reciprocity on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being specific on the impact of a donor’s gift for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors are not just giving to a cause but making sure that one other person benefits from these resources, which uses the psychological concept of reciprocity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Reasons to Believe was running several ebook offers with an instant donation page that presented the macro mission that the organization addresses. Across all offers, we were seeing a low instant donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that this messaging was not compelling enough for someone to make a first-time donation to RTB. If someone is not familiar with the organization than thinking about their overall mission may not be something they’re bought into yet. We wanted to test creating more specificity in our ask by letting the prospective donor know that we want to get more resources like this into the hands of people who need answers to their questions–will they make it possible so others can access these free resources as well?
This messaging was tested across 3 of their offers that generate the most traffic in order to capture a large enough sample size. Most of the traffic generated to these offers are from paid social ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experimentation is that being specific about the impact of a donor's gift on a potential recipient greatly increases the donor conversion rate. By using the psychological concept of reciprocity, donors feel a sense of obligation to help others benefit from the free resources provided by the organization. In this experiment, the treatment group saw a valid 84.3% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 98%, showing that this messaging resonated well with prospective donors.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue to explore and test messaging that is specific to the impact of a donor's gift. This could include highlighting the number of people that will benefit from a donation or showcasing real-life stories of people who have been positively impacted by the resources provided by the organization.

experiments.title: How the addition of a reader’s review for a giving premium impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a reader’s review of the giving premium help increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2017 - 02/21/2017
Every month Harvest Ministries sends an appeal that offers a premium to people when they give a donation. The primary value proposition on the donation page is about the premium with the secondary message focused on how a person’s donation will help Harvest Ministries in their mission. They hypothesized if adding a reader’s review of the premium offer to the donation page would help strengthen the primary value proposition and increase donations. They developed a treatment that featured a reader’s review while all other elements on the page remained the same as the control. They A/B split their traffic to test it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of a reader’s review for the premium on the donation page helped increase donations for their monthly appeal by 26%! What we can learn from this is that the review:
Strengthened the force of the value proposition on the page
 	Added credibility and relevance to the premium offer by giving a real-life testimony of the value it added to a person's life

experiments.title: How bringing clarity to a call-to-action impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Get Your Resource for a Gift of Any Amount Here
For a Gift of Any Amount, Get Your Resource Here

experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by bringing clarity to the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2018 -
On Focus on the Family’s broadcast pages, they offer a daily resource for each broadcast. The button below the resource said, “Give Now”. In a previous experiment we tested that button copy against copy that communicated what people would get when they would click the button. We used button copy that read: “Get the DVD Here”. We were able to significantly increase traffic to the donation page with this tactic, but we actually decreased donor conversion. We hypothesized that with this approach people were expecting to just get their resource by clicking the resource button on that broadcast page and abandoned the donation page once they realized that in order to get the resource they had to make a gift to Focus. To build upon these learnings, we proposed bringing clarity to the buttons rather than the donation page so that we set the expectation prior to them getting to the donation page. To do this we ran a three way test: “Give Now” was the control, “Get Your Resource For a Gift of Any Amount Here” was the first treatment and “For a Gift of Any Amount, Get your Resource Here” was the second treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both treatment versions increased donor conversion! But the first treatment, "Get Your Resource for a Gift of Any Amount Here" increased donor conversion the greatest with 98.9% validity by 18%! When we communicated not only what a person would receive, but also how they would receive it early on in the conversion process, we were able to decrease friction and bring clarity to the conversion process.

experiments.title: How does conversational, donor-focused language affect donation rates on an instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using conversational, donor-focused language on an instant donation page will achieve higher donation rates by putting the donor at the center of the solution and making the appeal more relatable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 04/14/2023
In an effort to improve the performance of an instant donation page for a content offer that was seeing healthy sign-up rates, we wanted to know how a more conversational tone and donor-focused language would affect donation rates.
We noticed that the instant donation page, as it existed, spoke a lot about the difference the organization was going to make—but we believed there was an opportunity to frame it in such a way that would put the donor at the center of the solution with more conversational language that would forge a stronger connection with the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using conversational and donor-focused language on an instant donation page can significantly increase donation rates. The treatment showed a 54.5% increase in donations for all types of traffic with a 97.9% level of confidence. Desktop visitors had a 1184% increase, returning visitors had a 2138% increase, referral visitors had a 71% increase, and 18-24 visitors had a 7421% increase in donations, all with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue using conversational and donor-focused language to engage potential donors and establish a connection between them and the organization. It may also be beneficial to segment target audiences and tailor messaging to specific age groups or referral sources to further optimize donation rates. Regular experiments and testing will help identify the most effective language and strategies for donation pages.

experiments.title: How reducing the options on a gift array impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of gift array options for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is less friction in the decision-making process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 03/09/2023
Because we had seen promising results on a similar test, we wanted to test reducing the number of options on a gift array for an organization’s French-speaking audience to see if they would also have an increase in conversion. The control donation page offered 5 donation amounts and an optional “Other” box to choose a gift of any amount. The treatment presented only a $10 ask and the “Other” field. We ran the test for 3 weeks splitting traffic evenly and measured results on donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for 3 weeks was the treatment produced a 116% lift in donations compared to the control with 96% level of confidence so the results are valid. We also saw a 136% lift in revenue with 98% level of confidence. However, we still had less than the minimum number of required transactions for these results to be statistically significant. We believe these results are due to the decrease in decision making because the donor does not have to spend additional time deciding how much to give. In future testing, we will want to test other gift amounts presented as an isolated option to see if the low $10 amount was the reason for the increase or if it was solely a result of reducing the number of options.

experiments.title: How a donation interruptor including a monthly upgrade ask affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
presenting a monthly upgrade ask for one-time donors during the donation process will achieve higher recurring donation rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2023 - 01/30/2023
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a public policy organization during a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor, both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits, and asking them to a) make a recurring monthly donation of $20 or b) proceed with their one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for approximately 2 weeks, we observed a  93% increase in monthly giving with a 99.2% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
And while at first glance it appears that the treatment produced a 12.6% decrease in donations and a 16.4% decrease in revenue, upon further examination it appears unlikely that these dips were caused by the pop-up since viewers technically "enter" the experiment upon page-load and the pop-up does not appear until they have already made the decision to give. Additionally, language in the headline of the pop-up indicates to the donor that their donation has not yet been processed to reduce instances of closing the browser window before the donation process is complete.
This leads us to believe that any decrease in donations and/or revenue is more likely a result of the visitor deciding to give when viewing the page, rather than a result of the pop-up itself.
Based on these results, we will explore additional opportunities to globalize this learning.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of a gift’s impact on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing greater clarity to how a donor’s gift is used for IMB’s audience will achieve an increase in conversion rate because donors can better imagine the tangible impact of their gift. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 03/15/2023
IMB launched an advent ebook offer at the end of the calendar year. Given that this resource would have a short lifespan we wanted to maximize donor conversion rate in the funnel. We noticed that the existing donation page might read as vague or intangible to the reader. We hypothesized that by not simply talking about what IMB does, but how (and more importantly why a gift makes this possible) it would bring clarity to the IMB’s ask as well as what the donor’s gift will be used for—we believe these two changes together will lift donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by IMB showed that providing greater clarity to how a donor's gift is used can significantly increase conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 262% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence.
What's interesting is that both control and treatment say where the donors gift will go. But where the control seems to focus on this more as a way of reassuring the donor about allocation (and only gets as specific as physical and spiritual needs) the treatment go into detailed bullet points. Cross-cultural training, medical supplies, clean water—these are tangible benefits that we can imagine versus trying to define what a "physical" and "spiritual" need is.
It's likely that the addition of these bullet points decreases any type of anxiety that the prospective donor may have about how their donation will be used.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition to focus on “more content like this” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the value proposition on “more content like this” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in conversion rate  because  specificity helps decrease cognitive friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2023 - 03/26/2023
Luther Seminary has run this experiment across multiple of their content offers running in paid acquisition through Facebook including study guides and an online course for Revelation. They recently launched a new online course about the Book of Psalms, so we wanted to test this concept on the instant donation page to see if it would produce the same lift in conversion as it did for Revelation (834.6% lift with a 99.1% LoC).
The control donation page focused on reasons to give that were given were very high-level, wide-ranging, and broad. We hypothesized that if they focused the value proposition on just one element—particularly, the online courses that the user had just signed up for, they could improve donor conversion rate.
So, we drafted a new donation page with this “micro-value-proposition” and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than three weeks, the single-value-proposition treatment produced a lift in conversion rate by 105.2% with a 98.2% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrated that Luther Seminary might significantly increase its donation rate by focusing the value proposition on a single element — in this case, the online course it promotes. By providing a "micro-value-proposition," which is more targeted and relevant, it helps decrease cognitive friction and increase conversions.
Furthermore, the experiment showed that the treatment was particularly effective for email visitors, desktop visitors, and returning visitors. Email visitors saw a 1564% increase in donations, desktop visitors had a 1350% increase in donations, and returning visitors had a massive 4817% increase in donations.
In future experimentation, organizations should focus on implementing a more targeted and relevant value proposition to improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How moving transactional language and fund codes from the header to the bottom of the copy impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that not leading with transactional information on a donation page for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because focusing on value proposition can reduce friction and further humanize the experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2023 - 03/12/2023
While the Navigators have implemented a complete radical redesign of their donation pages, there is one element on their donation pages that must be kept as part of the template due to platform restrictions. The “Support The Navigators Ministry” and Fund ID at the top of the donation page is needed for in-platform tracking. Being unable to hide this mandatory header, we decided to test moving the placement of this information. We hypothesized that this transactional language would not inspire more people give. We wanted to give people the opportunity to read through the entire value proposition before making their gift. So, we tested moving the transactional language and Fund ID to the bottom of the experience, beneath the gift array.
This placement was tested across all the active instant donation pages for the Navigators’ offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a valid 25% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95%. While this result did not align with what we believed would occur, we hypothesize that there are a few reasons that people may not have given.
The change headline actually made the "thank you" line not seem like a an actual header. Though the fund ID sounds transactional - the first sentence says "Support the Navigators" and it may be that this sentence is giving the reader more clarity as to what they're supposed to do on that page Seeing thank you at the very top with no other CTA may make the person think that this is the end of the interaction.
It is recommended that we run this test with a couple new treatments:
The thank you headline with a larger font. The thank you headline with a larger font and a transition sentence that will help the reader understand that there is more action to take on  this confirmation page.

experiments.title: How removing a paragraph of language regarding an unsubscribe affects gifts given through a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the unsubscribe language for journal followers will achieve an increase in conversions from the direct link because removing the extraneous language increases clarity..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge drove a lot of emails from JEN notifications, which were sent when a user posted a new journal entry. There were two primary links in this email. The first linked to the journal entry, which users read the post and, at the end, were invited to give. The second was a direct ask to support CaringBridge.
At some point in the last few years, unsubscribe language was wedged in between these two links. CaringBridge wanted to remove this language, but wanted to understand how this had affected them in arrears.
Unfortunately, they were not able to A/B test this email as it was a transactional email. So, they decided to remove the language and look at the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days preceding the removal versus the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days after the removal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing unsubscribe language for journal followers had a significant positive impact on donations for all traffic. The treatment achieved a 62.3% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that removing the extraneous language increased clarity and helped users focus on the call-to-action.
This particularly affected the direct ask, as it was positioned below the unsubscribe language. However, it also positively affected the traffic and conversion from the journal link, which showed that this extra paragraph negatively affected both the copy above it and below it.

experiments.title: How Removing Express Checkout Options from an Acquisition Donation Page impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the express giving option for acquisition donation pages will achieve a higher conversion rate because the express checkout creates friction on the page . because  the express checkout creates friction on the page .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/20/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry that receives tons of traffic through its acquisition video series. In the past month, we noticed that the platform that they are using for donations recently rolled out an update that included “express giving options”. We wanted to test the impact this new feature has on their donor base and prospective donors who have not given to FOTF before.
We decided to run an A/B test showing half of the people who hit the landing page the old donation form version without the express giving option, and another half of the people who hit the donation pages the new form that contains the express giving option.
We hypothesized that the express giving option could cause friction in the donation process due to the lack of clarification on the form as to what these express options were and what benefit or value they provided the donor. While options like Google Pay (visible to any google device) and Apple Pay (visible to any Apple device) are more widely known to donors, Anedot pay is a newer feature specific to just Anedot donation pages and could add mental friction for simply being an unknown payment option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test showed that removing the express giving option resulted in a significant increase in donations, revenue, and conversion rate.
We do not believe this indicates that express giving options themselves are the problem but rather the options used and the way they are presented. Simply saying "express checkout" on the form leaves the user with more questions than answers as to how these options benefit them. Moreover, Anedot pay is likely too unknown to provide value to the user.
That is why we want to test a version of the form that presents just Apple Pay and Google Pay with a value driven headline. Perhaps, "Save time filling out this form. Give quickly and securely with just 1-click using one of our express giving options."
We will test this next.

experiments.title: How adding new video creative to an existing offer affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new video for YouTube viewers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/21/2023
At Hoover, we had recently been testing video ads with a voiceover with some success. Some of our older ads did not have a voiceover and the cost to acquire a donor with those ads were increasing.  We wanted to test a creative refresh (that included a voiceover) and relaunch the campaign to compare performance side by side.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a new video with a voiceover for YouTube viewers significantly increased the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the new video, saw a 349.7% increase in donations compared to the control group. This result has a 99.7% statistical level of confidence, indicating a high degree of certainty.
Moving forward, it is important to implement the use of voiceovers in our video ads for better donor conversion rates. This experiment demonstrates that the absence of a voiceover in the previous ads was negatively impacting the cost to acquire a donor. By utilizing voiceovers, we can potentially lower the cost per acquisition and attract more donors.
Future experimentation should further explore different elements of video ads, such as the tone and messaging, to optimize donor conversion rates. It is also essential to consistently monitor and analyze performance metrics to identify any further improvements that can be made. By continuously testing and iterating on our video ad strategies, we can maximize our effectiveness in acquiring donors and furthering the mission of the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How a monthly-first tabbed donation form affected monthly gift conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the one-time recurring decision above the donation amount decision and pre-selecting the recurring option for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge’s main donation page received a steady but low volume of donations. They wanted to test ideas that would convert more of these gifts, which were mostly episodic gifts, into monthly recurring gifts. The monthly selection was below the donation amount selection, and they hypothesized that they needed to move the “one-time or monthly” selection above the gift amount. They also decided to make the donation form monthly by default, so that the user would select to move to a one-time gift. They launched a test of these two changes to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that moving the one-time recurring decision above the donation amount decision and pre-selecting the recurring option for main donation page visitors led to a significant increase in donation conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 339.3% increase in donations compared to the control group. This was statistically significant with a confidence level of 99.9%.
These results suggest that making it easier for donors to choose a recurring donation option and pre-selecting it by default can lead to higher conversion rates.
In analyzing this experiment, the team wondered if the word "recurring" was more confusing than "monthly", and set up a test to better understand the difference in nomenclature.

experiments.title: How sending journal traffic to a high-converting journal page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening journal entries to summaries on the journal listing page for site visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page. because  it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page. because  it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page. because  it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
CaringBridge had done lots of previous testing on their “journal listing” page, a page that was commonly accessed from the navigation section of a patient’s website. They had steadily increased the conversion rate on this page, but it consistently underperformed the “journal view” section of the site, an individual journal entry that had a “tribute widget” on the page.
The journal listing page put the full text of every journal entry available for reading, which made for a very long page. The team hypothesized that, if they truncated the posts and added a “read more” link that set the user to the “journal view” page, they would be able to get more traffic to a better reading experience with a higher converting tribute widget at the end of each post.
This was a highly technical test to set up, but they were able to create an A/B experiment to see which one drove more conversions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening journal entries to summaries on the journal listing page for site visitors can lead to an increase in conversion rate, because it sends more traffic to a higher-converting page. This was evident from the 131.6% increase in donations for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence achieved by the treatment.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue to test and iterate on the journal listing page, as well as other pages on the website, to identify opportunities for improving the user experience and increasing conversion rates. Additionally, it is important to ensure that any changes made are backed up by data and rigorous testing to ensure that they are effective and beneficial for the organization.

Timeframe: 04/25/2022 - 05/16/2022
Reducing the number of ask arrays (by removing the large ask array amounts) will increase conversion because donors will have less indecision with fewer choices. We reduced AA values from 6 to 4 based on supporting data suggesting a link between fewer values and decreased bounce rate.

Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (16%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (98%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (46%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.6%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (64%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.8%).
• Recommendation is to reduce the number of mobile ask array on the main donation form.
• Projected annual incremental lift of $180,000. The conservative net 60-month value of these donors is $2.3M.
• In future experimentation, donors should be presented with fewer ask array options.

experiments.title: How a second donate button impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second donate button for web visitors will achieve higher donor conversion rate because there is clarity in the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2023 - 03/02/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversion from web traffic, we decided to test adding a second donate button that directs to the Avila Army (monthly donation effort) in addition to the regular donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a second button to the Avila Foundation website that directs viewers to the Avila Army (monthly donation effort) can be successful at increasing donor conversion rate. After running an A/B test, we saw dramatic increases in Donations for All Traffic, Revenue, Email Visitors, Desktop Visitors, Returning Visitors, and 55-64 Visitors as a result of using the revised language with two donate buttons. The success of this experiment can be attributed to using clear and direct language which more clearly outlines the ask.
When creating future experiments, we should use this success as proof that implementing two donate buttons with clear and direct language can be effective in increasing donor conversions. Furthermore, we should pay special attention to the messaging associated with each donate button, as this can play a large role in converting visitors into donors.

experiments.title: How reducing friction in an instant donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor response because reducing the form length makes it feel like less work for the visitor and improves cognitive momentum.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 02/25/2023
The donation page software we were using rolled out a new update which changed the donation page template to offer express checkout options and allowed returning donors to “sign in” to complete their donation. We were curious if these changes to the donation page template were introducing unnecessary friction into the process and since these changes were automatically rolled out across all donation pages, we wanted to verify if the new template was helping or hurting donation page conversion. We developed a treatment that hid the express checkout and login options and kept all other elements of the donation page and form identical. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for one week, we observed an overall 41% increase in donations with a 99.7% statistical level of confidence so the results are valid. Our hypothesis was correct that the newly implemented donation page template was hindering donation page conversion by increasing friction on the form. Additionally, we saw a 366% increase in revenue on the treatment version.
The key takeaway from this experiment is that reducing friction, such as login and express checkout options, and making the donation process simpler helped increase cognitive momentum in the process and improved donor conversion. To further validate the results, we should continue to test this same concept on other donation page placements outside of instant donation pages, including the main donation page and high urgency campaigns where visitor motivation may differ.

experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that asking for a range of amounts from previous donors can lead to an increase in conversion rate.
Specifically, the experiment achieved a 36.0% increase in donations for all traffic with a 100% statistical level of confidence.
In the future, we can use this knowledge to focus on tailoring donation asks to different donor segments and understanding which ranges appeal best to each. Additionally, this experiment gives us a model to use when designing future optimized donation pages. Finally, it also highlights the importance of experimentation in understanding donor behaviors. This experience can be leveraged to refine experiments in the future to uncover new insights and maximize donation growth.

experiments.title: How framing your donation ask on an instant donation page around providing funding for other free resources affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a donation to fund the production of the next free biblical resource for study guide downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they saw the value in downloading the first biblical resource on the previous landing page therefore they’re more likely to want to see other biblical resources be produced in the future .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2023 - 02/19/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a free study guide on the book of Revelation through paid spend in Facebook.
In order to try and increase the donor conversion rate on the instant donation page, they wanted to test asking for a donation to fund other free biblical resources to be produced in the future. The treatment page also presented a “premium” in a way that if they gave a donation of $10 or more, they would be the first to receive access to the next biblical resource created.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 109.7% increase in donor conversion rate with a 97.3% level of confidence which makes us believe that these study guide downloaders are more likely to make a donation on the instant donation page when presented with the offer to receive another free biblical resource in the future, which they are helping fund the production of.

The treatment page also saw a 153.7% lift in revenue with a 95.9% level of confidence. We believe these donors are likely to give larger gifts when presented with what their donation will actually fund aka their next free resource.

These results suggest that offering future "premiums" is an effective way of encouraging donors.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly giving donation tab for a recurring giving ask impacted recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting the monthly giving donation form tab as the default tab  for potential donors  will achieve an increase in recurring giving because the monthly giving tab will align with donation ask in the page copy, maintaining the monthly giving momentum and thought process, and reducing donor friction. because  the monthly giving tab will align with donation ask in the page copy, maintaining the monthly giving momentum and thought process, and reducing donor friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2023 - 02/18/2023
A public policy client asks users to make a monthly donation and join their monthly donor program after signing a petition. The ask details the monthly donor program, highlighting its purpose and the benefits of joining. But when the petition signers reach the donation form, the form defaults to a one-time gift ask. The form consists of two tabs, one for making a one-time gift and one for making a monthly gift to “Join the Come and Take It Society” – as the tab is labeled.
We wondered if the misalignment of making a recurring giving ask but defaulting the tabbed form to a one-time ask was causing friction, disrupting the user momentum, and possibly even causing confusion among potential donors. To test this line of thinking, we simply built a treatment that set the “Join the Come and Take It Society” tab as the default. Would this tweak result in an increase in monthly donors?
It’s also important to note that the monthly giving tab is supported by a monthly giving callout which reiterates some of the value proposition from the page copy and isolates several benefits of joining the Come and Take It Society.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a valid increase in recurring giving, producing four recurring gifts to the treatment and none to the control. This validates our hypothesis that potential donors benefit from congruency between the recurring donation ask and the recurring giving donation form tab. It makes sense for the recurring ask to be backed by a recurring giving opportunity through the donation form. It is also important for potential donors to see and read the recurring giving callout as it could very well add motivation for them to convert as a recurring donor. If the donation form defaults to one-time giving then there is a strong chance the user will not click to the recurring tab and they would therefore never have the chance to read the callout.
This test is worth testing on additional offers that include a recurring giving ask at the end of the funnel.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving process from 2 pages to 1 page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experience to one step  for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2023 - 01/27/2023
In a previous experiment, the Navigators successfully increased donor conversion rate by reducing the number of steps in the giving process from 3 to 2. Our hypothesis was that we would reduce even more friction in the giving process by consolidating the experience into a single page. Unfortunately, the organization’s current giving platform was not able to accommodate a single step giving experience. Therefore, we used a completely different platform for this experiment’s treatment. We kept the control as-is in its current platform and the treatment on a new platform as a single-page experience.
We only ran this experiment in one ebook offer funnel and most of the traffic was driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. This was an unexpected result given the previous experiment's results. Our hypothesis is that making the treatment page on the new platform may not look similar enough to the organization's webpages. We will re-run this test on another offer and design the page to more closely match the aesthetics of the control.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a year-end fundraising campaign donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by humanizing and further conveying the value of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a faith-based education organization, we wanted to test how adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page would affect donation rates.
We set this experiment up as a redirect test with ~50% of traffic seeing the standard donation page, sans sticky bar, and the other half seeing a humanized sticky bar from one of the organization’s staff members (including a photo) making a personal appeal and expressing the value of the prospect’s donation.
We ran this test for 10 days, during the last stretch of the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 10 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 14.5% increase in donation rates with an 87.1% statistical level of confidence when compared to the control.
In addition, the treatment including the sticky bar increased revenue per visitor by 151% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that not only did the sticky bar encourage more people to give, it inspired them to give more generously.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a staff member of the organization was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and putting a face to the appeal, reminding people that this organization is comprised of real people, just like them, who are trying to make a difference.
And even though we did not receive a large enough sample size to statistically validate these results, we did observe a directional lift that indicates there is low risk associated with implementing this treatment and we therefore feel confident in recommending the inclusion of a humanized sticky bar to 100% of the traffic during their next high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How adding a dollar goal to a sticky bar impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the monetary goal in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/31/2022
During a high urgency campaign, RTB ran a sitewide sticky bar that prompted people to give towards their campaign. Mid-campaign the team noted that the stickybar did not state the dollar goal amount and adding this goal may motivate more people to contribute. In order to test this hypothesis we created an experiment where the control (the original sticky bar) did not have the goal amount and the treatment stated the goal amount for the site visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 32% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96%. This means that people were less likely to give when given the dollar amount in the sticky bar. This could be because this makes the experience more transactional for the site visitor. Additionally, it could be that the dollar amount was too large for the prospective donor and it made them feel like it was unattainable (and that their donation would not have an impact).

The IMB was running their calendar year end campaign, which was historically called the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. They wondered how prevalent the brand awareness of Lottie Moon was, especially among younger donors, as opposed to the organizational brand.
They decided to test this hypothesis through a sticky bar that ran at the top of the website throughout the end of the year. The control gave the donor the offer to help the IMB, and the treatment gave them the offer to help missionaries through the LMCO.

This presents an opportunity to refine messaging next year to show impact to a specific, beloved campaign name.
25-34 visitors had a 142.4% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had an 87.3% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

rows.name: Sticky Bar - Humanized with Dan's Photo

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/04/2023
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and run a 3-way experiment to see whether a sticky bar made an impact on donations.
For this experiment, our control was no sticky bar; variant one had a sticky bar with just copy; variant 2 had a sticky bar with a picture of Dan Burke, the founder of Avila. Both variant 1 and 2 had the exact same copy. We ran this sticky bar on all pages on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we expected a sticky bar to outperform no sticky bar, the results of adding in Dan's image were really interesting. The Dan image version of the stick bar had a 92% increase in conversion rates compared to both the control and variant 1. We believe that this is due to Dan's notoriety within the Avila community; everyone knows who he is, what he looks like, and by adding an image we humanized the message a little more, giving donors the perception that it was really Dan asking for urgent help during the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 89.4% increase with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Adding a “Start My Gift” Button to the Top of a Year-End Donation Page impacts donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “start my gift” button at the top of the year-end donation page will increase donor conversion by decreasing donor friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/18/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their calendar year-end campaign, they wanted to test ways of improving donor conversion, particularly for mobile traffic which has typically had a much lower conversion rate on their site. We hypothesized that the length of the copy on the page might be adding friction to motivated traffic and wanted to see if we could reduce that friction by providing a button that would invite visitors to “start my gift” right at the top of the page. Clicking the button would drop the visitor directly to the donation form.
We ran the experiment on their campaign donation page for almost two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not achieve a greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic, mobile validated a lift and all other primary channels showed a strong positive impact with a directional level of confidence. What was surprising was that this button also increased revenue. We excluded any gifts over $750 that would skew the results and still achieved a 237% increase in revenue from the treatment with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to do additional testing to dig into why this impacted revenue so strongly but we will use this button for the remainder of the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct visitors had a 30.6% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using more active language in a donation page headline impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using active language in a header for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/11/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign. In order to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page we wanted to test the language used in the donation page header so that it had more active language. The control for this experiment said: Help other overcome doubt with science and biblical truth. The treatment for this test said: Fight doubt with science and biblical truth. Our hypothesis was that the treatment language was much more active and helping the donor visualize what their gift is going towards.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a valid 103% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95.9%. These results validate our hypothesis in that the treatment language inspired and motivated more people to give. Words like "help others" is consistently used in their copy and using more active language may have captured the attention of more people and moved them to action. Additionally we saw a valid 389% increase in revenue from this experiment. So, not only were more people willing to give, they were willing to give in higher amounts.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 389.9% increase with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page for monthly partnership impacted donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conducting a radical redesign on a monthly donor membership donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in instant donor conversion rate because potential donors will gain clarity on the benefits of joining the membership club and will have a cleaner, more user-friendly page engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/05/2022
A public policy client has a special membership club for monthly donors. Someone can join this club when making a monthly donation of at least $10, and from there they’ll receive special perks and have an elevated impact on the organization and its cause. The donation page where someone can make a monthly gift to join the club is resulting in a low instant conversion rate. Considering the amount of traffic that reaches the page, and after brainstorming several potential ways to improve conversion, we’ve opted for a radical redesign test to determine its impact on the instant donor conversion rate.
We will test these elements:
Expanding the value proposition of the headline
Moving the inline quote outside of the main copy and into a quote box on the side
Calling out the specific impact of making a monthly gift via bullet points
Moving the (501(c)3) tax language to the bottom of the page below the giving button
Setting the default gift array amount to $10
Changing “Donor Information” to “Your Information” in the giving form
Removing an email subscription opportunity from above the giving button
Removing only on mobile the social proof slide in informing the page visitor that [someone] just joined the membership club
We want to determine if this combination of changes will improve the page/donation experience for the visitor, provide additional clarity on the benefits of joining the membership club, and ultimately increase donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results are in. The treatment (radical redesign) showed a valid 76.5% DECREASE in instant donor conversion rate. The control variant produced 8 conversions to the treatment's 2.
Our swing for the fences resulted in a whiff. The results were negative across segments. Unfortunately, a radical redesign cannot tell us how each change influenced the user experience and donor conversion rate overall. To simplify this test and make more sense of the results, our next test should isolate one or two variants. We may want to go down the list of changes so we can truly determine which change(s) turned potential donors off.

experiments.title: How simplifying a gift array impacted lapsed donor reactivation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that isolating a $10 ask on the gift array for lapsed donor survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2022 - 12/12/2022
We were running a survey to an audience of lapsed donors and followed the survey with an instant donation page. In previous testing, we had found that asking for a $10 gift in the body of the email copy increased donor conversion rate. We were interested in testing this same concept at the donation page level and hypothesized that reducing the number of choices on the gift array to only ask for a $10 gift would increase conversion rate from these lapsed donors. We removed all choices other than “$10” and “Other” from the gift array and split traffic equally between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed after running the experiment for 2 1/2 weeks was that there was a 79.8% increase in conversion rate with 99.5% level of confidence. We believe this is due to the reduced friction in the treatment experience where the donor did not have to pause and decide on an amount to give they could just decide "Do I want to give $10? Yes or no?" and continue their cognitive momentum from answering questions on the survey. We made the choice simple, reducing the perceived barrier in the decision-making process. While this treatment produced a better response in reactivating lapsed donors, in future testing we would like to test how this might impact new donor conversion as well. We also want to test increased gift amounts (such as $15 or $20) on the single gift array option to identify if it is the single choice or the lower amount that impacts results.

CaringBridge was curious as to how they could increase conversion rate on their tribute donation pages. As inflation had increased as a significant downward impact on the economy, they wondered if their initial ask amount of $50 was too high. They decided to test this by adding a $25 amount as a new lower barrier of entry.
They split the traffic to determine the impact of this new low ask amount on conversion and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the lower ask amount produced an 8% decrease in conversion rate, which also delivered less revenue due to a lower average gift. As they looked at the device segments, they noticed this was heavily weighed down by mobile traffic, which had a 10% decrease and made up more than 60% of the traffic.
Upon reviewing the mobile experience, they noticed a slight anomaly with the way the array laid out on mobile with four options, so they decided to test a slightly different design in a new test, just for mobile, to see if redesigning the options could affect conversion rate.
