experiments.title: How adding a transitional sentence in the header of the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a transitional sentence in the header of an instant donation page for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/12/2022
The IMB has an advent offer where they ran a donation page experiment on the instant donation page in order to increase donor conversion rate. Typically instant donation pages only have a thank you statement or acknowledgement as the header but in order for the page to not seem like a confirmation page, we wanted to add a transitional sentence to let people know that there’s more to read and take action on in the donation page.
Traffic to this donation page was driven by both paid facebook ads and housefile email traffic
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment indicate that IMB's audience do not respond to the transitional statement in the header. We saw a valid 74% decrease in donor conversion rate. This could be because in an effort to transition to the appeal quicker, we actually removed the "thank you" sentence originally in the control of this donation page. It could be that the act of thanking them first before asking them to take another action actually demotivated people to take the additional step to give.
A follow up test could be to include the thank you in the header and add the transitional sentence so that grateful sentiment isn't lost in the messaging.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 95.0% decrease with a 88% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had an 85.7% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had an 84.7% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by implementing various winning elements for website traffic will achieve an increase in response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly featured premiums.
For Giving Tuesday we decided to do a “radical redesign” to the landing page.
For the treatment, we implemented multiple elements that had won on their own in other scenarios.
These changes included:
Remove Dr. Jeffress’ images
Leading with a solution rather than the match
A conclusion that reinforces how the donor’s gift makes the solution possible
Removed friction of “Important” instructions
And changed the layout of items to be linear.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing friction points, clarifying the solution of the gift and making the experience single column, we observed a 112.5% increase in revenue with a 99% level of confidence.
Each of the elements changed in the treatment version had been previously tested on web, email or Facebook for Pathway to Victory and when put together we saw the dramatic increase in conversions.
With these learnings and results from Giving Tuesday, we will be able to implement this approach for year end.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 12/06/2022
For a content offer for a public policy client’s pledge, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure on a donation page would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar. No other elements were changed.
Because of the large volume of traffic the donation page received, we were able to validate the results of this experiment after only 2 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 48 hours, we observed a 38.6% INCREASE in donation rates, with a 96.9% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar, validating our hypothesis that a humanized sticky bar would increase donation rates.
We believe that the increase in donations occurred because the large and colorful sticky bar, featuring a well-known and well-respected public figure among this organization's prospective donors, increased the visibility of the perceived impact for potential donors.
The sticky bar also invites the user to "join" this public figure in achieving the outcome they want to achieve, while additional creating a message match from the pledge page seen before the donation page, as the pledge referred directly to the four values being presented in the sticky bar, creating momentum toward the conversion and leveraging the persuasion principle of "consistency" to inspire the user to follow up on their pledge by taking action.
It is also worth noting that the treatment including the sticky bar produced a ~15% higher average gift, perhaps because the additional value statement shown in the sticky bar increased the perceived value of a larger donation in the minds of donors.
Because of the positive results of this experiment, we will look for other opportunities to include a sticky bar on instant donation pages in future acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How revising a headline to continue the conversation impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that revising the headline to continue the conversation for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2022 - 12/09/2022
We had observed through testing with other organizations that removing “thank you” from a headline was improving donor conversion rates. We wanted to test this hypothesis on an instant donation page to call attention to the language below the headline. Our hypothesis was that visitors were seeing “Thank you for requesting your free devotional” as a cue that they did not need to take any other action and could exit away from the confirmation page. We developed a treatment that asked them to stick around and consider reading the remainder of the copy. We produced a treatment that revised the headline to “You will receive an email containing your free devotional in just a few minutes. But before you get started, could we take a moment to ask you something?” The remainder of the body copy on the page stayed the same. We split traffic 50/50 and launched the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment after 2 weeks was a 75.8% increase in donation rates with 99.4% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We believe this lift was a result of continuing the conversation on the donation page rather than treating it as a typical confirmation page that visitors see at the end of the process. The treatment headline indicated clarity about what would happen next and still communicated to the reader that their submission was successful but framed the experience that the reader had additional action to take before closing out the site. This same concept can be tested on other instant donation pages where the cadence makes sense to invite readers to stay around for the final step of the content offer process, which is making a gift. Since we only tested changing the headline and the rest of the donation page matched the control, we know that the difference in how the headline is presented was responsible for this lift across multiple segments and channels of traffic.

experiments.title: How adding specificity of impact to a high urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity to the donor’s impact for prospective Giving Tuesday donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/02/2022
During Reasons to Believe’s Giving Tuesday campaign they ran a test on the donation page to create more specificity around the impact of the donor’s gift on Giving Tuesday. Instead of generally telling the donor they need to give because it’s Giving Tuesday we highlighted the “why” of their gift, using language like: you will offer someone the chance to have their life changed forever… we need your help to continue providing these opportunities… etc.
The treatment for this donation page was a complete rewrite of the control. Traffic to this experiment was generated primarily through housefile emails and paid social ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 411% valid lift in donor conversion rate. While there are a lot of elements changed in the treatment, this informs us that RTB's audiences are highly motivated by knowing how their gift is being used and what their impact looks like. This treatment was rolled out to the remainder of the campaign to maximize results.

experiments.title: How using a ‘donation interruptor’ affects recurring giving rates during a Giving Tuesday high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up for donors on Giving Tuesday will improve recurring gift conversion rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/03/2022
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a faith-based client during a Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits.
We tested this same hypothesis with the same client during last year’s Giving Tuesday campaign, and while we saw an encouraging lift in recurring giving, we were unable to validate our results—so we were eager to see how the same concept would perform in a similar setting during this year’s campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to an increase in the number of donors (sample size) over last year's campaign, we were pleased to be able to pull a valid learning from this year's experiment—with our treatment producing a statistically valid 137% increase in recurring gifts with a 98.4% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
We also observed that the treatment produced a statistically valid increase of 210% in recurring giving among donors using mobile devices, important as we continue to see an increase in mobile giving over time.
These results are a positive indication that a similar treatment may work for other clients in the context of high-urgency campaigns and we will seek to globalize this learning by implementing it for new audiences and new giving contexts, such as content offers or direct appeals.

experiments.title: How making the organization the actor of a Giving Tuesday donation page value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making KCBI the hero of the value proposition on the Giving Tuesday donation page will decrease donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 11/29/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if donors were more motivated to give  TO KCBI because of what KCBI would do with their gift or if they would be motivated to give because of what their GIFT would do THROUGH KCBI. So, which “hero” motivated more visitors to the donation page on Giving Tuesday to become donors.
Historically, they’ve tried to always emphasize the donor as the hero in their donation pages.
For the control, they emphasized in the headline and body copy the impact the donor would have by giving. In the treatment, they tweaked it slightly to emphasize how the impact is through KCBI. Examples include….
“You can give practical help and biblical truth this Giving Tuesday” vs “Give Practical Help and Biblical Truth this Giving Tuesday Through KCBI“
“By making a gift right now, you will be broadcasting the sounds of hope and the Good News of Jesus, as well as ensuring that practical help and biblical truth is carried out through our community…”
vs
“By making a gift to KCBI right now, you will help us broadcast the sounds of hope and the Good News of Jesus, as well as ensure that we provide practical help and biblical truth to our community…”
“Will you give right now?” vs “Will you give to KCBI right now?”
“Please step forward and make your special Giving Tuesday gift using the secure form below. You will have an eternal impact on the lives of others.”
vs
“Please step forward and make your special Giving Tuesday gift to KCBI using the secure form below. Your gift will enable us to have an eternal impact on the lives of others.”
We split the traffic 50/50 and ran it during their Giving Tuesday campaign that began a week early online. We hypothesized that the control page with the donor as the hero of the value proposition would have the greater conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To our surprise, the control donation page that made the donor the hero (actor) in the value proposition had a decreased donor conversion rate and our treatment making KCBI the hero of the page increased conversion by 31%. When looking at the traffic by segment, all segments favored the treatment over the control.  In our analysis of the results, we looked to see if there were any large donations that may be skewing revenue and average gift results and found only a total of five gifts. Removing these gave us the following results:
Control (donor as hero):
98 donations, 24.7% conversion rate, $8,089 revenue, and $82.54 average gift
Treatment (KCBI as hero)
126 donations, $9,003.85 revenue, and $71.45 average gift.
This analysis did not change the level of confidence or the overall result. We still brought in approximately $1,000 in revenue on the treatment despite the lower average gift thanks to the higher donor conversion rate.
While our thinking has been that donors want to be the actor/hero, it is possible that the way KCBI emphasized this in their control body copy made it sound like the donor was doing "work." The donor could have anxiety about "saving lives" "sharing the Gospel" etc. The treatment page made it clear that KCBI did these things through the donor's gift.
More testing is needed to see the influence of a value proposition with KCBI as the hero outside of a unique campaign like Giving Tuesday. It is possible that the unique motivations of this day of giving influenced the results.

experiments.title: How adding a book as a premium offer increases the average gift on an eBook instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for ebook donors will achieve a higher average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2022 - 10/12/2022
The Avila Foundation has created a website, SpiritualDirection.com, where anyone can go and find answers to questions they might have about their spiritual journey. As part of the efforts to educate the laity through authentic Catholic teachings, Avila has created a series of ebooklets.
For one of these ebooklets, Insights on the Contemplative Rosary, we wanted to test whether adding a premium offer, in this case a physical copy of the book upon which the ebooklet was based, would help increase the average gift. Our hypothesis was that the combination of the two–using the ebooklet as a study guide to the book–would entice donors to contribute more to Avila.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was a slight decline in the total number of donations while we ran this experiment (38 to 37), the average gift on the premium treatment page increased by 58%, which also resulted in a 58% increase in total revenue with a 94% level of confidence.
We believe that by positioning the ebooklet as a companion piece to the book, those donors who were brand new, or had not yet gotten a chance to get the book, were convinced to give a gift of a higher amount.
While the average gift for the treatment version was below the $50 we were asking in order to receive a copy of the book, there was enough data to suggest that further refinement of this offer, as well as further testing with other premiums, is warranted.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial from an authority figure impacted course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial from an authority figure for free online course visitors will achieve an increase in email registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
On a previous test, we found that including a testimonial from an authority figure on a course registration page improved email conversion. We were curious if this optimization could be expanded to include additional courses so we developed a treatment that highlighted a quote and photo of the free online course professor to enhance the appeal, credibility, and clarity offered on the course registration page.
experiments.keyLearnings: Surprisingly this is the first time we observed a decrease in email acquisition by including a photo and quote on the course registration page. After running the experiment, we saw a 13.9% decrease in email conversion with 99% level of confidence. Multiple visitor segments experienced declined including email visitors, returning visitors, mobile visitors, and paid social visitors. It may be worthwhile to examine this quote compared to other quotes for alternate courses where a similar experiment was also run to see if there are any obvious differences that could be influencing the results. For a next iteration, we could choose a testimonial quote to feature that focuses more on what the user will receive or learn through the course rather than simply giving additional information about the book the course is centered around.

experiments.title: How adding a $60 giving option to the Lamplighters monthly gift array impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a third monthly giving option as an “in-between” amount  for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations and revenue as it will reduce giving friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2022 - 11/19/2022
The PFI Lamplighters program gives people the opportunity to support prisoners in ways that help them become closer to God. The current monthly giving options for Lamplighters are $15 to support one prisoner and $165 to support an entire class. We hypothesized that adding a middle amount as a giving option would reduce giving friction, giving donors an additional option that might nestle in nicely between an amount that is too low or not impactful enough and an amount that is too high.
To test this hypothesis, we added a $60 monthly giving option to support four prisoners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the $60 giving option to the monthly giving gift array resulted in a 79.3% lift in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence. Donations saw an 11.8% decrease but only with a 54.4% level of confidence.
The impressive increase in revenue tells us that users responded very well to seeing a third giving option between $15 and $165. The $60 option reduced decision friction to where the donor did not have to think of an amount to settle in between the extremes of $15 and $165 - instead, the option was presented to them! Additionally, the $60 option will bring up our average monthly gift.
It's worth noting that new visitors produced a 114% lift in revenue with a 98.3% level of confidence. This tells us that people that are less familiar with the Lamplighters program appreciate the $60 giving option, as they're likely too new to the program to be willing to make a $165 monthly donation. But they are willing to commit $60/month to the program.

experiments.title: How removing thank you from the header on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing thank you from the header on the donation page for course enrollees will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/11/2022
Luther Seminary is offering an online course to study the Book of Revelation. We had previously seen other organizations test the concept of removing “thank you” from the header of the donation page as that typically ends a user’s thought process when signing up for something or completing a purchase. Usually, when you see the thank you confirmation page, you think to yourself – Okay, I’m done now, so I’m leaving the page.
We predicted for this online course that users are leaving the donation page after seeing “thank you” at the very top, so we want to test changing that language so something that lets the user know that they are not done yet.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 132% increase in donor conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence. We believe by saying "But before you go, will you consider something?" that the user's thought process is no longer interrupted therefore they are more likely to stay on the page to read the value proposition rather than bouncing as soon as they see "thank you."
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 140.2% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
We believe revenue experienced such a high lift on the treatment page because we were able to keep double the amount of people on the page. After staying and reading the copy on why they should give, they were more likely to make a donation.

experiments.title: How “locking” the donation form on mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will locking the donation form on a person’s mobile device screen impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2021 - 10/30/2021
Focus on the Family continues to test into new methods to optimize donor conversion on their primary donation form. One of their main objectives is to increase mobile donor conversion. In the past, they had tested into using a two step form on mobile devices that saw a directional lift of 22%. While that test didn’t validate, it did give us insight into the giving experience and impact a tactic like that could have. To continue to test into this idea, but see if we could pull the lever on something that might increase conversion even more and validate the results, we tested into breaking the donation process into steps AND “locking” the donation form on the mobile screen once a person scrolled to the form. At that point, a user would either have to exit out of the donation form or make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The multi-step locked screen donation process increased donor conversion significantly by 36%! The locked screen captured the users attention and required them to take an action - either to leave or to donate. Because of this, more people decided to give because there were only two options for them to decide between when given the opportunity to just scroll the donation page, exit, or give. This increased the conversion rate on mobile devices to over 9%!

Here are other notable changes due to the experiment:
New visitors had a 44.9% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 25.0% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding project-specific value proposition language to a donation page affects donor conversion rate (Pt. 1)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding project-specific value proposition language for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2022 - 08/27/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift. The control of this experiment had the generic donation page and the treatment added value proposition language centered around Lottie Moon.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not result in an increase like we had originally thought. We saw a valid 40% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. While this was unexpected, we noticed that the highest negative impact was seen on desktop. On desktop this experiment is laid out as a 2 column experience. It could be that prospective donors had a difficult user experience with having to read the copy on the left hand side and having to scroll back up to make their gift on the right hand side of the page. Our hypothesis is that if we made the treatment a 1 column experience, we would see a lift in desktop.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign affected donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign  for potential monthly donors will achieve an increased conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2020 - 11/08/2020
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate for a monthly donation ask, we developed a treatment for the donation page with new copy, new graphics, and an increased monthly ask amount. We added exclusivity to the headline, increased clarity in the descriptions of the benefits for joining as a monthly donor, and relevance to the intro paragraphs. We also made the photo on the page more congruent with the ad they had seen before arriving on this page and increased the monthly gift ask from $10 to $15. We wanted to see how this would impact the overall conversion rate as well as average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we saw differing responses from individual segments, overall we saw a 31.6% decrease in conversion rate and turned the experiment off. What we would like to explore next is isolating the copy, image, and monthly gift ask to identify which stand-alone element, if any, was responsible for the decrease. Rather than taking these results at face value, we also need to evaluate if the increase in average gift potentially reflects a greater value than the decrease in conversions to see which is a more beneficial long-term approach. We did not reach statistical significance to have valid results for the increase in average gift to determine its effect on increasing revenue.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 40.2% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 44.8% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How personalization, reciprocity, and communal language affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing the headline, inserting communal language, and leveraging reciprocity for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/06/2021 - 01/31/2021
The Navigators were promoting a free eBook about Scripture memory. While the offer was doing well in acquisition, they felt that the donation page conversion rate could be increased through optimization. They had seen experiments where personalizing the page (since the visitor had just given them personal information) might increase conversion. To this end, they added “Thank you, FirstName” to the headline. Then, they looked at the value proposition presented to the visitor after accepting the free eBook. The language on the control donation page was somewhat abstract—after promising to deliver the eBook, it went into an explanation of disciplemaking, and then made a very transactional ask to the donor: “A gift of $50 means that 2,000 people can see this eBook.”
They hypothesized that communal language, rather than transactional language, might increase conversion. First, they changed the first paragraph to thank the visitor for their desire to memorize more scripture. This was designed to reinforce the visitor’s decision, and to build the relationship. Then, instead of just asking the visitor to “get this into the hands of more Christians”, they reminded the visitor that they had just received a free book and asked them to “pay it forward” with a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation page produced a 46.6% lift in conversion—which shows that these optimizations added up to have a significant impact on conversion.

Of note—the average gift for the treatment was lower than the control, which may have been an effect of removing the $50 gift handle. This is a great opportunity for a future test: how can that be included or suggested without clouding the value proposition?

Additionally, it was observed that the donation button says "submit" and this could be tested to increase motivation at the final point of conversion.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 48.3% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 59.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 76.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 454.4% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign goal to an acquisition landing page during year-end impacted results.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a campaign goal for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/28/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular eBook offers is their Lady of Lourdes Novena.
During their calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether including the year-end campaign goal amount, as well as some specific value proposition language, on the Lourdes instant donation page would help drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a large drop in conversion rates on the treatment, campaign-goal oriented donation page, with a 41% decline in conversion rates and a 99.5% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to a couple of factors. First, the Lourdes offer was not actively pushed during the campaign; most of the digital messaging on donation forms and the homepage mentioned the year-end goal. Therefore, people that landed on the Lourdes page through a generic Facebook ad might not have been aware there was a campaign going on. Moreover, the campaign goal itself, and why people should donate to it, was not very clear.

For future experiments we will test whether making the value prop clearer and more urgent will have a more positive impact on conversion rates.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 41.1% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How presenting the donor with a problem to solve affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the donor with a problem for page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2020 - 12/12/2020
The Navigators were sending traffic to a content offer called “Soul Care”. After signing up to receive the content offer, users were directed to a donation page where they were given the opportunity to donate and allow others to receive the offer they had just downloaded. Initially, donor conversion rates were very low. However, the team wanted to test a redesign of the page.
First, they hypothesized that the large image at the top of the page was distracting from the message, so they removed it. Then, they made several copy changes throughout the test, but one change was the primary lever that they expected to have the most impact.
The control page lacked any sort of problem for the donor to solve. It made it clear that the offer was free to the user, and that the ministry was donor-funded, but then actually made the content seem really popular—which might actually detract from the motivation to give. Finally, the CTA was vague. “Practical transferrable, and transformational” help was unclear and not inspiring (they hypothesized) enough to convince people to give.
On the treatment, they presented a problem in the third bullet: “that there are thousands of people who are faithful believers but are not fulfilling the Great Commission…” Then, after this problem statement, the donor was presented with a specific way they could help—a suggested gift of $50 would distribute the resource to 1,000 people. Finally, the offer was supported by asking them to prayerfully consider a gift—removing the burden of “give now”, and reinforcing that their gift will solve the previously stated problem.
They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 103.2% increase in conversion rate. While this experiment did not have more than 50 transactions, the split was large enough and unanimous enough across multiple devices, user segments, and channels to be statistically valid.
This shows the impact a problem, a clearly worded solution, and reinforcement of the donor's impact at the moment of giving will make on donor conversion.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 147.8% increase with a 99% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
All Traffic visitors had a 122.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 232.7% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 113.8% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 135.7% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 291.1% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 120.6% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 120.9% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal-based communication affects clickthrough rate on a matching gift popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language in the donation popup for website visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2020
CaringBridge was running a two-day match in the month of December as part of their year end campaign. They had previously tested into a highly effective popup during matches that used urgency and an hourly donor countdown to increase response during these time-sensitive campaigns. However, they wondered if more “communal” messaging that affirmed the donor in their relationship to the CaringBridge author and made a less direct ask would be more effective at getting people to enter the donation funnel.
They drafted a treatment that made some key shifts. First, it began by talking about the donor themselves—”You’ve been such a great friend by staying engaged through CaringBridge.” Then, it softened the ask by starting with “If it’s not too much to ask”, which gives the donor a sort of “release valve” from the pressure of “donate now”. Finally, the matching gift from the Board of Directors was positioned as an opportunity or service offering provided to the donor rather than something they were expected to respond to.
They split the traffic between the two popups and monitored downstream traffic separately to see how it affected both clickthrough rate and conversion, as they realized that the two do not always correlate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language produced a 45.3% increase in clickthrough rate. There are a few reasons behind what might have contributed to the increase.

First, the intro of "You've been such a great friend by staying connected through CaringBridge" not only affirms the donor, it piques their interest. It's a compliment, rather than the presumptive "your donation will be matched" language on the control. This additional engagement could have contributed to more people reading the next sentence—"If it's not too much to ask, would you consider giving today so that our Board of Directors can match your gift?".

This next sentence presents the ask in a completely different way. First, the donor is given an initial choice: "is this too much to ask, or not?", before they read about the incentive. Second, the incentive is offered in a radically different way than the control. Where the control actually thanks the Board of Directors for matching, the treatment gives the donor the chance to be the hero by making the gift, and places the match in a position that is responsive to the donor's action.

This test illuminated the possibilities of "communal" language for CaringBridge—which is a very "communal" organization in the first place. This opens up the opportunity do to more testing on the donation page downstream from this ask.

experiments.keyLearnings: People seeing the instant donation page don’t know if they want the whole DVD set yet, because they haven’t taken the course yet. We’re giving people too much before they’re ready, which impacted donations in a negative way.

experiments.title: How one-click giving for dual ask gifts impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the giving process impact revenue and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/27/2015
The Heritage Foundation has a unique process where, after every online gift, the donor is immediately prompted to make a second gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. All the donor has to do for this second gift is enter their donation amount since we already have their address and credit card information from the initial form. This secondary ask generally has a 5% to 8% conversion rate for donors making that second gift.
We had the opportunity to test a new feature in their giving system that allowed for one-click giving. Existing donors would only have to enter their zip code and credit card number to make a donation.  We decided to test this functionality out in their 2015 calendar year end campaign and monitor the impact on all aspects of the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the one-click giving process did not have any statistically significant impact on the initial donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that the decreased friction that came from the simplified form was balanced out but the confusion the process caused. While it was a simpler process; it was different than what the donors were use to.

However, while the immediate conversion was not impacted, the follow on donation opportunity saw a 90.4% decrease in conversion with the one-click giving. Since the donors did not have to enter their address or credit card for the first form, they would have to enter it for the second form (due to system limitations). This added significant friction to the second step in the process that was enough to significantly impact the gifts made to Heritage Action.

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.

Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

This strategy has changed their acquisition strategy dramatically and is one of the biggest drivers of new donor acquisition. Knowing this, they wanted to see what the impact was on offer the Resurrection Eggs as a product offer from their store vs a premium offer for a donation. What would the difference in donor conversion be between the two experiences.
Traffic was pushed to both of the experiences through paid Facebook ads that promoted the product/premium. The ads were identical for both experiences and funnels.

experiments.title: How matching gift copy affects clickthrough rate on a modal popup
experiments.researchQuestion: How does matching gift language affect clickthrough rate on a modal popup window?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2018 - 12/28/2017
FamilyLife was in the final week of their year-end campaign, and launched a modal popup to point web traffic to the donation page. The popup contained a countdown clock as well as urgency-focused message. The default version of the popup had language that echoed the headline, but they wondered if including matching gift language would increase clickthrough rate.
They created a treatment that kept urgent language, but added in the fact that the gift would be matched dollar-for-dollar. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (with matching gift language) produced a 56% increase in clickthrough rate. This test revealed that while urgency is key to any message with a deadline (especially at the end of the year), incentive can provide additional lift for action oriented copy.
In the control, the potential donor is presented with four elements to inspire them to click through and give: an urgent headline, a countdown clock, additional copy above the button, and the button copy. But in the treatment, the matching gift language provides a fifth element, without leaving out any of the previous four—incentive.
Many times, calls-to-action have one of these elements, but not the other. This test shows the power of combining the two.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an email appeal and donation page affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of both the email and the landing page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.
experiments.keyLearnings:
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.
The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.
The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.
This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.
They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.

experiments.title: How “locking” the donation form on mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will locking the donation form on a person’s mobile device screen impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2021 - 10/30/2021
Focus on the Family continues to test into new methods to optimize donor conversion on their primary donation form. One of their main objectives is to increase mobile donor conversion. In the past, they had tested into using a two step form on mobile devices that saw a directional lift of 22%. While that test didn’t validate, it did give us insight into the giving experience and impact a tactic like that could have. To continue to test into this idea, but see if we could pull the lever on something that might increase conversion even more and validate the results, we tested into breaking the donation process into steps AND “locking” the donation form on the mobile screen once a person scrolled to the form. At that point, a user would either have to exit out of the donation form or make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The multi-step locked screen donation process increased donor conversion significantly by 36%! The locked screen captured the users attention and required them to take an action - either to leave or to donate. Because of this, more people decided to give because there were only two options for them to decide between when given the opportunity to just scroll the donation page, exit, or give. This increased the conversion rate on mobile devices to over 9%!
Here are other notable changes due to the experiment:
New visitors had a 44.9% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 25.0% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a personalized call-out for recurring giving impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a handwritten note that called out the benefit of making a recurring gift, improve conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2020 - 09/10/2020
Focus on the Family offers one-time and recurring giving on their primary donation page. In an effort to increase recurring gifts, we hypothesized that adding a personalized call-out around recurring that communicate the value proposition might increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The handwritten note decreased overall giving by 14%. The most significant decrease was seen on desktop devices with a 22% decrease. That said, mobile devices actually increased giving by 18% (88% LoC).

Ultimately, the personal call-out added friction to the conversion pathway and negatively effected overall revenue by 13%. Continued testing is needed around this to see if we can highlight recurring giving in a different way and improve the number of recurring donors we get from the primary donation page.

experiments.title: How the gift frequency section on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the way the gift frequency section was presented and functioned for prospective donors will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2021 - 02/12/2021
Focus on the Family recently changed their donation platform. During that transition, the way the gift frequency section was presented looked and functioned differently than before. They heard from prospective donors that it was slightly confusing for them as they made a gift. To test into the best approach for that section on the page, we developed two different variations in hopes that we could increase clarity to the giving process. The first variation presented the two different gift frequency options as checked boxes – requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted to make through the checked boxes. The second variation presented the options as buttons. Again, requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted by selecting the specific button. This experiment originally ran during the last few weeks of their calendar year-end campaign and showed a negative impact on donor conversion from both variations. We proposed testing this again outside of year-end, during neutral motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings: Similar to when we tested these variations at year-end, there was a negative impact on donor conversion from both treatments. In fact, the most significant decrease (16%) in donor conversion came from the button variation. The checked boxes also saw a 10% dip in donations as well. This experiment is a difficult one to fully understand because the two variations that were developed were intended to bring clarity to the giving process - something that donors had communicated that they needed. Instead, we added friction to the giving process. We hypothesize that the current control requires no work at all. In fact, We think that donors don't really notice it or understand it so they just continue to progress down the giving form to make their gift. The two new variations may be making the donor pause and think about the action they want to take when they get to that section. Continued testing needs to be done to: 1) gain greater clarity to the motivation of the donor and elements of friction on the page 2) explore our hypothesis about the gift frequency section altogether. To do this, our next test is going to be to completely remove the "Choose your gift frequency" section and present a recurring option in a different way.

experiments.title: How presenting a micro-ask instead of a direct donation ask in a giving widget impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will presenting a softer call-to-action on the giving widget increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/26/2020
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, they feature a giving widget at the top of their navigation. On the left hand side they have a banner with value proposition copy and on the right hand side they feature a mini giving form where people can decide how much they want to give. After a person selects how much they want to give, they continue to the donation page to complete their gift. We questioned if this was the best approach to convert donors. So to test into it, we presented a softer call-to-action in the giving widget. We presented website visitors with a question where they would then decided “yes” or “no”. The question we asked was “Would you consider giving hope to families this Christmas?”. Both “yes” and “no” buttons led to a donation page where they could make a gift. We hypothesized that pushing the decision to give to the donation page might increase donor conversion instead of having people make the decision to give and decide how much they want to give right when they see the widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The micro-ask decrease overall donor conversion by 9.5%. To our surprise the direct ask and gift array selection actually improves donor conversion. Our hypothesis behind this is the motivation of traffic coming to the website during this season. There are so many channels pushing people to the Focus website to give. Once they get to the site, they may have already decided to give. Another hypothesis is that the soft micro-ask might be a bit of a whiplash. If I say "yes" I want to give families hope at Christmas and then am taken to a donation page, the experience may be too big of a jump.

Here are some additional observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment - most of them reinforcing our hypothesis around visitor motivation:
Email visitors had a 160.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 40.2% decrease in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 35.1% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 15.8% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How personalization, reciprocity, and communal language affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing the headline, inserting communal language, and leveraging reciprocity for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/06/2021 - 01/31/2021
The Navigators were promoting a free eBook about Scripture memory. While the offer was doing well in acquisition, they felt that the donation page conversion rate could be increased through optimization. They had seen experiments where personalizing the page (since the visitor had just given them personal information) might increase conversion. To this end, they added “Thank you, FirstName” to the headline. Then, they looked at the value proposition presented to the visitor after accepting the free eBook. The language on the control donation page was somewhat abstract—after promising to deliver the eBook, it went into an explanation of disciplemaking, and then made a very transactional ask to the donor: “A gift of $50 means that 2,000 people can see this eBook.”
They hypothesized that communal language, rather than transactional language, might increase conversion. First, they changed the first paragraph to thank the visitor for their desire to memorize more scripture. This was designed to reinforce the visitor’s decision, and to build the relationship. Then, instead of just asking the visitor to “get this into the hands of more Christians”, they reminded the visitor that they had just received a free book and asked them to “pay it forward” with a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation page produced a 46.6% lift in conversion—which shows that these optimizations added up to have a significant impact on conversion.
Of note—the average gift for the treatment was lower than the control, which may have been an effect of removing the $50 gift handle. This is a great opportunity for a future test: how can that be included or suggested without clouding the value proposition?
Additionally, it was observed that the donation button says "submit" and this could be tested to increase motivation at the final point of conversion.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a physical copy of the ebook for donors who give $50 or more will achieve an increase in average gift .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2022 - 09/04/2022
Navigators is a ministry organization that runs a variety of eBook offers. We hypothesized that offering a physical version of an already popular offer as a premium on the donation page would increase the average gift amount. This hypothesis stemmed from two ideas: 1.) providing a physical copy is seen as having greater value than the free digital version, and 2.) a premium is a bridge of appreciation to the user. You’ve generously given this, and we want to thank you for something in return. There was $50 gift minimum requirement to get this premium.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for 30 days and saw a 48.9% decrease in conversion rate with a 99.5% confidence level based upon 75 total conversions. The data surrounding premiums for a donation amount often varies. When offering the premium prior to the actual donation, one theory is that it places the donor in the mindset of exchange. When you attach an item (in this case a book) to a specific number it could cause the donor to shift from the decision-making process of generosity to a more typical evaluation of purchase: is the item worth this?
Consider that the book they just received for free, is now having a "price" of $50 attached to it, this could cause the donor to see less value in what the organization is offering. They've been taken from the mindset of giving to one of consumption, and as a result, the donor is no longer interested.

experiments.title: How lower grade level body copy readability impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the work required to read through a donation page for acquisition offer visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2021 - 01/16/2022
On a new acquisition offer, we wanted to test using more straight-foward copy that was brief and easy to read compared to traditionally policy-focused and academic copy on an instant donation page. The treatment copy read at a 7th grade level while the control was rated a 14th grade reading level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the treatment page with the 7th-grade reading level copy decreased conversion by 60% with 98% confidence. The control copy was more convincing that there was a problem and that it could be solved through the donor's support. For future testing, we will need to reevaluate how to keep the strength of the messaging while also altering the copy to be easier to read.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 48.6% decrease with a 87% level of confidence.
(Other) visitors had a 53.2% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 78.0% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 53.4% decrease in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 71.8% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How personalization, reciprocity, and communal language affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing the headline, inserting communal language, and leveraging reciprocity for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/06/2021 - 01/31/2021
The Navigators were promoting a free eBook about Scripture memory. While the offer was doing well in acquisition, they felt that the donation page conversion rate could be increased through optimization. They had seen experiments where personalizing the page (since the visitor had just given them personal information) might increase conversion. To this end, they added “Thank you, FirstName” to the headline. Then, they looked at the value proposition presented to the visitor after accepting the free eBook. The language on the control donation page was somewhat abstract—after promising to deliver the eBook, it went into an explanation of disciplemaking, and then made a very transactional ask to the donor: “A gift of $50 means that 2,000 people can see this eBook.”
They hypothesized that communal language, rather than transactional language, might increase conversion. First, they changed the first paragraph to thank the visitor for their desire to memorize more scripture. This was designed to reinforce the visitor’s decision, and to build the relationship. Then, instead of just asking the visitor to “get this into the hands of more Christians”, they reminded the visitor that they had just received a free book and asked them to “pay it forward” with a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation page produced a 46.6% lift in conversion—which shows that these optimizations added up to have a significant impact on conversion.

Of note—the average gift for the treatment was lower than the control, which may have been an effect of removing the $50 gift handle. This is a great opportunity for a future test: how can that be included or suggested without clouding the value proposition?

Additionally, it was observed that the donation button says "submit" and this could be tested to increase motivation at the final point of conversion.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 48.3% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 59.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 76.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 454.4% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How tangible donation language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a tangible “handle” for giving increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 04/06/2017
CaringBridge had done some message development around the value proposition of a donation. Donations to CaringBridge help power a platform that helps people stay connected. Their value proposition centered around this connection, as multiple tests had proven. However, they hypothesized that adding a more tangible offer for the donor into the ask might increase conversion. They calculated that it takes $30 to power a single CaringBridge site for a month, and developed a treatment of their tribute widget (which appears on the most highly-trafficked sections of their site) that called this out.
Then, they launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the tangible connection to the gift amount, produced a 34.3% increase in donations. This showed that while the emotional “connection-based” language had been (and was still) effective at attracting donations, there was a segment of users who needed to better understand what their gift did—and how their choice to donate could have a “tangible” impact when the entire platform they were supporting was virtual.
This prompted more testing, specifically around how to leverage this messaging in the gift array.

experiments.title: The impact on one-click giving for dual ask gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the giving process impact revenue and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/27/2015
The Heritage Foundation has a unique process where, after every online gift, the donor is immediately prompted to make a second gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. All the donor has to do for this second gift is enter their donation amount since we already have their address and credit card information from the initial form. This secondary ask generally has a 5% to 8% conversion rate for donors making that second gift.
We had the opportunity to test a new feature in their giving system that allowed for one-click giving. Existing donors would only have to enter their zip code and credit card number to make a donation.  We decided to test this functionality out in their 2015 calendar year end campaign and monitor the impact on all aspects of the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the one-click giving process did not have any statistically significant impact on the initial donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that the decreased friction that came from the simplified form was balanced out but the confusion the process caused. While it was a simpler process; it was different than what the donors were use to.

However, while the immediate conversion was not impacted, the follow on donation opportunity saw a 90.4% decrease in conversion with the one-click giving. Since the donors did not have to enter their address or credit card for the first form, they would have to enter it for the second form (due to system limitations). This added significant friction to the second step in the process that was enough to significantly impact the gifts made to Heritage Action.

experiments.title: How focusing on what the donor wants to accomplish impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the message on what the donor wants to accomplish with their year-end gift for TFAS Supporters will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2020 - 12/13/2020
The Fund for American Studies (TFAS) was readying themselves to launch their year-end campaign—and did the logical thing first: To grab last year’s page and use that as a starting point for this year’s campaign (because last year was rather successful).
In reviewing last year’s campaign page—they noticed just how “organizationally-centered” that language really rang. The page from last year really focused on what TFAS (the organization) intended to accomplish—not what the donor believed, or was attempting to solve (with their year-end gift).
Therefore, they wanted to experiment with a more donor-focused message that was relevant for the current mindset that their audience is feeling.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.4% level of confidence, we observed an increase of +119.4% in donor conversion rate for the "donor-centered" treatment against the "organizationally-centered" language of the control experience.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment include:
Desktop visitors had a 225.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 58.8% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 134.1% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 114.3% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
Executive Summary:
Many donation pages focus largely on the needs of the organization. It's just human nature ... we sit close to what we (as an organization) are doing, and where the funding priorities of our critical work must be at to maintain or expand the good work that we're able to do.
However, too often the donor is uninspired (unless thoroughly cultivated throughout the year) by this type of message. When possible, experimenting with what the DONOR believes and wants to accomplish with their philanthropic gifts can have a profound impact on getting more donors to say "YES" to your appeals.
Where you're able to—consider experimenting with channeling the donor's mindset and focus on writing a message in your donation page(s) that speaks directly to their beliefs, goals, and aspirations.

experiments.title: How testing a new messaging approach affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will be most effective at converting visitors to members and increasing revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2015 - 10/30/2015
As part of their membership campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine which messaging approach would be most appealing to their donors. At the start of the campaign, we created three landing page treatments based upon two new hypotheses of what the members would respond best to.
Our current control version focused on the current situation in American politics and framed it around the negative aspects of the “establishment.” Alternatively, we wanted to test a more positive tone using the same framework. The third treatment was a far more radical redesign using a more modern design that pointed to historical accomplishments and touted the benefits of membership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both variants resulted in a statistically significant lift

with the more radical redesign achieving a 87.2% lift over the control. This would lead us to hypothesize that the constituency is more likely to respond to a positive messaging approach. They may have frustrations with the current state of American politics but this is not what motivates them to give. They are responding to a message of hope and positive change.

experiments.title: How a layout of premiums impacts donation conversions

rows.name: Call out 1 giving level, with others below

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting premiums in an alternate layouts for desktop viewers will achieve reduce friction, decision fatigue and therefore increase donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers. Typically premium offers will have multiple giving levels that include bundles of various or multiple premiums.
For the month of October 2022, the premium was a brand-new book written by Dr. Jeffress titled, “18 Minutes with Jesus.” Dr. Jeffress traveled the country and did multiple interviews for this premium so the extra traffic provided an opportunity for us to run an A/B/C test.
Historically, Pathway to Victory will present premium giving levels in a grid format however in viewing other sites with multiple offerings we decided to test different layouts of the premiums to see if we could remove friction and help with decision fatigue in choosing a giving level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we had enough desktop traffic to run an A/B/C test.
For the "linear" version we saw the best conversion rates in donations with an increase of 33% with a level of confidence of 97.1%. For new visitors, we saw a 74.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the linear version is a layout that removes friction for the prospective donor by being a format in one column. Viewers could easily view the options, see differences in the level offerings, and make a well informed decision.
For further testing, we are going to explore a "most popular" call out, test into the language and description of offers to help further drive clarity and remove any decision fatigue the viewer may experience.

experiments.title: How decreasing the ask amount on a content offer instant donation page impacted conversions (physical resource)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the gift array  for new names acquired will achieve an increase in transactions because we have decreased the perceived sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that has physical resources to acquire new names through Facebook and website traffic.
When a resource is requested, the individual completes a form to receive the item and is then directed to an instant donation page.
The gift array used on these instant donation pages was $25, $50, $75, $95.
As these are brand new people to Pathway to Victory, we wanted to see if reducing the array would increase motivation to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, while we did see a decrease in average gift, $27.24 vs $16.15, we saw an increase of 130.9% in transactions. This increase in transactions contributed to an increase of overall revenue by 37.8% with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that by providing a lower ask in the gift array, we reduced anxiety for potential new donors. As new contacts are acquired through these resources, a $25 ask can be perceived as a "risk" whereas a $10 ask is more conservative and less of a risk and sacrifice to new prospective donors.
For these donation pages, PTV does not have a minimum donation amount required therefore they experience many gifts that are $1-5. However, with this experiment, we saw a DECREASE in these low-level gifts and saw more donors willing to make a $10 gift.
Our hypothesis is that by having the ask as $10 and "risk" being decreased, then more individuals were willing to make the full donation of $10 or more rather than a donation of $1-5.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.8% increase with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How specificity affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does broadening the recipient of the fundraising ask affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2015 - 03/14/2015
CaringBridge was launching a two-week fundraising campaign and wanted to run a test on the tribute donation pages for each Journal Site. Because traffic from tribute pages is generated from a specific person’s Journal, the CaringBridge team wanted to test the impact of making an ask just for that person versus an ask to support the entire CaringBridge community. The result would deliver valuable learnings for future fundraising campaigns and landing page tests.
experiments.keyLearnings: The broader ask introduced in the treatment reduced donor conversion rate by 17.6%. This told the team that people who begin the donation process from an individual’s page are more motivated by honoring their friend or loved one than by supporting the entire community.

experiments.title: How clarity and ordering on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will ordering the donation steps and clarifying language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/04/2016
The treatment with the ordered copy produced a 12.5% lift in donations, which proved that the user testing had unlocked a powerful principle. While CaringBridge saw their donation process as one big step, the donors responded positively when they were told what it took to complete each step with numerical ordering and increased clarity.
This is a powerful reminder that we must learn to see our donation processes through the eyes of the donor to properly assist them and markedly improve conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had done some user testing in anticipation of launching a new site experience. During that testing, a few users asked questions that suggested that the donation process was unclear and could use clarity and ordering. Based on these responses, they developed a treatment that numbered the header copy in the donation steps, used more personal and descriptive language, and increased perceived security around the donor’s information.
In the first header, they changed “Make your donation” to “1. Choose your donation amount”. Then, they added a number to the next call-to-action, “Write a Message of Support”. Finally, they changed the header “Your Information” to “3. Enter Your Information (It’s secure)”, which was designed to add clarity and reduce anxiety.
They launched an A/B test to quantify the impact.

experiments.title: How removing third-party quotes affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does third-party credibility decrease conversion when the primary value proposition is a personal experience?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2016 - 03/10/2016
This is a powerful test that has implications for future appeals and fundraising campaigns. While the power of story can be a compelling addition to a fundraising ask, it might get in the way for a site like CaringBridge that serves as the conduit for person-to-person interactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had featured quotes from a family who used the site on their donation pages for a monthlong campaign. After the campaign was over, the quotes stayed on the donation pages.
However, previous testing had shown that since CaringBridge is a very personal experience, and response often decreased when a third-party family (that was unfamiliar to the user) was introduced.
We developed a treatment page that removed the third-party quote and ran an A/B split test to determine a winner.
