experiments.title: How security indicators on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements increase the perception of form security increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/12/2017
CaringBridge had run a series of tests on their donation forms to significantly increase conversion over a period of three years. However, they proposed a new test, after reading a research study that showed that the most common reason web visitors didn’t complete transactions online was a perceived lack of security.
They wondered if their donation form was losing potential donors because it didn’t visually represent how secure it was. So, they created a special security box around the credit card section of form and added a padlock into the shaded box to represent that the form was secure. This didn’t make the form more secure—it was already PCI compliant and had the highest levels of security. All these design tweaks did was increase perception of security with the end user. They launched an A/B test to see if it increased donor conversion as they hypothesized.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the additional visual indicators of security produced a 14.4% lift in donor conversion, which showed that there was a segment of donors who were convinced to complete their transaction with the extra reassurance that their transaction was secure.
What’s interesting is that the design of the CaringBridge form puts this test far below the “fold” of the page, which indicates that some visitors might have actually started the donation process and “bailed out” because they didn’t feel that their transaction would be secure. This suggests further testing to increase the perception of security earlier in the process.

experiments.title: How straightforward copy and a quantified ask affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more straightforward language with a quantified ask increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2017 - 03/21/2017
CaringBridge had run an experiment that implemented more “need-based”, common language on their tribute widget, which was the start of the giving process on their journal pages. They had run this experiment as part of a match, so they wanted to re-run the experiment without the matching language to see if that impacted conversion.
The control asked visitors to “honor” their friend or loved one with a donation. The treatment used much more straightforward language: “Can you help power their site?” It also incorporated a quantified ask: “$30 keeps their site online for a month.”
They set up an A/B test to determine a winner in a non-match scenario.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy in the tribute widget produced a 63.9% increase in donor conversion—a full 23 percentage points higher than the copy with the match language. This suggests that CaringBridge hit on some powerful language that hits home with their donors, and the match language, while providing an incentive, might actually complicate the value proposition.
This experiment suggests more testing around the quantified ask.

experiments.title: How isolating a quantified ask affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: When isolated, what impact does the quantified ask have on clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 03/27/2017
CaringBridge tested a radical redesign that produced a big lift in donor conversion. This was a valuable finding, but they wanted further clarity on the lift, since two factors were changed. In the first experiment, they changed the headline of their tribute widget and added a “quantified ask”, with the offer that a $30 gift would keep their friend or loved one’s site online for a month.
They were curious which element had the most impact between these two. So they created two treatments: one that reverted to the old headline and included the quantified ask, and one that reverted to the old body copy (without the quantified ask), but kept the winning headline.
They launched an A/B/C test to validate a winner and determine the impact of each element on conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The first treatment, with just the quantified ask, produced a 26% increase in clickthrough rate. However, in further testing, it failed to produce any increase in conversion and produced a spike in refunds.
The second treatment, with the headline change only, produced a 10% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the cumulative lift in previous testing had more correlation with the presence of the quantified ask, but was boosted by the headline as well.
This prompted further testing to see if they could clarify the message on the donation page to reduce confusion and refund rate.

experiments.title: How an open field on a donation form affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an open field on a donation form affect donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2017 - 06/06/2017
CaringBridge had executed several tests on their main donation page and found that the motivation to give on that page was very different than on their “tribute” pages, which was where most of their revenue came in. Their main donation page had a high average gift, and the motivation to give had proven to be much less personal and much more mission-based.
Since average gifts were high on this page, they wondered if the six-button gift array (which had beaten a three-button array in a previous test) were actually hindering average gift and conversion. The suggested gift amounts were meant to provide relief to decision friction—but what if they added to it?
CaringBridge decided to test this array against an “open field”, which allowed the donor to decide on their own gift amount, with a stated minimum donation of $10. They split the traffic to determine which had a greater effect on donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 125.9% lift in conversion on the main donation page. This showed that the array, which was designed to combat decision friction, might have actually contributed to it. One danger in the open field treatment was that average gift would dip without the suggested amounts to provide “anchors”. CaringBridge kept a close eye on average gift, but found no valid difference in the two forms, which meant that there was a valid revenue increase from this test.

This prompted a series of tests to separate desktop traffic from mobile and determine the individual impact of each experience on conversion.

It is a good reminder that we need to prompt visitors to do our desired action but we also need to make sure we fulfill upon the original reason that they came to the site first. By having the ad at the end of the article, this allowed the visitor to first read the content they were looking for before presenting them with the next step.

experiments.title: How a button’s value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which language on the donate button most clearly communicates the unique value proposition of Online for Life?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2014 - 07/22/2014
The general donation button on Online for Life’s website had always communicated a value proposition beyond simply giving a gift. The buttons message of “Save a Baby” communicated what their gift accomplished. However, we wanted to test whether this button copy was the best way to communicate both value and the purpose of the subsequent page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Give” button resulted in 41% less conversions and a 30.5% decrease in revenue. Removing the value proposition from the button ended up significantly reducing the visitors likelihood to give.
On the other hand, expanding the audience beyond just “Save a Baby” to “Save a Child” increased revenue by 62.2%. This simple change reminded donors of the long term impact of their gift. We save want to save a baby from abortion because of who they will become over time.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 2.8% decrease in conversion rate
				× 66.8% increase in average gift
									62.2% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How a video impacts the conversion rate of a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a transcription of a video impact donor conversion rate compared to the video itself?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of their calendar year end campaign, Buckner International created a video of their president telling a story from the previous year and making a direct ask for support at the end. The message and imagery of the video was compelling but our concern was that visitors that did not necessarily respond to videos would lose interest before the ask was made. We decided to set up an experiment where we displayed a transcription on the page in place of the video. We then sent an email to their house file to see which would produce the best conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video transcription ended up producing a 560.2% lift to the overall donor conversion on the page. It reached both a statistical level of validity and a valid sample size.
The video was a great way to visually reinforce the messaging of the campaign and we saw high engagement when posted on Facebook but, when it comes to the actual ask, we are much better suited using text to convey the message so that the potential donor does not get distracted from the primary call to action.

experiments.title: How simplifying the design and expanding the content affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: A longer explanation of OFL’s unique process coupled with a simpler page design will allow donors to get a better understanding of their value proposition which will, in turn, increase the overall revenue for the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2014 - 12/28/2014
For their year end campaign, Online for Life used their unique process for reaching abortion-determined women and the cost that it takes to save a child as the primary value proposition. The question became how much the page needed to explain the details of the process vs. keeping it high level.  We created two treatments to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: It would appear that less explanation was needed than we had originally thought.  This may be the result of previous efforts to explain how OFL works or the urgency that comes with year end.  In any case, we will know that less is more for future campaigns.

				× 55.9% decrease in average gift
									78.2% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How an assumed value proposition affects traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional content and clarifying the headlines increase the number of visitors that take the action?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2015 - 10/07/2015
On the new Alliance Defending Freedom website, the primary pages within the navigation serve as a means for visitors to learn more about the various aspects of the organization. These pages offer multiple links throughout the page allows each visitor to “choose their own adventure” depending upon the information they want to learn more about.
In the About Us section specifically, each page ends with two equally weighted calls-to-action for both email acquisition and a donation.  They each have a very simple headline with a field or button to allow the visitor to complete the action.
We had a hypothesis that if we were able to increase the value of those sections by adding clarifying content and additional copy, we could increase the number of visitors that took the action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We experienced a 47% decrease in the number of visitors to the donation page with the changes made to the headline, the addition of copy, and the modification of the button.  Upon further reflection, the decrease in traffic may be due to the button addressing only religious freedom. ADF defends other issues such as sanctity of life and marriage.  It may also have decreased the clarity of the call to action causing people to be less likely to click.
By choosing to address only the religious freedom aspect of the organization, we may have pushed away the visitors who were more attracted to other elements of ADF.

				gift).

				× 47.0% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How a direct ask affects traffic to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a soft call-to-action or a more direct call-to-action drive more traffic and donations from the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2015 - 12/02/2015
This past Giving Tuesday, Alliance Defending Freedom took a different approach than most nonprofit organizations. Instead of making a direct ask to their constituency, they utilized a message of stewardship to their constituency. They created a blog post that explained the impact the donors’  support had over the past year with specific examples of people that had been helped. This post was sent out to both their social media audience and their email file.  At the end of the post, there was a short value proposition statement on why their donations were so important and a button with the soft call-to-action of “I Support Religious Freedom” that directed them to a donation page.
We had the hypothesis that since it was Giving Tuesday, we could be more direct in our call-to-action. We created a new treatment that changed the wording on the button “Make your Gift This Giving Tuesday” in order to add clarity around the action we wanted them to take.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The genesis for this experiment came from the learnings of two prior tests. In those tests, we learned that ADF’s blog traffic responds better to softer calls-to-action while their email traffic responds better to direct asks within the email. These two experiments proved that the blog and email file were two disparate audiences with differing motivations. Since this page was going to receive traffic from both audiences, we wanted to discover which tactic would prove most successful.
We learned that the direct call-to-action reduced traffic to the donation page by 56.5% and this reduction in traffic then resulted in 29% less gifts*. This reinforces the need to use softer language with additional value proposition for the blog audience.
* The 29% decrease in gifts was not yet statistically valid but was quickly approaching validity by the time the test was finished.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
56.5% decrease in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a direct ask on a blog impacts long-term donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can a direct donation ask on the blog have a significant impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2017 - 05/16/2017
For Alliance Defending Freedom, the mobile version of their blog received a significant volume of traffic from Facebook. Historically, we had used this as an opportunity to acquire new email addresses to the file. We decided to test whether this audience could also have the potential to be a good donor acquisition source. To do this, we replaced the standard email acquisition offer with a direct ask for one of their clients, Barronelle.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the visitors that saw the direct ask ad, were 81% more likely to make a gift than those that did not. At the same time, removing the email offer, reduced the email acquisition by 98%. This reinforced our expected outcome and gives us the knowledge that there are multiple levers we can pull depending upon our desired outcome.
Upon further analysis, we found that the majority of visitors did not make their gift immediately when they saw the direct ask ad. Instead, they came back to that donation page when prompted on a different channel and made their gift at a later date. This would indicate that the blog may be better used reinforcing the need rather than making a direct ask.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 81.4% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How an animated thermometer in an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including an animated thermometer in an email increased conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2015 - 09/29/2015
Hillsdale ran a fundraising campaign in September 2015 to help raise money to increase enrollments in their free online courses. For many years, they had used animated thermometers on their donation pages to increase response. They decided to test animated thermometers as a progress bar towards the goal of new subscribers in this email in to see if they can increase donor conversion from the email.
They created an animated thermometer that displayed in the email as an image, posted on a external server. The night before the campaign was due to end, they inserted this thermometer into a treatment of the email, which used a smaller sample size to reduce server load.
They split their email file and ran a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the animated thermometer, produced a 49% increase in conversion. Previous testing had shown that a countdown clock in an email could increase donor conversion, but this experiment showed that displaying progress towards a goal could have the same, if not greater impact.
The thermometer works because it brings the visual impact of the proximity to the goal one step closer to the reader. Previously, they would read about the goal but have to click through to the landing page to get the impact. This brings it to them, and provides an animated element to draw them into the copy.
There are many technical ramifications to running this test that must be considered, but the net impact makes the case that it is well worth it.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 49.3% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.
Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

				gift).

				× 142.3% increase in average gift
									11.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How giving donors an exit path affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the direct link to the course, found at the bottom of the page, impact conversion or revenue to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2015 - 07/22/2015
The Constitution 101 online course is one of the primary methods for email and donor acquisition for Hillsdale College.  This is a free course available in exchange for the visitors’ name and address.
After signing up, the registrants are led to a donation page in hopes of an immediate donor conversion. The trouble with this process is that some participants mistake the donation page as a payment page for the course which causes some frustration.
We wanted to find a way to clarify that the donation is not required while increasing the perceived value of the course.  We decided to add a link at the bottom of the page that allows them to start the course immediately in addition to giving a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The link found at the bottom of the donation page copy led to a 25.7% drop in donor conversion. This was a small change but it led to a significant drop in the page conversion. Whenever we give visitors an option to leave the donor funnel, there will be some that choose that option which impacts overall conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition and adding a call-to-action affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action and reinforcing the value proposition improve donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2016 - 06/13/2016
In an effort to optimize the main donation page for The Heritage Foundation, we took a look at the copy that was currently there to find ways to improve and increase the force of the value proposition. While the existing copy was strong and provided solid evidentials, we noticed it was missing an important element in the copy, an ask. Our hypothesis was that we would be able to increase donor conversion and overall revenue by adding copy, to what was already there, that included a call-to-action and reinforcement of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had arrived at the original copy through a series of prior experiments. As a result, we were relatively confident that it adequately communicated the value proposition of The Heritage Foundation, but by simply adding an additional paragraph that reinforced that message and asking them to make a gift, we were able to not only get more donors to give, but give even more generously. The treatment increased donor conversion by 49%.
Additionally, we saw an increase in average gift by 62.9%. While the average gift lift had not yet statistically validated by the time the test was shut off, the approaching validity seems to indicate that we would be experiencing a combined lift to revenue of 143.5%.
This experiment reinforced the importance of increasing the force of the value proposition by reminding people of what their gift will do and always having a call-to-action in the copy.

experiments.title: How the friction added with website navigation affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding the standard website header and navigation to the landing page ease the concerns of visitors about the cost of the course while not affecting or increasing conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2015 - 03/17/2015
Hillsdale College offers a free, online training series named Constitution 101.  After initially enrolling, participants are sent to a contextualized donation opportunity where we ask for a gift to help support the efforts of Hillsdale.
There had been complaints recently that the contextualized donation page gave the impression that the course was not free.  We wanted to alleviate those concerns while not reducing the revenue or conversion rate of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of navigation decreased conversion rate by 17%. Whenever we offer donors the opportunity to explore more areas of the website, we are increasing the likelihood that they will.  While we were trying to decrease anxiety, we inadvertently increased the friction associated with making the gift.

experiments.researchQuestion: Does the radically different approach to the Imprimis survey results in both more survey completions and additional donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2015 - 07/25/2015
This experiment was the first in a series of iterations done in the Imprimis Survey campaign. This first experiment tested a radically different approach to the existing Imprimis survey by reducing the copy at the top, changing the page design, making the survey into a two-step process with the donation ask following the survey, and simplifying the survey by adding conditional logic to only show certain questions based upon previous responses.
A subsequent experiment proved these results to be invalid due to instrumentation error.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a statistically significant 31.6% decrease in survey completions. However, a subsequent experiment proved these results to be invalid due to instrumentation error.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all “sold”.
This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 51.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all “sold”.
This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donations and revenue, in spite of a different design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/15/2016
The Heritage Foundation was sending lots of traffic to their “Conservative Declaration”, a document that asked conservatives to unite under shared principles. Immediately after signing, the visitors were presented with an offer to become a member of The Heritage Foundation by making a donation. The entire experience took place within a single design.
Heritage had another membership page that had been performing extremely well. However, design was a key part of this page, and it didn’t match the design of the Conservative Declaration. Knowing that congruency is a key part of conversion, they decided to test it anyways to see if increasing the force of the value proposition outweighed design consistency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 460% increase in revenue, despite breaking from the design of the original offer. This is a powerful learning that shows that the message is more important than the container.
This also points the way to a future test to see if revenue can be further increased by working the new value proposition into the existing design and increasing congruency.

									460.4% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition of a donation page affects conversion rate and negative feedback
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity of the value proposition (and enrollment) increase donor conversion and decrease negative feedback?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/10/2015 - 10/02/2015
Hillsdale College had run a battery of tests on the donation page for their Constitution 101 course. Conversion had increased steadily, but there were still a lot of complaints coming in to the support team that indicated that new enrollees thought that they HAD to give a gift to take the course. It became obvious that a lack of clarity was causing cognitive friction and reducing donations, so they created a treatment in an attempt to plainly state that the visitor was now enrolled.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did conversion increase significantly, there was a dropoff in the amount of negative feedback relating to this course’s enrollment. Making it clear that the visitor was enrolled was pivotal in reducing cognitive friction, which had a direct impact on the conversion rate. This copy was rolled out across all of Hillsdale’s course donation pages.

experiments.title: How testing the call-to-action on thank you page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action will produce the greatest amount of revenue in the long term?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2014 - 12/08/2014
The follow-on call-to-action of giving to Heritage Action produced significantly more gifts and additional revenue beyond what would be provided by the monthly donors. The revenue given in the Heritage Action donation even out performed the recurring donor revenue when the recurring donor gifts were multiplied to account for the recurring nature of the gift. The donors saw Heritage Action as another way to make their gift go further which carried more value in their mind.
This follow-on call-to-action will be rolled out to other donation forms maintained by Heritage and it will be utilized in future campaigns for join fundraising opportunities.-
experiments.keyLearnings: With the number of donors each month, the thank you page on the primary Heritage donation form offered an opportunity for testing. In an effort to further capitalize on the donor's motivation, we created two follow-on calls-to-action to either become a recurring monthly donor or to give a gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action.

experiments.title: How focusing on the product-level value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to visitors from the Daily Signal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2015 - 08/05/2015
The Heritage Foundation operates a political news organization called the Daily Signal. Part of the site has links back to the Heritage Foundation properties include a donation link found at the bottom of the page.
Since the donors coming from the site may not be as familiar with The Heritage Foundation, we wanted to discover what value proposition would be most appealing to the donors. We decided to create two treatments, one that targeted the primary value proposition of giving to The Heritage Foundation and the other that focused on a product-level value proposition of giving to the Daily Signal.
experiments.keyLearnings: By focusing the messaging on the value of the Daily Signal, we were able to better align with the visitors’ motivations. We were also able to keep visual congruence by changing the header of the page to reflect the branding of the Daily Signal. All of these adjustments led to a lift in revenue by 172.8%.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.
Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn’t stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 67.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 54.1% decrease in average gift
									85.1% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How adding incentive and removing donation form friction affects conversion

rows.name: Form with incentive and no dividers

experiments.researchQuestion: Does a lower initial donation ask in the gift array and added incentive improve the overall visitor conversion and revenue of the main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2015 - 05/27/2015
On Hillsdale College’s main donation form, we had done previous experiments to lift conversion rate 22% by modifying the format of the donation form and adding messaging around what we believed is both unique and appealing about Hillsdale College to potential donors. In an effort to continue optimizing this high traffic page, we shifted our focus to reducing friction and adding incentive to the gift.
We reduced friction by adding a lower starting donation amount to the gift array with the belief that a larger ask may make the donors feel a smaller gift would not be as impactful.  We also removed dividers between each section of the form to help move visitors down the page.  For incentive, we added the existing offer to receive a brick in the College’s Liberty Walk for a gift of $500 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new donation form treatment was able to produce a 32.5% increase in conversion and a lift to average gift of 13.8%. We noticed was that, despite adding the incentive for a $500+ gift, we didn’t see any donors give at or above that level so the incentive did not seem to have much impact on the average gift.

Our initial concern was that adding the $25 option to the gift array would lower the average gift enough to negate any increases in conversion. This proved to be false.  The lower gift was able to increase the conversion rate and it did not seem to make donors give less.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 32.5% increase in conversion rate
				× 13.8% increase in average gift
									50.8% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How conveying value in the donate button affects traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call to action can drive the most visitors to the donation page: “Donate Now”, “Make my Gift”, or “Support Imprimis”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2015 - 05/22/2015
Imprimis is the free monthly speech digest of Hillsdale College and is dedicated to educating citizens and promoting civil and religious liberty by covering cultural, economic, political, and educational issues. It has an audience of over 2.8 million so we saw this as a great opportunity to optimize the pathway to a donation.
Historically, the donate button was found at the top of the site with the text of “Donate Now.” We didn’t believe that really conveyed any value or ownership in their gift so we wanted to try two different treatments.
experiments.keyLearnings: None of the other buttons were able to drive a single visitor to the donation page. Upon further reflection, the “Support Imprimis” button was the only one that conveyed any value to the visitors. If they enjoy the publication, this is something that they would be interested in doing. The “Donate Now” and “Make my Gift” are actions that we want them to take but it is not necessarily providing any value or context to the visitor.

experiments.title: How replacing 3rd party credibility via social validation affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing recent donors will add to the credibility of the previously stated value proposition and will foster a herd mentality within the donor’s mind. This will increase conversion rate compared to other 3rd party credibility indicators
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2014 - 12/26/2014
As part of most campaign landing pages, Heritage has included 3rd party credibility indicators (such as external quotes and media appearances) as a way to reduce anxiety. However, we thought it might be possible to make it more relevant if the 3rd party credibility came from actual donors instead of well known sources. As such, we developed an embeddable widget that would scroll through recent donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Seeing recent donors did not carry as much weight as quotes and appearances in well known sources. In addition to using the 3rd party credibility indicators near the donation form, we will also include these facts in the copy and throughout the page design where appropriate.

experiments.title: How testing the call-to-action on thank you page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action will produce the greatest amount of revenue in the long term?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2014 - 12/08/2014
With the number of donors each month, the thank you page on the primary Heritage donation form offered an opportunity for testing. In an effort to further capitalize on the donor’s motivation, we created two follow-on calls-to-action to either become a recurring monthly donor or to give a gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The follow-on call-to-action of giving to Heritage Action produced significantly more gifts and additional revenue beyond what would be provided by the monthly donors. The revenue given in the Heritage Action donation even out performed the recurring donor revenue when the recurring donor gifts were multiplied to account for the recurring nature of the gift. The donors saw Heritage Action as another way to make their gift go further which carried more value in their mind.
This follow-on call-to-action will be rolled out to other donation forms maintained by Heritage and it will be utilized in future campaigns for join fundraising opportunities.-

experiments.title: How an engagement device affects clickthrough rate versus a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a survey ask instead of a direct donation ask increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
Hillsdale College wanted to send a digital follow-up to a direct mail appeal to a segment of their donors who had both a mailing address and an email address on file. These “chase” appeals had generated a significant amount of revenue in the past, but they wanted to try a new approach.
Previously, these emails just mirrored the direct mail package and made a direct ask for a donation. However, the direct mail package for their August appeal included a survey. They hypothesized that they could get better engagement by asking the donors to take a survey (that led to a donation) rather than putting a direct ask in the email. Ultimately, they hypothesized that this increase in clickthrough rate would result in more people reaching the donation page, and higher donor conversion. But first, they needed to get more clicks.
They recreated the survey online, split their email file, and send one segment a direct ask and the other a link to take a survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey, as expected, delivered a 693.1% increase in clickthrough rate. A soft ask almost always delivers more clicks than a direct donation ask, because the motivations are entirely different.
However, more clicks doesn’t always mean more donations, since people aren’t specifically clicking to make a donation. That warranted another test to see how the survey affected donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page redesign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign that removes friction and strengthens and emphasizes the value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2017 - 08/30/2017
Harvest Ministries had a donation page that produced above-average conversion rates (at nearly 20%). However, they viewed this as an 80% failure rate, and wanted to run an experiment to optimize for donor conversion.
Their control donation page kept the same template as their website (which is common for ministries and nonprofits). This template included navigation that gave many opportunities to leave the page and take other actions. The page copy also started with a premium ask, with little value proposition language to hook the visitor.
The donation form started with radio buttons, which presented possible usability friction for the visitor. The prospective donor was then presented with the choice to make a single gift or a recurring gift, the details of which were located on another page. This introduced possible decision friction into the funnel. Next was a designation. Designations can be helpful for the organization, but research has showed that they present another decision for the user and can bring the donation process to a halt.
Next, the visitor was asked to claim their premium, and fill out the typical information and payment fields, with a question about where they engage with Harvest in the middle.
The treatment removed all the excess navigation from the page and began with a simple, clear value proposition: “Your gift helps reach the lost with the gospel of Jesus Christ”. It then unpacked the value proposition, providing evidentials to each claim of value and connecting the gift to different forms of impact. Then, it repositioned the premium as a thank-you in response to the gift, rather than the reason for the gift.
The gift array was reworked into larger buttons that were easier to click and see which amount was selected. The information and payment fields were moved to the second step of a two-step screen.
Harvest launched an A/B test to discover which donation page flow resulted in more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page generated a 44.8% lift in donations — a big feat for a page that was already attracting highly motivated donors. This showed that some combination of all the elements of friction on the page were distracting would-be donors. It also proved that focusing on the clarity of the value proposition (rather than the incentive alone) can increase motivation to give.
This prompted a series of tests to remove more friction from the giving process and further clarify the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding membership level indicators affects membership renewal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding language on the donation form that informs donors what member level they qualify for and then telling them the amount needed to move to the next level increase the average revenue per visitor compared to the standard donation form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2015 - 01/08/2015
The Heritage Foundation has 12 different levels of membership that come with incremental benefits as the donors give me. The standard donation form uses member language but does not detail what level their gift qualifies them for. During their annual membership renewal campaign, we wanted to find a way to let donors know their membership level based upon their giving amount while finding ways to upgrade them.
We devised a donation form that would display what level their current gift qualifies them form and what it would take to upgrade to the next level.  We made sure to include what benefits would come with the next level in membership.  Before rolling it out, we wanted to validate that it would improve the results of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The membership language treatment was able to lift the average gift of the donor by 18.6% but
it negatively affected the conversion rate by 34.5%.
This negative effect on conversion was enough to adversely effect the overall revenue of the form.
Upon digging deeper into the data, we discovered that the language would lift the average gift for donors above $50 but would cause lower level donors, those below $25, to not give at all. We may test membership language for future campaigns but limit the language so that only the larger donors see the language.

experiments.title: How the strength of the value proposition affects pay-per-click donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion from search engine marketing?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/03/2016
The Heritage Foundation had run pay-per-click campaigns on brand terms (like “Heritage Foundation”, “Heritage Foundation membership”) for years. These campaigns ran at a positive ROI and gave users who expressed intent through search a clear path to make their gifts.
Because users had high motivation that was expressed through their search terms, a very transactional landing page had tested well. But Heritage wanted to see if a more emotional appeal might improve conversion — one that told a narrative story and utilized the four pillars of a successful value proposition: appeal, exclusivity, clarity, and credibility. The page also used photography, different background, and stacked member benefits right before it asked for the conversion.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page with the more thorough value proposition and member benefits produced a 44.1% lift in donor conversion. This tells us that we can actually affect donor motivation — even though someone is looking to make a gift, they aren’t always entirely convinced. The strength of the value proposition must capture them where they are and assure them even more in their decision.
We are never done “selling” the donation until they submit their gift. That’s why landing pages must always leverage each of the four pillars of the value proposition to maximize the force with which it is delivered.

experiments.title: How does cause-specific messaging on the donation form affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on different themes for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2025 - 07/28/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of focusing the donation form messaging around specific thematic areas of work. We hypothesized that more focused messaging would create a deeper connection with users and motivate them to give more often.  This experiment was based on market research the creative team conducted on different themes they thought might motivate users.
Control: Donate now to give every child a strong start in life.
Variant 1: Make an impact on a child’s life today–focus on rural poverty.
Variant 2: Make an impact on a child’s life today–focus on conflict.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether focusing the donation form messaging around specific thematic areas would create a stronger connection with donors and drive higher giving. The hypothesis: more targeted messaging would feel more relevant and motivate more people to give.

What the Results Suggest:
The drop in recurring giving for both treatments shows that thematic, time-specific messaging may encourage one-time gifts but discourage ongoing support. Donors may need to perceive an ongoing, unresolved need to commit to monthly giving, while focused themes can make the problem seem solvable with a single gift.
Desktop RPV was a bright spot for both treatments, but conversion dropped across the board on that device, suggesting higher-value donors may respond to focused messaging while casual browsers are put off by it.
Next Steps
Given the significant drop in recurring conversion for Treatment A, implementing either variant as-is is risky. Future tests should investigate integrating thematic messaging into the form, alongside a clear, recurring giving prompt—for example, begin with the theme and also emphasize the ongoing need before presenting gift frequency options.

experiments.title: How pre-selecting a monthly gift and amount for a Meta donation form impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting a gift amount for Meta donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2025 - 06/26/2025
Based on previous experiments, we had seen that a combination of pre-selecting monthly and $35 on the gift array had helped increase recurring conversions. We wanted to test whether this was the case across different channels; in this experiment, we focused on Meta specifically. The traffic was split 85% to control and 15% to treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether increasing the donation amounts shown on a Meta form would lead donors to give more. The hypothesis: higher displayed amounts would shift donor choices upward, raising the average gift.
Key Results:

What the Results Suggest
Two behavioral patterns help explain the lift:
The middle effect: Donors tend to gravitate toward middle options. Raising the array raised that middle choice.
 	Perceived appropriateness: The uptick in conversion suggests higher amounts may actually feel more fitting for the cause — a lower ask, like $25, may signal that the need isn't serious.
Next Steps:
Given the consistent directional lift across conversion, RPV, and average gift, and acknowledging that limited sample sizes reduce confidence in the findings, this test warrants further validation with a larger sample — particularly for desktop and recurring giving, where data was too limited to draw robust conclusions.

experiments.title: How increasing the one-time gift array on a Meta donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing gift arrays for Meta donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2025 - 12/10/2025
This test evaluated the effect of increasing the one-time ask array on Meta forms. Our hypothesis was that keeping the end anchor amount of $150 but increasing the amounts between would increase average gift without negatively impacting conversion. We had previously tested the one-time pre-selected gift array for Meta; the test results can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine if increasing the middle amounts within a one-time ask array on Meta forms, while keeping the $150 end anchor, would increase the average gift value without decreasing conversion rates.
What Drove the Lift
Most donors default to the pre-selected amount, so the gains were concentrated among active choosers — donors who navigated away from the default. Two dynamics appear to have influenced their behavior:
The compromise effect: Donors tend to avoid extremes and gravitate toward a middle option. Raising the middle amounts nudged that "safe" choice upward.
 	Reduced sticker shock: Tighter gaps between amounts made the transition from lower to higher options feel more natural.
Next Steps
We'll be rolling out the variant and continuing to test — both around the specific amounts and which value is pre-selected as the default.

experiments.title: How incorporating claims of value on a matching donation page impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating Bible League Canada’s claims of value on their campaign donation page will increase transactions because the added-value prop language will make the ask more appealing and better aligned with what potential donors care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 04/23/2024
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization that provides Bibles and training for local champions and believers around the world. They ran a Value Proposition Workshop to identify their key claims of value that are appealing and unique to BLC, which were then shared with their donors and subscribers in a survey.
The survey revealed that supporters were most motivated by the claims identified in the Value Proposition Workshop.
Bible League Canada follows Jesus’ model for reaching people by equipping local believers to share the Word of God with their neighbors and build healthy, sustainable churches.
Bible League Canada intentionally provides Bibles, one at a time, to people who want to learn more about Jesus through discipleship programs in their local community.
We set out to test whether emphasizing these key claims of value on a high-urgency campaign donation page would increase the number of visitors who donate. Our hypothesis was that if the donation page highlights the claims supporters care about, visitors will be more likely to feel aligned with the campaign and choose to make a gift.
We ran an A/B test in VWO: half of the visitors saw the original landing page, while the other half saw a version with the value claims.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating Bible League Canada's claims of value on their campaign donation page led to a significant increase in transactions. The treatment group, which saw the page with the value-added claims, experienced a 44.6% increase in transactions compared to the control group. This indicates that aligning the donation ask with what visitors care about can positively influence their decision to donate.
However, the statistical confidence level for these results is 87.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to fully confirm that emphasizing the claims of value is indeed the cause of the increase in transactions.
The treatment group also saw a 64.3% increase in their average gift amount ($38.08 vs $33.51 for the control), which supports the hypothesis that the added claims of value make the donation ask more appealing to visitors. This increase had a 86% level of confidence, so further testing is needed to fully validate this lift.
Future experiments should continue testing the impact of highlighting Bible League Canada's value claims on donation pages, such as through new A/B tests or by varying their presentation across different donation pages.

experiments.title: Does the placement of the payment module impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe moving the module to the end of the form for donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2025 - 04/21/2025
Due to a PCI update from our donation form vendor, the payment module now loads more slowly. We aimed to test where to place this module on the form to avoid adding friction. Our hypothesis was that placing the slow-loading module near the top would increase user friction and reduce conversions. To address this, we moved the module to the last step, giving it time to load.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of repositioning the PCI payment module within the donation form. We hypothesized that placing the module near the top of the form was causing friction due to its slow load time, leading users to encounter an unresponsive payment field before it fully rendered. By moving the module to the final step, we aimed to give it sufficient time to load before the user needed to interact with it.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate: +18% lift (80% confidence, meaning there is an 80% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
 	Overall Revenue Per Visitor: +8% lift (64% confidence, meaning there is a 64% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
Insights:
The improvement in conversion rate reflects a meaningfully smoother customer journey. Repositioning the payment module to the last step ensures it is fully loaded by the time users reach it, eliminating a key point of friction. Mobile results showed significant gains, while desktop RPV declined and warrants further investigation.
Recommendation & Next Steps
Based on these results, we have moved the module to the bottom of all top-trafficked donation forms and will adopt this placement as the default for new forms going forward.

experiments.title: Does pre-selecting higher default gift amounts within donation tiers increase overall revenue while maintaining average gift value?”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and relies on reader-supported giving to fund its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover sought to optimize its donation form experience to increase revenue per visitor while maintaining stable average gift values—particularly for prospect-heavy acquisition traffic.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how different gift arrays and preselection strategies affect conversion and gift value. For this test, we combined two variables: gift array size ($25/50/100 vs. $50/100/250) and preselecting the second gift amount on both arrays, resulting in four total variants. The test was run across multiple content offers to build sample size and understand how performance varied by offer type.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
directional but not statistically valid
Performance metrics
Total samples: 364
 	Total donations: 25
 	Overall average gift: $33.40
 	Control array ($50/100/250) was the lowest-performing variant on both transactions and revenue per visitor
 	Lower array variants ($25/50/100) showed higher conversion rates and higher revenue per visitor than control in some treatments
Audience and offer context
Baseline AOV for this offer is ~$40
 	~70% of traffic is from prospecting audiences
 	Preselection did not materially change performance for this offer or audience mix

experiments.title: How adding an option to donate through ACH impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ACH option for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the donation form’s payment section. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option, but would improve the user experience for those who would interact with it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the payment section on the donation form. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option but would create a better user experience for the users who would interact with it.

Key Results:

-       +10% non-statistically significant lift (81%) in overall conversion rate.

-       No change in overall RPV.

This test showed that by offering another familiar, often trusted option, we removed a potential barrier for donors who prefer or are accustomed to direct debit. Also, direct debit is often perceived as more secure and reliable, so the mere presence of an additional trusted option reduced friction for all users and made more donors comfortable completing the gift.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have implemented the direct debit option across all donation forms.

experiments.title: How adding match math language to a donation form impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the impact of a match gift for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2025 - 10/28/2025
Save the Children runs multiple match campaigns throughout the year; for their fall match campaign, we wanted to test whether adding in ‘match math’ to a donation form would help increase conversions. Our hypothesis was that by showing the impact of a donor’ gift through ‘math’– Your gift of $25 becomes $50 in impact— we will help users recognize the increased impact of their gift, which will increase conversion. This experiment had 3 variants, with a control and 2 treatments that had the match math either above or below the gift array selections.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
-       -14% non-statistically significant decrease (90%) in overall conversion rate for variant B and –17% non-statistically significant decrease (94%) for variant C.
-       26% non-statistically significant increase (88%) in desktop conversion rate for variant C.
-       68% statistically significant increase (98%) in desktop RPV for variant C.
This test shows that our hypothesis proved true for desktop users but added friction for mobile users. The additional copy on mobile led to a shift in layout each time they selected a new ask amount, which has been shown to correlate with higher abandonment.

experiments.title: Does Displaying Multiple Ministry Brands Strengthen Donor Confidence?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the logos of Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands below the donation button for Facebook-driven content audiences will achieve a higher conversion rate and increased revenue, as visible scale and brand familiarity strengthen trust and signal broader mission impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2026
Luther Seminary operates multiple well-known ministry brands, including Enter the Bible, Working Preacher, and Faith+Lead. Each brand serves a distinct audience while advancing Luther’s broader mission of providing free, high-quality biblical resources and leadership development.
Many donors first encounter Luther through a single resource, such as an Enter the Bible article or a Faith+Lead course, and may not immediately recognize that these offerings are part of a larger network. This raises an important motivational question: Does emphasizing the breadth of Luther’s ministries increase donor confidence and willingness to give?
To explore this, we ran an A/B test on Facebook-driven traffic to two content-based donation pages:
Enter the Bible: Matthew
Faith+Lead: Genesis to Revelation Course
Visitors were randomly split between:
Control: A standard donation page featuring only the originating brand.
Treatment: The same donation page, with the addition of logos representing Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands displayed prominently below the “Give” button.
The donation form defaulted to one-time giving in both versions to isolate the impact of brand presentation.
This experiment aims to answer a central donor motivation question: Does highlighting organizational scale and a family of trusted brands increase giving by reinforcing credibility and perceived impact?
The primary success metric is transactions (both one-time and monthly gifts), with revenue per visitor, one-time, and recurring gifts as secondary measures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The overall transaction result achieved 89.3% statistical confidence, which does not meet the conventional 95% threshold for declaring a statistically significant winner. Because of this, we cannot definitively claim that the treatment caused the decrease in conversion rate. However, the results are directionally consistent across nearly every metric:
Overall conversion rate declined (-15.14%)
 	One-time giving declined (-15.51%)
 	Recurring giving declined (-34.53%, low volume)
 	Revenue per visitor declined (-7.18%)
When multiple related metrics move in the same direction, it strengthens the directional signal — even if the test does not reach full statistical significance. Directionally, this suggests that displaying multiple affiliated brand logos below the donation button likely introduced distraction or diluted the primary brand connection at the moment of giving. For Facebook-driven, content-based traffic, donors may be primarily motivated by the specific resource or brand they engaged with, rather than by the broader institutional ecosystem. At the point of conversion, clarity and singular focus may be more powerful than signals of scale.

experiments.title: How a past year-end donation page performed compared to the current year-end donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running last year’s donation page copy versus this year’s donation page copy for CYE donation page visitors will yield no meaningful difference in overall donor conversion, because both pages communicate a sufficiently similar value proposition, urgency, and call to action, motivating giving at comparable levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2025 - 12/19/2025
During IMB’s CYE Campaign, we observed unexpected softness in overall performance. While several potential contributing factors were identified, we prioritized validating whether the donation page itself was contributing to the issue.
To isolate that risk, we ran a direct A/B test comparing last year’s donation page copy to this year’s version. Although the test ran for only a few days, the results were directional and clear enough to warrant decisive action during IMB’s largest fundraising season.
This year’s donation page copy converted donors at a higher rate, allowing us to confidently remove last year’s version to protect conversions and revenue at a critical moment in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that the treatment donation page (last year’s copy) drove a 32.6% decrease in overall transactions (81% LOC). While the test did not reach full statistical validation, the directional signal was clear and unfavorable, reflecting a negative audience response to the treatment.

Given the strength of this directional learning—and the risk of suppressing conversion during IMB’s largest fundraising season—we ended the test.

These findings confirm that this year’s donation page copy is converting donors at a higher rate, indicating that the donation page itself is not the primary driver of the campaign’s softness. As a result, additional testing was required to better diagnose the underlying factors impacting performance.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a year-end donation page impacts overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the condensed copy, countdown clock, and goal thermometer on the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion by reducing friction in the giving experience and allowing donors—particularly mobile users—to complete the donation process more seamlessly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/25/2025
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we launched a three-way test designed to introduce meaningful variation and identify elements that could improve donor conversion.
Control: Standard CYE donation page
Treatment A: CYE donation page with countdown clock and goal thermometer
Treatment B: Hyper-condensed copy with countdown clock and goal thermometer
We anticipated Treatment B would outperform the other variations, as it significantly reduced page length—an advantage for mobile donors—while preserving urgency and social proof through the countdown clock and goal thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results show that Treatment A led to a modest 3.4% decrease in overall transactions (15.2% LOC), while Treatment B drove a substantial 27.8% decrease in transactions with a high level of directional confidence (90.1% LOC). Although neither variation reached full statistical validation, the magnitude and confidence of the decline associated with Treatment B clearly indicated a negative impact on donor conversion. Given the critical nature of the CYE fundraising window and the risk of continued conversion suppression, we have decided to remove Treatment B from rotation. This allowed Treatment A to continue running against the control, enabling additional learning without materially jeopardizing overall conversion or revenue during a high-stakes fundraising period.

experiments.title: Can democracy-focused messaging outperform urgency-based copy on a donation page during EOY?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using EOY-reframed, democracy- and community-focused messaging for WUNC donors would increase donor motivation and conversion compared to crisis-focused copy because values-based framing can strengthen intrinsic motivation and reinforce donors’ identity as supporters of public media.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 11/25/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving North Carolina with a mission to inform, engage, and strengthen civic life, supported largely by listener contributions. As the organization entered the 2025 end-of-year (EOY) fundraising period, sustaining revenue growth and donor motivation remained key program goals.
This experiment tested donation page copy during a high-traffic EOY moment to understand whether reframing the ask around democracy and community impact could outperform WUNC’s existing urgency-based messaging tied to federal funding cuts. The test was prioritized as part of a broader effort to evolve messaging beyond crisis framing and assess when values-based appeals resonate most with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
Big takeaway: The urgency-based control outperformed the democracy-focused treatment on the primary KPI (donations). Secondary metrics show a similar directional pattern, though only one reaches near-significant confidence.
📊 Performance metrics (with confidence comparisons)
Total donations (primary KPI)Control: 25 / 126 (19.8%)Treatment: 13 / 111 (11.7%)Difference: +8.1 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~92%❌ Not statistically valid at 95%, but directionally meaningfulOne-time donationsControl: 19 / 126 (15.1%)Treatment: 8 / 111 (7.2%)Difference: +7.9 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~95%⚠️ Borderline statistical validity; strongest secondary signal in favor of urgency messagingSustaining donationsControl: 6 / 126 (4.8%)Treatment: 5 / 111 (4.5%)Difference: +0.3 ptsConfidence level: <10%❌ No meaningful or directional differenceRevenueControl: $4,100Treatment: $880Revenue strongly favored the control, driven by higher conversion volume.⚠️ No statistical confidence calculated, as revenue is not suitable for a two-proportion z-test in this context.
✂️ Segment interpretation
The performance gap was driven primarily by one-time donor conversion, not sustaining behavior.Sustainer response was effectively neutral between variants.
Next Steps
Continue running with the current urgency-based messaging for EOY. Additional messaging tests are planned to determine when donor responsiveness begins shifting toward values-based framing. Timing is a key variable under consideration, as this test ran immediately before Giving Tuesday, when urgency messaging historically performs better.

experiments.title: How adding tangible benefits about monthly giving impacts monthly donor conversion on video series acquisition donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clear, tangible benefits for becoming a monthly donor on acquisition donation pages will increase monthly donor conversion, as it makes the offer to become a monthly donor clearer and appealing, thereby increasing motivation to commit.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2025 - 09/23/2025
A nonprofit organization committed to supporting families through biblically based resources and guidance. As part of ongoing efforts to increase recurring donor conversion, we designed a test to evaluate whether highlighting exclusive monthly donor benefits would encourage more visitors to become monthly givers on high-traffic acquisition donation pages.
We ran this test across three acquisition funnels tied to popular video series: Proof You Need, Contagious Joy, and Becoming Unoffendable. Their donation pages are shown to users after they sign up for the video series. The pages request a gift of $30 or more, and the gift form defaults to a monthly option. The pages also show a yellow callout above the form to further promote monthly giving.
To build on that foundation, we tested the addition of a new message block near the bottom of the page that highlighted two concrete benefits for monthly donors:
A 40% discount at the  store, plus free shipping on all online orders
A free, hand-picked faith-building resource delivered monthly
We hypothesized that highlighting these tangible, recurring benefits would increase perceived value and commitment, ultimately leading to more recurring donor conversions.
This A/B test was structured as a 50/50 traffic split across all three donation pages, with the control maintaining the current design and messaging, and the treatment introducing the new benefits copy above the donation form. This write-up covers the aggregated results from the three campaigns.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to expectations, adding benefit language to promote monthly giving led to a decrease in both recurring and overall donor conversions. While not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level, the treatment showed a 24.4% drop in monthly donor conversions (48 gifts in the control group vs. 37 in the treatment group) at 80% confidence, and a 14.4% drop in total transactions (220 in the control group vs. 192 in the treatment group) at 89.4% confidence. These results fall below the standard threshold for statistical significance but indicate a consistent downward trend worth noting. This outcome was surprising, as we hypothesized that surfacing tangible donor perks would increase perceived value and encourage more visitors to become monthly givers. However, our interpretation is that introducing additional value proposition elements — even if well-intentioned — may have overwhelmed users on pages already rich in content. These acquisition donation pages serve multiple purposes: confirming video series sign-ups, offering a free book premium, explaining the mission, and prompting donations. By layering on monthly donor benefits, we may have inadvertently created cognitive overload, making the decision-making process harder rather than easier. This result suggests that in highly content-dense environments, more messaging is not always better — even when it adds value. In these contexts, simplicity and a focused message may be more effective than trying to incorporate every persuasive angle. It also reinforces the importance of considering where and how value propositions are introduced on a page. Next Steps:
Test placement and simplicity: Test simpler, more integrated monthly giving prompts (e.g., in the callout or post–one-time gift popup) to avoid competing with other key messages.
 	Explore benefit framing: Future tests could frame benefits around impact or mission alignment, rather than just transactional perks.

experiments.title: How a one-step donation form performs against a 2-step control for a content offer instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rates because of the reduction in friction on the form generated by the extra steps.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2025 - 10/15/2025
The Hoover Institution is a think thank that is affiliated with Stanford University. For the last couple of years, their donation forms had a 2-step process; the first step was fill out your information and the second step (you had to click on payment information) was add in your billing info. This was a format we had tested into many years ago, but given the data we have seen with other organizations, we wanted to test whether a one-step form would make an impact.
We ran this test on one of Hoover’s more popular content offers, and ran it for 3 months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders can lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment group, which used a one-step form instead of a two-step form, experienced a 12.7% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a directional level of confidence of 85%. There were no recurring transactions as a result of this test, so the results are for one-time donations.
Due to some platform limitations, the one-step process was still not as 'clean' as we would have liked (you still needed to click on 'show total' on the treatment form to complete the gift), but the level of confidence was high enough were we felt comfortable declaring a winner on this test.
We will continue to test this set-up on different offers to try and pinpoint if indeed it was just the simplification of the form that made an impact on conversion rates, or if it was tied to the offer itself.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a main donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: A monthly first ask on the main donation page will increase recurring giving with minimal impact on overall transactions, as it highlights the ease and effect of becoming a monthly supporter. Meanwhile, the preselected option subtly reinforces that most donors choose to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 10/07/2025
For the American Cornerstone Institute, our primary focus has been on growing their recurring donor base. To build on that momentum, we tested adding a recurring gift ask directly on the main donation page, inspired by strong results from earlier acquisition offer tests using a similar approach.
This A/B test ran for just over a month, with equal traffic distributed between the control and treatment versions.
