The control utilized the existing donation page without any modifications. The treatment mirrored the control’s copy but added details about the benefits of becoming a monthly partner and preselected the monthly giving option on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding a monthly first ask on the main donation page led to a significant increase in recurring donations, with a 223.0% increase in recurring donations for All Traffic. This indicates that highlighting the benefits of becoming a monthly supporter and preselecting the monthly giving option can effectively encourage more donors to choose recurring donations.
2. The treatment also resulted in a substantial increase in overall Transactions, with a 543% increase. This shows that while the focus was on increasing recurring donations, the strategy also had a positive impact on overall donation transactions.
3. The statistical confidence level of 93.6% for the increase in recurring donations and 95% for the rise in transactions suggests that the results are approaching validity, but further testing would be needed for confirmation.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to explore the impact of emphasizing recurring donations on the main donation page. Further testing can help us validate the results and assess the long-term impact on donor retention and revenue.
2. Consider refining the messaging and design of the monthly giving ask to optimize its effectiveness. This could involve A/B testing different languages, visuals, or incentives to increase recurring donations further.
3. Monitor the overall impact on donation transactions to ensure that the focus on recurring donations does not negatively affect one-time contributions. Finding the right balance between the two types of giving is essential for maximizing fundraising outcomes.
Overall, these findings provide valuable insights into the potential of strategic messaging and design changes to increase recurring donations. By analyzing the results and refining our approach, we can continue to enhance our fundraising strategies and foster sustainable support for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (ICE Survey)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested whether clarifying the gift type on the ICE Survey donation form’s submit button would increase completed transactions. The submit button previously did not specify whether the donor was making a one-time or recurring gift, which could introduce uncertainty at the point of decision. To address this, we ran an A/B test for just under 30 days, introducing a variation that clearly stated the gift type on the button (for example, “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”) to reduce friction and give donors more confidence in their commitment.
The goal was to see if clearer messaging on the submit button would lead to more completed transactions. This aligns with behavioral research showing that reducing uncertainty at critical decision points can increase conversion rates. The test measured the impact on completed transactions as the primary success metric, with button click-throughs as a secondary metric to understand engagement before completion.
If the treatment variation outperformed the control, it would demonstrate the value of transparent communication at the point of conversion. Insights from this test could inform updates to other donation forms and campaigns, ultimately helping drive overall donation growth. By clarifying donor expectations, we aim to create a smoother giving experience and encourage more supporters to complete their gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variation that added clarity about the gift type to the submit button resulted in a -63.7% relative difference in transactions compared to the control, indicating a substantial decrease in performance. The experiment reached a 93.1% confidence level, providing strong statistical evidence that the treatment negatively impacted conversions.

This suggests that adding gift type language to the submit button created unintended friction or confusion for donors, reducing their likelihood of completing the transaction. 

Future tests should focus on maintaining simplicity and momentum at the conversion point, while addressing commitment clarity at earlier, lower-friction stages of the donor journey.

experiments.title: How preselecting the gift frequency and gift amount on a giving form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting and expanding the gift array for Today in the Word acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help reduce decision friction for donors by clearly presenting the preferred giving frequency and gift size within the acquisition funnel .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/24/2025
Moody Bible’s giving forms have historically been designed without pre-selected gift frequencies or amounts. While this has always been their standard setup, it differs from many other donation platforms where defaults are commonly used.
To test whether defaults could reduce decision friction and encourage giving, we introduced two pre-selections:
Gift frequency: defaulted to one-time
Gift amount: defaulted to the lowest option in the gift array ($25)
Our goal was to streamline the donor experience—minimizing effort and uncertainty—while also testing whether this change could increase overall conversion and potentially lift the average gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that pre-selecting gift frequency and amount—combined with expanding the gift array options—produced a significant lift in donor conversion metrics.
The treatment group outperformed the control with a 454.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic (94.4% LOC) and a 1,015% increase in Revenue (100% LOC).
 	Notably, while the sample size was small, the treatment showed no negative impact on transactions or revenue, indicating a clear positive response from Today in the Word’s audience.
 	The average gift amount also increased by 116.7%, confirming our hypothesis that the treatment would encourage larger donations.

experiments.title: Do Visual Elements on a Donation Page Help or Hurt Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified donation page design for users visiting a fundraising campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because removing competing visual elements will reduce mental friction for the donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2025 - 07/28/2025
Bible League Canada typically uses a highly visual donation page style as the default format for all of their fundraising campaigns. These pages often include banners, images, and video elements, along with a FundraiseUp donation form embedded at the bottom. While these pages are designed to create an engaging donor experience, we didn’t know whether these visual elements were helping donor conversion, or adding unnecessary distraction and mental friction.
To explore this question, Bible League Canada tested whether removing visual components from the donation page would reduce friction and lead to more completed gifts.
The Control was their standard visual campaign page, and the Treatment was a simplified FundraiseUp campaign page containing only one image and the donation form. The test was structured as a 50/50 split URL experiment during an active fundraising campaign to determine whether visual richness improves performance or adds unnecessary friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we hypothesized that a simplified donation page would reduce friction and increase conversions, the original designed page outperformed the simplified version. The Treatment page saw a 24.4% decrease in donor conversion compared to the Control, with an 89.1% level of confidence—just shy of full statistical validation but directionally meaningful.
In addition to stronger conversion, the Control also generated a higher revenue-per-visitor. However, this may have been influenced by one or more larger gifts, which could skew the average and may not reflect a consistent trend.
This result suggests that in fundraising campaigns, donors may benefit from richer context and emotional reinforcement provided by visual elements like images, banners, and video. Rather than creating distraction, these elements may help reinforce the campaign’s importance and build trust. It’s possible that removing them made the experience feel less compelling or authoritative.
Future testing could explore which specific elements (e.g., video, banners, testimonial quotes) contribute most to conversion. Alternatively, testing a hybrid layout—retaining key persuasive visuals while simplifying navigation and layout—might reveal a better-performing balance.

experiments.title: How removing the footer on a donation page affects the conversation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Removing the footer on a donation page will achieve an increase in the conversion rate because people won’t get distracted by the many page options to visit and allow them to focus on the purpose of the page–to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2025 - 03/18/2025
Alliance Defending Freedom is one of the leading Christian law firms committed to protecting religious freedom, free speech, marriage and family, parental rights, and the sanctity of life.
We ran this test on the donation page for one of their grant programs. Traffic came to this page through house file email.
We want to see if removing the footer would stop people from bouncing off the donation page and increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the footer on the donation page led to a significant increase in transactions for all traffic. The 70.9% increase in conversions shows that the hypothesis that removing the footer would increase the conversion rate was supported by the data.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered fully valid. Further testing would be necessary to confirm that removing the footer consistently leads to higher conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of removing the footer on different pages or across different campaigns to see if this trend holds true. Additionally, it would be important to consider other elements on the page that may impact conversion rates and test those as well to optimize donation pages further.

experiments.title: How donor-focused language on the Tribute Donation Page impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-focused language for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in Tribute giving because the shift in language prioritizes the donor and their gift as the primary actors, rather than CaringBridge as the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2025 - 07/11/2025
CaringBridge’s Tribute donation page is a primary driver of the organization’s revenue, acting as the destination when users click on one of the many Tribute widgets displayed throughout the site. While the language on the Tribute widgets is very focused on the donor and their impact, the subsequent donation page is primarily focused on the organization: “CaringBridges helps keep [Name] connected…” “You make CaringBridge…” “When you give to CaringBridge…”
We hypothesized that by updating the headline to be more donor-focused, eliminating the many uses of the org’s name, and offering more educational copy around their tribute gift and its impact, donor conversion rate would be improved.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved donor-focused language on the Tribute donation page, did not perform as well as the control, with a 7.8% decrease in Tribute Donations.

These results indicate that the donor-focused language may not have resonated as effectively with users as hoped. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further explore the impact of different messaging strategies on donor behavior. There are two points for further consideration as we intepret these initial results and consider future testing opportunities:
While the goal of the experiment was to be donor-focused, there is the potential that the headline's specificty of "Your Tribute Gift" ended up feeling more impersonal and distant. Their gift is the primary subject, rather than they themselves.
 	Additionally, users often have confusion around Tribute giving because it is a donation to CaringBridge, which then by extension ensures the site can remain active and keep users connected. Potentially, this treatment language removed the "How" to the donor's giving when it removed the use of CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How a tabbed form on the main donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tabbed form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the tabbed form provides more clarity on what type of gift the user is giving, therefore reducing potential friction while giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/13/2025
For Time of Grace’s main donation page, we wanted to add a tabbed gift array. We previously tested this, but we made some changes to this version. The first version said “Single Gift” for one-time gifts, and also had a dark green color for the selected tabs which was a bit confusing. So for this version, we changed the copy to say “One-Time Gift” and changed the selected tab color to be the gradient green they have on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The addition of a tabbed gift array on the main donation page resulted in a 46.3% increase in transactions for all traffic, showing potential clarity on the type of gift reducing potential friction in the donation process.
 	The treatment group also saw a significant 659% increase in One-Time Donation transactions, indicating that the tabbed form may have been particularly effective for this type of donation.
 	Despite the positive results, the statistical confidence level of 92.3% for All Traffic is slightly below the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the tabbed gift array.
 	Future experimentation should focus on refining the tabbed gift array design based on these results, such as adjusting colors, copy, or layout to further optimize the donation experience. Additionally, conducting additional tests with a larger sample size could help validate the initial findings and provide more conclusive results.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
The addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, there was an 8.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in terms of transactions.
 	Despite the decrease in transactions, there was a significant increase in revenue by 489% in the treatment group with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that although fewer people may have donated in the treatment group, those who did contribute gave more on average.
 	The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in transactions is 82.7%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable or used for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How updating the messaging on a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by updating the donation copy to focus on the value of research and impact of the donor’s support for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in donations because it better aligns with the motivations of an intellectually curious, policy-oriented audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 04/07/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of donation page copy for one of their content offers, “Trickle Down” Theory and “Tax Cuts for the Rich” eBook. We wanted to see if we present a donation appeal that uses a rational, research-focused tone (Treatment) rather than an emotionally driven, values-focused tone (Control), then we will see an increase in donation conversion rate because it better aligns with the motivations of an intellectually curious, policy-oriented audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Takeaways:
Control outperformed Treatment in both conversion rate and average gift size.The emotional, values-based message appears to resonate more with this audience than the rational, research-focused appeal.While the decrease in conversion rate (-19.5%) is directionally meaningful, the confidence level (83.6%) does not meet the 95% threshold required for statistical significance.The average gift size also declined significantly in the treatment, suggesting not only fewer donors, but potentially less engaged or less motivated ones.
Future Testing Ideas:
Combine the data-driven credibility of the Treatment with the emotional resonance of the Control.Test a hybrid version that emphasizes the research but still uses inspiring language and appeals to personal legacy, patriotism, or civic duty.Break down the test further:Test just the headline change.Test impact of bullet points vs. paragraph style.Try swapping just the CTA phrasing (e.g. “Support research” vs. “Leave your mark”).

experiments.title: Does changing the headline language on a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headings to a question for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/15/2024
This test explored the psychological impact of changing the headings for the steps on the donation form. The goal was to increase conversion and average gift by creating a sense of empowerment for the visitor through phrasing the headings as questions.
Our hypothesis was that changing the headings on the donation form will increase donor conversion. This is because when users are presented with a softer, more personalized heading, they are more likely to engage, and ultimately convert. Changing the headings to a question provides the user with more sense of control during their interaction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headings on the donation form to questions did not result in an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the data and consider the statistical significance of the results before making any conclusions or changes based on an experiment. It may also be beneficial to test different variations of the headings or other elements of the donation form to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, considering factors such as the wording, tone, and placement of elements on the form could also impact donor conversion rates.

Overall, it is essential to approach experimentation with a mindset of continuous learning and improvement, using data-driven insights to inform decision-making and optimize the donation process for the best results.

experiments.title: How removing the message field from the tribute widget for single page posts affects Tribute donations

rows.name: Remove message field from tribute widget

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the message field from Tribute widget for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because removing the field would reduce confusion and friction and improve clarity of the widget.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2025 - 03/25/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to remove the message field from the tribute widget to see if it would lead to an increase in tribute donations. The message field allowed a user to send a tribute message along with their donation. We wanted to remove the field to see if that would reduce some confusion among users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing the message field from the tribute widget led to a decrease in donations. Despite the decrease, the results do not have a high level of statistical confidence, which indicates that further testing would be needed to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of removing certain features from a widget. While the hypothesis suggested that removing the message field would reduce confusion and friction, it appears that this feature may have actually encouraged donations from users.

When designing future experiments, it is important to thoroughly analyze the potential impact of changes and to ensure that the results are statistically significant before drawing conclusions. Additionally, considering the preferences and behaviors of users is crucial in determining the effectiveness of different features in donation widgets.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonal to a donation page help conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial to the donation pages for the spring fundraiser campaign will achieve more conversions on the page because the added donor testimonial will include social proof on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
Right now, KCBI is in the middle of its annual Spring Fundraiser. This campaign drives traffic to the campaign donation pages more than the donation pages would receive outside of an on-air campaign.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase conversions on the donation page by adding a testimonial to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the donation pages for the spring fundraiser campaign resulted in a 35.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. Although the results were not statistically significant at a 95% confidence level, the 94.5% confidence level suggests that the trend towards decreased conversions is strong.

In future experimentation, it is important to further test the impact of adding donor testimonials to donation pages before fully implementing this change. It might be beneficial to explore different types of testimonials, placement on the page, or the content of the testimonial itself to see if there is a way to present social proof more effectively to increase conversions. Additionally, considering other factors that could have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign or the overall messaging, could provide more insights into how to optimize donation pages for better results.

experiments.title: Will adding a button on mobile donation pages impact gifts?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing the donation page for mobile users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the button will allow users to jump straight to the donation form instead of having to scroll.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2025
Moody is running their Spring Share campaign which generates a ton of traffic to their donation pages from mobile.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could make the donation page be mobile first by adding a ‘start my gift button’ at the top of the page. When clicked the button will anchor a user down to the form.
Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that optimizing the donation page for mobile users with a 'start my gift button' did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donor conversions. In fact, the treatment resulted in a decrease of 18.1% in donations for all traffic compared to the control.

Based on these results, it is clear that the 'start my gift button' did not have the intended effect on donor behavior. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies to optimize the donation page for mobile users. This could include exploring other design changes, such as simplifying the donation process, improving mobile responsiveness, or testing different call-to-action buttons. Additionally, conducting more rigorous testing with a larger sample size and longer testing period can help ensure more accurate and reliable results.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout on the general donation page impacted transactions and recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an Avila Army callout for Avila’s audience on the general donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts and increase transactions because highlighting the program’s mission and impact will inspire donors to see themselves as part of a dedicated community making a tangible difference.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2025 - 03/04/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether adding a banner about their recurring program, the Avila Army, impacted recurring donors and transactions. The Avila Foundation encourages people to join their recurring program to be apart of the battalion of friends and followers actively deployed in the war for souls through the monthly support of the mission of the Avila Foundation.
We wanted to see if putting information and a testimonial about this program would encourage others to sign up as well as donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Takeaways:
Recurring Callout Reduced One-Time Gifts – The Avila Army banner led to a 58.1% decrease in one-time donations, likely by distracting or overwhelming donors.
 	Minimal Impact on Recurring Giving – Only one new recurring donor was added, with low statistical confidence (68.4%), indicating the callout wasn’t compelling enough.
 	User Experience Issues – The callout might have disrupted the natural donation flow, making giving less intuitive.
Next Steps & Ideas for Promoting Recurring Gifts:
Balance One-Time & Recurring Giving – Test a softer recurring ask (e.g., an optional checkbox) rather than making it the focal point.
 	Refine Messaging – Emphasize impact, ease, and community benefits rather than duty-driven language.
 	Segment Audiences – Show recurring gift appeals only to engaged donors or past givers, rather than all visitors.
 	Test Avila Army's multiple giving levels – Offer tiered giving levels with exclusive perks (e.g., special updates, donor recognition, or impact reports) for recurring donors.
 	Use Social Proof – Feature multiple testimonials and highlight how recurring donors create lasting change.
 	Retarget One-Time Donors – Send personalized follow-ups thanking them and inviting them to upgrade to recurring.

experiments.title: How a change in layout impacts engagement on a donation landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic will achieve a higher engagement with the site because the items are easier to view with less scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go when they click the main donation button the PTV website.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic resulted in a 19.7% decrease in donations for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence was at 92.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the grid layout on engagement with the site.

In addition, we saw:
11% decrease in time spent on the site for treatment
 	an increase from 1 to 6 Pathway Partner (recurring gifts) between control and treatment

experiments.title: Will adding a heart to the monthly tab increase monthly transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form will achieve a higher number of recurring transactions because the heart will the users eyes to the monthly tab and lead them to giving a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/18/2025
In the past we have tested adding different hearts to elements to catch the users eye and get them to take the action that we are wanting.
In this case, we wanted to see if we could add a heart on the monthly tab on the donation form would increase the number of monthly transactions.
We ran this test on the main sustainer donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 69.4% decrease in recurring transactions with a 92.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

While the results are not statistically significant at the usual 95% confidence level, they are approaching validity, suggesting that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the heart icon on user behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of visual elements like hearts on donation forms, as they may not always lead to the desired outcome. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with different visual cues or design elements to see which ones are most effective in increasing recurring transactions. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand the reasons behind the results and make informed decisions for future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience on the donation page increase donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy to have a guided reading experience for the main episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the copy will be laid out in an easy to understand manner that puts the donor as the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
In the past, we have tested adding a guided reading experience to donation pages during a campaign.. We have found that it can be helpful to help guide readers down the page in an easy-to-understand way.
We wondered if we could find this same result during a non campaign season. For this test we adjusted the donation page copy to lay out the problem, solution, and how the individual donor can make a difference. We ran this test on the main episodic donation page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the guided reading experience on the main episodic donation page did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease in transactions by 14.0% compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 81.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making. In the future, it may be beneficial to continue testing different approaches to optimize the donation page, keeping in mind the importance of clear and easy-to-understand messaging for donors. It is also advisable to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How updating the value prop on a donation page with urgency and relevancy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and relevancy to the value proposition for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in the instant donation coversion because there will be more urgency and relevancy making the donor feel more connected to Hoover’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society.
Our hypothesis was that if we update the instant donation form copy to be more related to an immediate need, then the urgency will cause an increase in the instant donation conversion. If we update the value proposition with urgency and relevancy to the times it will encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the instant donation form copy to include urgency and relevancy to a current need resulted in a significant increase in instant donation conversions for the Hoover Institution. The treatment group experienced a 42.9% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 87.7%.

Why the Test May Have Won:
Stronger Immediate Relevance & Urgency: 
 	The test opens with, "Before you go, we’d like to ask you one more thing," which feels more immediate and action-oriented than the control’s "While you wait for it to arrive."
 	It emphasizes the present need for scholars like Thomas Sowell to continue their work, connecting the ask to an ongoing, timely cause.
 	Clearer Value Proposition:
 	The test provides concrete examples of what donations will support: "Examine the causes of current crises," "Enable our team of expert scholars," and "Advance a strategic communications program."
 	The control remains more abstract, focusing broadly on "data-driven research" and "an educated citizenry."
 	More Compelling Call to Action:
 	The test includes a clear call to action: "Will you consider making a tax-deductible gift to the Hoover Institution today?" followed by bullet points that break down the impact of a gift.
 	The control’s call to action feels less direct and more passive with phrases like, "Would you partner with us?"
 	Focus on Impact Over Philosophy:
 	The test copy shifts the focus toward what Hoover does and the tangible outcomes of its work.
 	The control talks more about philosophical beliefs, like "the importance of an educated citizenry" and "the American way of life."

experiments.title: Does adding a donor testimonial work on an evergreen donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the goaa/soaa donation page during CYE will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”. because  having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/05/2025
For CYE this year, we wanted to see if adding a right rail donor testimonial on the donation pages would make an impact on transactions. We wanted to test this on all three major donation pages for Focus, the main campaign episodic giving page, the main campaign recurring giving page, and the GOAA/SOAA broadcast pages that generate a ton of traffic each day.
For this test, we wanted to see how adding the testimonal on the the GOAA/SOAA donation pages would impact conversion. We thought this would be neat to see more of an ‘evergreen’ type of donation page even during a high urgency campaign
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the GOAA/SOAA donation page during CYE did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 15.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.8%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different types of donor testimonials or placement of the testimonials on the page to see if there are any variations that could lead to increased conversions. It is also important to take into consideration the timing of the experiment and the specific campaign or context in which it is being conducted to ensure that the results are relevant and applicable.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal sticky bar to the donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar to the sohl donation page for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar that contains a donor goal would increase the appeal to give as well as convey the message that we’re wanting to send to the users. By adding a sticky bar on the family donation page we found that it will increase conversions. Below is what we found for the SOHL audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar containing a donor goal to the sohl donation page for the CYE campaign resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests or experiments to confirm the effectiveness of adding a sticky bar to the donation page. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other strategies or elements that could potentially increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday” donation page impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday appeal” donation page will increase donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce the 2-day match with additional value prop, while also tying the email into the larger December year-end goals.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, traffic from the email appeal is typically sent to a simplified form that is similar to the length of a general donation page – with match language added. We hypothesized that a longer, “holiday appeal” donation page that aligns with the overall campaign goals communicated in email and reinforces the 2-day match alongside the larger year-end goals will increase conversion rate
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned "holiday appeal" donation page can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 42.5% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the additional value proposition and alignment with the 2-day match and larger year-end goals had a positive impact on donor behavior.

However, the statistical level of confidence was 91.6%, which is just below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed for validity.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of leading email traffic to specialized donation pages tailored to specific campaigns or appeals. This experiment suggests that aligning the donation page with the messaging and goals of the email appeal can lead to increased donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How $100 Prefill vs a $100 Pre-Select in the Array Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $100 prefill for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because whereas a 3-option array seemed to introduce friction, an open $100 prefill accomplished the desired impact on average gift without introducing more options for the user to choose between..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2024 - 01/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page, $100 is the default pre-selected amount. However, how that number has been presented to the user has varied. The standard practice has been to have no gift array, but simply an open field with $100 prefilled. Alternatively, some pages utilized an array of three options with $100 as the preselected amount. It is worth noting that currently, neither of these options were present on the CaringBridge main donation page due to a migration glitch. We hypothesized that when testing between the two options, an open field with $100 prefilled (the way it was originally intended) would improve donor conversion over a 3-option array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment of using an open field with a $100 prefill on the main donation page resulted in a 35.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 92.0%, indicating a high likelihood that the increase in revenue was due to the treatment and not random chance. Furthermore, the hypothesis that a $100 prefill would improve donor conversion rates compared to a 3-option array was supported by the results of the experiment.

Ultimately, these results help enforce two next steps for action items. 1. That CaringBridge should consider testing their other donation pages which have a gift array by default, to see whether an open field pre-fill can also improve conversion rate. 2. CaringBridge should prioritize correcting the current main donation page so that the $100 prefill is represented, rather than the current blank open field (0 is the prefill)

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to the top of a donation page reinforce the need for users to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a sticky bar that has specific donor goal language for the monthly family campaign giving page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar to the campaign donation page that contains donor goal language would increase the donor conversion rate on the page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a sticky bar with specific donor goal language to the monthly family campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic by 397.1% with a 91.0% level of confidence.

2. There was also a 48% increase in Transactions - One-Time with a 96% level of confidence.

3. While the results were positive, the statistical confidence level of 91.0% for Revenue for All Traffic falls slightly below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

4. Further testing is recommended to confirm the effectiveness of the sticky bar in increasing donor conversion rates.

5. Future experimentation should focus on refining the donor goal language in the sticky bar, testing different designs or placements, and running the experiment for a longer duration to ensure reliable results.

6. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies to optimize donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts for the nonprofit "Focus on the Family".

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience improve donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by arranging our giving page copy in a problem/solution format for the calendar year end campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donation page will be easier to read and understand the impact that the donor makes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2024 - 12/27/2024
For our year-end campaign, we wanted to explore whether presenting the donation message in a problem/solution format would help donors feel a stronger connection to the impact of their calendar year-end gift. Specifically, we aimed to see if framing the need for support in terms of how donations directly provide a solution to more readers with the valuable materials and guidance offered by Today in the Word would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a guided reading experience to the donation page resulted in a 28.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was at 83.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should be tested further during another campaign or during a season without a campaign.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. It might be worth revisiting the guided reading experience concept in future tests, possibly with adjustments or improvements, to see if it consistently leads to increased donor conversions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to continue experimenting with different elements on the donation page to optimize for the highest conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the revenue goal thermometer  for The Navigators CYE Donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page. because  it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To improve donor conversion rates during The Navigators’ year-end campaign, we conducted a test by adding a goal thermometer to the donation page.
The experiment ran from December 27 to December 31, 2025, with 50% of site traffic exposed to the treatment.
Our hypothesis was that the thermometer would positively influence donor conversion by serving as social proof, signaling that others are contributing and encouraging more people to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a goal thermometer to the donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates for The Navigators' year-end campaign. In fact, there was a 5.1% decrease in donations for all traffic during the experiment period.
Despite these results, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for making valid conclusions. As a result, these results should not be considered conclusive or used for future decision making but they do suggest that The Navigator's audience had a neutral reaction to the goal thermometer.
Moving forward, it is important to understand that not all experiments will yield the expected results, and it is essential to carefully evaluate the data and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations or factors to determine what truly influences donor conversion rates for The Navigators. Additionally, considering factors such as timing, audience segments, and messaging may also provide valuable insights for optimizing donation campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase donor conversion rate by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2024
IMB is running a direct ask offer where people can give to fund Bibles, but we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. We hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamically changing slider for donors to select the level of their impact did not increase donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 35.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

While the results are approaching validity, with a statistical level of confidence of 93.8%, further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the donation page. It may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research or user testing before implementing a new feature to ensure it resonates with donors and effectively communicates the impact of their contribution. Additionally, testing smaller changes or variations of the dynamically changing slider could help pinpoint what specifically led to the decrease in donations in this experiment.

experiments.title: How making the problem relatable to the donor affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the problem more relatable to the donor for survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it creates an emotional connection, helping donors see how their contribution directly impacts real people and situations they care about, making them more likely to feel personally responsible and motivated to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2024 - 10/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey on voter fraud through paid media and email prospecting efforts. They wanted to try and increase in the instant conversion rate on the donation page. To do so, we decided to test making the problem of election fraud more relatable to the donor by adding a paragraph that says “imagine casting your vote only to find out the fraudulent activities affected the impact of your vote.”
This experiment ran for 1 month to 50% of the traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a paragraph to make the issue of election fraud more relatable to donors did not lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates most with donors and leads to a higher conversion rate. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or external events that could have impacted donor behavior.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results should not be used as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How guided reading subheaders impact donor conversion on e-book donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that guided reading subheadings for users on an instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because increases readability of the copy while also clearly imparting the problem, solution, and how the donor’s gift makes a difference. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2024 - 11/19/2024
We believe that guided reading subheadings (that outline a problem, solution, and what you can do) on instant donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although it would appear that guided reading subheadings on instant donation page can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The statistical level of confidence was only 80.%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. In addition, the sample size is very small with a total of 5 transactions in the test.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence, ideally above 85%. This will help to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.

experiments.title: Will aligning the value proposition from the donate button and the episodic giving page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the value propostion from the donate button to the doantion page for main episodic donation page will achieve a higher number of transactions because the congruency between the button and the donation page will decrease friction and reinforce motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 11/01/2024
In this series of testing we have found that using Biblical Resources on the main donate button increases the number of clicks to the donation page.
We saw that aligning the value proposition with the main donation button on the monthly page increased transactions by 154%.
We wondered if we could duplicate those results for the episodic donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the value proposition from the donate button to the donation page for the main episodic donation page led to a 13.7% increase in transactions for all traffic. This indicates that having congruency between the button and the donation page can potentially decrease friction and reinforce motivation for users to make donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of aligning value propositions on donation pages to see if this trend holds true across different scenarios. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied approaches to alignment could help validate these findings further and provide more conclusive evidence. It is important to note that while the results of this experiment are encouraging, they do not meet the standard threshold of 95% statistical confidence, so further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift with a partner’s benefits call-out on a donation page impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner will increase the number of recurring donations because it provides added value to donors, strengthening their connection to the organization and making the recurring gift feel more rewarding and meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization dedicated to strengthening marriages and families through biblically-based resources, events, and guidance, supporting individuals as they build enduring, healthy relationships. Previously, we tested a recurring gift default on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages, which led to a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 99.9% level of confidence. Based on this success, the recurring default has been implemented as a permanent feature across the donation microsite. In this experiment, we aim to test a new approach to the recurring gift call-out, focusing on language that highlights the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner, to assess if this approach will further encourage monthly giving.
If we emphasize the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner (e.g., access to insider ministry updates, private events, and a curated library), then users will be more likely to commit to a recurring gift, as the added value and connection to the FamilyLife community will increase their perceived return on investment and loyalty.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version used a recurring gift call-out that stated, “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” The Treatment version introduced a new call-out focused on membership benefits: “Receive exclusive benefits: insider ministry updates, invitation-only events, access to our curated library, and membership to our private Partners-only network.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and conducted on their best performing premium donation pages: Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and the Ferret Flush Couples Pack.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if emphasizing exclusive benefits for recurring donors would increase the number of monthly donations on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages. However, partway through the test, two of the three offers (Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and Ferret Flush Couples Pack) were turned off due to budget limitations. As a result, traffic to the test was reduced, which impacted our ability to achieve a conclusive result.
Recurring Donations Results: The Control outperformed the Treatment in terms of recurring donations. The Control had a 0.59% conversion rate (13 recurring gifts from 2,210 samples), while the Treatment had a 0.30% conversion rate (7 recurring gifts from 2,366 samples), representing a relative decrease of 49.7% at an 86.1% level of confidence. This difference did not reach statistical significance, so the results are inconclusive.
Overall Transactions Results: When examining all transactions, the Control had a 4.8% conversion rate (107 total gifts from 2,210 samples) compared to a 4.9% conversion rate in the Treatment (115 total gifts from 2,366 samples). This represents a slight relative increase of 0.4% in the Treatment, with a very low level of confidence (2.4%). This result also did not validate, indicating that the new messaging had no meaningful effect on overall transactions.
Theories on Donor Response:
Mismatch with Motivation: FamilyLife donors may be motivated more by the immediate impact of their giving rather than exclusive, long-term benefits. The Control message, which directly highlighted the impact of equipping families, may have resonated more with donors’ desire to see their contribution create tangible change. The Treatment, which shifted focus to membership perks, may have unintentionally distracted from this primary motivation.Perceived Complexity: The mention of exclusive benefits and a private network may have introduced an element of complexity or commitment that was unappealing. Donors may respond best to messages that feel straightforward and immediately impactful. The “Partners-only network” or “curated library” might not have been clearly valuable in this context and could have made the ask feel less accessible.Different Expectations for Monthly Giving: Some donors may view recurring giving as a simple way to support the organization consistently, without needing additional incentives. This audience might value the ease and simplicity of monthly giving over exclusive access, making the Partner benefits message less motivating.

experiments.title: Does a missional or initiative ask convert donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a inititive ask for a high urgency campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a tangible ask for a short period of time can be easier for a potential donor to convert on.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2024
KCBI runs a Fall Fundraiser each year that brings in traffic to their website.
For this campaign, we wanted to see what type of messessaging converts donors during a high urgency campaign better.. Missional or Initiative.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that having an initiative ask for a high urgency campaign may potentially lead to a higher donor conversion rate compared to a missional ask. While the treatment did show a 52.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

It's also important to note that donors gave more generously when they saw the treatment of "share 25,000 Bibles". The average gift for the treatment was $205 and the average gift for the control was $123.

experiments.title: Does aligning the copy on the donation page to the mission of the organization affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that editing the copy on the donation page to be in alignment with the mission of the organization for the recurring donor drive campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the ask to be a sustainer would match exactly what the mission of the organization is.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
TITW ran a recurring donor drive this past summer. Since this is a very specific ask, to become a monthly donor, we wondered if aligning the copy on the donation page to the mission of TITW would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that editing the copy on the donation page to be in alignment with the mission of the organization for the recurring donor drive campaign did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 24.7% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

This suggests that the initial hypothesis was not supported by the data and that the messaging alignment with the organization's mission may not have resonated with donors as expected. It is important to note that the results are not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level but are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the findings.

experiments.title: How changing copy on the donation page for prospecting audience impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy on the donation page for Hoover’s prospecting email audience will achieve an increase donations because the prospecting audience who is unfamiliar with the Hoover Institution will feel more connected with their mission since it’s more clearly stated.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 09/03/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover sends prospecting emails to any potential audiences or leads that have not yet engaged with the organization. The goal of a prospecting email is to introduce the organization (usually through a content offer), spark interest, and encourage the recipient to take the next step, such as signing up for a content offer.
For this test, we tested consolidating the copy and driving the value prop of The Hoover Institution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the copy on the donation page for Hoover's prospecting email audience did not lead to an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions, indicating that the longer copy in the control group may have been more effective in this case.

Since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid. Therefore, it is important not to make any decisions based on these results alone.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of copy length and content to see what resonates best with the prospecting audience. It is also crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a long enough duration and with a large enough sample size to obtain statistically significant results. By continuously testing and learning from different variations, Hoover Institution can better understand what drives donations from their prospecting audience and optimize their email campaigns accordingly.

experiments.title: How the addition of impact bullets on a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact bullets to the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the impact bullets give a more tangible connection for the donor’s impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/10/2024
We are currently running a direct ask offer for IMB called God’s Word for the Nations which is a Bible Direct Ask. This offer asks individuals to donate so that IMB can fund the translation and distribution of Bibles in native languages across the world.
While this funnel was performing relatively well, we wanted to further optimize the process and lower our gross cost per donor.
Therefore, we tested the addition of impact bullets. These bullets notated how much impact a $5, $25, $50 gift would have in terms of number of Bibles.
$5 will provide 1 Bible
$25 will provide 5 Bibles
$50 will provide 10 Bibles
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding impact bullets to the donation page for the Bible Direct Ask offer resulted in a 15.1% increase in donations for all traffic. While this increase is substantial, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Furthermore, we saw a 1.3% lift in revenue at a 52.2% LOC for the treatment. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that IMB's audience resonated more with the impact bullets.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the impact bullets or similar strategies in future experiments to determine if they consistently lead to increased donor conversions. Additionally, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically reliable results before making any conclusions. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size, we can validate the effectiveness of adding impact bullets and make more informed decisions about optimizing the donation process for the God's Word for the Nations offer.

experiments.title: How a skip the donate page button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “skip to my course” option for instant donation page viewers will achieve the same amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS offers free online courses as content offers. When somebody signs up for a free course, they then go to a instant donation page. This has causes some course enrollees to get confused and email DTS asking why they need to pay for a free course. We decided to do an A/B/C test and test putting “You can access your course immediately by clicking here.” in the copy and adding a button to the bottom of the page that says “No thanks, take me to my course now.” We wanted to see if adding clarity would impact donations and impact the amount of people emailing DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "skip to my course" option on the instant donation page did not lead to the same amount of donations as the control group. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. While the results are not statistically significant at the 95% level, they are approaching validity at 86.9%. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact on donations.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and design of the instant donation page. While clarity is crucial in guiding users to their desired action, such as accessing their course immediately, it is also important to balance this with the donation call-to-action. It may be beneficial to test different variations of the messaging and design to find the optimal combination that drives both course access and donations.
Overall, the results from this experiment provide valuable insights for future testing and optimization of the instant donation page to improve user experience and donation conversion rates.

experiments.title: How localizing the language on a donation page impacts donor conversion rates – part 2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that localizing the impact of a donation for potential donors will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to test new copy on their primary donation page. Our goal was to assess whether incorporating language that emphasizes North Carolina and focuses on the donor’s personal connection would impact donation levels.
This experiment is the 2nd iteration of an original experiment we ran here, https://app.winstonknows.com/e/jm3z. In this version, we increased the level of localization by adding additional value proposition statements and impact on North Carolina.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which emphasized the local impact of donations in North Carolina and focused on the donor's personal connection, resulted in a 31.6% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control.

2. While the results show a positive impact of the localized copy on donation conversions, the lack of a higher confidence level means that these results should not be used for future decision-making.

3 . In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing variations of copy that emphasize the local impact and personal connection to donors, while also ensuring that the sample size is large enough to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. This will help in determining the true effectiveness of such messaging strategies in increasing donation conversions for WUNC.

experiments.title: How offering a premium version of the content offer impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/13/2024
Hoover Institution has a quarterly research digest, featuring 30 of their scholar’s work, which is promoted as a free PDF download. We have run experiments in the past where adding in a premium offer–in this case asking for $125 to get all 4 quarterly editions mailed to you–has an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
In this experiment, due to increased mailing and production costs, we want to see whether increasing that ask to $150 would make an impact on response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which asked for a higher donation amount of $150, resulted in a 23.7% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level of 81.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. In this specific case, the higher donation ask did not lead to an increase in response rates and could have potentially deterred donors from completing transactions.
4. It is important to carefully analyze the impact of donation amounts on response rates and not assume that a higher request will always result in higher revenues.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Conduct further experiments with different donation amounts to see the impact on response rates and revenue.
2. Ensure that experiments have a statistically significant confidence level of at least 85% to ensure the validity of the results.
3. Consider other factors such as messaging, timing, and audience segmentation in future experiments to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior.
4. Continuously analyze and iterate on experiments to optimize donation strategies and maximize revenue for Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: Does Adding Leading Copy on the Donation Page Affect Generosity?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the body copy for the acquistion donation page will achieve a higher average gift because the copy tell people exactly what their money is going to and how they (the donor) is making a difference.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2024 - 08/12/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if changing the copy on the donation page would affect how generous donors give.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the body copy on the donation page led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 690.9% and a 362% increase in Email Acquisition. The treatment group that received the new copy that clearly explained how the donation would make a difference saw higher average gifts from donors.

These results suggest that providing specific and impactful information about where the donor's money is going and how it will make a difference can significantly influence their decision to give more generously. Utilizing this approach in future experimentation could result in higher donation amounts and increased revenue for KCBI.

It is important to note that while the results were promising, further testing is recommended to ensure the validity of the findings. By continuing to iterate and test different messaging strategies, KCBI can continue to optimize its donation pages and ultimately drive more impactful giving from donors.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it makes it more personalized to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/01/2023
This organization wanted to test how using donor-centric language on their donation page for Giving Tuesday affected conversion rates overall. Traffic came to this page through paid Facebook ads, housefile emails and website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page will likely lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the donor-centric language, saw a 23.3% increase in transactions compared to the control group with an 88% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version saw an 9% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing the use of donor-centric language and other personalization strategies to optimize the donation page. It would be beneficial to run longer experiments to gather more data and improve the statistical validity of the results. Additionally, testing different variations of donor-centric language and comparing them against each other can help identify the most effective messaging.

experiments.title: How using a credible figure to talk in first person on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a donation page like its a letter from an important figure for potential donors will achieve a increase conversion rate because it adds credibility and trust to the process. because  it adds credibility and trust to the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2023 - 12/14/2023
