As apart of ACI’s calendar year-end campaign, they sent a survey to their housefile asking for feedback on how the organization is doing. After you complete the survey, you go to an instant donation page that includes value proposition on making a donation before the end of the year. ACI’s founder, Dr. Ben Carson, is a huge voice in the organization and to its followers, so we decided to test writing the donation page as if it were a letter from him.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language that presents the donation page as a letter from an important figure, such as Dr. Ben Carson in this case, can cause an increase the conversion rate. The results of the experiment showed a 29.6% increase in transactions for the treatment version with a statistical level of confidence of 83.4%.
Because we saw a directional lift in conversion rate, we believe that Dr. Carson writing the donation page as a letter does increase credibility and trust with the organization and the user.

experiments.title: How positive value-focused headlines affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing positive value-focused headlines for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
The Navigators had been trying to increase email acquisition rate on a offer they had that was doing very well in acquisition. The headline on the page offered the value proposition “Get the help you need to memorize scripture”. They hypothesized that this might imply that the visitor needed help, which might be off-putting to them.
So they decided to try to new approaches. First, they wanted to ask a question that implied that the visitor wanted to gain more knowledge: Do you want to memorize more scripture?
Then, they decided to test a headline that teased the future “superpower” of the visitor after they had absorbed the content: “Be equipped with the right scripture at the right time.”
They tested all three with the torrent of Facebook traffic that the page was receiving.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither headline was able to validate, with more than 21,000 conversions. This was partially because the motivation from the Facebook ad was very high and the email conversion on the page was more than 35%. However, it also showed that the changes might not have been radical enough to warrant a large difference. In addition to testing upstream and downstream of this page, they wanted to next test a more radical approach.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form gift array button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s gift array and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
The Heritage team wondered whether this flag image would appeal to potential donors on another direct appeal donation page. They believed testing this image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, due to both the image’s appeal to the donors’ patriotism and the suggestion of $100 as the “preselected” array option.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon only in the $100 gift array button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's gift array button may not always lead to an increase in conversion rate as expected. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 42.6% with an 88.3% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.
This result may indicate that the flag image was a distraction from the task at hand for this direct appeal. The image on the gift array button specifically may have confused or dissuaded some potential donors who planned on giving an amount other than the preselected amount.
In future experimentation, it would be important to further test the impact of adding the flag image in different elements of the donation form, such as the monthly tab as well as the submit button, or even testing different patriotic symbols or messaging to see if that resonates better with potential donors.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for ebook downloaders will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/13/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Guide to the Constitution eBook offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that changing the tab titles on a donation form to add clarity and donor autonomy led to a 32.6% increase in overall donor conversion rate at a 91.2% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%, the results were approaching validity. The treatment version, which used the language "Make a One-Time Gift" and "Make a Monthly Gift", was rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations of tab titles and messaging on donation forms to further evaluate their impact on donor conversion rates. It would be beneficial to explore additional iterations of language that provide clear guidance and empower donors to make informed decisions. Additionally, considering other elements of the donation form, such as design, adding icons that resonate with the donor's motivation, and call-to-action buttons, in conjunction with the tab titles, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of how to optimize the donation process for increased conversions.

experiments.title: How adding a headline impacts conversions pt1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headling for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because there is additional clarity in the offer. because there is additional clarity in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month, PTV promotes a different book with a gift of a generous amount.
For April, we decided to test adding a headline to the donation page. We have seen with other organizations that a headline can drive clarity and, therefore, drive results as it presents clear, brief, easy-to-read information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment is that adding a headline to the donation page for Pathway to Victory did not lead to the expected increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations for all traffic. While the results did not reach a statistically valid level of confidence, they are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of adding a headline on web traffic.

As this saw a significant decrease, we did run the test for the entire month of April. We still believe our hypothesis is true but question the copy utilized. In the next iteration we will adjust the copy.

experiments.title: How changing the color of form donate buttons impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the color of the donate button for donation forms will achieve a decrease in donor conversion because the color is darker than the previous and is more difficult to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 02/21/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary is celebrating their centennial this year and for it, they had an update to their brand.
For this test, we wanted to see if the updated colors would impact overall donor conversion due to the new yellow/gold being darker than the previous.
We ran this test across all donation pages, including acquisition pages and the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The updated color of the donate button for donation forms resulted in a 9.9% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

2. Although the increase in One-Time Donations was statistically significant at 81.4% confidence level, it did not meet the standard threshold of 95% confidence for valid results.

3. While the darker color of the donate button may have initially been thought to be more difficult to read and potentially decrease donor conversion, the results showed a positive impact on conversions and revenue.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations in colors, designs, and elements on donation forms to understand their impact on donor conversion and revenue. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and reach a higher level of statistical confidence before making conclusive decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Does a book image or video convert more donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image on donation page to a video for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because the video provides additional details of the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 03/08/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s newest book “Are We Living on the End Times” we decided to test swapping the book image for a video promoting the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the image on the donation page to a video for web traffic led to a 40.9% increase in revenue for all traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 83.4%, below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

Therefore, we cannot consider these results as valid and should not make decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing and refining different elements to optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 55.5% lift in recurring donations at an 86.3% level of confidence. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test because we did have some one-time donations also coming through.
We did see a drop in revenue at 38.6% with a 62.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.
Although these results did not validate, the results, in terms of number of recurring donors, were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.
While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 02/28/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 35.6% lift in recurring donations at a 73.3% level of confidence, as well as a 29.3% lift at a 93.1% level of confidence for overall transactions. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test, hence one-time donations also coming through.

We did see a slight drop in revenue at a 10.8% drop at a 32.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.

Although these results did not validate, they were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.

While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for acquisition funnel donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/01/2024
As a follow up to previous experiments, we wanted to see if using a tabbed donation form for visitors to an acquisition donation page would improve donor conversion rate and recurring donor acquisition. In previous experiments, we found no difference in one-time giving, but had seen 99% lift in recurring giving. However this was tested on the main donation page where motivation to give is very different than giving through an acquisition funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a tabbed donation form, resulted in a 29.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

2. While the statistical level of confidence (90.3%) is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, the results are approaching validity. This means that there is a strong indication that the treatment is effective, but further testing is needed to confirm its impact.

3. The previous experiment on the main donation page showed a 99% lift in recurring giving with a tabbed donation form. However, the current experiment on the acquisition donation page did not see a significant difference in recurring giving. This suggests that the motivation to give may differ between the two situations.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate its effectiveness. Consider running additional experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to improve statistical confidence.

Additionally, it may be valuable to explore the impact of different language and messaging on the donation form. Testing variations in messaging can help determine the most effective language that resonates with potential donors and increases conversion rates.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment suggest that a tabbed donation form has the potential to improve donor conversion rates on acquisition donation pages.

The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a tabbed donation form, resulted in a 29.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

2. While the statistical level of confidence (90.3%) is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, the results are approaching validity. This means that there is a strong indication that the treatment is effective, but further testing is needed to confirm its impact.

3. The previous experiment on the main donation page showed a 99% lift in recurring giving with a tabbed donation form. However, the current experiment on the acquisition donation page did not see a significant difference in recurring giving. This suggests that the motivation to give may differ between the two situations.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate its effectiveness. Consider running additional experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to improve statistical confidence.

Additionally, it may be valuable to explore the impact of different language and messaging on the donation form. Testing variations in messaging can help determine the most effective language that resonates with potential donors and increases conversion rates.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment suggest that a tabbed donation form has the potential to improve donor conversion rates on acquisition donation pages.

experiments.title: How linking straight to a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by going directly to the donation page for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate in gifts because it is a single step process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV shared a book of the month. The process to receive the book is for the email subscriber to make a donation in order to receive the book.
We observed that a majority of email subscribers were making donations in the “generous gift” level therefore we decided to test by skipping the landing page where they choose the level to going straight to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that skipping the landing page and going directly to the donation page for email subscribers resulted in a higher conversion rate in donations. The treatment group saw a 23.2% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, it should be noted that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 81.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered definitive or used for future decision-making.
The average gift for the treatment did decrease by 25% due to the lack of large-level gifts, as the option is not presented on the donation page, only on the landing page.

experiments.title: How adding an anchor button on an experience page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an anchor button at the top of the page for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because it makes the offer more easily accessible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2023 - 11/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For November 2023, PTV released a brand-new children’s book by Julia Jeffress Sadler. To promote this page, PTV built an experience page with tons of graphics, testimonials from readers and images of the interior of the book. At the bottom of the page is where the web visitor can select the offer bundle they would like to receive with their generous donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was a decrease of 11.6% with a 92% level of confidence in transactions.
We believe that the version with the button actually caused web visitors to miss all value proposition for the offer and made it more transactional in nature.

experiments.title: How having short direct copy impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having shorter copy for all users will achieve increase donations because they could skip the copy more quickly. because they could skip the copy more quickly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2022 - 07/15/2022
Wrong images
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The hypothesis that shorter copy would lead to increased donations was proven wrong. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

2. The statistical level of confidence for the overall results is below the threshold of 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

3. However, it is worth noting that there was a significant decrease in donations from visitors aged 25-34, with a 99% level of confidence. This suggests that the shorter copy may have had a negative impact on this specific demographic.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the hypothesis before conducting the experiment. The result of this experiment indicates that shorter copy may not always lead to increased donations, so it would be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior.

Additionally, it may be worth conducting further research or experiments specifically targeting visitors aged 25-34 to better understand their behavior and preferences. This could help in creating more effective strategies and messaging for this particular demographic.

experiments.title: How adding a Security Callout to a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the security of an online gift with a callout line for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because eliminate a potential point of friction to giving — mainly the suspicion that sharing payment info or making an online gift isn’t secure.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 12/26/2023
CaringBridge’s Tribute Donation Page already has elements to reinforce security around the giving experience: a shaded box around the payment info and a lock icon. However, we hypothesized that an added line of copy in the payment section, “Your Information is Secure” would help tie together these elements, call out the security of an online gift, and increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a line of copy to reinforce the security of an online gift in the payment section of the Tribute Donation Page resulted in an 8.2% increase in donations. Although the results are not statistically valid, they are approaching validity with a 87.5% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a follow-up experiment with a larger sample size or longer duration to increase the level of confidence and validate the results.

Overall, the findings of this experiment suggest that calling out the security of the giving experience can help reduce any suspicions or concerns potential donors may have about sharing payment information. By addressing this potential point of friction, organizations like CaringBridge can potentially increase conversion rates and encourage more online donations.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page. because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How using priming language on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment page using “priming” will increase the email sign-up rate for the video series because it primes visitors to associate positive emotions and a sense of warmth with ‘The Inklings’ and the video series, making them more inclined to take action by signing up for access.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 01/26/2024
Hillsdale College is offering a free video series on “The Inklings” through paid Facebook advertising. They wanted to test how using the psychological concept “priming” would affect email conversion on the page to sign up to get the video series. On the treatment page, the priming language helps associate the video series with positive emotions and a sense of warmth like the Inklings experienced when they were discussing literature and theology.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment page using "priming" actually resulted in a decrease in email sign-up rate for the video series. This means that the hypothesis that priming visitors with positive emotions and a sense of warmth would increase their likelihood of signing up for access was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of priming on the desired outcome. While priming can be a powerful psychological tool, it is not always effective in achieving the desired results. This experiment suggests that in this particular case, priming may have had a negative effect on the decision to sign up for the video series.
Therefore, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies and messaging techniques that could potentially increase email acquisition. This could involve testing different types of language, imagery, or incentives to better appeal to the target audience. Additionally, conducting further research to gain a deeper understanding of the target audience's motivations and preferences could also inform future experimentation and optimize the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How an image of the premium impacted revenue for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for existing donors will achieve a higher revenue because their is a visual appeal of the new book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book in the promotion resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence is not quite at the desired 95%, the results are approaching validity, indicating that the image likely had a positive impact on donor response.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of images in promotions to further validate the results. This could involve testing different types of images, such as a picture of the author or a graphic related to the book's content, to see if they have a similar effect on donor response.

Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze other metrics, such as click-through rates and conversion rates, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of including images in promotions. This will further inform decision-making and provide a more accurate assessment of the effectiveness of different promotional strategies.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that including an image of the book in promotions can have a positive impact on donor response. By continuing to test and refine this strategy, the nonprofit can potentially increase revenue and attract more donors.

experiments.title: How Turning Campaign Graphics into a Plain-Text Design Affects Conversion on Donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain-text donation page rather than stylized campaign graphics for RTB’s FYE campaign  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it positions the copy/value as being from a human..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 07/03/2023
For Reason to Believe’s Fiscal Year End campaign, they had created an array of graphics illustrating their goals and the impact of their Scholar Community. We hypothesized that removing these graphics on the donation page in favor of a plain text design would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. Note that any copy in the images was translated into bullet points for the plain-text treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Plain-text donation pages are more effective in converting donors compared to stylized campaign graphics. The treatment group experienced a 332.0% increase in donations, indicating that the plain-text design resonated better with potential donors. The plain-text design may have created a more personal and relatable experience for visitors, leading to higher engagement and donations.
The statistical level of confidence for the increase in donations is 93.2%, which is approaching the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to ensure the results are consistent and reliable — the test should also have a greater sample size than the 7 donations for validity.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing and refining the plain-text donation page design. Further experimentation can be conducted to validate the results and explore additional factors that may influence donor conversion rates.
Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights for improving donor conversion rates through the use of plain-text design and a more human-centric approach. By continuing to iterate and test, the organization can optimize its donation page for better fundraising outcomes.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of a donation with visual cues affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2023 - 03/18/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at or near the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a security “lock” icon to the button affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a security "lock" icon to the donation button resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the treatment showed a 122.8% increase in revenue, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.0%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid but can be considered directional with potential for future iterations.
However, the positive impact observed in this experiment suggests that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements may be effective in addressing donor hesitancy and increasing donation rates. Therefore, it would be advisable to continue testing and experimenting with different security enhancements in future campaigns to further validate their impact on donor conversion rates.
It is important to note that future experiments should consider a longer testing period to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. This will ensure that the results are more reliable and can be used for making informed decisions.
Overall, the key learning is that security-themed enhancements have the potential positively increase average gifts and overall revenue. Further experimentation is needed to verify these findings and determine the most effective security enhancements to implement in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of donation information with an impact statement beneath the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more prevalent security-themed statement below the donate button will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 04/01/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitant or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the security of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The inclusion of a security-themed statement below the donate button had a negative impact on donation rates, with a significant decrease in donations for the treatment.

2. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid due to a level of confidence below 85%, the consistently negative trends observed across multiple metrics and segments indicate that caution should be taken when considering the implementation of a security-themed statement in future experimentation.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to further investigate and address the underlying drivers of hesitancy or concerns among potential donors. Future experimentation should focus on optimizing the donation process, simplifying the user experience, and building trust through other means such as social proof, testimonials, or transparency in how donations are used.

Additionally, it may be valuable to conduct user research or surveys to better understand the reasons for hesitancy and gather feedback from potential donors. This qualitative data can provide insights into the specific concerns and help inform future experimentation and optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How does using specificity and more active language that more closely relates to the mission in a donation page headline affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more active/specific language to the headline on Care Net’s main donation page will increase donation rates by better matching donor motivation and adding value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/07/2023
While Care Net’s homepage (which drives ~40% of traffic to the donation page over past 12 months), their mission, and even the gift array are focused largely on choice/options/alternatives for parents as a means to prevent abortion, this aspect is absent from the donation page headline, which is currently focused on saving lives, post Roe.
This could be causing confusion for potential donors as to “why to give” and a simple headline change may create a stronger “message match” and more incentive for potential donors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved adding more active/specific language to the headline on Care Net's main donation page, did not have the intended effect of increasing donation rates. Instead, it resulted in a 28.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in donations for all traffic was 88.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity. However, the results are approaching validity, meaning that this is a directional indicator that this result could happen again.

3. When looking at specific segments, the treatment had a significant negative impact on returning visitors, with a 583% decrease in donations and a 98% level of confidence. It also had a significant negative impact on visitors aged 45-54, with an 829% decrease in donations and a 99% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the treatment did not resonate with potential donors and had a negative impact on donation rates. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in the donation page headline to ensure it aligns with donor motivations and adds value. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the specific segments that were significantly affected, such as returning visitors and visitors aged 45-54, to better understand their preferences and tailor the messaging to their needs.

experiments.title: How a humanized donation page impacted gifts from survey takers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a donation page for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the message is aligned from a relevant staff member and positioned as a personal request.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/05/2023
On an instant donation page after visitors completed a survey, we wondered if the value proposition message would resonate more strongly with potential donors if it was presented in the first person. Since the survey was relevant to investigative journalism, we tested writing the donation page language as a message from an investigative journalist “Ryan” and signing the donation page with his name as if it were a personal message to the reader. We positioned this against the winning control from our previous test – using language around the principle of scarcity and referencing that only 1% of survey takers would take an invested next step toward change by making a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personal message from an investigative journalist did not resonate more strongly with potential donors. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 12% decrease in donations compared to the control with 83% level of confidence.

This experiment suggests that potential donors may not respond favorably to a personal message from a staff member. It is important to carefully consider the messaging and tone used in donation pages to ensure that they are effective in motivating donors to take action.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other messaging strategies that may be more effective in driving donations. It may also be valuable to further analyze the specific group of new visitors who saw a significant decrease in donations and determine if there are any underlying factors that contributed to this outcome.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and iterating on donation page messaging to continually optimize and improve conversion rates. Since this was a radical redesign from the existing copy, we could also pair the 1% language as a humanized message from Ryan going forward and see if pairing the winning message with an isolated variable makes a positive impact on conversion rate.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt.3)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety. In the second experiment, we saw a decrease in conversion rate when using “page.”
In this particular experiment we tested this concept on a separate offer that used slightly different verbiage in the button and started with the language “page” under the first assumption that people responded more to the use of “page”. Therefore, the control is “page,” treatment 1 is “site,” and treatment 2 is “journal.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment affirms what was uncovered in experiment #2 - "page" (the control in this experiment) is not universally beneficial. In fact the usage of "site" resulted in a 93% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 80%. While the use of "journal" resulted in a 138% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 91%.
Based on this, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of "journal" as a potential alternative to "site" or "page".
Overall, these findings suggest that there is value in exploring alternative language options to reduce perceived commitment or anxiety when prompting users to create a site on CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted monthly giving for membership renewals
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign with customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals. because  the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 07/02/2023
For visitors looking to renew their membership from the homepage, we wondered if the existing donation page language for renewals was too focused on the organization. We developed a radical redesign language with customized value proposition for existing members who are already familiar with the organization and had shown historical motivation to make a gift. We hypothesized that increasing the value prop elements of appeal and exclusivity would frame a significantly more attractive way of describing what the user desires. Even if the internal goal is to get a user to renew their membership, the user wants to help protect our nation’s founding principles and keep their membership active. We wanted to communicate this clearly on the page to see if it would increase conversion. We updated the language to recognize that we were talking to visitors who had previously supported the organization and had arrived on this page to make an additional gift to support the organization. We also tested the presentation of the monthly ask, changing it from a checkbox after the gift amount had already been selected to a tabbed form with “Join the Leaders Club” as the text for monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two months, the radical redesign treatment of the donation page with a customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew resulted in a 97% increase in Leaders Club Member conversion with 92% level of confidence. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results are directionally approaching validity. This suggests that the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focused on what the user wants to do rather than the organization's internal goals successfully attracted more donors. The treatment version also removed an additional step that asked the visitor to select which area their donation should go towards, so this reduced friction could have also played a role in the increased conversion.
We also saw flat results for one-time donations on this page, so there is opportunity to isolate the value proposition copy and do further testing to increase one-time gifts as well as monthly. We believe future iterations could include additional clarity around the benefits of being a Leaders Club member in addition to the tabbed form design and title to help more visitors choose this option.
Overall, the key learning is to focus on the user's desires and motivations, enhancing the value proposition in alignment with what we already know about the intended audience, in order to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a content offer instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s School Choice survey is producing a low instant donor conversion rate once users complete the survey and are redirected to an instant donation page. In an effort to increase donation conversion rate, we’ve built a treatment that leverages a premium offer when someone makes a donation of at least $10.
The premium is TPPF’s Pocket Constitution. We’ve seen past success when offering the Pocket Constitution as a free offer, so we want to see if using the content offer as a premium in this instance proves to be successful through an increase in donations.
The only difference between the control and treatment donation page is the closing bold paragraph in the treatment that informs page visitors of the premium opportunity and incudes an image of the premium to the right of the paragraph for visual appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, the treatment produced a 100% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 84.5% level of confidence. The treatment produced zero donations compared to two to the control.

Despite the nearly valid level of confidence, the outcome of this experiment should be considered lightly due to a total of only two transactions. The recommended number of transactions to confidently review outcomes is 50, so these results are well below that bar.

The traffic sources to this experiment was limited to only a Dear Reader, as we turned the School Choice offer off in paid social due to consistently low performance. Thus, this experiment did not benefit from the healthy amount of traffic it needed to generate more transactions.

The treatment would be worth testing again on another more popular/active offer to gain a better idea of the effectiveness of the premium concept and the specific premium offer.

experiments.title: How a Donation Interruptor Impacts Monthly Giving on an Offer’s Instant Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donation interruptor  for users who pre-select a one-time gift will achieve an increase monthly donation conversion because psychologically it lowers the amount of their original gift, while providing value prop copy about how that smaller amount each month has a far greater impact..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 07/02/2023
The Donation Interruptor is an experiment that we’ve tested on IMB’s general donation page in the past. For a greater collection of data, we wanted to test the experience on one of their offers, a Course on Prayer. The tabbed form on the donation page pre-selects One-Time, we hypothesized that a pop-up appearing directly after they submit their gift amount (but before they provide their payment info) will increase monthly gifts. The reason being that the popup will present the user value copy around how a lower amount (but each month) can make a greater impact even though a lower gift upfront.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing the Donation Interruptor on the Course on Prayer offer resulted in a significant increase in monthly donations for all traffic. Although the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, and the results are approaching validity.
This suggests that the interruptor pop-up, which highlights the impact of smaller monthly donations, has the potential to influence donors to choose the monthly giving option instead of a one-time gift. However, further testing is necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.
One observation is that although this increased monthly gifts, the treatment does appear to lower the total number of gifts. This however, did not validate as a statistical result. It is something to continue observing, while also keeping in mind that repeated monthly gifts can still be an exponential revenue gain even despite a small decrease off in total gifts

experiments.title: How donation page value proposition led by evidentials impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that angling donation page value proposition based on evidentials for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain a better grasp of the problem and become more motivated to contribute to the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI has launched a state fiscal responsibility survey that culminates with a donation opportunity once someone completes the survey. We want to test two donation pages each with a different approach.
The control will test into clarity: Painting a picture for potential donors of why we put together this survey for them, what the plan is behind it, and why their help is needed.
The treatment will test into evidentials, alerting potential donors to the extent of the problem by pointing to multiple facts and research. We want to see which approach resonates best with them and leads to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control approach of painting a clear picture and explaining the "why" for potential donors is more effective in achieving donations compared to the treatment approach led by evidentials.

The treatment resulted in a 68.0% decrease in donations for All Traffic with a nearly valid 93.0% statistical level of confidence. Revenue also saw a 29% decrease through the treatment with a 66% level of confidence.

Donors appreciated the clarity of the control which gave them a clear understanding of why we're offering this survey, how it will help improve Illinois fiscal responsibility, and why we need their help. One possible reason that donors did not respond as well to the treatment is because they already have a firm understanding of the problem at hand and did not need the additional background or context.

We should keep these experiment results in mind as we build value proposition for future offer donation pages. It may be wise to test into these approaches again to gain more data. But in this instance, clarity seems to be the key!

experiments.title: How testing gift images in a dear reader helps influence gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding images of the thank you gifts for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates because people want to see the gifts they will be getting as a thank-you for their donation..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. During their spring pledge drive, we wanted to run a 3-way test to see whether promoting their premium thank-you gifts using a dear reader style placement would help influence more donations.
We’ve tested dear reader asks in the past, with little lift in direct donations. So this test was designed to see whether having the images, versus not (and no-dear reader at all) would help influence overall online donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a lot of direct donations that can be attributed to the dear readers, we did see a slight lift approaching a valid level of confidence by using the dear reader overall, with a higher lift in donor conversion rates through using the images of potential thank you gifts.

Further testing of this concept would test the placement of the dear reader, as well as strengthening the language and making the connection between those reading the news articles and the 'why' they need to donate to support the work of WUNC.

experiments.title: How the way we address page visitors once they arrive to the donation page impact donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing page visitors as “Friend” or by their first name will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will appreciate the personal touch of a salutation and feel like they’re being spoken to conversationally rather than just visiting a regular old web page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
ACI’s Cancel Culture donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to their other offers. We wondered if the way we address donation page visitors once they arrive on the page would impact donations.
We separated this test into a, b, and c variant.
The “a” variant is a regular headline
The “b” variant implements “Friend” in the headline
And the “c” variant implements the user’s first name in the headline
We want to see if either the “Friend” or first name headlines move the needle in terms of increasing donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, each treatment did produce an increase in donor conversion rate compared to the control. Treatment 1 (Friend acknowledgement) produced a 28.9% increase with a 63.1% level of confidence, while treatment 2 (first name acknowledgement) produced a 42.4% increase with an 80% level of confidence.
Treatment 2 also produced a 20.6% increase in revenue with a 69% level of confidence.
It's clear that our donation page visitors prefer a personal acknowledgement over no acknowledgement at all.
Interestingly, new visitors led to the highest increase in donations when being addressed as "Friend". This segment resulted in a 64% increase with a nearly valid 83.1% level of confidence. Our existing visitors responded best to the first name acknowledgement.
We plan to run a new a/b test as our next iteration testing "Friend" vs First Name against each other, eliminating the current control, to see which generates the greater increase in donor conversion. This test should be able to run for the fully recommended 30 days to see if either variant reaches a valid result.
It will be interesting to see if we see the same trend in the results for the way new visitors and existing visitors are being addressed when we run the a/b test.

experiments.title: How personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality impacted donations during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality for pledge donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. Since a lot of web traffic during pledge drives is driven by offline sources (mainly radio) we wanted to see whether personalizing a sticky bar with a quote/image from a specific on-air personality, who would be on the air during a specific time and day, would make an impact on overall online donations.
We ran this as a 3-way test, with a control, sticky bar with quote but no image, and sticky bar with quote and image of on-air personality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test whether personalizing a sticky bar with a specific on-air personality would impact overall online donations for WUNC, a nonprofit NPR station. The treatment showed a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic, with an 87.6% statistical level of confidence, indicating that personalizing the sticky bar had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the results are not entirely valid and require further testing to ensure the intended impact.
While results varied from day-to-day, the overall lift that adding a sticky bar to the homepage during the pledge drive was impactful, with an 87% level of confidence that adding one would help increase overall donations.
Interestingly enough, while the image treatment version had a slightly lower conversion rate vis-a-vis the impact on overall online donations, when digging into the directly-attributed results, the treatment version had 6 gifts compared to the no image version of 3 gifts.
Further testing is definitely warranted, as is trying to quantify the connection between offline (radio) and online traffic.

experiments.title: How listing resources as a bullet list impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by listing resources with bullet points for web traffic will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is clarity and readability in what is being offered. because there is clarity and readability in what is being offered.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 06/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
For PTV’s book about Daniel, we decided to test listing out the resources for each giving level with bullet points. Historically PTV has listed the items as a paragraph.
experiments.keyLearnings: For this experiment, we saw a decrease in donor conversions by 6.8% with a level of confidence at 88.6%. While the results are not valid we were intrigued to see a decrease in performance.

experiments.title: How listing resources as a bullet list impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by listing resources with bullet points for web traffic will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is clarity and readability in what is being offered..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/01/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
For PTV’s book about Daniel, we decided to test listing out the resources for each giving level with bullet points. Historically PTV has listed the items as a paragraph.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment tested the effectiveness of listing out resources for each giving level with bullet points instead of a paragraph for a book about Daniel by Pathway to Victory (PTV). The hypothesis was that bullet points would increase donor conversion rates due to clarity and readability. However, the treatment resulted in a 9.7% decrease in revenue for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 84.4%.
From a donations perspective, we saw an 8.5% decrease in donor conversion with an 86.2% confidence level.
Both variations did see

experiments.title: How a Wiki-Style Dear Reader impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wiki-Style Dear Reader on Hour of Power‘s daily devotional pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is more visually arresting and transforms into a sticky bar if the user scrolls past it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Hour of Power is a Christian broadcast ministry that shares inspiring sermons, ebooks, and daily devotionals through their media presence and website. They have seen success in increasing email acquisition on their website’s daily devotional pages by adding Dear Readers below the devotion, and for their spring high-urgency campaign we decided to see how their traditional Dear Reader asking readers to become sustaining monthly donors would perform against a Wiki-Style Dear Reader.
The traditional Dear Reader appears on devotional pages underneath the devotion. The Wiki-Style Dear Reader appears closer to the top of the page and is above the devotional content. If a user scrolls down the page and goes past it, the Wiki-Style Dear Reader will disappear and a smaller version of it will appear at the bottom of the screen like a sticky bar.
The traditional Dear Reader only has one CTA button which brings the user directly to the campaign’s donation page, but the Wiki-Style version has two CTA buttons. The first button also leads the user directly to the donation page, but the second button that says “Give a Different Amount” opens up a small gift array within the Wiki-Style Dear Reader. The user can then select or enter the amount they would like to give and then they will be redirected to the donation page with their selection automatically chosen.
Our hypothesis was that new placement and style of the Wiki-Style Dear Reader would be more arresting and eye-catching for users on the page and would thus increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not fully validate, but we saw a decrease in donor conversion on the Wiki-Style Dear Reader with a 90% LOC. What this reveals about Hour of Power's audience is that they do not respond as well to arresting popups/sticky bars that use high-urgency language. Instead, it shows us that their audience is more responsive to CTAs that take a conversational approach.

In future testing we can try using the same kind of conversational language in the Wiki-Style that we use in the Dear Reader to clarify if their audience wasn't responding because of the language used in the Wiki-Style or if it was due the placement/presentation of the Wiki-Style.

experiments.title: How removing a donation page’s header image on mobile affected donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a donation page’s background header image for mobile viewers will achieve an increase in instant donation conversion rate because they will be less distracted by imagery and likely to read more of the value proposition copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase instant donation conversion rates on a policy organization’s voter fraud survey offer, we created a donation page treatment that removed the background header image on the mobile experience.
We believed that by removing this header image and lessening the amount of negative space above the fold, the viewer would read more of the page’s content and be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
Removing the background header image on the mobile donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing the instant donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 54.1% decrease in donations compared to the control version.The treatment version also resulted in a 73.2% decrease in average gift compared to the control version.
The experiment only ran for two weeks, but the decrease in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence and decrease in average gift prompted us to stop the experiment before results could fully validate.
Reasons for the decrease in donations and average gift could include:
The lack of the header image may have led to visitors thinking there was too much copy to read.The header image of the organization's building may contribute to a feeling of legitimacy that helped prospective donors make the decision to contribute.

experiments.title: How increasing the ask amount on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by increasing the “ask amount” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue for this page because there will be a larger average gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
This theological seminary client offered a new biblical resource, the Revelation Online Course. The control donation page for the Revelation Online Course had a $10 donation ask in the copy. The treatment page had the original control donation page copy but also included a larger gift amount ask of a $50 or more donation. This treatment page experiment was set up to show to 50% of viewers as an A/B test. We wanted to test if asking for a larger gift amount in the treatment donation page could increase the average gift and revenue for this online course.
We have noticed in past experiments that including specific donation ask copy can produce a lift in conversion rate. We thought that this donation page copy for this new biblical resource would be a great place to ask specifically for a larger donation of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount of $50 or more to the donation page copy for the Revelation Online Course, did not lead to an increase in average gift and revenue. In fact, the treatment page saw a 56.1% decrease in conversion rate. While the statistical sample size is approaching validity, the level of confidence was 88% which did not reach above 95% to validate this experiment. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to explore alternative ways of encouraging larger donations, such as providing more details on the course they will get if they donate $50 or more.

experiments.title: How positioning match oriented language impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2023
During a recurring gift campaign, we wanted to test which message would be more motivating to donors to upgrade from a one time to a recurring gift. On the control page, we highlighted that a matching gift was in place which would double their donation. For the treatment donation page, we went into more detail about the match and focused the value proposition more around doubling the impact of their monthly donation. We split traffic to each page 50/50 for the duration of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors did not achieve the expected increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment page resulted in a 26.5% decrease in donations with a statistical level of confidence just below the acceptable threshold. Therefore, further testing is necessary before making any definitive conclusions about the effectiveness of messaging related to matching gift campaigns for recurring donors. We learned that the match language—on its own—was less effective in converting donors than keeping the values-based language and mentioning the match as one additional incentive to give. In future experimentation, it may be helpful to focus on more targeted messaging and testing smaller changes in order to better understand the impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test other types of incentives beyond matching gifts to determine which strategies are most effective in motivating donors to convert to recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How asking for less money on a parenting premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/05/2023
Focus on the Family is a prominent global ministry that provides video content resources and insights for parents and families on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
We previously tested a lower gift array ($30 instead of $50) with FamilyLife’s Cherish Your Spouse series which led to a valid increase in donor conversion of 37.6% and an increase in the average gift amount. You can find that test here.
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether the impact of the lower gift array would be consistent across all verticals of Focus on the Family’s acquisition offers by running the same test on their Recognizing Your Son’s Need for Respect donation page (a parenting offer) and on Winning your Inner Battles (a faith offer). The results of the Winning Your Inner Battles test can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In our other experiments testing this lower gift array for the Cherish Your Spouse and Winning Your Inner Battles donation pages we saw validated increases in donor conversion, but during this experiment we weren't able to get greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic.
However, while this test did not validate any increase in donor conversion from the lower gift array, it did show in a directional lift of 68.8% with a 85% level of confidence. When looking at mobile traffic, the primary source for the offer, the LOC increased to 91%.  While this result does not meet the statistical level of confidence of 95% or higher, it does show a trend towards more users converting on the treatment version of the gift array. This result is in line with the results of the testing on Focus' other offers and reveals that, at least right now during high inflation, the lower gift array increases donor activation without harming revenue.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations- attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/01/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
This is our second try at this test. We extended the deadline of the campaign and will re-run this test.
The initial test resulted in a 7.8% increase with a 34.8% level of confidence to the treatment and 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
Let’s see how our second test performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the implementation of a goal thermometer for donation page visitors did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant 46.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group with a nearly valid 83.5% level of confidence.
The treatment went in the wrong direction compared to going in the right direction the first time we ran this experiment. That said, each experiment did produce an increase in revenue through the treatment.
1st test: 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
2nd  test: 16.7% increase in revenue with a 56.4% level of confidence.
Oddly, mobile donations through the treatment increased by 58.7% in the first test but saw a 77.5% decrease the second test. The second test did have a significantly smaller sample size, however.
It would be wise to test this again for a future high urgency campaign. An additional hypothesis would be that a more attainable goal would help produce an increase in donations. The goal was not even halfway reached for this campaign, which may have spoiled donor motivation because they felt their gift would not make a big enough dent towards accomplishing the giving goal. Revenue went up in both experiments, so it'd be interesting to see if it would go up the next time this experiment is run as well.

experiments.title: How prioritizing value proposition on an instant donation page’s header impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because we are reducing anxiety by focusing on “fund” language and giving people a clear call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2023 - 04/30/2023
This experiment serves as a follow up a previous experiment (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/8GVA) where we moved the transactional fund ID language from the header. We hypothesized that this transactional language would not inspire more people give. We wanted to give people the opportunity to read through the entire value proposition before making their gift. So, we tested moving the transactional language and Fund ID to the bottom of the experience, beneath the gift array.
When we initially ran the experiment we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that there were several factors that contributed to the decrease in donations:
The change headline actually made the “thank you” line not seem like a an actual header. 
Though the fund ID sounds transactional – the first sentence says “Support the Navigators” and it may be that this sentence is giving the reader more clarity as to what they’re supposed to do on that page 
Seeing thank you at the very top with no other CTA may make the person think that this is the end of the interaction.
So we ran a followup experiment where we ran 2 treatments.
The first treatment enlarged the “thank you” line so that it looked like an actual header. The second treatment enlarged the copy and  added a transitional sentence to let people know that this was not just a confirmation page, nor is it the end of the interaction. This transitional copy served as a replacement for the “Support the Navigators” copy and had more value prop-based language.
This was tested across all the active instant donation pages for the Navigators’ offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors can achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. By providing a clear call to action in the header and removing the transactional language (fund ID), the treatment resulted in a 60% directional lift in donations for all traffic, with a 92.0% statistical level of confidence.
Results indicate that the font size of the header had no real impact on conversion rate but when paired with the transitional sentence we saw a significant impact. Our initial hypothesis was correct in that it's likely that ebook downloaders believed that this donation page was actually a confirmation page with no further action to be taken. Additionally, the use of more value based language ("do you want to make resources like this available to others who want to grow in their faith") inspired more people to take action and refocuses people on the "why" of their gift.
