experiments.title: How a lower ask amount affects revenue on a Wikipedia-style article appeal during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a lower recurring donation ask on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal banner will achieve greater revenue by offering a lower commitment, thus increasing donations enough to offset the lower ask amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 03/24/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on recurring donor acquisition, we wanted to know if lowering the ask amount on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal would increase recurring donor rates by offering a lower commitment given that donors would be giving on a monthly basis.
This test followed on the heels of a previous experiment (during the same campaign), where we learned that the Wikipedia-style appeal tactic outperformed both a banner ad and a slide-in appeal when presented on article pages being viewed by prospective donors.
We set this up as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $30 ask and roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $15 ask in the hopes that donor volume would outweigh the lower ask and provide a net increase in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 5 days, we were unable to validate our hypothesis that a lower ask amount would lift donations enough to overcome the lower ask and produce a net increase in revenue over an assumed 11-month period.
The treatment was only able to produce one additional recurring gift and the 48% decrease in revenue (81.4% level of confidence) did not justify continuing to run this experiment. Based on these results, we elected to run the $30 ask for the remainder of the campaign since it appeared that the decreased ask amount did little to increase the volume of donors while cutting overall revenue nearly in half.

experiments.title: How removing organization-centric language impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing organization-centric language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because we speak about the donor and what they believe and can accomplish rather than ourselves. because  we speak about the donor and what they believe and can accomplish rather than ourselves.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2023 - 03/26/2023
When reviewing performance on an instant donation page, we noticed there were several organization-centric references throughout the copy. We wondered if replacing the phrases where we talk about our organization and what we believe with language that instead spoke about the donor using “you” focused messaging could help us achieve a lift in donor response. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we observed a 39% lift on the treatment with 90% level of confidence so the experiment did not validate, but we were approaching statistical significance. We believe these results demonstrate that reducing organization-centric language for donation page visitors can lead to an increase in conversion rate and revenue. The 39.4% increase in donations for all traffic and a 556% increase in revenue further support this hypothesis. In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the effectiveness of donor-centric messaging and consider incorporating it in our donation pages.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain clarity and motivation as to how serious the problem is that we’re asking them to help solve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/10/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis Survey instant donation page is getting an instant conversion rate below 1%. In brainstorming ways to potentially increase conversion rate, we wondered if the addition of evidentials would further clarify and intensify the problem that the donor’s gift would help solve. For a test treatment, we added three bullet points towards the beginning of the value proposition copy, each one stating a research fact that speaks to the problems at the border.
Will statistics and facts lead to more clarity and motivation, and ultimately more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy may not necessarily increase donations. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. The treatment resulted in a 52.3% decrease in conversions with a statistical level of confidence of 83.2%. These results suggest that potential donors may not be motivated by research facts and statistics. We can also surmise that donors already have a strong understanding of the problem and that they don't need evidentials to help with their understanding.

As the problem is clearly known and communicated throughout the offer funnel, future tests need to focus on other components of the instant donation page, such as the solutions or the ask. Potential donors see eye to eye with the organization on the problem, but maybe they are not on the same page with the solutions we are offering, or maybe the solutions are not clear enough.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain clarity and motivation as to how serious the problem is that we’re asking them to help solve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/10/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis Survey instant donation page is getting an instant conversion rate below 1%. In brainstorming ways to potentially increase conversion rate, we wondered if the addition of evidentials would further clarify and intensify the problem that the donor’s gift would help solve. For a test treatment, we added three bullet points towards the beginning of the value proposition copy, each one stating a research fact that speaks to the problems at the border.
Will statistics and facts lead to more clarity and motivation, and ultimately more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy may not necessarily increase donations. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. The treatment resulted in a 52.3% decrease in conversions with a statistical level of confidence of 83.2%. These results suggest that potential donors may not be motivated by research facts and statistics. We can also surmise that donors already have a strong understanding of the problem and that they don't need evidentials to help with their understanding.

As the problem is clearly known and communicated, future tests need to focus on other components of the instant donation page, such as the solutions or the ask. Potential donors see eye to eye with the organization on the problem, but maybe they are not on the same page with the solutions we are offering.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including a dynamic donor countdown to a banner ad affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner for potential donors will achieve increased clicks to the donation page because people respond to an urgent message.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2021 - 05/08/2022
If we added a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner, donor conversion will increase because donors respond to urgent messaging. We used an emergency alert banner vs. an emergency banner with a countdown as our variable treatment.
Note – Dynamically counts down from 25. After a gift, the number will decrease to confirm with donors that the counter is valid.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant results.
• Unique banner click rate was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Donor conversion was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Recommendation is not to add the dynamic counter to the emergency banner.

experiments.title: How validating each form field impacted overall completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding form field validation for petition signers will achieve an increase in petition signatures because the checkmarks will reduce friction by adding cognitive momentum and clarity in the user process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 12/16/2022
When reviewing petition signature pages, we wondered what how we could create a treatment that would provide additional cognitive momentum to reward visitors for beginning the process and encourage them to complete the remaining steps. We developed a green checkmark that would appear when someone had completed a required field to let them know they had successfully completed that step and could move on to the next field. If a user missed a step on either the control or the treatment, they could clearly see an error message that would prompt them to return to that form field and correctly complete it, but the treatment also interjected a “Looks good!” message to confirm they could keep moving forward. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding form field validation did not result in an increase in petition signatures for all traffic. However, the treatment did lead to a significant increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Moreover, the treatment showed mixed results across different visitor segments. For instance, there was a decrease in signups for mobile and email visitors, a significant increase in tablet visitors, and a considerable decrease in signups for female and male visitors.

Given these results, it is important to keep in mind that implementing form field validation may not improve petition signatures for all segments of visitors. Therefore, future experimentation should focus on identifying which visitor segments may benefit from this treatment and which segments may not. Additionally, the revenue increase observed in the treatment group should be further investigated to understand the underlying factors contributing to this effect. Lastly, it is worthwhile to continue experimenting with different design treatments that can reduce friction and encourage visitors to complete the signup process.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a post-petition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more you-focused language for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the emphasis is less on the organization and more about what the donor wants to see happen.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/12/2022
When evaluating our standard donation page language presented to someone after they signed a petition, we noticed the language was very organization-centric and we wondered if the value proposition could be reconstructed. The control copy says “Would you consider donating so we can show this petition to people on social media?” and we wondered if this was what the donor actually wanted to do or if we could make a more compelling case for support using more donor-centric language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment revealed that using more donor-centric language for petition signers resulted in a 15.4% increase in donations with an 89.6% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, the results are approaching validity, implying that further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness. Additionally, the (Other) visitors had an 86% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

The key takeaway from this experiment is that a shift towards more you-focused language is likely to increase donor conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the language to gain further improvements in the results. Based on these insights, Canadian Taxpayers Federation can improve its donation conversion rates by creating more compelling donor-centric value propositions.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky “my cart” button when a user begins a donation affects conversion rates

rows.name: Cart button at top of page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky cart button for donors who add an item to their cart (scroll persistent), will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2021 - 09/27/2022
Added a sticky cart button that appears when users add an item to their cart (scroll persistent).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the sticky cart (42%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Mobile revenue per visitor was higher for the sticky cart (68%) at a statistically valid LoC (98%).
• Recommendation is sticky cart addition to all gift catalog-related pages.
• Projected yearly incremental lift of $200,000.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-goal slider that stresses urgency to a sponsorship page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency for potential donors will achieve increase sponsorship conversion  because there is a clear CTA. because  there is a clear CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2022 - 03/28/2022
Adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency will increase sponsorship conversion because there is a clear CTA. When a user enters the Child List Sponsorship page in the variant, a popup will appear after 3 seconds on the page. It presents a variable number of lifeline children that need to be sponsored. The popup scrolls with the page and can be closed by the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Desktop donor conversion was higher for the variant (28%) at a statistically valid confidence level (99%).
• Recommendation is to add the slider to all sponsorship-related pages.
• Overall, the variant produced an increase (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (92%).
• RPV was higher for the variant (13%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (84%).
• Desktop RPV was higher for the variant (34%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (95%).
• Mobile donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (16%).
• Mobile RPV was slightly lower for the variant (-6%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (66%).
• Based on 2021 figures (assuming fixed traffic and average gift), we project an incremental lift of $76,000 annually. The net 60-month value of these donors is approximately $1.4M.
• Given the statistical significance in desktop donor conversion (28%) and limited directional downside on mobile, we recommend adding the slider to all sponsorship pages.
• Results may have had an even greater significance if not for the Ukraine emergency. The experiment was close to validating five days in, but highly motivated traffic related to Ukraine evened out the treatments.
• Additionally, we look to test different versions of copy and visual elements employed by the slider to optimize the experience further.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed donation form affect recurring conversion rates on a primary donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for donation page visitors will achieve higher recurring donation rates by making this giving option and its impact more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 02/10/2023
We wanted to know how a tabbed donation form (featuring single and monthly gifts) would affect recurring donations on the primary donation page for a public policy organization.
In this experiment, the control presented the option to make a recurring gift by featuring a checkbox below the gift array that asked the visitor if they would like to make their gift on a recurring basis. Beneath the checkbox, an impact statement communicated the impact of making a recurring gift.
The treatment instead presented the option to make a recurring gift in a tabbed form format, with “single gift” and “monthly gift” shown side-by-side above the gift array and the impact statement shown above the tabs.
For this experiment, ~50% of traffic was shown the control and ~50% of traffic was shown the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed that the tabbed donation form produced an 11.0% decrease in recurring gifts with a 25% level of confidence, a 25.4% decrease in overall donations with a 99.2% level of confidence, and a 32.1% decrease in traffic with a 94.4% level of confidence.
These decreases were most severe among returning visitors where total donations decreased by 29.5% with a 99.7% level of confidence and revenue decreased by 40.6% with a 97.6% level of confidence indicating.
For new visitors, we observed a large increase in revenue (177%) -- but because this increase was a result of just one additional donor, it is apparent that this is being skewed by a large gift and not indicative of overall performance.
These results disprove our hypothesis and indicate that in the context of visiting a primary donation page (high donor motivation), a tabbed donation form with the impact statement above the gift array may have distracted the donor or created friction by presenting multiple options before they made the decision to give.
It also suggests that it is more persuasive to ask someone to "upgrade" their donation after they have already made a micro-commitment by selecting their donation amount from the gift array.
Based on these results, we will look to test a monthly giving checkbox against the tabbed donation forms currently running on this organization's instant donation pages tied to acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page will increase donations by providing more “reasons to give” to prospective donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2022 - 01/21/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation by improving the “reason why” they should give.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed 12.5% decrease in donations from traffic that was redirected to the treatment donation page with more value proposition language. The low sample size of the experiment prevented us from validating these results, however, it does not appear that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page motivated more people to give and may even have demotivated visitors from giving.
And while we cannot draw statistically valid conclusions about these results, it is worth noting that this outcome may have been influenced by user motivation. Specifically, after reviewing results, we are led to hypothesize that because someone who clicks a "make a donation" button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to "stay out of their way" by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
This suspicion is supported by the fact that "new visitors" who we may assume would be the exact segment that would benefit most from increasing the "reason to give" gave 28% less (86% level of confidence) when visiting the treatment with longer copy than they did when they visited the control with minimal copy above the donation form.

experiments.title: How a callout emphasizing a year-end matching challenges impacts donor conversion on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout emphasizing a year-end matching challenge to the acquisition offer donation page will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal of making a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/01/2023
Focus on the Family is an international ministry that equips families to grow closer to one another and to God. During their year-end campaign, they wanted to test the impact of emphasizing a year-end matching challenge on their acquisition offer donation pages to see if that would increase donor conversion on the page. They hypothesized that the opportunity to “double your impact” would increase the appeal of making a gift and with it the donor conversion on the page. The control page mentioned the matching challenge in the body copy. For the treatment page, they would add a callout box right under the donation button, the point of decision, to emphasize the matching opportunity there as well. The text in the box would read, “Don’t delay. The match expires on Dec 31. Give now to double your impact.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to our hypothesis, the callout box did not increase donor conversion. There was an increase in donor conversion on desktop traffic, however since the vast majority of traffic to the page is from mobile devices, there were too few donations in the segment to validate a learning. It seems that the box may have added friction by extending the length of the page on mobile devices which outweighed any of the benefit from reminding the donor of the available match.

Further testing is needed to see if the audience on acquisition offers is as motivated by "year-end" language on the donation page as their housefile audience would be. These visitors are less likely to already be invested in the ministry of Focus on the Family and thus less likely to be motivated to give a "year-end" gift.

experiments.title: How an exit intent impacted donor conversion during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an exit intent for web visitors will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth.
For previous high-urgency campaigns, we have tested exit intent popups but have not been able to see a valid response. We decided to test this again with the calendar year-end match campaign.
Previous tests, https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4829 and https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4417
For Giving Tuesday through December 31st, PTV offered a matching campaign of up to $525k for any donation made.
The exit intent ran on the entire website for anyone that visited a site to make a donation but did not complete the donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we were very close to achieving a valid level of confidence. We saw a 4.8% increase in donor conversions with a level of confidence of 92.9%.
Compared to the 2 other times we have run this experiment, this one achieved the highest level of validation for donor conversions.
Due to these results we will move forward with rolling out the exit intent popup during high urgency campaigns and begin testing into copy, CTA and strategy within the popup itsself.

experiments.title: How showing a reminder pop up to users that abandoned the donation process affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a pop up reminder to complete a donation for people that have closed out a donation page, without making a donation will achieve a higher amount of revenue than allowing users to remember on their own.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 09/30/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would cause users to complete their transaction…instead of abandoning their donation all-together. This experiment was based on abandoned cart functionality often seen in the e-commerce space, but seldom used in the NPO space. Their current donation platform does not natively allow for this functionality, so we built a test using the user’s last click on a donation amount (on an abandoned or closed-out donation page) to launch a pop up with that amount, asking the user to complete their transaction with a simple nudge.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for 41 days. While it did not reach statistical significance (only an 87% Level of Confidence), we did have some very interesting learnings. Since we were testing for revenue lift, we wanted to be sure the data was not skewed by abnormally large donation amounts from either the control or the treatment. FOTF often gets large donations that can skew a revenue experiment if we do not normalize the data. After we ran the experiment, we created a custom Google Analytics segment that only counted donations that were less than $1k in the sample size. After doing so, we noticed that the revenue for the treatment was significantly higher - an 85.2% lift in revenue. This took the average gift of this new sample group from $75.54 to $131.86. It is to be noted that prior to removing the gifts over $1k, the control had a sample size much closer to the treatment. Did this weigh into the final results? Are the motivations different for people that come to the main site vs coming from an article page or elsewhere? WHile this test provided some exciting potential lifts, it is still unclear if motivations based on entry point could skew this data. The next step in the process will be to run the same experiment, but split into two different ones, and test if the revenue lift is better or worse when directly visiting via the main page or visiting instead through a secondary page.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2022 - 11/25/2022
When launching a new content offer, we had similar donation page language in place from previous content offers and we knew that the housefile visitors would likely be returning visitors. We wanted to test a new approach that condensed some lengthy copy on the treatment into 3 specific items the reader should know. We also updated the headline from “Thank you” to “Congratulations” and mentioned the title of the specific offer they had requested. We split traffic equally between the two pages and ran the experiment for 2 weeks before evaluating results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After two weeks, we saw a 25.7% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence, so the results were indicating a directional decrease. We believe the treatment language in the headline "you're all set" was causing visitors to leave the page before reading the remaining message. We saw valid decreases for many visitor segments, so we decided to end the experiment early and reassess the best treatment to run for these visitors. On future testing for this page, we will reconstruct the headline language that asks people to read the message below before they exit the site. Then, we can better evaluate if the "3 things you should know" approach was the true cause for the decrease, or if there was another element from the radical redesign (in this case, the headline) that was inadvertently causing friction that interfered with the test and prevented people consuming the message.

experiments.title: Does Adding Social Proof on a Donation Page Increase the Number of Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof to an instant donation page for people who have signed up for a free video course will result in a higher number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2022 - 11/14/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of donations on an Instant Donation Page by adding a statement of social proof.
We also wanted to see if there was an impact based on where the social proof was located on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test we found that adding social proof above the point of decision (on the donation form) led to a lift 27%, but did not reach validity with only a 52% level of confidence. Even though we were not testing for increases in revenue, we did find that while total donation amounts were similar between the control and variant 2, the average donation amount was around $3 higher for the variant with social proof. Did the social proof increase motivation to give more? Further testing would be needed to say with certainty.

We also discovered that placement did seem to matter...as adding the proof below the form near the submit button resulted in nearly a 50% decrease over the second variant. My hypothesis is that if a donor is to make it that far they do not need the extra motivation to donate, and in fact, could have led to more friction in the giving process.

There will need to be further testing to see if the variables can produce positive results. For our next experiment, we will test the social proof in variant 2 against different copy to see if the way the proof was written will affect the results. And, since we did not have a clear winner, we will start with the same control of no social proof against various statements of social proof above the donation form.

experiments.title: How adding first name personalization to the headline of an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding first name personalization to the headline of an instant donation page for page visitors will build trust and relations between the potential donor and the organization, and will ultimately achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2022 - 11/07/2022
For TPPF’s newest offer, the Top Issues in Texas survey, we were interested to see how users would take to first name personalization in the headline of the offer of instant donation page. Once a user submits the survey by providing their first name, last name, and email address, they would be redirected to the instant donation page where the headline would refer to them by their first name.
We wondered if this personalization method would make the user feel acknowledged, and build trust and relations with the visitor, leading them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment validated with the treatment resulting in an 86.3% DECREASE in donor conversion rate (95.7% level of confidence). There was a significant decrease across all segments, revealing that users either lost motivation to give or did not gain motivation to give when they were acknowledged by their first name when reaching the instant donation page. This may be an audience that is suspicious of seeing their first name when they do not have a personal relationship with the organization. This experiment may be worth testing with another offer to determine if the TPPF audience overall is turned off by first name personalization, or if the response is specific to the audience for this offer.

experiments.title: How adding first name personalization to a donation page affected donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by personalizing the headline with a first name for survey participants  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2022 - 07/30/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a survey offer that directed users to an instant donation page after completion. We wanted to see an increase in the instant donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that by personalizing the headline with a first name it would create a greater connection with the reader and communicate a deeper appreciation for their participation. Additionally, we suspected that the control headline was communicating to the user that there was no further action to be taken: “Thank you for your insights and for giving your opinion.” Without changing the primary message, we believed using a personalization would make for a headline that is less likely to be ignored.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for 50 days, and saw a 124% increase in donor conversion rate with a 83.5% confidence level.
The level of confidence and small low number of donations (preferably at least 50) still encourages room for testing. There are a few notable hints in the data regarding our initial concept. Our hypothesis was that a name personalization would show greater appreciation and stand out to the user. One interesting comparison is that the treatment caused little to no change in conversion rate for new visitors, but did cause a slight increase for returning visitors. But alternatively, Organic Search visitors had a 67% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence. Is there a learning to be had on when users perceive the most value in personalization? Is there thought to be more value in someone "remembering" our name, than an organization using it directly after it was provided?
Ultimately, more testing is still required in order to validate these findings.
Revenue experienced a 85.0% decrease with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 17.1% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 9.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 14.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 66.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making a headline more than just a thank you/confirmation on instant donation page affects results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by better aligning the headline copy with the body copy on the instant donation page for users completing the survey will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2022 - 10/27/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a survey offer that directed users to an instant donation page after completion. The body copy told a testimony of someone who was able to reconcile their doubts between faith and science through RTB’s resources. The ask was that their generosity is what makes this possible, and would continue to bring more skeptics back to the faith.
We wanted to see an increase in the instant donor conversion rate. We believed this number was low because users were not actually reading the testimony, but simply exiting because the headline didn’t communicate that there was more to do. The original read, “Thank you for giving your insights and for giving your opinion.” We replaced this with a headline that we believed would encourage the user to read on, “Thank you for your insights. We also want to see more skeptics come to Christ. Here’s why your response matters.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for 3 months, but could not reach an adequate sample size of at least 50 donations to determine validity. The result was that the treatment actually resulted in a 68.1% decrease in donations with an 85.7% confidence level. However, it should be noted that the entirety of this decrease seemed to result from mobile traffic. The desktop view showed a 1.3% increase in conversion rate (though with low confidence), meanwhile, the numbers on mobile view accounted for 84% of the 85.7% decrease at a nearly valid level of confidence (94.5%).
Aside from the change in messaging, the biggest difference between the treatment and the original is that the treatment is twice as long. If the problem was the actual message/value of the headline, one would think that it would cause a similar decrease whether read on desktop or on mobile. In other words, if the problem was an uncompelling sentence, it would stand to reason that it would be uncompelling whether you read it on small screen or on a big one.
This is not the case. Instead, the treatment didn't show any decrease in the desktop view. 84% of the 85% decrease came solely from mobile users. It's possible that by doubling the length of the headline, it increased friction for mobile users by eliminating the body copy from view unless they scrolled down. It appears that most of these mobile users were also new visitors. Meaning that if there appeared to only be a headline with no body copy beneath it,  a majority of these users had no prior experience to make them think otherwise.
The key learning is more of a reaffirmation: mobile traffic continues to be a huge factor in your results and the mobile experience should always be tested and considered.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 82.2% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 68.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had an 84.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had an 81.1% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 10/31/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign from October 1st – October 31st. We previously tested a countdown clock on the donation page for a majority of the campaign time, but we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion with the countdown clock.
We decided to test a goal thermometer for their $50,000 campaign goal on the last 4 days of the campaign. The thermometer started at $30,000 which is how much they had raised so far. The thermometer was set to grow every time someone made a donation on that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal thermometer saw a 19.5% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 89.2% level of confidence. Similar to the countdown clock, we can see from this experiment that the Working Preacher audience did not respond better to seeing the goal thermometer on the donation page.

We would like to test this concept further on another one of Luther Seminary's campaigns that's targeting a different audience so that we can try to understand if this type of "donor goal" experiment only performs negatively for the Working Preacher audience or for the Luther Seminary audience as a whole.

experiments.title: How Moving the Global Navigation Donate Button Onto Its Own Menu Line impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the “give now” button onto its own global navigation menu line will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2022 - 07/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a prior test, they’d found that the addition of a “monthly” donate button to their global site navigation menu increased donor conversion. However, there were some issues with that test that made the results unclear. We had several factors that changed in the experiment (the placement of the “give now button,” the wording on the buttons, and the addition of a monthly donate button) that clouded the results. In order to understand WHICH factor led to the increase in donor conversion, we decided to test simply moving the “give now” button out of the menu line with the other options and onto its own line. We believed this would give it prominence as it would stand on its own in the navigation bar.
Since this would only impact Desktop traffic, that is the only source of donors we examined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn't wait to reach a 95% or greater level of confidence, there was no period during the test or segment of donors where we were seeing a positive impact and it was clear that moving the button was having a directional decrease on conversion, so we ended the test.
This showed us that donors were not more likely to click the "give now" button when it was in its own menu. Next, we will test the language of the donate button, "give now" vs "donate now" to see which is the most converting.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a main donate page affects recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly call out to the main donate page for prospective donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 07/09/2022
The Navigators had an initiative to increase the acquisition of monthly donors. At the heel of their recurring donor campaign, we wanted to implement a simple way to acquire more recurring donors outside of campaign season. When looking at their main donation page we noticed that the monthly option blended with the other options presented for giving, among “Anonymous Gift” and “In memory of.” It was our hypothesis that the monthly option was not prominent enough for the prospective user to select this option. Furthermore, there was no value proposition language that positioned monthly giving as a better option for the prospective donor. We decided to implement a monthly callout with two variables. The first treatment simply stated what their monthly gift would do – the core value proposition: “Help believers become disciplemakers with your monthly gift today.” The second treatment gave the prospective donor a goal and identified the person as a believer who could help others mature in their faith: “We’re looking for 250 believers to give monthly to help others mature in their faith. Will you be one?” Traffic to this main donate page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for nearly a month we saw the most impact on desktop users. For the first callout we saw a nearly valid 50% lift in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 94%. In the second callout we saw a 11% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 34%. When looking across all channels we did see a 40% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 91%. Given that the highest and most statistically valid lift was seen from the first callout, we had decided to rollout this variant to 100% of their traffic. These results indicate that the Navigators' audience are more motivated by their core value proposition to create more disciplemakers. They did not respond nearly as much to the second, goal-oriented variant. It could be that the goal of 250 seemed too large for the end-user and could've deterred them from making their monthly contribution. We are proposing to test this same concept on the Navigator's staff pages as well.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacted donor conversions on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve a change in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/01/2022
Luther Seminary was offering a free online course called Cosmic Adventure that explained the relationship between science and faith including the history behind this relationship. They offered this free course through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email marketing. This offer was running for almost a year and the conversion rate on the donation page was on a steep decline.
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding a humanized sticky bar, featuring a message and image of Andy Root, Professor at Luther Seminary and Chair of Youth and Family Ministry, would have an impact on donor conversion rates from prospective donors coming from this digital acquisition offer.
The message tried to focus the prospective donor’s attention on why they should make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 61%, but with only an 86.7% level of confidence. We hypothesize that this is due to the nature of who we included in the messaging, as well as the messaging itself. While Andy Root is well known in his community at Luther Seminary, he might not be that well known to those prospective donors who come onto the file through digital acquisition efforts--they are downloading the content because it appeals to them, and not necessarily because of who it's coming from.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 59.9% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a personalized monthly call out to staff donation pages affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized monthly call out  for prospective staff donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Navigators recently ran experiment where they added a monthly call out to their main donation page. When they saw promising results through this experiment, we decided to run this same type of experiment on their staff pages. In an effort to increase monthly donations we drew attention to the monthly giving option on the donation page by adding a callout and adding a personalization. This personalization pulled in their first name in the call out so as to humanize the giving experience. Our hypothesis was that if we highlighted giving monthly as an option and we reminded the donor that they were in fact giving to a person, we would convert more monthly donors.
We tested this concept on a select group of staff members who were actively fundraising and were receiving a high amount of traffic on their pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at all recurring gifts acquired through this experiment we saw a 22% decrease in recurring gifts with a level of confidence of 66.5%. However, when examining a specific segment, new visitors, we saw a nearly valid decrease of 43.9% with a level of confidence of 94.6%. These were surprising results compared to what we experienced on the main donate page.
We concluded that:
The main donate page's callout was actually much more focused on the impact of the monthly gift "making disciplemakers" as opposed to just stating they should make a monthly gift like this callout. New site visitors may not be as swayed to see a specific person's name on the callout. Adding the personalization in third person, i.e. "Support Joe", may seem to organizationally-centric and could deter potential donors.
Our recommendation is to run this test again across all staff pages, change the statement so that it once again focuses on the value and impact of monthly giving, and change the personalization so that it reads "Support me/us."

experiments.title: How removing branding and fund type in an appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: Impact of reducing mental anxiety by adding, “cancel anytime” to a monthly donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that Adding “cancel anytime” language to our monthly donation page for visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2021 - 11/14/2021
We know that decreasing mental anxiety leads to an increase in donor conversion. One potential point of anxiety when asking for a monthly donation is the concern that the donor is then “locked into” a recurring transaction that they can’t get out of. We decided to test adding “cancel anytime” language to the donation page for our Fall monthly donor campaign to see which converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Due to the limited duration of the test (one two-week campaign) we couldn't validate the results on all audiences, however we had enough mobile traffic to validate based on that segment. Here, we found a 38% increase in donor conversion. Most other segments also reported an increase in donor conversion by adding the "cancel anytime language." ( the overall average was a 12% increase). This means that by reducing this mental anxiety we can convert more visitors into monthly donors. We will roll this out to other pages.

experiments.title: How a premium impacted donor convertion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a book premium for survey takers from the email house file will achieve increase donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/18/2021
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to find new ways to incentivize their email house file to make a donation. As part of their education survey instant donation page, they decided to test out including a free copy of the Little Patriots first children’s book with a donation of $25 or more. Since the donor audience is a bit older, the book was framed as a great way for them to impart this same curriculum to their children or grandchildren.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After receiving over 150 donations, we found that the book premium did not improve statistically donor conversion--it actually seemed to have a negative impact (although that was not statistically significant.) Since this premium had incremental costs associated with it, we decided to end the experiment prior to it validating.
With an average instant donor conversion rate of 18.5%, the next logical step is to find ways to increase the value received from the donors rather than trying to getting more of them to give. The average instant donor conversion rate is normally between 1% to 4%. With this in mind, the next experiment will focus in increasing recurring donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How simplifying the primary header on the Focus site for mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by removing additional clutter and creating clarity to the navigation header?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2021 - 09/10/2021
Focus on the Family was running a high urgency giving campaign called SeeLife. During the campaign they featured a donation wikibanner at the top of the site’s primary header and navigation. When looking at the experience on mobile devices, there was a lot of clutter with the addition of this wikibanner to what was already on the site. We hypothesized this was causing friction to the donation pathway. To test into this, we developed two variations. For one of them, we removed the CTA’s in the header that were not needed to make a gift, but kept the “donate” button. For the second version, we removed the CTA’s in the header that were not needed to make a gift, removed the donate button, but added a line of copy above the gift array that said, “please select the amount you would like to give”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the experiment, but we did see get a directional lift with one variation and got even greater clarity with the user experience.
We are learning that donor's are experiencing the most friction with the wikibanner itself. When we added the call to action on top of the gift array, we were able to increase donor conversion (directionally) by 11%! Revenue experienced a 15.4% increase with a 92% level of confidence.
Just adding the donate button back in to the experience had no impact on donor conversion.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Search visitors had a 113.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 12.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 127.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had an 84.4% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How specificity in the value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity to the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2021 - 08/06/2021
Prison Fellowship International was running a campaign to acquire donors for their Lamplighter program, which told prisoners about Jesus through a Bible study. The page they had developed  had the headline “Bring Light Into the Darkness”. Since the audience they were targeting was primarily Christian, they wondered if they should be more specific and give the donor the opportunity to “Share Jesus with a Prisoner in Darkness”.
They developed this new headline and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though it did not validate, the new copy produced a 23% decrease in conversion rate. There are a few possible reasons for this:
First, the new copy, while clearer, might be less engaging to the reader and not bring them into the body copy as much. Second, the new value proposition might not align with the rest of the page. Finally, it might come across to the reader as negative to cast the "prisoner" as someone "in darkness".
Ultimately, this experiment was directional and slow to validate, so more radical approaches must be tested to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing the donation page with experiential language for fiscal year end donors will achieve an increased donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2021 - 08/13/2021
During a fiscal year end campaign, we were seeing lower donor conversion compared to the previous year so we decided to develop a treatment to the donation page for the remainder of the campaign to see if we could improve performance. We modified the headline from “Help DTS reach the world with solid biblical training” to “You can help DTS train students to reach the world with the gospel of Jesus Christ!” We also removed body copy that seemed organization-centric that referenced enrollment trends and their accredited degree programs and instead of featuring the high-caliber quality of the training gave examples of vocations that students pursue after their education and training (counseling, pastoring, etc.) to humanize the institution and presented this as an experiential outcome for how they would “share the good news and strengthen believers” which aligns with what the donor wants to accomplish through their gift. We also introduced copy that highlighted the option to give monthly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since the experiment only ran during the final 2 days of the campaign, we would need to run an additional test with similar language to truly test the concept without the existing urgency of the deadline that could be influencing results. The treatment donation page had a 19.1% increase in conversion rate with 93% level of confidence. We want to take this directional lift in gifts on the page and see where else we could apply this learning.
While we did not reach statistical significance to validate the experiment, we did observe some notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment that are outlined below:
Revenue experienced a 47.3% decrease with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New  visitors had a 39.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 23.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had an 88.5% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 76.4% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language impacts donor conversion on a hero banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on the hero image may increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/27/2021
Care Net gets a healthy conversion rate on the hero image of their website. The call to action is to make a (monthly) gift, though they get a high volume of one time gifts from it as well. To see if we could increase the conversion from the banner, we wanted to test communal language on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we weren't able to validate the test but we did see a 27% directional lift from the communal language. Additionally, this increased overall revenue by 33% at a 86% level of confidence. What is really interesting is that returning visitors had an even greater difference in conversion. The communal language increased donor conversion for returning visitors to the site by 49%! All of these results reinforce the idea that when we talk to the visitor as if they are a friend and even recognize and call-out the fact that we know and appreciate that they are fierce advocates for the cause, we increase their likelihood to give and ultimately convert. The conversion become less about the transactional nature of the gift and more about the impact and the donor.

An additional observation of the impact on another visitor segment in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 55.0% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
While we will want to further experiment to reach a valid conclusion to identify the optimal version of the form, based on these learnings, we will want to make sure future appeals for tablet and email visitors are pointing to donation pages that indicate evidentials from a credible third party to maximize average gift from these segments and drive overall revenue.

experiments.title: How communal language impacts donor conversion on a hero banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on the hero image may increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/27/2021
Care Net gets a healthy conversion rate on the hero image of their website. The call to action is to make a (monthly) gift, though they get a high volume of one time gifts from it as well. To see if we could increase the conversion from the banner, we wanted to test communal language on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we weren't able to validate the test but we did see a 27% directional lift from the communal language. Additionally, this increased overall revenue by 33% at a 86% level of confidence. What is really interesting is that returning visitors had an even greater difference in conversion. The communal language increased donor conversion for returning visitors to the site by 49%! All of these results reinforce the idea that when we talk to the visitor as if they are a friend and even recognize and call-out the fact that we know and appreciate that they are fierce advocates for the cause, we increase their likelihood to give and ultimately convert. The conversion become less about the transactional nature of the gift and more about the impact and the donor.

An additional observation of the impact on another visitor segment in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge was running a matching gift campaign for two days as part of their holiday appeal. They had previously tested a sticky bar during a previous match that employed “communal” language (as opposed to transactional language) that increased both clickthrough rate and conversion rate during the match. They wanted to extend that language to their tribute donation pages, where people can give in honor of a specific journal author.
The original page positioned CaringBridge as the hero—it helped the author stay connected to family and friends. Then, it positioned the reader as the one who makes that happen. Instead, they decided to double down on language that addressed the prospective donor as the hero, removing CaringBridge entirely. First, they changed the headline to thank the donor for directly helping the author stay connected to their loved ones. Then, they reinforced that (and introduced CaringBridge) by adding exclusivity to the subheadline.
Finally, they applied the communal language theory to the call-to-action that mentioned the match. The original CTA offered a “dollar for dollar” match if the donor gave—which appeared to be transactional language. It also brought the brand “CaringBridge” back into the conversation. They removed the brand name from the CTA and provided a “pressure release valve” that put the power of whether to participate or not into the hands of the donor. Finally, they removed the “dollar for dollar” language and launched a test to see if their hypothesis was proven correct.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language did not appear to have a significant impact overall. However, when the team dug into the device types, they found that desktop visitors—which made up the majority of donations—showed a 20.2% increase in conversion rate. On top of this increase in conversion rate, desktop visitors showed a 29.6% increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Not only did more people give—they gave larger amounts!

This showed the potential impact of replacing transactional language with communal language. Next, the CaringBridge team wanted to re-test this language in a non-match scenario to see if it would have year-round impact.

experiments.title: How does a homepage takeover affect year end donations and revenue?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Sending direct homepage traffic a calendar year end giving page will increase year end donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, on December 30th and 31st, we tested redirecting 50% of the Oblates direct homepage traffic to the calendar year end donation page. The hypothesis was that most people who come to a page on that day will be interested in giving, or more likely to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test saw an 11% increase in donations, which resulted in 45 more gifts. We recommend including this in calendar year end tactics in the coming year.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 19.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 16.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 18.8% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the focusing on the fact that a content offer is free impacts donation conversion on the acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on the fact that an offer is free may decrease donation conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2021 - 04/12/2021
As Focus on the Family increased their acquisition efforts, complaints that the donation page was misleading increased as well. People expressed concern that the donation page language made it seem as though they had to donate to receive the offer. So we tested their control copy with a focus group and only 61% of the people who read the page said it was clear that the video series was free. Our next step was to include the word free when the video series was mentioned on the donation page, in the body copy and in the header. We tested that with a new focus group and 70% of them said it was clear that the video series was free. So it was time to test it in the market place to see if this new donation page language would increase or decrease donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The emphasis on free throughout the copy on this page decreased donor conversion by 21% at a directional level of confidence. This held true across all devices and demographics.

Our hypothesis is that the consistent mention of the series being free may have confused the reader around the purpose of the donation page. It may have also made it easier for them not to consider a donation. Or the control may have just made people feel like they needed to give.

Our next step is to test more straight forward language. This test included the word free in many places, but it never addressed the donation ask head on. We recommend testing language that is very clear in the purpose behind the ask such as "We want to be honest with you. You don't have to give to receive the video series. It's in your email inbox now. But it's only there because people have given to Focus on the Family so that you can have it. If you think this ministry is important to share with others, will you join our donors in reaching more people who really, truly need this video series?"

experiments.title: How a lower gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A lower gift array will raise donor conversion on an acquisition page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2021 - 05/27/2021
Focus on the Family had a variety of gift arrays on their acquisition donation pages. We wanted to see which array worked best for acquisition, especially since people new to Focus would be more likely to give if the ask was not too steep for a first time donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, most of the segments saw a directional or a valid lift in donor conversion with the lower gift array. And the lower gift array did not decrease conversion in any segments with large enough samples or any meaningful level of confidence. For instance mobile saw a 17% increase at an 88% level of confidence, while desktop saw only a 2% increase at a 13% level of confidence. All indications are that the lower gift array helps and it does not hurt any large segment.

Our hypothesis is that starting at $25 rather than $50 gives a mental ladder up to the $50 gift for the premium, rather than just starting at $50. So more people would give because a $25 gift would be easier donation for most of the page visitors to give. We expected this to be especially true to new visitors who were not as familiar with Focus on the Family and would be turned off at such a high initial donation ask. But the data suggest that primarily returning visitors saw this page and that they were also more likely to give if the array started off in a smaller dollar ask.

And, the best news is that this lower gift array did not lead to a decrease in revenue. There was a 2.3% increase in revenue per visitor at a 53% level of confidence. This tells us that with more people donating, the slight decrease in average gift (from $53 to $48) did no harm to the overall revenue. So we will be rolling this gift array out to all acquisition pages and recommend it is rolled out to Housefile donation pages as well.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 33.8% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 17.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 150.8% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 12.4% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 38.4% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 3.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 10.4% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a two-step donation form impacts donor conversion on mobile devices during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion:
Will a two-step donation form increase donor conversion on mobile devices during a high-urgency campaign?
The two-step form saw a directional lift of 22%! While we weren’t able to validate the results, we were able to see a positive increase in conversion rate! This positive directional lift is mostly due to the high volume of email traffic coming to the donation form. People’s motivation is slightly higher from this traffic source and the decrease in perceived friction to the giving experience helped to convert more of these people. We would like to test this again during non high-urgency campaigns. We would also like to test into the order of the information we are asking for from people. Take a look at other interesting data points from specific segments:
New visitors had a 19.4% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
Email visitors had a 42.3% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/26/2020
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we wanted to see if we could improve donor conversion on mobile devices. Mobile donor conversion has been something that has been difficult to impact in the past during high-urgency campaigns. So to continue to test into this, we developed a more radical giving experience by splitting the donation form into two steps. We hypothesized that it might be easier and a better user experience to break the form into separate steps rather than showing people the full form all at once. We hypothesized that we might be able to decrease the perceived cost of the number of form fields on the page with the two-step approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two-step form saw a directional lift of 22%! While we weren't able to validate the results, we were able to see a positive increase in conversion rate! This positive directional lift is mostly due to the high volume of email traffic coming to the donation form. People's motivation is slightly higher from this traffic source and the decrease in perceived friction to the giving experience helped to convert more of these people. We would like to test this again during non high-urgency campaigns. We would also like to test into the order of the information we are asking for from people. Take a look at other interesting data points from specific segments:
New visitors had a 19.4% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 42.3% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a person’s name to the confirmation page of a pledge impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the instant donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2020 - 11/16/2020
Live Action uses a pledge as an email acquisition opportunity. Through recent experiments with other organizations, we found that by personalizing the instant donation page that people are taken to after they sign a pledge can increase donor conversion. To test this with Live Action, we simply added the person’s first name to the headline of the confirmation/instant donation page. This data was passed through the URL from the form field on the pledge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren't able to validate this experiment, but we did see a directional 14% decrease in donor conversion. We saw this similar result across all channels, device types, and user types. Our hypothesis with seeing a decrease in donor conversion when using this personalization tactic is that instead of increasing motivation we increased friction on the page. Maybe the use of their first name was too invasive for the prospective donors.

We'd like to continue to test into this theory around personalization as we've seen it perform consistently well with other organization and with similar offers.

experiments.title: How the way the gift frequency section on the the primary donation page is presented impacts conversion during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the way the gift frequency section was presented and functioned for prospective donors during year-end will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2020 - 01/08/2021
Focus on the Family recently changed their donation platform. During that transition, the way the gift frequency section was presented looked and functioned differently than before. They heard from prospective donors that it was slightly confusing for them as they made a gift. To test into the best approach for that section on the page, we developed two different variations in hopes that we could increase clarity to the giving process. The first variation presented the two different gift frequency options as checked boxes – requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted to make through the checked boxes. The second variation presented the options as buttons. Again, requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted by selecting the specific button. This experiment was run during the last few weeks of their calendar year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the experiment didn't validate, we did see a directional lift in donor conversion from the checked boxes variation, and no difference in conversion from the button variation.

One of the big questions around this experiment was the impact the variations had on recurring giving . We hypothesized that the control variation may see an increase in recurring because of the lack of clarity it presented to donors about how they could make a one-time gift. In analyzing that data, we found that the control did see a significant increase in recurring donor conversion. In fact, the checked box variation decreased recurring transactions by 41% and the button variation decreased recurring transactions by 33% (88% valid). That said, the organization is seeing an increase in recurring gift cancellations right now. We hypothesize that this is because of the confusion that the control may have been causing in the kind of gift a donor was making.

Additionally, this experiment was run during a time of year when motivation of donors is at its highest. Because of this and the impact we saw around recurring gifts, we are retesting this approach this month so that we can have a larger data sample and more neutral motivation.
