experiments.title: How prequalifying stickybar audiences to a campaign impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that buyer language used on-site to promote a campaign for web visitors will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2021
The Fund for American Studies was running on-site stickybar and home page popup promotions for their Founders’ Day Campaign and wanted to experiment with whether “selling the click” or “selling the donation” would be most effective for their audience visiting the website.
We decided to call these treatments “shopper” language (to sell the click) and “buyer” language (to sell the post-click donation).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the deadline approaching for the campaign, we decided to take the version of the stickybar/popups that was showing a lead for the remaining few days.

We did observe some other statistics that are worth noting:
Ad clickthrough rate on the "buyer" version of the ad copy did see valid decrease during the valuation period — showing 39.1% less clicks (LoC: 99%).
 	However, because the fewer people clicking on the "buyer" version of the ad did convert (as shown above) at a higher rate, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate after they clicked the on-site ads.
 	Combining more donors (as noted previously), the "buyer" version of the ad produced a more generous donor to the campaign — showing an increase of +18.4% increase in the size of the average gift.
 	Because of that higher donor total and a higher average gift, the revenue for the "buyer" ads actually was a lift of +183% (LoC: 93.6%).

experiments.title: How simplifying “Dear Reader” ads on news articles impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the “Dear Reader” ad experience for newsroom visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2021 - 04/02/2021
As a part of on-site promotions of a recent open letter (petition), Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying their “Dear Reader” ads — which are ads that promote new offers and acquisition funnels at the end of news articles — by replicating the “simplified ad experience” that was so effective within their Facebook advertising campaign.
Would this simplified ad experience also improve results for their on-site visitors to their newsroom pages?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a nearly valid 89% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate of +390%. Because Americans for Prosperity was looking to maximize results on the limited time they had available to promote this offer, we decided to roll this out pretty quickly.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 832.5% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 390.8% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 1.0% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
It would appear that using the simplified ad experience (presenting a question, with a "Yes" or "No" button alongside it as a call to action is also as effective in on-site promotions through the "Dear Reader" ads as it was on the Facebook advertising campaign.

experiments.title: How breaking your fundraising goal down into a smaller ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting a specific goal of donors and targeted gift amount for campaign prospects will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/03/2021
During the Leadership Institute’s “2021 Kickoff” campaign, we wanted to experiment with asking donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific gift amount to see if it would increase the donor’s likelihood to give, or their generosity altogether.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 88.9% level of confidence, we observed a revenue increase of +52.1%.
This was driven by an increase in the donor conversion rate of +24% (LoC: 73%) and an average gift that was 22.6% higher than gifts made from the control segment.
Although this didn't fully validate, the results were directionally sound, which means that through the course of a longer campaign and more gifts, we could expect to see this hold true in 17.7 of 20 times that we ran this experiment.
When looking at what donors gave against what they were asked for, we noticed some interesting facts, which are:
40% of treatment gifts received gave more than what we asked for, representing 43% of the dollars raised in treatment experience.38% of the treatment gifts received gave exactly what we asked for, representing 37.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.22% of the treatment gifts received gave less than what we asked for, representing 19.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.
Additionally, when looking at what we would have asked for from donors who gave in the control experience, we saw that donors gave only 48.6% of what we would have asked them to give.
Meanwhile, donors who were in the treatment experience ended up giving 71.6% of what we would have asked them to give.
What does this mean? It means that we're 42% more likely to receive what we ask for when telling donors what we want them to give. Otherwise, when left to their own devices, donors are less likely to give at all, and when they do, we're less likely to raise the dollars that we would have otherwise asked them to give.
Finally, we decided to compare the gifts made to this campaign to the donor's most recent contribution (MRC) and highest previous contribution (HPC) values. The findings were incredible!
When comparing donors in the "control" experience against those in the "treatment" experience, we observed the following:
74% more donors decided to give "more than" their MRC when in the treatment experience.These donors produced 86.5% more revenue when in the treatment experience than donors in the control experience.3x as many donors decided to make their largest gift ever through the treatment experience. And,This represented a 257% increase in revenue from the treatment segment who decided to give a gift to this campaign that was higher than their HPC value when compared to those in the control segment.
All of this is to say: When a donor is left to their own decision on how much to give, they often choose to give less than they could — so we should tell them exactly what we want them to give in this sort of way.
It is through this clarity that donors know exactly what they can do to help you (the organization).
With this in mind, further experimentation may be required through a longer or larger campaign, but it stands to reason that we're likely to see consistent results on future campaigns with rolling out this treatment experience moving forward.
Where possible, increase the clarity in how donors can contribute to the overall goal and make change in the world happen. Don't make them come up with what they should give, but rather ask them to do something that is attainable and within reach for them to contribute to success, and overall more donors will say yes.

experiments.title: How value proposition in a handwritten note about the impact of a higher gift affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the direct impact of a higher gift will increase the average gift and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights needed to increase its average gift and in turn its revenue from acquisition, that in turn will decrease the net cost per donor. We were looking for ways to increase the average gift without damaging the number of donations. To encourage higher gifts, we devised concise copy that would show the impact and worth of their $50 gift. Then we used a handwritten note to set it apart and catch the reader’s eye. This was run on three donation pages to acquire the needed samples. All of them were acquisition offers for eBooks that were advertised on social. We had to run it for two month, longer than typically advised, to acquire the needed samples.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tactic worked primarily on desktop and for new visitors. It increased the revenue of new visitors by 55%, with an $11 increase in average gift. However, it decreased revenue for mobile visitors by 35%. Our hypothesis is that the required placement for mobile led to a less desirable user experience. It also slightly decreased revenue for returning visitors, but not at the same rate as it increase gifts for new visitors. The average gift was only $4 lower for returning visitors. It also decreased the number of gifts.

Our hypothesis is that returning visitors who see this more than once are less likely to give, because this message is no longer new, but is instead a turn off. But new visitors, who haven't seen this element before, are intrigued and inspired.

We will roll this treatment out to just new visitors and desktop experience. And we will continue to search for other ways to increase giving for returning and mobile, that also works for new visitors and desktop.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Tablet visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 55.0% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the homepage callout influences recurring donations

rows.name: One Button w/ New Value Prop

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using the homepage callout to emphasize recurring giving increase the number of recurring givers acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 01/26/2018
During the month of January, there was a concerted effort to acquire new monthly donors through the Friends of Focus on the Family program. Included in these efforts was radio programming that pushed listeners to go online to become a recurring donor. To take advantage of this visitor motivation, we wanted to see if focusing the callout on the homepage on recurring giving would help influence the visitors’ likelihood to become a recurring donors. Along with this experiment, we wanted to validate if a value proposition that spent more time laying out the benefits of ongoing support could further increase acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 2 weeks, this experiment did not produce a statistically valid result. When evaluating the conversions, we realized that the recurring gift offer may be the wrong offer for this section. While percentages may seem wildly different, the number of recurring gifts made, regardless of the treatment, was not high enough to get close to validating. We also saw that a donor is 6x more likely to click on the one-time gift option in the navigation than they are the recurring gift option. With this finding, it may make sense to repurpose that homepage callout section to focus on individual gifts in the short term. Concurrently, we can test new value propositions on the recurring gift donation page and, once validated, we can retesting the new value proposition on the homepage.

experiments.title: How visitor motivation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will lead to the most donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a thank you page and given the opportunity to make a gift.  The page was a two-column design in keeping with layout of the previous petition page.  The left side had value proposition language relating the media coverage to the Gosnell trail and the initial Planned Parenthood video embedded in the copy.
It was our hypothesis that we were assuming too much with the visitors. By placing the donation form at the top of the page, we were visually making an ask without first giving the visitors a reason to give. Additionally, the copy focused on what MRC had done in the past instead of what it was currently doing to combat the Planned Parenthood media outage.  As a result, we created a new treatment that utilized a single column design, removed the video from the page, and tweaked the copy to focus on Planned Parenthood instead of Goznell.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The single column landing page resulted in a 32% decrease in donor conversion. It is our hypothesis this decrease is less about the page layout and value proposition used and more about the motivation of the visitors. With a campaign topic as emotional as the Planned Parenthood scandal, the visitors were already highly motivated to give. By moving the donation form down on the page, we added additional friction to the process. The visitors did not need the additional reasons to give so when they didn’t see the donation form, the abandoned the process.
Most landing pages will see increased results when we place the value proposition copy before the donation form however, it is important to recognize the influence that external factors have on the donors’ motivation to give.

experiments.title: How adding an item instead of a free book increases donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Could we get more donations by providing a more appealing item as an incentive if they donate $25 or more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 06/15/2019
We wanted to know if providing an item such as a rosary would be perceived as more valuable than a book and increase donations. We tested this with a Facebook Ad and an Email that lead people to one of these donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: If we can provide an item that is perceived as more valuable we can increase the amount of donations. We can use this going forward for other Holidays like Christmas. Instead of offering a free book we can provide a certain item for $25 and increase donations.

experiments.title: How a YOU-centered call to action impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will framing the ask in the context of what you—the letter signer—just did, believe, and are looking to accomplish by taking this action impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/09/2020 - 10/23/2020
As a part of the SCOTUS 2020 confirmation hearings for (now) Justice Amy Coney Barrett, Americans for Prosperity had designed a digital activism opportunity for citizens to sign a letter and send it to their senators to tell them that they approved this nomination and were urging their elected officials to represent them by voting to confirm the nominee when it came time to officially vote.
The letter-signing page, once completed, would redirect signers to a donation page where they would be asked to make a financial gift to support the continued efforts to confirm the nominee.
In reading the “control” message, we felt that it was very organizationally-centered, and thought: What if we provided a more you-centered call to action, but because there was so much traffic available, we also decided to test a treatment that outlined the services that are available to donors to the organization, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 92% level of confidence, we were able to achieve a +50.9% increase in donor conversion rate for the 2018 variant (which was YOU-centered in it’s appeal to the letter-signer/prospective donor).

Although this didn’t fully reach the target 95% level of confidence, we decided to turn off the study and roll this treatment out to 100% of the traffic, considering there was only a week or so left in the confirmation hearing process, and therefore the promotional campaign.

The key learning here is that we should always focus on what action someone just took, and think about the reasons WHY they just took that action. If we channel this line of thinking, we can present to them a reasonable and compelling reason as to why they would make a donation after having completed that action.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically redesigned donation page post-survey increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2020 - 07/09/2020
We wanted to increase donor conversion and thought the current donation page copy was causing a disjointed experience for the donor. We changed several elements to create a radical redesign test. Instead of leading with a “Success!” confirmation message upon survey submission, we wanted to draw the reader in further through personal appeal and direct continuation of the conversation. We also updated the copy with enhanced urgency and added a direct ask with a clear outcome “for a tax-deductible gift of $50, we’ll be able to inform 200 people about what’s at stake” rather than a general ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we hoped implementing a radical redesign would help us validate results, we did not reach a valid level of confidence and saw a directional decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis for the decrease is that the new copy did not draw as much attention to evidentials as the control. But for future tests, we will look into testing each variable—headline, evidential points, specific ask, and tone—individually so we can identify where the loss is incurred.

experiments.title: How personalization and brand reduction affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalization and brand reduction affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2020 - 09/05/2020
Hillsdale College was running a pre-registration campaign to promote their new course “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They wanted to test two concepts on the donation page that followed the pre-registration. The first concept they wanted to test was personalization. Since the user signed up and gave their first name, they wanted to test a version of the donation page that congratulated the user by name for signing up. They were interested to see if this personalization would increase engagement on the page and humanize the “transaction”, resulting in an increased conversion rate. They also noticed that the approved copy had multiple repeated mentions of both Hillsdale and the full course name, “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They hypothesized that removing these repeat mentions, replacing with “our” and “this course” would both “get the brand out of the way” and increase the readability of the page, resulting in increased conversion. Since they expected a large influx of traffic for this pre-registration period, they launched a three-way test to see if they could find a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatments had positive increases on the control, but in different ways.
Treatment one, with the added personalization, actually had very little impact on conversion but resulted in a statistically valid 26% lift in revenue. This indicates that personalization may increase the force of the value proposition in the mind of the donor, inspiring them to give more generously.
Treatment two, which removed the brand and course mentions, produced two increases, neither statistically valid but both directionally interesting. First, it increased conversion rate by 10.6% with a 86.9% LoC. While this did not validate, it did show a trend that, with more traffic, might be proven favorable. It also produced a 19% lift in revenue, with a 91% LoC—again, not statistically significant, but revealing a learning to be re-tested in a future campaign.
Hillsdale's team decided to combine the two treatments in a future offer to see if the combination of the personalization (which had a valid lift to revenue) and the reduction of brand terminology (which had a near-valid lift to conversion) would work together to produce a valid lift overall.

experiments.title: How personalization and brand reinforcement affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page
The impact of using a donor’s name on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will seeing your name on a donation page after giving your name in a survey increase your liklihood to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2020 - 07/20/2020
As we continued to test the impact of using smart content on donation pages with DepositFix, we decided to take our high performing “Abortion in America Survey” Facebook campaign and test smart content on the donation page. Mainly, if someone was visiting the page already in our database, they would see their name on the page. Since they just gave their name on the survey, we thought that the continuity might increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for one month, we achieved a 35% increase in donor conversion. However, our revenue decreased by 11%. This was due to a smaller average gift on the variation (smart content) page. The control page received two donations that were $250 and multiple that were $100.
With the lower revenue, we may not move forward with smart content on these campaigns. Revenue and donor conversion are both important, but ultimately revenue is the prevailing concern.

experiments.title: How simplifying the reading level of an open letter impacted downstream revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the reading level of the open letter increase email signup, donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/19/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, American’s For Prosperity made available to concerned Americans an opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress as they planned a government response to provide citizens aid, as well as focused stimulus dollars to “restart the economy” again after the pandemic.
The open letter had become a wonderful engagement piece for the housefile, so they decided to open it up to prospective supporters to give them the opportunity to add their name alongside tens of thousands of other signers, as well.
Because the open letter was so popular, they decided to put a donation appeal behind it and test acquiring new donors, and did so very effectively.
But as time went on, the open letter signup (or email capture) rates were beginning to decline, so they wanted to experiment with increasing email signups.
The original letter was carefully crafted by government affair and policy experts and when graded in a readability tool, it was projected to be at a 15th grade reading level. If the 5th grade reading level they tried previously was determined to be “oversimplified,” they then thought to try a 12th grade reading level in an attempt to bring it closer to the original letter contents.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed that with a 92.7% level of confidence, that this “simplified readability” open letter decreased revenue by -32.5%.
This decrease in revenue was driven by two factors:
A directionally valid 24% decrease in donor conversion rate (LoC: 80%), and
A 13% lower average gift for the fewer number of donors that gave through the treatment experience.
It’s worth noting that we did observe a fully validated increase in email signup rate on the simplified open letter by +12.7% (LoC: 100%). This is particularly interesting, because although it did facilitate more email signups, that greater volume of people moving through to the donation page were NOT as motivated as those coming through the higher grade reading level in the control experience.
This experiment’s findings seem to validate the original hypothesis, which was that the increased reading speed works against the psychological approach and learnings gathered by adding the mini checkboxes—in that we found when we can slow someone down as they move through the funnel, that they become more likely to read and consume the content within the open letter. And when they do that, they are more likely to convert as a donor on the subsequent instant donation page because they fully understand and align with the contents designed to grow their motivation.

experiments.title: Does a personalized (smart content) donation page bring in more monthly donors from an email campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page containing personalized text perform better as part of an email donor campaign than a “static” donation page that was the same for each email recipient.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2020 - 04/04/2020
We have been looking at possibly switching to a donation platform that is built on Hubspot, allowing us to use smart content on the donation page (names, prior giving history, etc). Our goal being to reduce as much friction as possible.
The first test we launched of this new donor solution provided by DepositFix was to test our short monthly donor campaign. We were going to send 3 emails to our file seeking to activate 100 new monthly donors. For each of these emails, we split the list 50/50 and one set got a personalized email with a link to a static (non-smart) donation page in RaiseDonors, our current donor platform. The other half of the audience got a link to the smart donation page in DepositFix.
experiments.keyLearnings: In addition to a 50% higher donor conversion rate, the Deposit Fix page had an 8% higher average gift ($29.16 vs 26.97). While we didn’t quite hit 97% significance, the smart content page outperformed the static one in every email appeal of the campaign. We will continue testing this in future appeals.

experiments.title: How reducing the required steps affects perceived value and  donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the required email acquisition form from the process reduce friction enough to improve the number of new donors acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2015 - 12/02/2015
On Giving Tuesday, the Texas State Historical Association was offering their visitors a free eBook as a way for TSHA to give back to them. Immediately after requesting their free eBook, they were given the opportunity to make a gift. Historically, we had required visitors to fill out an email acquisition form before they would be sent the eBook even if we already had their email address.
We had the hypothesis that if we could remove this step, we would get more visitors to the donation page which would lead to an increased number of donors. So we created a treatment that would send an email with a link to the eBook when the visitor first got to the page and then immediately lead into the opportunity for membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned from this experiment that while the email acquisition form did add friction to the process, it also increased the perceived value of the eBook which made the donor conversion easier on the second page.  The treatment that just gave them the eBook resulted in a 41% decreased the number of donors acquired.

experiments.title: How a pre-selected value on the gift array impacted donor conversion rate and revenue from acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “most popular” pre-selected value on the gift array impact donor conversion rate or revenue for acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking into ways to increase their donor conversion rate and revenue from the investment they were making in acquiring donors through instant donation forms online. In reviewing the forms, we decided to try adding a “Most Popular” pre-selected option in the gift array to see how it would impact these key metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This treatment improved revenue by 94.4% (with a 92.3% level of confidence). Additionally, the donor conversion rate was showing an increase of 29.5% (although the level of confidence achieved was only 66%). Although these results didn’t officially validate, the lift in revenue is certainly directional and the decision was made to roll this out to gain the apparent benefits of the treatment option.

This increase in revenue was largely driven by an increase in the average gift amount of +55%.

Additionally, the number of $50 or greater sized gifts acquired through the treatment option was doubled in the treatment.

When donors are considering giving a gift during the instant donation page in your acquisition efforts, one of the decisions they have to make is “how much should I give?” This “Most Popular” treatment appears to decrease the friction in the process of making a donation because the donors now have a “starting point” provided to them as to make a decision about “how much” to give.

experiments.title: How replacing older donation page copy impacted donor conversion rate for a new offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Would reusing the donation page copy previously used to send a series of appeals work behind a new content offer for a related subject?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2019 - 12/13/2019
When launching a new content offer related to the impeachment of the president and how the process works, we decided to launch with to donation page treatments–one that had been used as the donation page for a short series of emails when the news was released, and another that had not yet been tested. This approach was used to mitigate risk with new donation page related to the topic, and since the housefile emails along with the control donation page had produced some nice results, we assumed we would be able to capitalize upon this page’s positioning of the fundraising appeal for new content downloaders.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page (which was previously unseen by the housefile) showed an aggregate increase in donor conversion rate of 25.1%, but did not validate (LoC: 85.5%) because we turned it off early.
Why? Because we actually saw no difference between the treatments for new visitors, but saw a decline in donor conversion rate of 34% (LoC: 92%) for returning visitors to the control page.
Our hypothesis is that donors that had previously seen the control page through the earlier series of appeals felt like they had already given a gift in response to that ask. For this reason, they decided not to give another gift to the page, but for new visitors–the treatment performed slightly better (whereas the control message had no difference).
For that reason, we decided to roll out the treatment copy as the new control, as it showed no negatives for any audience type, which will remove the risks associated with returning visitors feeling like they have already given to “this initiative,” and presenting them with a new ask instead.

experiments.title: How inserting a gift array on a primary donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will inserting a gift array on a primary donation page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2018 - 12/04/2018
Compassion International’s main donation page featured an open field with a $100 suggested gift, and a drop down to choose whether to make the gift one-time or monthly. They were curious to see what happened when the one-time ask was replaced with a range of giving options ($50 / $100 / 250) and when the monthly recurring gift ask was brought out of the dropdown and into the decision flow as a “binary checkbox”. They created a treatment that did both of these and launched and experiment to find a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The gift array, after several months, produced an 18.5% decrease in giving, although some of this may have been due to the placement of the array—instead of being in the same position, it was moved down below the main image. However, this coincided with a 385% lift in recurring donations. Further analysis must be done to determine if the losses from one-time giving are offset by the increases to monthly giving.

experiments.title: How moving Lourdes water to a one page donation increased donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to learn if a one step checkout process would help increase donations for Lourdes Water.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2019 - 10/27/2019
We get a lot of traffic to make a donation for Lourdes Water. It’s our most popular item, so we wanted to try to find a way people could make a donation easier.
experiments.keyLearnings: It increased donations and conversion rate, but the revenue stayed about the same. For our longer checkout process we have an area that asks them to make an additional donation. We found that this is why there was less donations, but similar amount in revenue. We are going to move forward using the one step process for the Lourdes Water, but continue to test different ways to increase the average gifts.

experiments.title: How moving Lourdes water to a one page donation increased donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to learn if a one step checkout process would help increase donations for Lourdes Water.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2019 - 10/27/2019
We get a lot of traffic to make a donation for Lourdes Water. It’s our most popular item, so we wanted to try to find a way people could make a donation easier.
experiments.keyLearnings: It increased donations and conversion rate, but the revenue stayed about the same. For our longer checkout process we have an area that asks them to make an additional donation. We found that this is why there was less donations, but similar amount in revenue. We are going to move forward using the one step process for the Lourdes Water, but continue to test different ways to increase the average gifts.

experiments.title: The impact of using Facebook’s video creation tool on an ad campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a 15sec video created with the Facebook video creation tool convert more people into donors on the ad than a photo?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2019 - 08/11/2019
We’ve had a successful campaign advertising a pledge that led to a monthly donation page on Facebook. In a previous experiment, we found that using an image of a newborn baby on this offer significantly increased donor conversion (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2321)   We decided to test that image against a new video using the video creation tool in Facebook. They hypothesis was that a 15sec video would get more people to convert than the image.
experiments.keyLearnings: 218% more people who clicked the video ad became monthly donors than who clicked the photo ad. This was despite the fact that the photo ad had  14% more impressions. We will begin using and testing videos in more of our campaigns to see the impact they have on conversion.

experiments.title: How presenting segmented offers based on a person’s engagement on parenting articles impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering people targeted premium offers increase donor conversion on parenting articles?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 03/26/2019
Focus on the Family has done significant testing within the articles on their website. From testing the kind of offer to the placement of the offers, they have optimized and continue to optimize the conversion opportunities on the articles pages for the significant volume of traffic. Most recently they learned that presenting people a premium/book at the end of the articles, that readers could receive by giving a gift of any amount, had the most significant impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this learning, they hypothesized that presenting people with a targeted offer based on their engagement on the Focus on the Family website might increase donor conversion. Focus identified the top premium offers based on what is most requested and the kind of content that people read and consume from their site and offered premiums that closely aligned with those topics. This approach was tested against offering people one single premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we’ve continued to monitor and dig into the results of this experiment, we have discovered an interesting finding that is misrepresenting the results and learnings. A significant portion of the traffic on the article pages are either first time visitors to the Focus website, or this is the first article they’ve consumed from Focus. This audience has not been receiving targeted content offers based on cookies. Because of this, 68% of the donations from the treatment results have been receiving the single content offer/premium.
After discovering this and learning what was happening, we have paused the current experiment and relaunching it to target that audience with targeted content offers based on the current article they are reading. This small nuance to the experiment is critical in knowing whether a targeted content offer based on the kind of content people consume helps increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a membership offer on an instant donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a membership donation ask outperform the generic donation traditionally used?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2019 - 02/10/2019
As part of the efforts to validate the potential for a membership program within Alliance Defending Freedom, we identified three of the highest donor-motivated traffic portions of the website. One of these areas was the donation page shown after a visitors signs the “Statement of Belief” found on the blog and on the homepage. For this page, we then created a treatment that introduced the concept of membership and asked for a gift to support the “broad and far-reaching” work being done.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach a statistical level of significance with this experiment. Directionally, the membership concept seemed to produce a 36% decrease in donations directly to the campaign but this is something that needs to be further validated with additional testing.

experiments.title: How making the donor a hero impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “donor as the hero” treatment increase donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2019 - 06/30/2019
Buckner International was launching a new prospect acquisition offer that included an instant donation opportunity. The initial version was focused on the impact of the organization and what their gift will accomplish. However, we had run an experiment on a similar offer in the past and found that a donation page that focuses on the potential donor and builds them up to be a hero had stronger conversion. With this in mind, we decided to test this concept on the new donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we discovered that the “Donor as the Hero” treatment showed a directional lift of 66%. Due to high costs of acquisition, we ended up turning off the advertising before this page could validate statistically but the directional lift would align with the results we saw on the 7 Scriptures offer. It is a key messaging tactic to keep in mind for future appeals.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2019 - 08/15/2019
Based upon the low traffic volume to the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page, we knew that we would need to try something radical in order to validate an experiment. With this in mind, we decided to launch a radical redesign of the page in conjunction with a similar experiment on Buckner’s main donation page. Both experiments tested similar elements:
We modified the form headers to be numbers steps instead of just labels.
We removed the gift array which we’ve seen help with organizations that have a large volume of mid-level and major donors
We increased the perceived security near the credit card form
We reiterated the gift size and impact near the donation button
experiments.keyLearnings:
While it did not statistically validate and it had a small number of conversions, we did see a directional lift with the radical redesigned donation page. What is surprising is that the experiment run on the main donation page that mirrored these changes saw a completely opposite result (41% decrease.)
When we analyzed both experiments, what we found was that the decrease on the main donation page was the result of a decrease in donors less than $100. The SOS donation page did not have this same issue (we actually saw an increase on this page.) The key difference is in the final subheadline on both donation pages. The SOS donation page makes an ask for $25 and the main donation page did not suggest any gift amount.
It is our hypothesis that removing the gift array from the main page increased the decision friction for the small-dollar donors. The SOS donation page did not experience the same impact since it had the $25 in the final call to action.

experiments.title: The impact of making a monthly donor appeal in a homepage hero image (Hint: 1,392% increased revenue)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which would raise more money, a hero image making a one-time donation appeal, or one that would appeal for new monthly donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2019
Every two weeks, we would change out our homepage hero image featuring a donation CTA as well as our Top Ad donation CTA. We would swap these out to match the language of the most current bi-monthly digital appeal. These hero images were almost always for a one-time donation page.  We hypothesized that, instead of making a one-time donation appeal, we should try acquiring new monthly donors through the hero image. We did not set up a strict AB test, but rather replaced the hero and top ad images with a new monthly appeal and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Control had a higher donor conversion rate, but much fewer clicks. The variation attracted far more visitors to click and, since the appeal was for a monthly appeal, rather than a one-time appeal, resulted in significant more revenue. We ran the test for 72 days. Here are the results:
Control: 327 clicks, 13 donations, $981 raised.
Variation: 1057 clicks, 24 donations, $1,220 raised in monthly revenue, $14,640 in yearly revenue.
The variation had a 227% increase in clicks,  84% increase in donations, and a 1,392% increase in revenue! So, while fewer visitors to the donation page were prepared to become monthly donors, the significant increase in clicks and revenue more than made up for the lower conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a personalized sticky bar on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the sticky bar on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2019 - 06/08/2019
CaringBridge had run experiments to show that inserting a “sticky bar” at the top of donation pages could increase conversion rate during one of their matching gift appeal. However, they had recently updated their site code to be able to personalize the messages on the sticky bar to match the person’s donation page that the bar was running on. They wanted to test the impact of this during a match appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The personalized version of the sticky bar increased conversion by 10.1%, though it did not reach full statistical validity because it only ran for two days of the match period. This is a good directional learning, though it is muddied by another result—when this test was re-run later in the second match, it decreased conversion by 9.7%, also not statistically valid.
Ultimately, this might be too small of a change to validate a consistent result. However, the sticky bar has continued to generate increases in conversions across the board, especially on mobile.

experiments.title: How simplifying the donation page increased the donor conversion rate on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the messaging on the donation page increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 06/25/2019
The Heritage Foundation was running an acquisition campaign to provide prospective donors with a free guide to the religious freedom debate. While scaling up spending to increase top line results, we needed to experiment with a way to increase the donor conversion rate.
The messaging on the control donation page seemed to take too long to get to the point. With an audience that is likely eager to get to their inbox to download their free guide, we felt that we needed to simplify the donation page and get right to the point more quickly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment only achieved an 86.6% level of confidence, the results were directional enough for us to agree to roll out the 31.8% increase in donor conversion rate that we had achieved over the last 14-days.
When it comes to an instant donation page behind an acquisition offer, it’s critically important to keep the donation page messaging as simple and straight forward as possible.
This will mitigate friction and increase the likelihood that an instant donation will be collected.

experiments.title: How the Freedom Quiz button text on the button promotion impacted petition click rates

rows.name: yes, i’ll sign the petition now
read the petition now - email capture last

experiments.researchQuestion: Which button text at the end of the quiz will generate the highest click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 04/05/2019
Americans for Prosperity launched a new quiz, which led to a petition signature page. At the end of the quiz, we wanted to test which button text would generate the most signatures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 88.5% level of confidence, the treatment text on the button increased petition button click rate by 77.9% over the control.
With our goal being to get as many people from the quiz to the petition (and subsequently the donation page), our learning here is that we should err on the side of “soft asks” on button text. The control message forces the quiz-taker to decide whether or not they want to sign the petition before they’ve ever seen it.
Because the email capture rate is so low across both segments, we wanted to end this experiment quickly to launch an email capture at the front of the quiz experience (instead of at the end of the quiz) to increase overall email capture rates.

experiments.title: How reducing navigation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing donation page navigation increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2016 - 09/08/2016
Americans for Prosperity Foundation was preparing to launch a website redesign. In the few weeks they had before launch, wanted to run a quick donation page experiment to see if they could lift conversion.
The current donation page had a large navigation menu at the top that gave the visitor plenty of opportunities to leave the page before making a gift. They knew that removing these would prevent “unsupervised thinking” and they hypothesized that it would increase conversion.
They used Adobe Target to create a treatment with this navigation removed, and launched an A/B test to measure the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with no navigation produced a 195.1% increase in conversion, although the results came just short of statistical validity. They ended the test before arriving at validity to launch the new site, although the test will be repeated with the new design.

experiments.title: How the reasons NOT to give affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: By giving reasons NOT to support DTS on Giving Tuesday, will we be able to drive incremental traffic to the donation page and which will lead to an increased number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2014 - 12/04/2014
Giving Tuesday has become a powerful fundraising response to the consumerism of Black Friday and Cyber Monday. But with so many nonprofits are soliciting gifts on the same day, it becomes more difficult to stand out in the crowd. DTS tested a contrarian messaging approach that would both intrigue their supporters and increase traffic to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test treatment was able to increase the click through rate by 528.3% but was not able to convert enough of those visitors to donors to be a statistically valid experiment.  With that said, the results would certainly indicate that this tactic may work in the future as long as we then focus our efforts on optimizing the landing page for the varying motivations of the visitors.

This same tactic of driving an increased number of visitors to the page will be employed in the future but more effort needs to be placed on developing a page that fully addresses the reason for the visit. Our hypothesis is that we did not spend enough time explaining the reasons NOT to give before leading into reasons TO give.

experiments.title: How active language in the call to action increases donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language on a donate button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2017 - 06/14/2017
Live Action wanted to increase donations on their main donation page. They tested the copy used on the “Donate” button in the navigation of liveaction.org. After finding that the copy “Defend the Preborn” had a 60% increase in clicks over the original “Donate” copy and the other treatment, “Support Live Action”, they tested which copy converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control was “Defend the Preborn” and treatment #1 was “Support Live Action”. “Support Live Action” had a 46.5% increase in donations over “Defend the Preborn.” So while “Defend the Preborn” was able to get more people to the donation page initially, more people gave a donation using the text “Support Live Action”. What this tells us is that even though people were more motivated to click on “Defend the Preborn”, the lack of clarity in that copy to where they would be taken resulted in less people converting to what the primary objective was on the page – to give a gift. By using more active language with greater clarity, we were able to increase traffic to the donation page and increase donations and revenue.

experiments.title: How the right premium affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What will be the impact of offering the Texas Almanac as a premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2015 - 10/26/2015
At the Texas State Historical Association, our previous tests had proven how popular the Texas Almanac was for their audience. In many campaigns, we had started using it as a premium for members who joined at the $50 or more level.
While looking through their website for optimization opportunities, we found that the primary donation page had not been modified for a while. We decided to use this page to create a test that would allow us to isolate the impact the Almanac has on overall donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By offering the Texas Almanac as the primary incentive on the page, we were able to increase the member acquisition by 135%.
Every premium has an associated cost that needs to be accounted for when determining its overall effectiveness.  In this situation, a 135% increase to the number of members acquired with an 11% increase to average gift is more than worth the cost associated with sending out the Almanac to each new member.

experiments.title: The importance of reinforcing the value proposition for sake of giving, even if we lose more emails.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which petition landing page would acquire more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2016 - 04/20/2016
As part of their moneybomb, SBA List created a petition to help drive more traffic to their donation page. Traditionally, the petition landing page has value proposition copy and the CTA to sign it. They wanted to test a treatment of the petition landing page that didn’t have the additional value proposition language and looked more like a letter to the people they created the petition for. Traffic for both of the petition landing pages was pushed through an email that had value proposition copy and a call to “sign the petition”. They A/B split test the petition landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ended up getting twice as many emails to it than the control. But once people got to the donation page, they weren’t motivated enough to make a donation. We want more donations, not more emails for this campaign.  Since this test is approaching validity, we can learn from this that the presence of the value proposition  on the control motivated people to not only sign the petition but to give a donation as well and increased donor conversion by 25.1%. We need to reinforce value proposition for the sake of giving, even if we lose more emails.

experiments.title: How copy on a donation page affects the force of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: By providing a clear and credible reason to give, we can increase both the average gift and conversion rate of the main donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2014 - 04/30/2014
John Cornyn has served as a U.S. Senator from Texas for more than 14 years and is the current Senate Minority Whip for the 113th Congress. As election season begins, a torrent of traffic comes to JohnCornyn.com. This traffic includes likely voters looking to confirm their affiliation, donors looking to support the Senator, and people seeking information about all of the candidates. The Senator Cornyn’s team wanted to make sure that friction and anxiety were minimized on their main donation page and that credibility and clarity were increased so that the most motivated potential donors would make a gift to support the Cornyn campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment delivered a 258.1% increase in donor conversion, with a statistically valid sample size. Team Cornyn learned a few things about their donor base through this test:
1. Experience matters. Even though the donor might be motivated to get to the donation page, they need to be reminded throughout the process that Senator Cornyn aligns with their core beliefs and is worthy of their donation. Donors to Senator Cornyn are thoughtful — they can’t be expected to just react to a donation opportunity, they must be persuaded along the way.
2. Design matters. Simply adding the copy to the page hurt results. Keeping continuity in the user experience throughout the entire donation process is crucial to a positive lift.

experiments.title: How reducing form functionality affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2015 - 08/05/2015
Jews for Jesus had been running a series of optimization experiments on their primary donation form in an effort to improve the overall revenue generated by the page. Our first attempt resulted in no significant improvement when we removed elements of friction from the form and added language to the top of the page highlighting the organization’s unique value proposition.
We decided to attempt another treatment that would modify the value proposition of the page while leaving in place the changes to the form. With this new treatment, we wanted to utilize a personal letter from David Brickner, the organization’s CEO. Analysis of the email program had shown that he was popular with the constituency so we hypothesized the a shorter appeal, signed by him, could lift the average gift on the page enough to impact revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we saw that the new treatment decreased the conversion rate on the page by 13.8%. The language that was used have already proven successful in a previous appeal so we analyzed what else could have been impacting conversion. Upon further reflection, we discovered that, in an effort to reduce the number of decisions made on the form, we may have inadvertently make it more difficult to complete.
One of the features of Jews for Jesus’s donation system is the ability for previous donors to log in to complete their gift without entering any additional information. We removed this feature to make room for quotes from credible sources and to remove the number of decisions a donor has to make. However, with this being the primary location existing donors come to make their gift, this caused additional friction for that key segment. This cause the number of gifts made to the form to decrease.
Future experiments will add this functionality back to the form and we will focus our efforts on purely refining the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of value proposition copy on the donation page increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2016 - 05/05/2016
Jews for Jesus sends out a monthly newsletter via email. The newsletter has articles and videos people click-through to read and watch, and they recently added an appeal to the newsletter. In efforts to improve donor conversion, they developed a treatment of the donation page for the newsletter appeal. The donation page that was the control didn’t have any value proposition language on it. For the treatment they added value proposition copy to the page to reinforce the message in the newsletter appeal and used large buttons for the gift array instead of radial buttons. They split traffic and tested the donation pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the addition of value proposition copy on the donation page saw a decrease in donations by 38%. While value proposition usually helps improve conversion, these results suggest that the presence of the value proposition copy may have actually created friction for this highly motivated audience. This audience was already motivated to give once they arrived to the donation page and didn’t need or want additional reasons why they should make a donation.

experiments.title: How two different value propositions for an eAppeal affected donor conversion.

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a Christ-follower

experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christ-follower in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel. They decided to test these two different value propositions in the eAppeals and donation pages to see which their audience was most motivated to give to.
Then, they split-tested this with their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this test didn’t validate, initial data tells us that people were more motivated to give to a tangible need and event than the general value proposition of the ministry. In fact, the treatment that was focused on the Israel outreach event saw a 47.3% increase in donations. This was our hypothesis, but because of the relevancy of the control, it was important to test our hypothesis.
We championed the treatment and sent it as the resend to non-opens with a new subject line.

experiments.title: How the landing page layout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which layout will produce the greatest number of donations for the year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2012 - 01/01/2013
As part of their year end fundraising campaign, Media Research Center wanted to find the best way to communicate their unique value proposition that would lead to increased giving. The standard donation page layout had always been a two column approach in order to get the donation form closer to the top of the page. We wanted to see if that was the best approach to communicate the case for support to the potential donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the donation form below the form, we allowed the value proposition communicated within the copy to better “sell” the gift. The single column layout was effectively making an ask before adequately explaining why the gift was needed.  The test did not have a high enough level of confidence to be proven valid so further testing would need to be done to fully validate these results.

experiments.title: How the popularity of 3rd party credibility can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which visual layout or 3rd party sources will produce the greatest conversion rate for the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 07/31/2015
For their primary donation page, the Media Research Center wanted to find what 3rd party sources lend the most credibility to their value proposition. Many conservative figures cite their research in their work but the different individuals have varying levels of popularity.
We created a test of the existing donation page and replaced a one public figure (Laura Ingraham) with another (Rush Limbaugh). The ordering was also changed up to bring the animated element of the page closer to the top. We wanted to see if this new arrangement would improve the conversion of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: By placing a more well-known figure, and his endorsement, closer to the donation form, we were able to reduce the anxiety of the donor enough to improve the conversion rate by 25%.

experiments.title: How the value communicated in a link can affect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does communicating the value of the gift in the link increase the subsequent donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2015 - 08/31/2015
Media Research Center was running a petition campaign to force the major news networks to spend more time covering the Planned Parenthood undercover videos. We had learned in previous tests that the signers of the petition were highly motivated individuals that did not need a lot of value proposition copy to be convinced to make a gift. As a result, we utilized a “quick donate” strategy in subsequent emails that let the donors select their donation amount in the email and skip directly to the donation page.
While this strategy was proving successful, we wanted to find a way to boost the average gift of the donors since the average gift was below what is normally seen. We had a hypothesis that adding value to the individuals links would inspire larger gifts. As a result, we created a new treatment that applied value proposition copy to the links as a method of communicating greater value to the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this test did not reach the 95% level of confidence necessary to be considered statistically significant, it was quickly approaching a valid result. While additional testing will be necessary to be certain, the increase in donations and revenue would indicate that the value proposition language boosted the overall campaign results.

experiments.title: How directly connecting the value proposition to the ask impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a stronger value proposition increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2017 - 05/23/2017
As Mother’s Day was approaching, Live Action sent a fundraising email asking people to give a gift in honor of a mom in their life. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase clicks from the email to the donation page by better connecting the Mother’s Day holiday to the value proposition of Live Action. We developed a treatment with this strengthened value proposition and tested it against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but the data we did collect shows that by strengthening the value proposition of the email, we were able to increase clicks by 22%. Here is a great takeaway from this experiment: When we present an ask, more people are willing to click through (and hopefully give) when we can connect the ask to a strong reason to give. If we don’t do this, the ask sounds and feels transactional. People give not just to give, but to have an even greater impact. A strong value proposition can clearly communicate this and result in more people being motivated to give.

experiments.title: How a direct donation ask vs a premium offer affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a direct donation ask effect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2016 - 03/09/2016
Online for Life created a petition called the End Abortion Declaration. People were pushed to the petition through Facebook advertising and banner ads. After someone would sign the petition, they were presented with a thank you and donation page. In efforts to increase donations, they set up a test. The control was presenting people with the opportunity to get a “Save the Baby Humans” in exchange for a gift to Online for Life. While this had worked well for previous offers, they wanted to test how it would perform against a direct donation ask with value proposition language that supported the End Abortion Declaration. They did an A/B split test to see which donation experience would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The sticker offer had a much higher conversion rate and resulted in the direct donation ask having a decrease in conversion rate by 57.4%.
This tells us that even though the direct donation landing page reinforced the value proposition of the declaration they just signed, they weren’t motivated enough to give to the cause. But when presented with something they could get – the sticker – they converted instantly and gave a donation. The perceived value of receiving the sticker also caused people to give at a much higher rate as represented by the average gift.
The sticker offer was deployed for the remainder of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock affects the value proposition and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which countdown clock would increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2015 - 01/20/2016
As part of their year-end campaign, Online for Life wanted to find a way to boost the conversion rate on their donation page.
In a previous test, we had learned that the addition of the “Every 26 seconds” clock on the donation page had increased donor conversion rate. Now, we wanted to test it against a treatment with a timer that counted down to the end of the year.
Our hypothesis was that people would be more motivated to give based on the urgency of the year-end. We ran a split A/B test. The control donation page had the”Every 26 seconds” clock and the treatment was the donation page with the year-end countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The year-end countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask and it also reinforced the value proposition of the donation page. This lead to the increase in conversion rate by 41.6%, but a decrease in average gift by 4.7%. However, overall, the addition of the year-end clock with messaging that supported it resulted in a 35% increase in revenue.
In the future, the countdown clock with financially focused messaging will be deployed towards the end of large year-end campaigns to increase revenue in the final days of giving.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition on a landing page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a revised value proposition on the landing page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2019 - 03/31/2019
DTS was running a value proposition test on their Hebrews landing page, that focused on how the course would benefit the reader rather than focusing on the teacher of the course or the course content itself. We also adjusted the headline of the landing page. The end donation page and following workflow remained the same in both the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had little to no impact on landing page signups, but something surprising happened in this test. The instant donor conversion rate on the following page from people who signed up on the treatment landing page increased by 35% (not statistically valid, but really close). This could be directly related to the fact that we've just told them the value they're going to get out of this course and the impact it will have on their walk with Christ, rather than focusing our value proposition on course content or course teacher.
Moving forward, further testing is necessary on the value proposition of the landing page and how that flows into the donation page content. Continuing to reiterate the eternal impact of the course is key to higher donor conversion.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the donation page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/16/2019
Back to the Bible sent out an email to introduce the new Bible teacher for their radio broadcast. The email linked to a donation page that continued with the biographical information of the new Bible teacher and had a donation form at the bottom of the page. We hypothesized that if we were linking people to a donation page a stronger connection between the Bible teacher and the donation ask needed to be made in an effort to motivate people to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Engagement with the email appeal was fairly healthy, but when it came to giving from this, the audience was pretty lethargic. With only a couple of donations coming in from this email appeal, the donation page treatment had no impact on conversion because overall motivation was so low. What we learned from this experiment is that it is a far stretch to go from introducing an audience to someone they never knew in a few short paragraphs to then asking them for a donation. This kind of email would have better been suited as a cultivation piece rather than an appeal.

experiments.title: How a long-form, donor-centered appeal decreased donor conversion rates on the primary donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a donor centered message help improve donor conversion rate (or revenue) on the primary donation form for Heritage?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2019 - 03/13/2019
We wanted to test a donor-centered message on the primary donation page, as every treatment historically has been focused on an organizationally centered series of value propositions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 94.7% level of confidence, we validated that the treatment decreased the donor conversion rate by -37.8%.
Although the donor conversion rate decreased in the treatment segment, because the average gift in the treatment jumped pretty substantially (+61.3%) compared to the control, the revenue difference between the segments was only -0.6% (with a 50.86% LoC).
Our hypothesis is that the copy length led to the decrease in donor conversion rate, but we were encouraged by the increase in average gift from the treatment, so we will now develop and launch a short-form version of a donor-centered message to experiment against the short-form control message.

experiments.title: How adding a photo to a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a photo to a landing page increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2019 - 03/13/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. After sending the first two emails out to a split landing page test, where they tested the presence of gift handles that explained what each suggested gift amount would be used for, they concluded that adding just one line of copy around the gift handles did not increase likelihood of giving.
The Hillsdale team wondered about including a photo of some of the students who received Constitutions onto the landing page. Rather than telling the donor about what they were doing, they could show them with a photo. Would adding a photo of schoolchildren receiving the Constitution increase donations? They hypothesized that adding the photo would show proof, causing an increase in trust, and would increase emotional appeal. They hypothesized that each of these would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 15% lift from the treatment with the photo. Because this experiment only ran for one day, the experiment results shouldn’t be rolled out to the rest of the donation pages. However, this result was enough for the Hillsdale team to roll out to this landing page for the duration of the campaign.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement on the donation page increases mobile conversion during a match
experiments.researchQuestion: How will visual reinforcement affect donor conversion on the donation page during a short-term matching gift appeal?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/20/2019
CaringBridge was running a Valentine’s Day fundraising campaign and had a $25,000 matching gift. They knew that most of their donations on these short, high-urgency appeals came through a few donation funnels that landed on the same page. While previous tests had focused on driving traffic, they wanted to see if they could optimize conversion by driving home the match language on the two days that the gift was applied. They created two versions of a sticky top banner for the donation page—one that emphasized the match for “your” gift, and another that emphasized the match for “all donations”. They launched a three-way experiment split between mobile and desktop to see how it affected conversions on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: The personal match language expressed on the sticky bar increased conversion by 16.5%. Though this only reached a 90.1% level of confidence (and is not valid) it was close enough  (especially when combined with the desktop results) to show that this visual reinforcement is a powerful tool to provide extra motivation through the donation process—even if it is only one step.

experiments.title: How social validity impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a quote from a trusted and famed source provide credibility to make others want to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2019 - 02/10/2019
The Leadership Institute has helped many political leaders. To prove their credibility, they wanted to see if including a quote from Vice President Mike Pence would increase conversion on their landing page. They tried a control page without the quote and image of Pence against two treatments: one with the quote and image along the bottom of the copy, and one with the quote and image, right-aligned in the copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the experiment itself did not validate, the combinations of treatments 1 and 2 conclude that the use of a quote from someone favorable and familiar will increase donations. The Leadership Institute should place the validity quote of Vice President Mike Pence on the rest of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a free offer-focused value prop convert more donors than a mission-focused value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2018 - 11/05/2018
Hoover Institution has implemented a one-click sign up for Defining Ideas newsletter in their offer chaser emails. Essentially, it takes them right to a donation page instead of requiring them to fill out a landing page before getting to the donation page. They wanted to increase donor conversion on this page, and hypothesized that changing the value proposition on the page to a more resource-focused value prop instead of mission-focused value prop would fulfill this.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn't validate, but saw a directional lift of 120% in donations with the revised value proposition on the page. This version of the value proposition focused on the immediate value of current and future free resources that Hoover would be able to provide because of their gift, rather than focusing on the greater mission of Hoover. This concept should be tested again on future offer donation pages to fully validate the learnings.

experiments.title: How adding additional giving options copy to the primary donation page impacts conversion

rows.name: Image with Additional Giving Options Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding the additional giving options on the donation page impact donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/04/2018
