Boys Town deployed a new primary donation page that when tested against their original donation page, increased donor conversion by 20%. At the initial launch of the new donation page, they had removed the additional giving options. This feature was not part of the new donation page, but was an important element to the Boys Town donor experience. After finding that the new donation page increased donor conversion, they wanted to see what the impact would be if they added the additional giving options back into the donation page. To do this, a line of copy was added just below the hero image. We tested this against the current winning version that only had the hero image.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the experiment didn’t quite validate, we did find that the addition of the copy for other giving options didn’t negatively impact donor conversion. Even better, the additional copy actually increased donor conversion. Knowing this, Boys Town can now keep the additional giving options on the primary donation page without decreasing donor conversion, and at the same time give people who are looking for other ways to give to Boys Town the opportunity to do so.

experiments.title: The impact of moving the donation ask earlier in the funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donation ask earlier in the funnel increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2018 - 06/29/2018
The blog for Alliance Defending Freedom get significant traffic on a daily basis. Over the past several months, we had been able to test into a series of new offers that had lifted the email acquisition rate from less than 1% to 4% – 6%. This meant several hundred new emails were coming on to the file on a weekly basis and each of these subscribers were shown a donation page once they submitted their email. This meant a certain percentage of them would convert to donors instantly.
ADF had recently developed a new embeddable donation form that we wanted to test out on the blog. Historical tests had found that, when linking to a donation form, the blog did not serve as a significant source for donations. We wanted to determine if moving the ability to donate directly on to the blog would allow us to reduce the friction associated with the donation funnel and, thereby, increase donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Upon first glance, the embedded donation form appeared to have a 60% decrease to overall conversion. However, when digging deeper into the numbers, we discovered that while there was a 60% decrease between the people that saw the treatment compared to the control, the actual conversions that came from the email acquisition offer compared to the donation offer told a different story. If we looked at just those conversions, the direct donation ask had a 264% increase (at 91% level of confidence).
This variance is the result of the high converting traffic that occurs during the fiscal year end period. Essentially, subscribers would read a blog post and they later got an email asking them to make a donation (which was unrelated to their actions on the blog post). However, since they saw the original blog post, their donation was then attributed to the experiment.
What we can take away from this experiment is that we always need to dig deeper into the top level results reported by the various systems we use. We also now know that we need to re-test this concept once the fiscal year has passed.

experiments.title: How an emotional value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the more emotional value proposition generate more donations than the more tactically-focused value proposition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2018 - 03/28/2018
In a recent experiment, Heritage Action for America had found a new value proposition that had appealed to Heritage Foundation donors in the serial ask found on The Foundation’s donation page. This experiment had nearly doubled the number of donors that were now giving to Heritage Action via the serial ask.
With the success of this experiment, we wanted to see if it would have the same impact on the main Heritage Action donation page. When comparing it to the copy found on that page, the new treatment relied far less on the tactical elements of Heritage Action and focused more on the emotional reasons to give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly two months, we found that the new value proposition was able to increase giving by 69%. Now, it should be noted that there was a relatively low number of visitors and conversions compared; we generally want to see more visitors before turning off a test. However, with a long running test, we have to start being concerned about historical distortion and given the low traffic volume sent to the Heritage Action main donation page, it wasn’t likely that we would see a significant boost in conversion or traffic.
With that said, this value proposition should be further validated with additional experiments in areas that will experience greater volumes of traffic like email campaigns.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition on the acquisition and donation page impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a value proposition specific to the content offer help motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2018
Care Net developed a Lenten Devotional content offer and used Facebook to push people to their site to get the content offer. After people signed up for the content offer, they were then presented with an instant donation ask. This instant donation page used a value proposition that was Care Net focused. We hypothesized that having a donation page specific to the actual content offer might increase donor conversion. We created a treatment where the value proposition was specific to the Lenten Devotional and tested this against the control
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did see a significant directional lift with the treatment of 142%! This directional lift tells us that when we align our messaging with the value that people just experienced, we are able to motivate more people to take action and give.

experiments.title: How reducing friction in a shopping cart process affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing friction by removing the child biography page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/18/2018
Compassion International had sought to identify gaps in their customer funnel through a deep-dive data analysis. They learned that the most direct path to sponsorship (with the fewest steps in between) had the highest conversion rate.
In looking at their child sponsorship funnel, they wondered if the “child biography” section, where a user was redirected after clicking the “choose me” button beneath a child was extraneous. If the user was actively selecting “choose me”, did they really want to just add the child directly to their cart, or was the biography an extra, necessary step in the consideration process?
They decided to better understand the consideration process through an A/B test. Half the users would not see the child biography page and would go straight to the cart. The other half would see the biography page as normal. They would look at conversions to understand the importance of the interstitial biography page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the child biography page led to a 26.8% increase in conversion, and also generated a lot of confusion among users who wanted more information on their child. Not only did removing this page affect conversion, it removed the user’s ability to return to that page, which created a negative user experience loop.
The decrease in conversion (though it didn’t achieve 95% significance due to stopping the test) and the feedback from users shows that while a direct path to checkout is ideal, the child biography page is an integral part of the consideration and conversion process.
This illustrates the principle that not all friction is bad friction. Sometimes, an extra step is helpful—and even essential—to the donation process.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition on a donation page can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would acquire more donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 06/20/2017
The treatment value proposition that was focused on the spiritual impact of a person’s gift increased overall revenue by 50%! There were not only more people who were motivated to give, but these people were motivated to give more. People were willing to invest in a kingdom-focused mission more generously.
While people cared about the event being successful and effective, they responded more when they knew their investment would have a larger impact – an eternal one.
This experiment didn’t validate, but it was leaning towards being valid and was rolled out for the rest of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency campaign leading up to their large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. On the donation page for the campaign, the value proposition and call-to-action was centered on making the event a success. It had more of a logistics angle to it. Because the event was an evangelism outreach, we hypothesized that people might be more motivated to give if the value proposition was focused on the spiritual impact. We developed a treatment and split the traffic coming to the donation page.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition in the body copy and CTA affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/26/2016
For Harvest Ministries mid-month appeal they wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on the landing page. The control and the treatment had the same value proposition, but the treatment had subtle additions to the copy of the headline, body and CTA to stregthen the value proposition of the page. They ran a split test to see which landing page would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment landing page had an increase in donations by 22.8%. The reinforcement of the value proposition throughout the treatment landing page strengthened the message and ask, resulting in more people giving a donation.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer in an appeal affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the Tell Someone course offer receive more donations than the Tell Someone book offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/22/2016
Normally, for Harvest’s mid-month appeals, they offer a resource (normally a book or DVD) to people if they give a gift to the ministry.
For Harvest’s August mid-month appeal they ran a test to see if they could acquire more donations through offering a book (the Tell Someone book) or through a free online course (the Tell Someone course). For the book offer, people were taken directly to a donation page where they could get the book and give their donation. For the course offer, people had to first sign up for the course and then be asked for a donation. Using 40% of the email file, they A/B split tested this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results didn’t validate, the initial data pointed to validity and the treatment (the Tell Someone course) had an increase in opens, clicks, donations (25%) and revenue. Using this data, they then sent the remaining 60% of the email file the Tell Someone course offer.
Even though the test didn’t validate, deciding to send the remaining file the Tell Someone course offer was not only the best decision from what we were seeing with initial donations and revenue, but the long-term benefit and value of these donors is so much greater with the course than it would be with a more transactional offer like the book. The course gives people the opportunity to engage with Harvest on an ongoing basis at a deeper level. And at the same time, it allowed them to instantly support the ministry. By offering people something out of the ordinary that had a higher perceived value, people were much more motivated to give through the course offer than the book.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in email copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will copy with a clearer call-to-action increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
We devised a treatment that moved some of that copy into the email, incentivizing the click by giving them a timely call-to-action to find out how to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
This treatment produced a 58.7% decrease in clickthrough rate, but we wanted to measure conversion on the following donation page to see if those that clicked had a higher motivation to complete the intended transaction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The clearer call-to-action in the email did produce a 55.1% lift in donor conversion. However, because the email drove much less traffic, the control won in terms of overall revenue.

experiments.title: How the friction added with website navigation affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding the standard website header and navigation to the landing page ease the concerns of visitors about the cost of the course while not affecting or increasing conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2015 - 03/17/2015
Hillsdale College offers a free, online training series named Constitution 101.  After initially enrolling, participants are sent to a contextualized donation opportunity where we ask for a gift to help support the efforts of Hillsdale.
There had been complaints recently that the contextualized donation page gave the impression that the course was not free.  We wanted to alleviate those concerns while not reducing the revenue or conversion rate of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of navigation decreased conversion rate by 17%. Whenever we offer donors the opportunity to explore more areas of the website, we are increasing the likelihood that they will.  While we were trying to decrease anxiety, we inadvertently increased the friction associated with making the gift.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity and specificity of the value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2015 - 12/04/2015
Hillsdale College launched a popular new course studying the life of Christian apologist C.S. Lewis. Donor conversion was low in comparison to other courses, so they investigated the copy on the donation page. The value proposition presented described Hillsdale’s online courses in general. They knew that this course attracted a lot of people who might not be familiar with the Hillsdale brand so they drafted a second copy treatment to explain why they teach the course, and how it is free. Then they launched an A/B test to see which copy treatment performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that specificity does increase the force of the value proposition, which in turn increased donor conversion rate. This may not be the case with all of Hillsdale’s courses, but when attracting a new audience, we may need to be more educational in why the courses are presented, and how they are funded.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:
1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.
2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.
3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: How the power of match language affects conversion

rows.name: "Double My Impact" Call to Action Copy
Action Oriented Call to Action Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that because of the urgent message and the clear impact of the campaign, more donors would respond to a call-to-action that showed the impact of their money.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2013 - 06/06/2013
Americans For Prosperity ran a one-day fundraising campaign that had a matching gift it — a generous donor had offered to double every gift. These are common in fundraising, and are usually a boost to any campaign — but we wondered how much the match actually incentivized the gift. Would more people give if they knew their gift was being matched, or would more people give to support the outcome they wanted?
We devised a test to see which treatment would convert more visitors to donors: match language or impact language?
experiments.keyLearnings: The new call-to-action decreased donor conversion 26.6%, showing that “doubling the gift” is a powerful motivational incentive. This led to a focused effort to secure more matching gifts for online campaigns and continuously promoting the match throughout the fundraising initiative.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Membership campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will result in the greatest lift to donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 06/30/2017
Neither of the treatments reached statistical validity. After further analysis, a big takeaway from this experiment can be found in the experiment setup itself. With a radical redesign, the goal is to test a variety of significant factors in order to achieve a bigger lift. However, when we do that, we are unable to identify the specific element that caused the lift.
With this test, we tested multiple factors but they were relatively minor to the conversion process. By testing the headline, background, and a button, there was no cohesive hypothesis that could be isolated and learned from in the future.
For the future, we need to focus on isolating individual factors on a page or making significant modifications to the elements of a landing page that we know influence conversion (copy, donation form, etc).
experiments.keyLearnings: During the Heritage membership campaign, we wanted to test out a radical version of the existing Join Heritage donation page. The new version utilized the size of the Heritage member base as a way to increase credibility of the headline. It also removed the majority of the design elements to try to make the content better flow together. A third version was prepared that also added a “Donate” button to the top that would allow people to jump straight to the form.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party evidential quotes impact conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd-party evidential quotes improve donor conversion on the primary donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 01/03/2018
In experiments that were run several years back, we found that added 3rd-party quotes from well-known figures would boost conversion rate in fundraising campaigns. However, over the years, this is something that had fallen off of the primary donation page. With Heritage making the news headlines and receiving accolades from significant figures, we decided to re-test this strategy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two treatments. As a result, we have shut off the experiment and continue forward with the control version.

experiments.title: How the specifics of a plan influenced conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding specifics about a strategic plan increase likelihood to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2017 - 11/25/2017
During Heritage’s Annual Member Survey, after a subscriber had filled out their survey, we presented them with a donation page. On this page, when making the solicitation, we were talking about the national budget blueprint that Heritage had created. At the end of the page, the call-to-action was to get the plan in as many lawmaker’s hands as possible.
However, we wondered if adding more specifics about the plan would help sell the importance of it. We created at treatment that did just that.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not reach a statistical level of validity to make any claims about whether the increased details helped or hurt the results. It is something that would need to be tested more in depth at a later date.

experiments.title: How adding images to a donation page can impact conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding images to the donation page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/08/2017
On the primary donation page for NBCF’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase donations by adding images to the page. The control donation page just had copy and the treatment they developed had three images added to the top page. They split the traffic coming to the page and tested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the images on the donation page had a 30% increase in donations. This test didn’t reach validation, but the initial data shows that the addition of images to a donation page motivates more people to give.
For an organization like NBCF, images can be very powerful. The added images helped to reinforce the value proposition message and visually showed people what and who their donation would be going towards.

experiments.title: How presenting a relevant premium offer on a page with high volumes of traffic can impact donor conversion.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding a donation appeal impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 09/05/2017
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate get significant traffic to their Daily Prayers webpage. People are coming to this webpage from a daily email. Knowing the significant amount of traffic the page gets, we hypothesized we might be able to convert some of these web visitors into donors. We tested adding a donation opportunity on the page with a relevant premium offer. We split the traffic and tested the two variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the results weren’t statistically valid (83.5%), we were able to increase donor conversion by 23.2%. What this shows us is that we can convert high volumes highly engaged traffic into donors when we present them with a donation opportunity with a relevant premium offer.

experiments.title: How year-end specific copy affects main donation page conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does year-end specific copy increase donations on a main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2017 - 01/02/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running a test on their main donation page for year-end. They tested year-end specific copy against their year-round main donation page. They also removed an image and made the headline bigger.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment did not validate, we saw a trend upwards with the year-end specific treatment, with a 12% increase in donations and a significantly higher average gift with the treatment copy. This is likely due to the urgency in the copy along with an automatically more urgent page due to the timing of the test (highest fundraising time of the year).

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn't significantly impact conversion - positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.

experiments.title: How removing elements of friction and strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2017 - 09/26/2017
The primary donation page for FamilyLife used a large image at the top of it with their traditional value proposition copy on it. They recently developed their value proposition and wanted to test a radical redesign of their primary donation page that tested this copy and removed other elements of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign increased donor conversion by 14.3%. By removing elements of friction on the page and strengthening their value proposition, we were able to not only increase the number of people giving a gift, but we were able to also motivate them to give more generously resulting in a 94.4% increase (77% valid) in revenue as well because the average gift increased.

experiments.title: How removing a banner in an email appeal impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the banner from the email copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2017 - 12/20/2017
For a FamilyLife email appeal during their year-end giving campaign, they inserted a banner in the body of the copy that served as a call-to-action. This banner then would take people to the donation page. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion if we removed the banner and used text. We split the email file and tested this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing the banner from the email, we decreased clicks by 46%. While this metric is important, we ultimately care about donor conversion. Even though there was a 46% decrease in clicks from the email that didn’t have a banner we were able to increase donor conversion by 90%! The donor conversion metric didn’t quite validate (89% valid), but it is leaning towards validity and is at a point that we feel confident enough to draw learnings from.
This tells us that even though we got more people to click through from the control email with the banner, those people were not as highly motivated donors as the people who clicked the text and raw link. The visual piece in the email created an easy click-through point for people, but those people weren’t ready to donate once they got to the donation page. The banner was also a visual piece of friction that possibly prevented people from reading the whole value proposition. By removing the banner, more people read the copy and we were able to fully communicate the value proposition and motivate people to give once they got to the call-to-action at the bottom of the email.
This test also shows why it is important to not base our testing decisions off of up-stream metrics when we can. If we would have done that, we would have championed the version that increased clicks but decreased donations.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused banner increase donations more than a match-focused banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that focused more on the value a gift provided would increase donations more than a match-focused banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, but the treatment banner produced a 21% decrease in donations. Because there were multiple factors tested in the banner, it’s hard to say exactly which one produced a decrease, but the removal of matching gift language and a less urgent color on the banner likely contributed to the decrease, because the matching gift language proved to be a motivating factor for FL donors in other testing.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the querystring).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generousity.

experiments.title: How the location of a success indicator affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design treatment will produce the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 07/01/2015
The donation page is the most critical step in online fundraising. Any improvement in the conversion rate on the page, even minor, will lead to increased revenue. During Dallas Theological Seminary’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to see if the placement of the campaign progress meter would affect the conversion rate on the form.
Our goal was to gain insight into the donor’s thought sequence but determining where an element that shows process towards a goal should be placed, right at the top of the form or after the impact of a donation has been stated?
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was able to statistically validate but given the short timeframe and the relatively low sample size, we would want to run this experiment again to be certain of these results.  The increased motivation of donors towards the end of a fiscal year end campaign may have impacted the overall performance of the test.

experiments.title: How message tone of an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does messaging tone affect donation response rate in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2015 - 01/22/2015
As  part of their new monthly email appeal strategy, Hillsdale College wanted to learn which messaging tone produced the best response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment utilized two distinct emails and two distinct landing pages. In the experiment, the softer tone email generated 257% increase in click through rate.  This increase in traffic illustrates a concept that we have observed in other experiments.  By reducing the "sharpness" of the ask in the email, we can increase the volume of traffic to the landing page, however the visitors will be less motivated causing the on-page conversion rate to be lower than the Control.   However, if the increase in traffic is significant enough, the net result will be more revenue.
The softer tone treatment delivered a response rate 85% higher than the original control with a 40% higher average gift.
This experiment is a great example of the importance of analyzing the entire conversion pathway, from email delivery to successful gift, when determining a winner.

experiments.title: How the right value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 08/05/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to increase donor conversion rate on their main donation page. Two previous tests had failed to do so by adding value proposition language and changing the open gift field to pre-selected buttons.
The question was raised — what if the first value proposition test had the right language, but the wrong presentation? DTS crafted a new version of the value proposition that started with a fundamental truth that the audience could agree with: “The world needs Biblical truth now more than ever.” This copy also included a quote from Chuck Swindoll that was inline with the text, as opposed to offset in the previous test.
Finally, DTS added a subheadline and strengthened and bolded the call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new landing page produced a 186.5% increase in donor conversion. This told us that presenting the right value proposition in the right way has a massive effect on donor conversion. The results of this test inspired many more tests across different donation pages and acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing copy for highly motivated donors affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing donation page copy increase conversion for highly motivated donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2015 - 08/07/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary sent a survey to their donors and prospects to better understand what they valued most about the organization. The survey was immediately followed by a donation ask. DTS hypothesized that after completing the survey, donors wouldn’t need as much copy to be convinced to make a gift. So they developed a treatment landing page that removed much of the copy — which to a highly motivated donor, could pose as friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page with reduced copy increased conversion by 134.3%. This means that the survey did a sufficient job of motivating the donors — all DTS had to do was ask.

experiments.title: How the wrong value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will collect the highest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2015 - 06/22/2015
The primary donation form for Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) had remained consistent for the last several years. It was a well-designed form but it made the assumption that those donors that make it to the page are already convinced to give their gift. This was evident from the lack of any copy or significant calls to action at the top of the form.
In an effort to increase the perceived value of the donation to potential donors, we decided to create a new treatment. This new page would utilize the same donation form but would add copy to the top that discussed one of the unique value propositions of DTS. It also included quotes from well known figures in the Christian community supporting the work of DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation form treatment resulted in a 28.1% decrease in donor conversion. This would indicate that the value proposition communicated on the page did not appeal to the potential donors. Additionally, by adding the wrong copy and pushing the donation form further down on the page, we introduced friction into the donation process. These two factors combined to reduce the donors and revenue to the page.

experiments.title: How additional premium-focused value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of premium focused value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2016 - 09/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had found a popular offer in their free Gospel of John course. After the user signed up, they were given the opportunity to make a gift.In exchange for a gift of $100 or more they were offered a commentary as the course companion book for the Gospel of John course. DTS decided to try to see if they could increase conversion rate by integrating review content for the commentary into the donation page. They pulled some reviews from a popular book review site onto the page, showed five stars, and gave a positive review for the commentary, hoping that this would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 47% decrease in conversion, although these results were not statistically valid due to low traffic to the page. However, the treatment also produced an increase in average gift, which may suggested this motivated a certain segment of people who enrolled in the course to increase their average gift to receive the commentary. However, this additional value proposition appeared to distract from the main value proposition, which converted more registered users into donors. Wally learning to not produce a positive impact on this donation page further testing for how the book is offered throughout the course might produce a lift.

experiments.title: How refreshing imagery affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an image more closely tied to the mission increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2015 - 11/12/2015
CaringBridge wanted to increase conversion on their tribute donation pages by adding imagery that resonated with their mission of connecting people during life’s most difficult times. They tested an image of clasped hands to see if it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This was a fascinating test, as a simple image change produced a significant result. This test inspired a new test to see if simply changing the image randomly (which can produce novelty bias) can produce a similar (or greater) lift.

experiments.title: How personalization in donation page headline copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personalized, donor-centric headline outperform a clear, direct headline?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 04/11/2016
Previous CaringBridge tests had shown that in the tribute funnel (where visitors give in honor of their friend of loved one), personalization improved the likelihood of donor conversion. However, previous experiments had also shown that a clear, direct headline (in this case “Give to CaringBridge”) resulted in the highest donor conversion.
The team wanted to test whether a personalized, donor-centric call to action, though longer and less direct, would improve the clarity of the value proposition, present a stronger incentive, and result in higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment showed that visitors’ likelihood to convert increased significantly when they were reminded of the impact of their gift. With CaringBridge copy, there has always been a balance between clarity and personalization. Through this tribute funnel, where the gift is closely tied to a personal relationship, personalization wins.

experiments.title: How organization-centric language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing organizational-centric language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 05/05/2016
CaringBridge had performed several successful experiments to personalize the copy on their “tribute” donation pages. But user feedback had shown one interesting organizational fact: most people didn’t know CaringBridge was a nonprofit organization. This had produced confusion before, so they developed a single sentence of copy to add to the page in pursuit of clarity:
“Nearly 90 percent of our funding comes from people like you who have experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand.”
This copy was never tested, and we developed a hypothesis that it might be what we call “organizational-centric” language. This type of language is not inherently bad, but it might clash with the tribute page, which is highly personal.
We developed a treatment that eliminated this sentence and set out to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (without the sentence) increased conversion by 6.6% — not an overwhelming amount, but enough to show that taking the donor’s attention away from the person impacted by the gift reduced the likelihood that they would give.
This doesn’t solve the marketing problem that many people don’t know CaringBridge is a nonprofit, but it tells us that the donation page is not the place to communicate that.

experiments.title: How a direct ask in an exit intent affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct ask increase traffic or donor conversion rate for an exit intent banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2017 - 06/01/2017
Toward the end of a matching gift campaign, Buckner International wanted to test out an exit intent on their main website promoting the challenge. Since this was the first time this approach had been used, we wanted to find out which approach (a “learn more” or a direct ask) would have the greatest impact on a visitor’s likelihood to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the direct ask approach reduced to the number of visitors to the campaign page by 50%. While it did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would strive for, it was quickly approaching validity before the campaign ended. However, the goal was not necessarily to get clicks, it was to get donations.
When we looked at the number of actual donations, there was not a statistically significant difference between the two treatments. We were able to drive more traffic to the page but this did not result in more total donations. For future tests such as this, we will need to go with a more radically different treatments to achieve validity.

experiments.title: How the style of popup influenced year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: What style of popup will have the biggest influence on year end giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2017 - 01/01/2018
During the last few days of December, Buckner International was running a popup across their website. Historically, we had always used an exit intent so that they would only see the ad when they were finished reading the content. However, given the importance of year end, we decided to test a standard popup that would appear when a visitor first arrived at the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither of the treatments statistically validated for donation or clicks to the website. However, we did see a surprising trend. The exit intent got more clicks to the donation page than the popup did but the popup received more donations over time. This may be related to a greater number of people seeing the popup which then influenced their decision to give when they received an email later that day. This is something that will definitely need further testing downstream to validate.
For posterity-sake, here are the results of the number of clicks on the popup.

experiments.title: How the page layout impacts visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a sidebar impact visitor engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2017 - 02/21/2017
With the ADF blog, we wanted to find new ways to increase overall visitor engagement. Engagement is defined as anything from donations and email acquisition to visitors exploring the site. One way that the blog increased visitors exploring the site was by listing recent articles at the bottom of each post. We had the hypothesis that this may not be seen by a significant volume of visitors if they didn’t finish each post. To overcome this, we decided to test out “sticky” sidebar that would remain on the right side of the page for desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 3 weeks, the sidebar did not produce any significant impact to either emails acquired or visitors exploring the website. The trend seemed to indicate that it may be negatively impacting these metrics so we decided to shut off this experiment in leu of others that we felt may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How a testing into a new landing page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation landing page will produce the greatest amount of donors and dollars?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2015 - 06/08/2015
Hillsdale College had recently launched a new microsite to handle all of their online giving. The new microsite had a variation of their standard design but made it easier to create contextualized donation forms and took into account certain principles we learned from previous experiments such as removing banners and navigation, adding value proposition language, and an easier to use donation form.
One of their more traffic donation forms was a general donation form for their online classes.  Each class has its own donation form but, to handle general website traffic, we wanted one that could be applicable to all courses.  Before making the switch to the new microsite, we wanted to validate that the new page would achieve the lift in conversion rate and revenue that we were expecting.
experiments.keyLearnings: The learnings achieved through previous experiments held true with this new landing page and help achieve a 35.8% increase in donation conversion rate. The experiment was a good example of leveraging the learnings from multiple experiments to craft a new, more effective landing page.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to donors to increase the donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 06/05/2015
For Hillsdale College, the Constitution 101 online course has proven to be a great source of new email addresses and new donors for their file.  In particular, the landing page that is shown after someone signs up has the highest volume of traffic compared to their other donation offers. As a result, this page has been the focus of multiple testing iterations over the past several months.
The first big lift to conversion rate came from modifying the value proposition displayed on the original page. After that initial experiment, we tried experimenting with different tactical elements of the page. None of these experiments were able to boost results.
Since the value proposition was proven to be the best way to boost results so we wanted to try refining this value proposition even further. We created a new copy treatment that removed the organizational goals and instead explored the reasoning for the course in greater depth.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the organizational goal of having 50 million people take the class and instead emphasizing why it is so critical that the class be offered for free, we were able to boost conversion rate by 22.6%. This is a good example of examining the message through the eyes of the donor to focus in on what is most important to them.  The “50 million people” goal wasn’t necessarily bad but this test proves it was the deeper value of the course that they valued more.

experiments.title: How a radically different message approach affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically different message approach on the main donation page improve the conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2015 - 04/17/2015
The Hillsdale College donation page made the assumption than once a visitor made it to the page, they were committed to making their gift.  As a result, the copy on the page mentioned other ways they could make a gift but did not try to reinforce the visitors decision to make that gift.
In an effort to improve overall performance of the page, we decided to run an experiment testing out multiple elements of the page.  The goal was to reduce the friction that came with the out-of-the-box donation form (making it mobile-friendly and easier to use), remove choices the visitor had to make by removing the navigation, and improve the value proposition expressed on the page by talking about what was appealing and exclusive to Hillsdale.
experiments.keyLearnings: The biggests lifts will often come when trying a totally different approach.  This radical redesign tested multiple factors in order to improve the overall conversion experience which resulted in a 22% lift to conversion and an 18.9% lift to average gift.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”. A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue — but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate. Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner.
The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment’s gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the “Other amount” option.  This is an example of “unsupervised thinking” which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale’s next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How live chat on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How does live chat affect donation conversion rate and average gift amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2015 - 02/04/2015
The Heritage Foundation tested a Live Chat feature on their donation page as a way to boost engagement with the potential donors. The audience was split between existing members and nonmembers in order to monitor how it affected each audience.  The results below are for nonmembers only.
experiments.keyLearnings: The segment targeted at existing donors showed no significant difference between the segment of visitors seeing live chat and those not seeing it.  For new donors, the live chat dropped the conversion rate slightly but was able to increase the average gift by 21.6%.  While it is not statistically significant, this test directionally indicates that live chat can increase the average gift.  Further testing would be necessary to validate this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How reducing form friction on email acquisition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does dividing the registration form in to multiple steps increase the overall acquisition of emails, addresses, and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/08/2015 - 07/07/2015
Hillsdale College’s Constitution 101 course is one of the biggest source of new emails and new donors for the college. Previous experiments had been able to increase the conversion rate of the donation page and had increased the number of visitors to the page.  However, we had been unable to build upon the success of the initial treatment created for the name acquisition page.
We decided to create another treatment that would divide the existing form into two steps; name and email would be first, followed by the participant’s address. When we devised this experiment, we made sure to set up tracking across every step of the process to ensure any improvements in one area didn’t adversely impact the other important metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two step form successfully increased every metric along the registration process.  Email acquisition increased by 17.6% and the number of home addresses acquired increased by 9.0%. The biggest lift was achieved with the final step of the process with a 93.4% increase in donor conversion.

 

By spreading out the steps, we reduced the amount of mental friction associated with each step of the process. The forms looked shorter and, as such, increased the ultimate conversion along each step.

experiments.title: How a broader value proposition in the headline affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a broader value statement in the headline increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2016 - 05/03/2016
Hillsdale College was promoting their free Constitution 101 course through multiple online channels. The control signup page converted nearly 40% of visitors to enroll in the course, but it also assumed that the primary value proposition, “Understand the Constitution like never before” resonated with each visitor.
They wanted to test whether a broader statement might capture the attention of more people who did not already have an inclination to “understand the Constitution like never before” and convince more to enroll. They also hypothesized that a “value statement” in the headline might result in more donations. So they created a second page, with the headline “The Constitution secures your freedoms as an American” and the subheadline “Understand it like never before in Hillsdale College’s free online course”.
They launched an A/B test to determine the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page with the broader headline actually decreased email acquisition conversion rate by 2.7%. However, looking at the entire funnel, we noticed that visitors to this page were 14.4% more likely to donate.
This presented an interesting finding: while the broader statement resulted in fewer course enrollments, it increased the motivation of those who did enroll. This seems to suggest that while the new headline was more divisive among initial visitors (which caused slightly lower enrollment), it increased the appeal of the course by playing to the values (rather than just the surface desires) of those who accepted the offer. This reiterates the importance of measuring the entire funnel when there is both email acquisition and donor acquisition at stake.

experiments.title: Validating a series of experiment results using an A/A experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Is there an instrumentation error that has prevented the new ATA template from properly validating an experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2015 - 08/19/2015
After a series of experiments to optimize the Imprimis survey, we started to believe the new ATA survey template may be causing errors. We decided to run a straight A/A test where both the control and treatment were identical. If we still managed to create a statistically significant lift, we would know the template was causing errors.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment produced two statistically valid lifts for both donations and number of survey completions. This result leads us to the conclusion that the new template must be causing issues with the way the A/B testing platform is collecting data. Most likely, this is the result of how the survey was added to the page in an iFrame.  The results of all previous experiments should be ignored.

experiments.title: Does the placement of a donation module on specific campaign pages impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module within content for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation module to the content section of a landing page versus adding it to the hero image. We hypothesized that adding it further down the page would allow new users to ingest our content, be motivated by it and then provide an easy option for donating at that point.
We tested this on various campaign pages—in this case campaign refers to specific areas where Save the Children operates (Ukraine, Gaza, South Sudan, etc)
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test compared the donation module's effectiveness in the hero section versus further down the page.
We hypothesized that placing the module lower on the page would give new users time to engage with the content, build motivation, and then encounter a natural opportunity to give.
Key Results:
Desktop conversion: ↑ 186% (91% confidence, not statistically significant)
 	Mobile conversion: ↓ 62% (97% confidence, statistically significant)
What Happened:
The results revealed a sharp split between desktop and mobile behavior.
On desktop, moving the donation module into the content showed a strong (though not statistically significant) lift in conversion. This suggests that desktop users may benefit from more time and context before being asked to give.
Overall, placing the donation module further down benefits desktop users but significantly reduces mobile conversions.
Overall Performance:
Desktop performance:
Mobile performance:
Recurring performance:
One-time performance:

experiments.title: How removing the dollar ($) sign from a gift array impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe removing the dollar sign for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 07/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the dollar sign from the ask array levels on the donation form. We hypothesized that the dollar sign signaled the users brain to enter into transactional mode rather than staying connected to the emotion of the cause. Removing the dollar sign would allow the user to stay connected to the mission and would thus increase their willingness to give, potentially at a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We set out to test if the dollar sign adds friction for donors.
We thought seeing a “$” might feel transactional, deterring emotional giving. Removing it, we believed, could keep donors focused on the mission and increase giving.
What Happened:
The results told a very different story.
Instead of reducing friction, removing the dollar sign significantly hurt performance—especially on desktop. Both conversion rate and revenue per visitor dropped, in some cases dramatically.
This suggests the dollar sign isn’t a distraction. It’s actually doing something helpful: giving users clarity about what’s being asked of them.
Overall Performance:
Across all users, conversion dropped from 40.5% to 33%—a 17% decrease (52% confidence). Revenue per visitor declined even more sharply, falling 64% with 98% statistical confidence.
Desktop Performance:
The desktop saw the most dramatic impact. Conversion rate fell from 48.1% to 18%—a 62% decrease (95% confidence). Revenue per visitor dropped 75% (99% confidence).
It’s worth noting this segment had a small sample size (15 gifts), but the magnitude and consistency of the drop are hard to ignore.
Mobile Performance:
Mobile told a more mixed story. The conversion rate increased from 36.2% to 40.9% (a 13% lift), but with only 29% confidence, it is not statistically reliable.
At the same time, revenue per visitor dropped 58% (90% confidence), indicating that even when more users converted, they gave less.
One-Time Gifts:
For one-time donations, conversion declined from 32.4% to 24.2%—a 25% decrease (62% confidence). Revenue per visitor fell 69% (98% confidence), reinforcing the broader pattern: fewer gifts, and smaller ones.
Recurring Gifts:
Recurring performance showed a slight increase in conversion (8.1% to 9.1%), but with very low confidence (13%) and only 6 total gifts.
Revenue per visitor was essentially flat, decreasing just 0.97% (50% confidence). With such a small sample, these results aren’t directional.
Takeaway:
The main takeaway: keeping the dollar sign significantly improves donor clarity and response.
The dollar sign is not a barrier; it provides important clarity and guides users effectively through the donation process.
Without it, donors may feel uncertain, which can lower both participation and donation amounts.

experiments.title: Can a “Start My Gift” Button on the Main Donation Page Reduce Friction for Year-End Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the main donation page for visitors during the year-end campaign will increase donor conversion by reducing friction for highly motivated donors and allowing them to quickly begin the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/31/2025
During the year-end campaign, many donors arrive on the main donation page after clicking through from ads, emails, and other campaign touchpoints. While the page includes a detailed value proposition explaining the impact of giving, the team was concerned that the longer messaging could introduce friction for highly motivated donors who were already ready to give.
Specifically, visitors had to scroll past the page’s text before reaching the donation form, which could delay donors who simply wanted to give after responding to an appeal.
However, the team also feared that shortening the page would be counterproductive, as many visitors arrive still unconvinced to donate.
To address these concerns, we decided to test a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the donation page. The test variants were:
Control Version: visitors saw the standard donation page experience, where they would scroll down the page to reach the giving form
Treatment Version: a “Start My Gift” button was placed near the top of the page. When clicked, the button automatically scrolled the visitor directly to the donation form, allowing donors to skip the body copy and begin the giving process immediately
The goal of this test was to determine whether reducing friction for highly motivated donors would increase the donation page conversion rate during the year-end campaign. The test ran from December 19 – December 31.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall Conversion
Conversion rates were virtually identical between the two versions — 12.9% for the control and 12.8% for the treatment. Adding the "Start My Gift" button didn't hurt overall conversion, suggesting that a faster path to the form didn't undermine the page's persuasive messaging.
Revenue Per Visitor
There was no significant difference in overall one-time revenue per visitor. However, a meaningful shift emerged among larger donors: for gifts of $1,000 or more, the treatment increased revenue per visitor by 34.9% at 93.5% confidence. This suggests the button reduced friction for highly motivated mid-level and major donors who arrived ready to give and simply wanted a faster way to do it.
Monthly Sustainer Gifts
The clearest tradeoff was in recurring giving. The control generated 50 sustainer gifts compared to 31 for the treatment — a 36.8% decline at 95.8% confidence. A likely explanation: the button allowed donors to skip past the page's value proposition, reducing exposure to messaging that might have inspired a monthly commitment.
Mobile vs. Desktop
No meaningful differences emerged between devices. The button performed similarly across mobile and desktop.
Key Takeaway
Reducing friction helps unlock larger one-time gifts without hurting overall conversion — but it may come at the cost of recurring giving. Further testing should focus on whether a fast-access path can be designed in a way that preserves exposure to the monthly giving ask.

experiments.title: How does the placement of an employer match question impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing an employer match widget for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2025 - 06/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of moving the employer match widget on the donation form. We hypothesized that placing the widget near the ask array would motivate people to use it and potentially increase the average gift if the user originally chose an amount not supported by their employer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test examined whether moving the employer match widget closer to the gift array would encourage more donors to use it — and potentially increase the average gift size for donors whose initial amount wasn't matched by their employer.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate
+16%

Overall RPV

Conversion and RPV both trended upward, though neither reached the 95% significance threshold. The average gift did not increase. Due to tracking limitations, we weren't able to measure how many donors actually interacted with the employer match widget.

What the Results Suggest:
The most notable signal came from the desktop, where conversion jumped 44% at 90% confidence — the closest result to statistical significance in the test. Mobile was largely flat. Recurring gifts showed a consistent directional lift in both conversion and RPV, suggesting the widget placement may resonate more with donors already inclined toward ongoing giving.
Next Steps:
To move forward, extend the test duration or increase the sample size to validate the desktop lift. Enhance tracking of widget interactions to determine if the placement change directly impacts employer match usage or if conversion improvements stem from another factor.

experiments.title: How the placement of a featured gift in a gift catalogue impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the placement of a featured gift for gift catalogue donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2025 - 06/03/2025
Earlier in the year, we tested whether featuring a gift at the top of the gift catalogue affected conversions, and the decrease in conversion rate suggested the featured product actually increased friction in the customer journey. Rather than providing the user with an easy donating option, it added an additional decision point and increased the scroll length of the page..
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether the placement of that featured gift made a difference.
Control: no featured gift
Variant A: The featured gift is at the top of the list. (Similar to the variant in the original experiment)
Variant B: The featured gift aligns with the gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether adding a featured product to the all-products page would reduce friction and boost giving. The hypothesis: too many options and constant scrolling were overwhelming donors and impeding gifts.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate
+48%

Overall RPV
+13%

Treatment 1 showed slight lifts in conversion and RPV; Treatment 2 was flat or negative. Neither reached 95% significance, and the sample was too small for rollout.

What the Results Suggest
Treatment 1 performs well overall, but device matters. On desktop, both treatments showed strong lifts, though the sample size was small. On mobile, Treatment 1 held up, while Treatment 2 dropped sharply, showing that the device greatly influences donor experience.
Next Steps
The sample size is too small to make a rollout decision with confidence. Next steps: rerun this test with a larger audience, and analyze results separately by device, given how differently mobile and desktop users responded.

experiments.title: How removing value prop copy on a mobile donation form impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for mobile donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 06/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing donation form copy for SMS traffic. We hypothesized that the copy in the SMS motivates the user to take action and thus encountering more copy on the donation form was adding friction for the user.  So for this experiment, the treatment variant anchored directly to the gift array section of the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test looked at what happens when the donation form copy is removed for visitors arriving via SMS. The thinking was that since SMS messaging already motivates the donor to act, repeating copy on the form may create unnecessary friction — particularly for donors considering a recurring gift.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate
4.2%

What the Results Suggest:
Overall, one-time giving dipped slightly, but the sample size wasn't large enough to draw firm conclusions. The more interesting finding is the sharp increase in recurring donations. This suggests the existing form copy — which is oriented toward one-time giving — may be nudging donors away from choosing a recurring gift.
Next Steps:
To focus on growing recurring giving, expand testing to all mobile users. This will also ensure statistical significance.

experiments.title: Does Problem–Solution Messaging Increase Year-End Main Donation Page Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying and sharpening the problem statement on the year-end main donation page will increase donor conversion because clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and their motivation to give. because the clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 11/25/2025
Recently, we tested stronger problem–solution messaging on the main donation page, clarifying families’ challenges and positioning the donor’s gift as a direct solution. This language led to a 20% increase in gifts, suggesting that clearer articulation of the problem and the donor’s role effectively boosts  motivation to give.
Based on these results, we aimed to test if applying problem–solution framing to the year-end donation page would also increase donor motivation and improve conversion rates.
Control Version: focused on the “Help Deliver Hope and Joy” theme, highlighted the matching gift, and listed ministry activities supported by donations.
Treatment Version: reframed the message to highlight the crisis families face and positioned year-end gifts as ways to restore hope, strengthen marriages, equip parents, and help children experience Christ’s love. It also reinforced the ministry’s impact and directly linked gifts to outcomes.
The test goal was to see if clearer problem framing and a stronger donor-impact message would boost conversion during this critical year-end fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the page generated 119 gifts with a 9.6% conversion rate, compared to 138 gifts and a 10.8% conversion rate on the control, representing an 11.2% decrease in gifts. However, this result did not reach statistical significance (68.4% level of confidence), meaning the two versions of the page performed very similarly overall.

The test also did not reach the required sample size of 4,807 visitors, receiving only 2,513 total visitors before it was paused. Because of this limited traffic, it is difficult to draw a definitive conclusion about the impact of the messaging change on donor conversion.

In terms of donor behavior, both versions performed similarly in generating monthly sustainer gifts (5 on the control vs. 7 on the treatment), suggesting the messaging change did not meaningfully affect donors’ likelihood to start recurring giving.

However, the test was paused after six days due to a concerning trend in revenue performance. While conversion rates were relatively close, the average gift size differed significantly:
Control average gift: $44.95

experiments.title: How updating a callout with value prop on a campaign page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the value prop more topical for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2025 - 09/01/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the current value prop on the homepage from being a general organization focused to topical focused. With changes on the ground in Gaza, we wanted to test whether referencing that situation would make an impact. We hypothesized that this would help the user connect more with the organization by presenting a topic relevant and important to them.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of shifting the hero value proposition from a general, organization-wide focus to a topical, issue-specific focus. The hypothesis was that surfacing a topic personally relevant to the user would strengthen their connection to the organization and drive higher engagement.
Key Results
Overall Conversion Rate: -14% decline (65% confidence) — not statistically significant
 	Revenue Per Visitor: -3% decline (55% confidence) — not statistically significant
Insights
The results suggest that a topical focus may inadvertently alienate users who don't feel personally connected to the featured issue. Rather than a narrow topic, users appear to prefer a holistic snapshot of the organization — who we are, what we do, and the breadth of our impact. A general value prop may better serve the range of motivations users bring when they arrive.
Recommendation & Next Steps:
We plan to retest this module with two adjustments: reintroducing the donation module within the hero and refining the copy. This will help us isolate whether the topical framing itself — or the absence of a clear giving prompt — was the primary driver of the decline.

experiments.title: How removing value prop copy on a mobile donation form impacts conversion rates – part 2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for mobile donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 07/02/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the donation form copy for SMS traffic. We hypothesized that the copy in the SMS motivates the user to take action, and that encountering more copy on the donation form adds friction for the user.  So for this experiment, the treatment variant is anchored directly to the gift array section of the donation form.
This test was run before on a different form, so we wanted to see if we got the same results on different forms tied to different SMS messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the donation form copy for SMS traffic. We hypothesized that the copy in the SMS motivates the user to take action, and that encountering more copy on the donation form adds friction for the user.
Key Results:
