experiments.title: How testing a premium focused donation page vs a mission focused donation page affects donor acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a missional focused funnel for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor acquisition rate because having the copy be missional focused would encourage the user to give to the overall mission.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/09/2026
Time of Grace has a new premium of the month, and they send emails to the housefile to offer them a chance to donate and receive it.
Usually, the copy on emails and donation pages focuses on the value proposition of the premium being offered. For this test, we wanted to explore a funnel focused on the overall mission of Time of Grace rather than just the premium.
We ran this test on both the email and donation pages, dividing the list into those who received the premium-focused or mission-focused versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that a mission focused funnel for Time of Grace Ministry's housefile did not increase donor acquisition rates. The treatment group actually experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control group. Because statistical confidence is below 95%, these results are not valid for future decision-making. It is essential to keep testing different strategies and funnels to find the most effective way to increase donor acquisition rates. For future tests, carefully design experiments with a clear hypothesis and ensure the sample size is large enough for valid results. Also, test a variety of messaging strategies. Use both premium focused and mission-focused funnels to see which best resonates with the audience and drives donations.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition to reflect the impact of the entire organization—rather than a single brand—affects donor conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the “One Moody” approach for donation page visitors will not change donor conversion rates because most donors support Moody’s mission as a whole rather than a single ministry, making the broader institutional language unlikely to create friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2026
To better understand the impact of consolidating the brands across Moody, we a tested more unified “One Moody” language to see how the Moody Bible Institute’s audience responded to this shift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment suggests that implementing a more unified “One Moody” approach on the donation page was associated with a 22.6% decrease in total donations across all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level was only 69.5%, well below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, indicating the results are not statistically reliable and should not be used to inform future decision-making. While overall donations declined in the treatment, the average gift increased. The control page generated an average gift of $20.50, while the “One Moody” treatment yielded an average gift of $23.79—an increase of approximately 16%. Because the test did not reach statistical significance, additional testing is needed before drawing conclusions about how unified “One Moody” messaging may influence donor behavior. Future experiments should prioritize achieving higher confidence levels and may benefit from further exploration of how branding and messaging influence donor perception, motivation, and giving behavior.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to point to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will increase the donor conversion rate because the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate. Donation buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page, and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the donation buttons on site profiles from leading to the tribute donation page to the non-tribute donation page resulted in an 8.4% decrease in donations across all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experiments, it is important to ensure the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the timing of the experiment, such as avoiding conducting tests during peak donation periods like the Calendar Year End campaign. It's also important to carefully monitor and analyze the impact of any changes on donation metrics before making broader implementations.

experiments.title: How changing the headline on a donation page to be more tangible affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headline for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because having a more tangible ask will help provide clarity to the user as to what the goal is, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 12/26/2025
As part of Time of Grace’s Calendar Year End campaign, our goal was to understand how the messaging on our donation page could influence giving behavior. Specifically, we tested whether a more tangible, outcome-oriented headline would drive higher donor conversion compared to a more general, open-ended ask.
The hypothesis was that clearly articulating the impact of a gift would make the donation opportunity feel more concrete and persuasive, thereby encouraging more visitors to complete a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: Looking at the results, there was a slight decrease in donations on the treatment version. However, looking at the revenue, there was a 141% increase in revenue to the treatment. Control - $8,605 Treatment - $21,830. This suggests that while the tangible ask didn't increase donations, it encouraged users to make larger gifts, resulting in an increase in revenue for TOG during their campaign. Therefore, this campaign was a success.

experiments.title: How Elevating Matching Gift Messaging on the Donation Page Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that highlighting the matching gift higher on the donation page would increase donor conversion rates because it would immediately reinforce urgency and perceived impact at the moment of decision, encouraging donors to complete their gift while the match was available.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment ran during The Heritage Foundation’s year-end fundraising campaign, when matching gifts were a core component of the overall appeal strategy. At year-end, donors expect urgency and leverage, and the presence of a large match was already baked into campaign messaging. The question was not whether to use the match, but how prominently it should appear once a donor reached the donation page.
At this point in the funnel, visitors had already demonstrated strong intent. The experiment tested whether elevating the match higher on the page—immediately after the headline—would increase conversions by reinforcing urgency and perceived impact, or whether it would be redundant for donors already primed to give.
The test was conducted on the donation page, specifically in the sub-headline area directly beneath the main headline. All donation page visitors were randomly assigned to either the control experience (no sub-headline; match mentioned later in the page copy) or the treatment experience, where the match was surfaced immediately at the top of the page.
Previous tests suggested that donors arriving at the donation page already have high intent, raising an important question: does additional urgency messaging at this stage help—or does it create friction? Because matches are one of the strongest traditional fundraising levers, we wanted to test whether bringing the match forward earlier would reinforce commitment and improve conversion, or whether donors would respond better to a calmer, more streamlined donation experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not produce a statistically valid result, but the directional outcome suggests that bringing the match higher on the donation page did not improve performance. The treatment showed a lower conversion rate than the control (-8.9% relative difference), while revenue per visitor was essentially flat. Although average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, this did not offset the decline in conversions.
These results suggest that for high-intent donors, surfacing the match earlier may be unnecessary—or even distracting—once they have already decided to give.
Moving forward, we will avoid prioritizing prominent match messaging at the very top of the donation page and instead continue placing match details further down the page, where they can reinforce value without interrupting the donation flow. This test supports a broader pattern across year-end experiments: at the point of conversion, clarity and simplicity outperform added urgency.
Match messaging remains a powerful lever earlier in the funnel—emails, landing pages, homepage messaging—but on the donation page itself, the focus should be on reducing friction and making it easy for motivated donors to complete their gift.

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Moody Radio website radio player visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because  it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Radio Player.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for two weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment showed that adding an image to the Moody Radio Player led to a 20.6% decrease in clicks overall (77.1% level of confidence). Because this outcome was unexpected, we took a deeper look at the data to better understand what may have driven the decline.
To isolate potential drivers, we analyzed click performance by device type.
On mobile, the treatment resulted in a 12.7% decrease in clicks (48% level of confidence).
On desktop, clicks declined by 44.8% (72% level of confidence).
On tablet, clicks dropped by 52.7% (62% level of confidence).
These results suggest that the image had the greatest negative impact on desktop and tablet users, where the decline in engagement was most pronounced.
Transaction data followed a similar pattern. The control generated four gifts, while the treatment generated just one—representing a 75% decrease in transactions (82% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact statement below the donate button affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic impact statement  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission. because  the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/02/2025
During Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test adding a dynamic impact statement below the donate button for CaringBridge on their donation pages. The statement pulled the number of donors on the donation page during the day of Giving Tuesday. The statement read as follows –
Your gift’s impact will combine with the X other donors who have also stepped forward to DOUBLE their Giving Tuesday gift alongside the $50,000 match!
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic impact statement on the donation page did not have a significant positive impact on transactions during Giving Tuesday. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 31.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
This suggests that the dynamic impact statement did not effectively encourage visitors to make a donation based on the number of donors who had already contributed. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of social proof elements on donation pages to ensure that they resonate with visitors and positively impact their decision to donate.
It is also essential to conduct experiments for a longer duration to gather sufficient data and increase the confidence level of the results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the findings. Additionally, considering different types of social proof elements or adjusting the messaging based on visitor behavior and preferences could be explored in future experiments to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a double impact slideup affects transactions during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors will increase transactions because it highlights the urgency and potential impact the visitor could have, encouraging them to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/03/2025
We have seen that slide-ups have been very successful for CaringBridge during high-urgency campaigns. For Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test a regular slide-up vs a slide-up that highlighted the chance for the user to have their gift doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors did not yield the expected increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 45.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making. In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the experiment's duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and sufficient time for the impact of the changes to be accurately measured. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the double impact message or explore other ways to create urgency and highlight the potential impact of donations to see if different strategies yield better results. It is also important to consider the overall messaging and timing of the campaign to ensure it resonates with the target audience.

experiments.title: How does transparency in communicating gift allocation affect donor conversion performance?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including explicit language about gift allocation on the Deaflympics donation page will increase donor conversion metrics, as transparency builds trust and reassures donors that their gifts directly support the mission, rather than administrative costs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2025 - 10/05/2025
To improve donor conversion on IMB’s Deaflympics donation page, we introduced more direct, transparent language about how each gift is used.
While we’ve often said, “100% of your gift goes directly to missionaries on the field,” this version takes it a step further—explicitly clarifying that no portion of these funds is used for administrative or overhead expenses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that including explicit language about gift allocation on the Deaflympics donation page resulted in a 15.7% increase in transactions across all traffic. This suggests that transparency regarding how donations are utilized can positively impact donor conversion metrics by fostering trust and ensuring that donors know their gifts directly support the mission. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 45.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for making future decisions. It is also important to note that the treatment received a $60,000 gift, which significantly skewed our revenue metric. In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to generate statistically significant results.

Additionally, considering factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as external events or changes in donor behavior, can help in designing more robust experiments. Overall, the concept of transparency in communicating how donations are used is promising for improving donor conversion rates. Future experiments should continue to explore this idea while also focusing on obtaining valid and reliable results to inform decision-making effectively.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Guide to the Constitution)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment aimed to increase completed transactions on the Guide to the Constitution donation form by adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time vs. monthly) to the submit button. The existing version of the form used a generic “Make My Gift” button, which provided no clear indication of the commitment donors were making when completing their gift. We hypothesized that this lack of clarity could create hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process.
To address this, we created an A/B test with two versions of the submit button. The control version used the existing generic button, while the treatment version explicitly included the gift type within the button text, such as “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift” depending on the donor’s selection. This approach was designed to reinforce what the donor had chosen earlier in the process and reduce any last-minute confusion before completing the transaction.
The test ran for a little over two weeks, with traffic randomly and evenly allocated between the two page versions to ensure comparable audiences and sessions.The primary metric for the test was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including transactions monthly, one-time and overall conversion rate from page views to completed gifts. By clarifying the commitment on the button itself, we expected to see an increase in donor confidence and a corresponding lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key findings of this experiment are the treatment that added gift type clarity to the submit button resulted in a -28.4% decrease in transactions compared to the control, with a confidence level of only 48%. This is well below the typical threshold for statistical significance, indicating that the observed difference is likely due to random variation rather than a true effect. Rather than improving clarity and increasing conversions, the added text on the button may have created friction or uncertainty for donors at the final step.

These results suggest that clarifying gift type at the point of submission is not an effective strategy for improving conversions and may even discourage donors from completing their gift. Future efforts to improve clarity should focus on earlier points in the giving flow—such as during gift selection or form completion—rather than on the submit button itself.

experiments.title: Does impact-focused or campaign-focused language lead to more engagement and an increase in downstream metrics?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact-focused language for Moody Radio radio player visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the donors to connect emotionally to the impact of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2025 - 08/07/2025
For Moody Radio’s 48 Hours of Impact campaign, we wanted to test whether campaign-focused copy or impact-focused copy resonated more with donors. Moody has historically been very campaign-focused with their appeals, since their audience is most likely accustomed to this type of appeal. We wanted to know whether the additional value of their gift would be more impactful than traditional language.
Control: Campaign-focused
Treatment: Impact-focused
This test ran on the Moody Radio radio player for about two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact-focused language did not perform better than the campaign-focused language in terms of generating clicks for Moody Radio's 48 Hours of Impact campaign. The treatment actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Furthermore, we used custom metrics in VWO to track gifts that came from the Radio Player.

experiments.title: How using identity-based messaging without a clear problem affected donor conversion on the main donation page

rows.name: Control - Improves Perception of the Problem

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that affirming the donor’s identity and shared values on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people are more likely to take action when it reinforces their self-concept and aligns with their core beliefs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2025 - 08/05/2025
Focus on the Family’s main donation page is a critical point of conversion—but historically, it has resisted optimization, with most tests yielding no improvement. Our goal in this series of experiments is to uncover which messaging strategies can reliably lift donor response by better aligning the value proposition with what motivates giving.
In the previous test, we validated that problem-centric copy—focused on the family crisis and the donor’s role in addressing it—produced a meaningful increase in donor conversion, particularly in recurring gifts. Building on that insight, this second test explored a different psychological lever: identity alignment.
In this A/B test, the Control used the previous winning treatment—copy that framed the family crisis in vivid terms and emphasized the donor’s role in addressing urgent cultural and spiritual problems. The Treatment, by contrast, shifted to language that affirmed the donor’s identity, values, and motivations. It positioned the act of giving not just as a solution to a problem, but as a personal expression of belief and belonging—“You believe in biblical families. So do we.”
The A/B test ran on the primary donation page for users who clicked the main “Donate” button in site navigation. Traffic was evenly split. This is the second in a planned series of tests aimed at refining how Focus on the Family frames its impact—and understanding what kind of value proposition most effectively motivates giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test offered a valuable reminder of a core fundraising principle: donors are more likely to give when they clearly understand the problem and how their gift solves it.
The identity-based Treatment helped affirm the reader's shared beliefs and values, but it failed to clearly establish why support was urgently needed. It lacked the language about critical family struggles and the donor’s role in addressing them that made the Control language so compelling. As a result, recurring donor conversion dropped by 76%, with a 94.9% level of confidence. Other results were statistically flat, indicating no measurable lift in total or one-time giving.
This suggests that psychological distance may have widened in the Treatment. Without a concrete problem to solve, the act of giving may have felt more abstract or optional—especially for recurring support, which requires higher commitment. While identity alignment can enhance message resonance, it appears insufficient on its own to motivate action on a donation page without a compelling “why give now?”
Our key learning here then is that an effective appeal should try and incorporate both identity and clarity of the problem.
Next step:
We’ll test a hybrid value proposition that combines the emotional clarity of problem perception with the motivating power of identity alignment, to see if this blended approach can drive stronger overall conversion and deeper commitment.

experiments.title: How does specific value prop for Youtube traffic impact conversion on main donate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “video-specific” value prop for Youtube traffic coming to the main donate page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it acknowledges where they’re coming from and focuses in on an aspect of the mission that they have experienced themselves.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2025 - 04/28/2025
For Time of Grace, we observed an increase in the amount of traffic coming to the main donate page from their YouTube channel. We hypothesized that leading this traffic to a main donate that was “video-specific” acknowledging the type of content they were coming from and focusing on this form of outreach in particular would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the "video-specific" value proposition for YouTube traffic coming to the main donate page did not increase donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 71.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

One way of understanding these results is that perhaps focusing the copy too much on one aspect of the mission, left the overall value prop less effective, indicating that users respond better to copy about video, but also the other missional outreach Time of Grace is doing.

experiments.title: How does a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out the premium, impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out that donors will receive a gifted copy of the latest resources with their donation, for will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/27/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, we hypothesized that a sticky bar calling out the book premium available to every donor would increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar on the donation page emphasizing the book premium available to donors may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. However, the results of the experiment only showed a 3.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 20.1%, which is below the 95% threshold necessary for validity.

Originally, the motivation behind this sticky bar was to call out the premium for the reader, in a way that didn't require them to fully read the donation page. However, it's likely that donors arriving on this page from Facebook ads or the Homepage banner may have already been aware of this thank-you gift—making it a non-unique piece of information to have pinned.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed form on acquisition donation pages impact donor conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed form for users on Time of Grace’s acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it helps remove unnecessary friction and choose how much they’d like to give after clarifying how often they’d like to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/16/2025
We’ve seen a tabbed donation form perform well for Time of Grace on their main donation page. We hypothesized that applying the same experience to their acquisition donation pages, receiving Facebook traffic for book premiums, would result in a similar increase. We believe that a tabbed form removes unnecessary friction, making it clear the type of gift a donor is making, which by extension can better clarify how much they may like to give and how often.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:The tabbed donation form on Time of Grace's acquisition donation pages resulted in a significant increase in transactions, with a 26.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, a larger takeaway is that revenue experienced a substantial 64.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This is a very promising result that shows the potential impact of the tabbed donation form on donor contributions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate the positive impact on donor conversion rates and revenue. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating learnings from this experiment, such as the design elements and user experience of the tabbed form, into future tests can help optimize the donation process and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How increasing specificity and personalization on the Tribute donate button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treatment copy for the call-to-action on the Tribute donate button will achieve an increase in conversions because the two options leverage either greater specificity around the kind of a gift a user is making, or personalizes with who they’re making the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/17/2025
On CaringBridge’s Tribute donation page, the Donate button’s CTA was simply: Submit Secure Donation. We developed two treatments to improve the conversion rate on this page that targeted this final call-to-action. The first, hypothesized that greater specificity: Submit My Tribute Gift would increase donor conversion—better reflecting the kind of gift the user was making. The second iteration, tested personalization of the CTA button. A tribute gift is a donation to CaringBridge made in honor of a particular person. We believe that displaying that person’s name in the donate button itself would increase conversion rate—reiterating who the tribute was being made to.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which aimed to increase conversions by either providing greater specificity in the call-to-action or personalizing the donation button, did not result in a statistically significant increase in donations. In fact, there was a 1.4% decrease in Tribute Donations with a 19.6% level of confidence. This means that the control was more effective in this case. Additionally, it's worth noting that both treatments resulted in a decrease (with statistical validity) in the average gift versus the control donate button.

experiments.title: How changing the color of the call to action button on tribute widgets affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the tribute widget call to action color for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donor conversion rate because changing the color would provide more visibility to the button and provide more clarity as to what the button is for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/07/2025
For CaringBridge, their website primarily contains their purple brand color. That is the case for the call to action buttons for the tribute widgets as well. That can potentially cause the button to be blended in with the other elements on the website and not be entirely visible for users. For this test, we wanted to test how changing the button to blue would affect tribute donations. This was tested on all of site tribute widgets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the tribute widget call to action color from purple to blue resulted in a 2.8% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic, but with a low statistical confidence level of 38.3%. This indicates that the change may have had a positive impact on donor conversion, but the results are  inconclusive.
 	However, Revenue - Tribute experienced a significant 19% decrease with a high level of confidence (100%). This indicates that while the number of donations may have increased, the total revenue generated from tributes decreased.
The impact of changes on multiple metrics: While an increase in donations is positive, it is essential to also consider the impact on overall revenue. It's important to strike a balance between increasing donations and maintaining or increasing revenue.
 	Statistical confidence level: Results with a low statistical confidence level like the one in this experiment should not be relied upon for decision-making. It's crucial to ensure that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant to make informed decisions.
 	Further testing: To validate the impact of changing the tribute widget call to action color, additional testing with a larger sample size and longer duration may be necessary. This will help in drawing more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that the addition of a goal thermometer on the FYE donation page led to a 2.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence at 19.1%. This means that while there was a positive impact, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

However, revenue saw a substantial 400% decrease with 100% confidence, indicating a clear negative impact from the goal thermometer. These results suggest that Moody Bible’s audience may be more inclined to make smaller donations when a goal thermometer is present, potentially due to the high fundraising target set for the FYE campaign. Further testing is needed to refine these insights and draw more definitive conclusions. It may also be worth exploring the use of a daily campaign goal, which could help reduce the perceived pressure of reaching a large target and encourage more frequent contributions.

Moving forward, it is important to take into consideration the statistical significance of the results when evaluating the effectiveness of different strategies. While the idea of leveraging social proof through a goal thermometer showed some promise in increasing donor conversion metrics, further testing and optimization are necessary to draw valid conclusions. It is important to refine the messaging and design of the goal thermometer to ensure it effectively communicates progress towards a communal objective without negatively impacting revenue. Future experimentation should focus on testing variations of the goal thermometer to find the most effective approach for driving donor conversions without compromising financial outcomes.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because is creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Today in the Word’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry. Ultimately, the goal was to drive an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while the treatment version with the goal thermometer led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic, it resulted in 1.5% decrease at a 10.8%level of confidence, which is below the threshold of 95% for validity. These results indicate that Today in the Word's audience didn't respond negatively or positively to the presence of the thermometer.

However, revenue saw a 9.6% increase at a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the goal thermometer was effective at motivating donors to give larger amounts. Therefore, we should find ways to incorporate thermometers during other Today in the Word campaigns.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of the goal thermometer on revenue and donor behavior. It may also be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the results, such as the messaging and design of the goal thermometer. Additionally, conducting the experiment for a longer period of time and with a larger sample size could provide more reliable insights.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the goal thermometer can be a valuable tool for driving increased revenue and encouraging larger donations, even if it does not directly impact the number of transactions.

experiments.title: How adding gift equivalencies to each individual ask array button impacts conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift equivalencies for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2025 - 06/12/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding equivalencies to the ask array section, where each gift amount would have a related ‘item’ that it could fund.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Adding gift equivalencies to the ask array section resulted in a 4.6% decrease in donations for all traffic.
 	The statistical level of confidence was only 40.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.
 	Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.
 	It is essential to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions from A/B experiments.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the results, such as the presentation of gift equivalencies and donor preferences.
 	Further testing with a larger sample size and adjustments to the gift equivalencies may be needed to draw more accurate conclusions about their impact on conversion rates and average gift.

experiments.title: How adding a money back guarantee statement on the donation page affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a money-back guarantee message to the donation page will increase donor conversion by reducing perceived risk and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2025 - 05/07/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
As part of their commitment to transparency and donor trust, CitizenGO has a policy that allows donors to request a refund of their donation at any time, no questions asked. While this policy has long existed, it had never been clearly communicated on the donation page itself.
We hypothesized that explicitly including a money-back guarantee on the donation page would reduce the perceived risk of donating and increase donor conversion rates. By clearly communicating CitizenGO’s refund policy, we aimed to reassure hesitant visitors and increase their likelihood of completing a donation.
This experiment was launched during CitizenGO’s monthly donation campaign titled “FR MD L Expand our MD base in [COUNTRY] 2025.” To ensure broad applicability across CitizenGO’s global audience, we tested this experiment on donation pages in the following languages: English (EN), French (FR), Italian (IT), German (DE), Spanish (ES), and Portuguese (PT).
Control: No changes were made to the donation page.
Treatment: We added the following money-back guarantee message to the donation page:
“Our Promise to You: If you ever feel that your donation isn’t making the impact you expected to defend fundamental human rights, just let us know, and we’ll refund 100% of your gift—no questions asked.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 27 days and found that overall donor conversion decreased by -9.8%, with a confidence level of 77.9%. While not statistically significant, this directional result suggests that the addition of the money-back guarantee may have introduced doubt or hesitation rather than reassurance for prospective donors. It’s possible that mentioning a refund option on the donation page raised concerns about the organization’s impact or credibility. Further testing could explore alternative placements or softer language to build trust without unintentionally undermining confidence. Here are the results split up by language:
English: Treatment saw a -3.2% relative decrease in conversion compared to control (20.0% confidence).
 	French: Treatment saw a +36.9% relative increase (57.7% confidence).
 	German: Treatment saw a -14.3% relative decrease (59.6% confidence).
 	Italian: Treatment saw a -46.4% relative decrease (90.7% confidence).
 	Spanish: Treatment saw a -15.3% relative decrease (62.5% confidence).
 	Portuguese: Treatment saw a -10.5% relative decrease (21.0% confidence).
Next Steps:
Explore softer, mission-aligned language that emphasizes donor trust without using retail-style guarantees.
 	Test different placements for trust-building language (e.g. near testimonials or impact statements rather than near the gift form).
 	Consider emphasizing positive reinforcement—such as donor impact or satisfaction—rather than offering an opt-out.

experiments.title: How removing the ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the processing fee option for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 05/31/2025
Many non-profit donation forms have a ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature where a donor can choose to increase their gift to cover any processing fees the non-profit might have to pay their payment providers. For Save the Children US, we wanted to test whether removing this function had an impact on conversion rates and revenue. We ran this one one of their evergreen donation forms, so the results were not influenced by any specific campaign driving traffic to that form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the processing fee option for potential donors resulted in a 5.2% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 41.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
Moreover, when reviewing overall performance based on revenue per visitor, the treatment also had a decrease (this removes large gifts), with a 37% decrease in revenue per visitor with a 99% level of confidence. This, however, makes sense, as the 'tip-up' function adds revenue to the overall gift.
Desktop:
Control: 30% conversion rate
Treatment: 24.6% conversion rate, which is a decrease of 18% with an 88% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 34% with a 95% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 14% conversion rate
Treatment: 17% conversion rate, which is a 20% increase with a 79% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor, however, deceased by 35% with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How aligning donation page copy with giving level impacts average gift during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact bullet to the donation page for a larger amount for April Eagle Lake donation page visitors will achieve an increase in average gift because the donation page copy calls out a higher impact bullet that is more in line with their previous giving levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/04/2025
Every spring The Navigators run an April Eagle Lake campaign. At the beginning of this campaign, we ran a gift array test based on the donor’s lifetime revenue, but didn’t quite see the results that we anticipated.
In an effort to get a valid learning, we tested adding an additional impact bullet to the donation page for the two higher segments of lifetime donors to try to boost their average gift. The idea behind the test is that adding a higher level of impact bullet would lead to larger gifts from a segment of people who are financially dedicated to The Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment group, which included adding an impact bullet at a higher level to the donation page for higher segments of lifetime donors, saw a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 67.3% as well as a 300% increase in transactions at an 89.3% LOC. However, the average gift for the control was $150 and $63 for the treatment. This indicates that The Navigators audience was more likely to give with the higher impact bullet amount added but they were more likely to give smaller gifts.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of emphasizing higher donation levels to significant donors. By refining the experiment design and testing different variables, we can continue to optimize our donation page and enhance donor engagement and revenue in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a specific gift on a gift catalogue page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing a specific gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2025 - 04/11/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of highlighting a “priority” product to the top of the gift catalogue all products page for Save the Children. The gift catalogue is something that STC has used over the years to generate support for their mission. In this treatment, we showed one product out of a possible five that we chose to highlight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by highlighting a specific gift in the gift catalogue, there was a decrease in donations for all traffic by 18.1%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 63.0%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, revenue per visitor also saw a decrease of 14%, but only with a 67% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does a visually disruptive donation ask generate more donations than a contextually integrated one?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that placing the donation ask at the end of the article, with visual contrast and a gift array, would increase donor conversion because users would be more likely to consider donating after consuming the article content and be more inclined to act with a clearer path to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the effectiveness of in-article versus end-of-article donation asks on article pages. The “Dear Reader” style messages were designed to prompt donations in a low-friction, editorial-style format.

experiments.title: How using an open amount field on a donation form impacts mid-level conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for potential mid level donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to see whether mid-level donors would respond more to a donation form with an open field instead of the standard gift array. The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for mid-level donors did not result in an increase in conversion rates and average gift as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in donations with a statistical confidence level of 34.5%, indicating that these results are not reliable or valid for decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequately large to increase the statistical confidence level. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of donation forms, including those with ask array amounts and open fields, to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations from mid-level donors.

Overall, it is crucial to interpret results carefully and avoid making decisions based on inconclusive data. Further testing and refinement of donation forms may yield more reliable insights for optimizing fundraising efforts in the future.

experiments.title: Does adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding other ways to give for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2024 - 09/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of adding additional ways to give, specifically Crypto, on donation forms. We added some language to the bottom of the form, under the ‘donate’ button, that gave donors links to be able to give through their preferred vehicle.
Our hypothesis was that adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form will make it easier for people to donate in their preferred method. This will decrease friction for the users looking for these options without impacting the overall conversion of the donation form for all other users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding additional ways to give at the bottom of a donation form did not result in the intended outcome of increasing conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical confidence level was not high enough to draw definitive conclusions.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of adding new elements to a donation form and to carefully assess whether these changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and conclusive results.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to conduct further research and testing to better understand the impact of offering different ways to donate on conversion rates. This could include gathering feedback from donors, conducting surveys, and running additional A/B tests with a larger sample size and more rigorous statistical analysis.

experiments.title: Does adding options to donate by crypto and DAF impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding crypto and DAF giving options for donation page visitors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2024 - 12/02/2024
This test explored the impact of adding DAF and Crypto buttons to the donation form. The goal was to increase the use of these payment methods without detracting from regular form conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding crypto and DAF giving options to the donation page did not have a significant impact on conversion rates. The treatment actually saw a slight decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 44.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid for making future decisions. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to provide reliable results and that the statistical significance threshold is met before drawing conclusions. It may also be worth exploring other variables or factors that could potentially impact donation conversion rates and testing them in a controlled manner to determine their effectiveness.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor countdown on the donation page help conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown on a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page will achieve more transactions because the donor countdown will provide extra social proof, showing that others are doanting too. It also gives an exact number needed which can lead into exclusivity and ultimately increasing motivation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio just wrapped up their annual Spring Share campaign.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if we could include a donor countdown on the main campaign donation page to reinforce the need and provide extra motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the donor countdown on a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing transactions. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.5% decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 54.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the design of the donor countdown feature before drawing any conclusions. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the campaign, messaging, or external events. Additionally, incorporating qualitative feedback from donors and testing different variations of the donor countdown feature can provide valuable insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: Will adding a timer on the donation page increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign will achieve more transactions because because the added element will increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio recently wrapped up their Annual Spring Share campaign.
This campaign drives a lot of traffic to the donation pages from on-air, paid social and paid search, as well as organic. This campaign is about a month long which can seem like a long time for a donor to have the opportunity to give to where they can push it off.
We wanted to see if we could increase the urgency to give on the donation page instead of waiting by adding a countdown time to the end of the campaign. To make sure this was the most effective, we waited to launch this until the last 4 days of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign did result in a 6.7% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.8%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficiently large to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to capture more data and allow for more conclusive findings.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to take caution in interpreting the results and to continue conducting rigorous experiments to drive decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: How adding in more copy on a recurring donation page affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in more copy for recurring donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the copy will provide some clarity about why they should donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 03/27/2025
For this test, we tested adding in some more copy on the monthly donation page as a way to provide more clarity and more value proposition to the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more copy to the recurring donation page did not have a positive impact on donation conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level below 95%. Therefore, we cannot draw reliable conclusions from these findings.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing hypotheses and variations on donation pages, but with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure more valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider qualitative feedback from users to understand their preferences and motivations when it comes to donating.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a continuation headline for April TMS Sign Up Donation Page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the treatment allows the visitor to understand that there are more steps to take on the page therefore increasing engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/30/2025
Each month, The Navigators have been running scripture memory campaigns via text. After the user signs up to join the next text campaign, they are asked for a gift. We tested into this in a previous scripture memory campaign, but the results did not reach validity so we decided to test again.
Control: You’re in the challenge!
Treatment: You’re in! Now, would you consider making a gift so even more believers can experience this Scripture memory challenge?
The treatment was shown to 50% of traffic of the April TMS Sign Up Donation Page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included a continuation headline asking for a donation, resulted in a 6.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 25.6%, which is below the threshold of 95% required for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the treatment to see if a stronger impact on donor conversion metrics can be achieved. It is also worth considering other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign, the audience targeted, and any external factors that could have impacted donor behavior.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend towards increased donor conversion metrics with the treatment, the results should not be relied upon for making future decisions due to the low statistical significance. Further testing and optimization are necessary to determine the true impact of the continuation headline on donor conversion.

experiments.title: Will having headers on the campaign donation page help increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding headers to the doantion pages for the spring campaign will achieve more transactions because the headers will make the checkout process easier to follow.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
KCBI runs a Spring fundraiser each year where the on air hosts are fundraising and leading traffic to their page to give a gift or to call in.
For this campaign, we wanted to see how we could help increase conversions by adding headers or steps to the donation form. We tested this during CYE, but the results were not concrete. We wanted to see how this could perform during a different season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding headers to the donation pages for the Spring campaign actually led to a decrease in donations, instead of the expected increase. However, the results were not statistically significant with only a 77.9% level of confidence, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

With the steep decrease in transactions, we turned this test after just a couple of days. This test inadvertently could have caused more donor friction and anxiety by adding the headers. In future experimentation, it would be worth testing the language used.

experiments.title: Will a side by side layout work better for a donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the form to be a 2 column layout for the Spring Share doantion page will achieve more donor conversions because  the new layout has the potential to make the form easier to read and fill out.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/24/2025
Moody Radio runs a Spring Share fundraiser each year where tons of traffic from digital channels as well as on-air listeners are directed to a donation form.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the layout of the form had the potential to increase donor conversion on the form
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the form layout to a 2-column format for the Spring Share donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.3% decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. However, when you narrow down the results by traffic we can see that the mobile experience for this test is what caused the overall decrease. Looking at desktop-only results we are seeing a 9.6% increase in conversion at a 57% LoC. While not valid, the results of the desktop do not have a negative impact on conversion.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% in order to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further research to understand why the 2-column layout did not perform as expected. This could involve gathering feedback from users, conducting usability tests, or A/B testing other elements of the donation form to identify factors that may impact donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for Avila’s donation page viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the audience will feel more aligned with the mission and goal of the Avila Foundation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2025 - 03/10/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether enhancing the donation page copy on their Ecce Homo offer with value proposition would increase donor engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Adding a Value Proposition Increased Conversions – The treatment led to a 16.6% lift in conversions, suggesting that highlighting Avila’s mission resonated with donors.
 	Higher Average Gift Amount – Donors in the treatment group gave more on average ($43.46 vs. $37.77), indicating that mission-driven messaging may encourage larger gifts.
 	Results Show Potential, But Need More Testing – The 57.4% confidence level suggests further testing is needed to confirm the impact.
 	Mission-Driven Appeals Strengthen Donor Alignment – Emphasizing Avila’s role in addressing cultural and spiritual challenges may have helped donors feel more connected.
 	Framing Matters – Positioning donor support as essential to the mission (rather than just covering costs) likely contributed to the increase in engagement.
Next Steps:
Test with a larger sample size to improve statistical confidence.
 	Experiment with different messaging elements (e.g., urgency vs. donor impact).
 	Consider adding donor stories or incentives to further boost conversions.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image to a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the anonymous missionaries for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask Donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the image provides visual representation as to what an anonymous missionary is—therefore, helping visitors grasp the idea more quickly and evoking a stronger emotional connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 03/03/2025
To improve donor conversion rates on IMB’s Anonymous Missionary Direct Ask, we tested the impact of adding a relevant image to the donation page.
This specific offer can be challenging to communicate effectively, so our hypothesis was that an image depicting “anonymous missionaries” would provide greater clarity to visitors, ultimately leading to higher donor engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of anonymous missionaries to the donation page for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask did not lead to the expected increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid, but the results are trending in a negative direction.
Due to these results, we looked further into the where the traffic came from to see if it performed better or worse on different types of devices.
Sure enough - mobile users saw an almost valid 71.8% decrease in transactions (94.5% LOC). This indicates that the image negatively impacted potential donors who visited the donation page on their mobile device.  This could be due to the fact that image was somewhat larger and required more scrolling on mobile that desktop.
Desktop users saw an invalid 114.9% lift in transactions at a 47.4% LOC. In future testing, it may be beneficial to only test large images like this on desktop to minimize the amount of friction on the page for mobile users.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and consider the statistical significance before drawing conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that the hypothesis is well-grounded and based on solid evidence. Further testing may be needed to determine the true impact of adding an image to the donation page for this specific campaign. Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other potential factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as messaging, layout, or incentives.

experiments.title: How is the best way to present an ask on the homepage during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a lower third element for the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower third element is new, whereas the sticky bar we have relied on heavily in the past and could have banner blindness. The lower third also has additioanl gift options that will help increase donor conversion where as the sticky bar only has a give now button.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar is really the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a high urgency campaign.
This test we took inspiration from the Chicago Sun Times and placed what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ element on the page. This test will only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the lower third element displayed on the homepage did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the sticky bar. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations. However, because the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the possibility of banner blindness with the sticky bar and to continue testing new elements to optimize donation conversions. It may be valuable to explore different designs, messaging, and placements to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision-making.

 

Along with the increase in transactions, there was an increase in revenue by $12,000 by showing the lower third sticky bar.

experiments.title: Will having shorter copy on the monthly donation page hurt or help recurring transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the monthly donation page will achieve more recurring transactions because the shorter copy reinforces the need to give without the extra copy that may create decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
Historically, the homepage monthly and episodic donation pages for Focus on the Family have been hard to get a valid test to help increase transition and/or revuene. Last Spring and through the summer we found that mentioning “Biblical Reosurces” on the pages had a positive impact on donations and revenue. We wondered, if we could build off of that finding to improve the donation page copy even more.
For this test, we shortened the copy on the monthly donation page to only communicate about the impact that a donor wold have in empowering families with biblical resources. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the copy on the monthly donation page did not result in an increase in recurring transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

Since the statistical level of confidence is only 20.7%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to run future experiments for a longer period of time to gather more data and ensure the results are statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies on the donation page to see what resonates most with donors. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other factors that may impact donation behavior, such as the design of the page, the suggested donation amounts, or the overall user experience. By continuing to test and iterate on different elements of the donation page, Focus on the Family can optimize for increased donations and revenue.

experiments.title: Will adding a countdown timer to a donation page effectively increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last week of the camapgin will achieve more transactions because the countdown timer will reinforce that time is running out and your donation is needed now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase urgency on the donation page by adding a countdown timer to when the campaign ends.
We started this test the last week of the campaign when urgency should be at it’s peak.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the donation page during the last week of a campaign can potentially increase revenue for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was at 0.0%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. It may be beneficial to run similar experiments with a larger sample size to accurately determine the impact of adding a countdown timer on donation page conversions.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to use caution in interpreting the findings and to prioritize statistical significance in making data-driven decisions for future campaigns and optimizations.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial to the sustainer donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the campaign donation pages will achieve more transactions because because the donor testimonial will lend to social proof and increase trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/19/2024
This CYE we wanted to see if we could present a donor testimonail on the donation page to increase social proof and trust, in turn increasing transactions.
For this test we added a box right above the giving from that had a donor testimonal in it. We added this to all of the main campaign giving pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that this test only ran for a couple of days due to the stark decrease in conversions. In the future, it would be worth testing this again to see if it just got off to a slow start.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring other forms of social proof or trust-building elements, such as testimonials from beneficiaries, impact stories, or trust seals. It is also important to ensure that experiments run for a longer period of time in order to gather more reliable and valid data for decision making.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar with a donor countdown on the episodic donation page impact transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar  for the campaign episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the sticky bar will increase the urgency and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if adding additional urgency/ motivation on the campaign episodic donation page would impact the number of transactions made.
We tested this on the monthly donation page and found that it did increase transactions and revenue. You can see the results of that test here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to the campaign episodic donation page can increase transactions, potentially due to the increased urgency and motivation it provides to donors. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 53.8%.
Similar to the test on the recurring donation page, we also saw an increase in average gift when the stickybar was present on the page. ($288/avg. gift vs. $388/avg. gift).
This campaign was ran towards the end of December when urgency is at it's highest. It's important to note that the treatment was winning up until the 28th (last day this was scheduled to run). In future testing it might be worth testing this approach earlier in the campaign and not at the end with the urgency for most people to give is already there.

experiments.title: How a headline on the donation page achieve a higher donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that saying ‘deliver hope’ instead of ‘give hope’ for the family episodic donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fotf audience across their website has responded better to it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/20/2024
In our testing series of figuring out if ‘deliver hope’ vs. ‘give hope’ resonates more with the fotf audience. We’ve found that on the homepage and monthly donation pages are showing promising results of the ‘deliver’ message being the more effective across their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
