The key learning from this experiment is that the 'deliver hope' messaging did not result in a higher donor conversion rate on the family episodic donation page compared to the 'give hope' messaging. The treatment group actually experienced a 6.4% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.8%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to have a statistical level of confidence of at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions from an experiment.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is large enough and that the statistical analysis is robust to generate valid and actionable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the Focus on the Family audience, taking into account the results of previous experiments to inform future decision-making.

experiments.title: Will changing the headline on the donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing ‘give hope’ to ‘deliver hope’ for the family monthly donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’ve seen the fotf audience respond better to deliver hope vs give hope.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/20/2024
Following suit in our first couple of experiments for CYE, we wanted to see if we changing out ‘give hope’ to ‘deliver hope’ would have the same effect as it did on the wiki header and the sohl monthly donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing 'give hope' to 'deliver hope' on the family monthly donation page resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 176.8% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the language used in donation appeals to see if 'deliver hope' consistently outperforms 'give hope' with a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to explore other variations of messaging to further optimize donor conversion rates. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments run for a longer duration to gather more data and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results.

experiments.title: How testimonials on the donation page increased donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they can clearly see the impact of their gift during CYE.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/16/2024
During the high-traffic month of December, we wanted to know if adding an “end user” testimonial would further inspire potential donors to give or give at a greater amount because they were reading about the impact of their gift at the time of decision. Similar to how an e-commerce page might use testimonials for a product, we wanted to know the impact of adding this additional copy from the end user to the Today in the Word CYE donation page would make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the main campaign donation page resulted in a 17.3% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that having end-user testimonials on the page can increase credibility and potentially encourage more people to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials or other forms of social proof on donation pages. By conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform our strategies for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the humanized sticky bar on the CYE Donation page for email 16 visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the personalized message boosts trust and creates a more personalized experience for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2024 - 12/30/2024
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we implemented a humanized sticky bar on the donation page as part of the strategy for Email 16.
This sticky bar ran for a full day, with the test limited to users who accessed the donation page via a link embedded in Email 16.
The sticky bar treatment was shown to 50% of the page’s traffic, allowing us to evaluate its effectiveness in driving engagement and conversions in this specific segment of our audience.
The control featured no sticky bar.
The treatment sticky bar featured Chuck Anderson, sender of Email 16, and had the following copy: “There’s only one day remaining to support IMB missionaries through your year-end gift to the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering®. As a reminder, your gift will play a crucial role in sharing the joy and peace of Jesus with those who have yet to hear His name. – Chuck Anderson”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the humanized sticky bar featuring Chuck Anderson on the donation page for Email 16 visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment saw a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 25.6%, indicating that the control group performed better.
It is important to note that the statistical confidence level is below the threshold of 95%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of humanized elements like sticky bars on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of humanized elements or personalize them based on donor preferences to see if this can lead to an increase in conversions. Additionally, ensuring a longer experiment duration and a larger sample size can help in obtaining more reliable and valid results for future decision making.

experiments.title: Does changing donation form copy to be more donor-focused affect conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor-focused language for users who downloaded the Fall Digest will improve conversion rates because emphasizing a sense of belonging and camaraderie taps into donors’ intrinsic motivations to contribute to a shared cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2024 - 01/11/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The experiment evaluated the impact of copy variations on the conversion rate of the Fall Digest instant donation page. The test focused on donor-focused language inspired by top-performing offers, with the goal of enhancing a sense of belonging and camaraderie to drive higher conversions. This experiment targeted users who downloaded the digital version of the Fall Digest and were directed to a donation page.

experiments.title: How will adding a visual cue to a donation page effect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a visual thermomether for the capaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the thermometer would add urgency to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2025
During CYE we know that there is a ton of traffic that make their way to the KCBI donation page on the last 4-5 days of the year. Due to this high volume of motivated donors to give to KCBI, but also to give before the tax deadline of December 31st.
For this test we wanted to see if we could add a visual cue that would show the amount of time left to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a visual thermometer to the campaign donation page resulted in a 29.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, these results should not be considered valid or used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further experiments or iterate on the design of the visual thermometer to determine its true impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience on the donation page convert donors better?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addinga. guided reading experience for the campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion because the copy makes it easier to understand what problem the organization is solving .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For the CYE campaign this year, we wanted to test into having a guided reading experience on the main campaign donation page. We’ve seen this work well in acquisition where it lays out the problem, the solution, and how the donor can help.
We wondered if running this during a high urgency campaign would work as well as it did during non campaign months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the guided reading experience on the campaign donation page resulted in a 23.6% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 46.0%, which is below the threshold of 95% needed to consider the results valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

While this test is more of a coin toss on whether it will help transactions, it did increase the average gift from $84 to $106 by adding in the subheaders to the copy.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the guided reading experience and iterate on the copy to better communicate the problem the organization is solving and the impact of donor contributions. Conducting more experiments with different variations and analyzing the results with a larger sample size will provide more reliable insights for decision-making in future campaigns.

experiments.title: Does focusing on a physical premium on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by referencing the downloaded content offer and leaning into the electronic versus physical nature of the benefit, the test version would create a more seamless donor experience and increase conversion rates by making the Sustainer benefit more prominent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2024 - 11/09/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of tailored messaging on a content offer donation page for “The People’s Pharmacy.” The goal was to determine whether referencing the content of the offer and emphasizing the electronic nature of the benefit would enhance donor conversion rates by making the experience feel more seamless and highlighting the value of becoming a Sustainer.

experiments.title: Will having a guided reading experience on the donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a guided reading experience for the main campaign cye donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the copy will be laid out in a way that makes it easier to digest and explains how the donor makes a difference.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For our CYE campaign, we wanted to see if having a clear guided reading experience on the main campaign donation page would influence people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a guided reading experience for the main campaign donation page had a positive impact on donor conversion rates, resulting in an 8.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 24.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% needed to consider the results as valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to take into account the level of statistical confidence when interpreting results. While the increase in donations is promising, it is crucial to conduct further testing to validate the impact of the guided reading experience on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other aspects of the donation page and conduct A/B tests to optimize the layout, copy, and design for improved donor engagement and conversion. By continuing to experiment and gather data, Care Net can make more informed decisions to enhance its fundraising efforts and ultimately achieve greater success in driving donations for its campaigns.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial on the donation page reinforce the need to make a gift?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating. because  the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could get more people to become donors on the giving page by having a donor testimonial on the page itself.
Adding in extra value proposition to a donation page is not a new thing to test out, but with this test specifically we wanted to see if we could present it in a different way where the testimonial stands out and is able to convert more donors because it draws the eye to it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page could potentially lead to an increase in donor conversions. While the treatment did see a 3.0% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 13.2%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95% for validity.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials on donation pages, but with variations in how they are presented or combined with other elements to determine the most effective way to leverage social proof and value proposition. Additionally, it would be important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to generate statistically significant results, which can be used with confidence to inform decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: How the placement of a Dear Listener message vs. an Impact Pop-up on a donation page impacts donor conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Impact Pop-up for FamilyLife’s calendar year-end campaign will increase donor conversion better than the Dear Listener message because the pop-up creates a more immediate sense of urgency and engagement, encouraging users to take action right away.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2023 - 12/16/2023
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, ran this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign, which began on December 2nd. Historically, FamilyLife has relied on a “Dear Listener” tactic at the end of their content—an approach that appeals directly to their audience and has proven successful in driving donations during previous high urgency campaigns. For this test, however, we sought to explore whether a different tactic could outperform this traditional method in terms of donor conversion.
To determine whether a more interactive and urgent format would yield higher conversions, we tested the Impact Pop-up against the “Dear Listener” message. The “Dear Listener” message was used in the Control group, maintaining the traditional approach where the appeal is made at the end of the content. In the Treatment group, we implemented the Impact Pop-up, which was triggered during the user’s session and emphasized the urgency of supporting the mission. The pop-up also featured an image of a family reading a Bible to reinforce the message and visually engage users.
This experiment was set up as a split A/B test in VWO, with the traffic being split 50/50 between the Control (Dear Listener) and the Treatment (Impact Pop-up). We tested these tactics on their podcast pages. Both versions used similar messaging, but the key differences were the format and the visibility of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran from December 2nd to December 16th, a two-week period, and although it did not reach the ideal 30-day threshold for full validation, the Impact Pop-up showed a promising 51.8% increase in conversions with a 67.6% likelihood of conversion (LoC) compared to the Dear Listener message. The Dear Listener generated 9 transactions, while the Impact Pop-up generated 14 transactions. While the result is compelling, it was still a bit too early to validate fully, but given the urgency of the campaign, the decision was made to roll out the Impact Pop-up.

The Impact Pop-up likely performed better because it created a sense of urgency by appearing during the user’s session, prompting immediate action. Unlike the Dear Listener message, which required users to scroll down the page to see it, the Impact Pop-up was triggered after 7 seconds of page engagement, ensuring that most visitors to the page would see it. This guaranteed visibility likely contributed to higher engagement and conversion, as users didn’t need to actively seek out the message. The emotional appeal of the image of a family reading the Bible further reinforced the urgency, making the call-to-action feel more relevant and personal for users.

Given the success of the Impact Pop-up in this initial period, we recommend rolling it out as the primary tactic for future high urgency campaigns. However, since the experiment didn’t run long enough to validate fully, we suggest retesting, continuing to monitor its performance closely, and conducting follow-up testing to further optimize the pop-up’s design and messaging. Additionally, testing with a larger sample size and longer duration could help validate its effectiveness across different user segments.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial box with a social proof headline impacts donor conversions during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial box with the headline “Make an Impact on readers like __” to the right side of FamilyLife’s donation page will increase donor conversions by providing social proof and illustrating the tangible impact of donations, thereby fostering trust and emotional connection with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2024 - 12/31/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing biblically-based resources and support to families. This experiment was conducted during their high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, a critical time for driving donor engagement and conversions.
Historically, FamilyLife’s donation pages have focused on clearly presenting the campaign’s messaging and donation options without additional elements like testimonials. For this test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating social proof could enhance donor trust and urgency.
To test this, we added a testimonial box featuring a real quote from a FamilyLife supporter, paired with a social proof headline: “Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!” This headline aimed to create a sense of community and inspire donors to contribute by showcasing the tangible impact of their support.
The Control was the standard donation page without a testimonial, and the Treatment included the testimonial box prominently placed near the donation form. This was a split A/B test built in VWO with 50/50 split traffic, ensuring an equal number of users experienced both the Control and the Treatment.
The goal of this test was to determine whether the addition of social proof could increase donor conversion rates during this critical fundraising period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Treatment featuring the testimonial box with the headline, "Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!", led to a 6.2% decrease in transactions compared to the Control at a 70% level of confidence (LoC). While the results did not validate due to insufficient data over the testing period, the downward trend suggests that the testimonial box may have negatively impacted conversions.

We hypothesize that the addition of the testimonial box could have distracted users from focusing on the primary goal: completing the donation. While the testimonial and headline aimed to build trust and provide social proof, it may have introduced unnecessary cognitive load or drawn attention away from the donation form itself. Alternatively, the headline or the quote could have lacked relevance or resonance for some users, reducing its effectiveness as a motivator.

Next Steps:

Given the negative trend, we recommend retesting this concept with adjustments:
Rework the Design: Explore a more streamlined and less visually dominant placement for the testimonial box.
 	Refine the Content: Test different quotes or headlines that may better resonate with the audience or align more closely with the campaign’s urgency.
 	Alternative Social Proof Strategies: Consider integrating testimonials in a subtler way, such as below the donation form or as part of an impact statement, to avoid distracting users.

experiments.title: How Positioning a Monthly Ask Effects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning a monthly ask to be more about the donor for the giving tuesday ask will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because positioning the ask to be more donor focused instead of transactional will create a sense of community.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For our CYE campaign this year we decided to have Giving Tuesday be it’s own micro goal.
To make Giving Tuesday stand out we created a micro goal to get 50 monthly donors in one day. Since this was strickly a monthly ask, we wondered what the best way to position the ask would be. Will having the ask be more inclusive or transactional make a difference?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that positioning a monthly donation ask to be more about the donor for Giving Tuesday can potentially lead to a lower donor conversion rate. While the treatment did see a 1.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing with different messaging and positioning strategies to see what resonates best with donors. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to increase the statistical power and confidence level of the results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the ask, the target audience, and the overall campaign messaging can also impact the effectiveness of the donation appeal.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further testing and optimization of donation asks to maximize donor conversions and support for the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How does adding value prop and description of a premium to the donation page impact conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop and description of the devotional for web visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because there is more information about the offer which leads to confidence in knowing what you will receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/06/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During November and December, PTV promotes their annual Daily Devotional. During this time, we decided to test adding copy to the donation page.
For this test, traffic came from the PTV website, email, and paid media channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop and a description of the devotional on the donation page did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group, although the statistical level of confidence was only 42.6%.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration in order to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment and the specific audience targeted.

When designing future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different versions of the value prop and description to see if there is a more effective way to communicate the benefits of the devotional. Additionally, exploring other elements of the donation page, such as the layout and design, could provide further insights into what encourages visitors to complete a transaction.

experiments.title: Which type of language (community membership or preserving free access) results in more donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that Variant 1 will result in more donations because it emphasizes the tangible perks and thank-you gifts that donors receive. The WUNC audience is often motivated by rewards and incentives, and offering gifts or exclusive benefits may create a stronger sense of immediate value and gratification.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The goal was to test which type of messaging language would make website visitors more likely to donate. Both messaging approaches were presented as slide-up prompts on the homepage of WUNC’s website.
Variant 1: Joining a Club
This approach emphasized becoming a Sustainer—a donor who gives regularly and enjoys special perks, such as thank-you gifts and prize entries. It targeted individuals who want to feel like long-term supporters of the station and value a sense of belonging to a community.
Variant 2: Keeping It Free
This approach focused on preserving WUNC’s free, paywall-free access to news and programming. It highlighted the station’s reliance on listener donations to remain freely available. The messaging aimed to appeal to individuals who value open access and might be motivated by gratitude to make a one-time contribution.
By testing these two distinct strategies, the experiment sought to identify which type of language—community membership or preserving free access—resonated more with potential donors and encouraged greater contributions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results of this experiment, Variant 1 (Joining a Club) outperformed Variant 2 (Keeping It Free) in terms of overall donation conversion rates. Variant 1 achieved a 2.6% conversion rate compared to Variant 2's 2.4% conversion rate, representing a 5.7% higher performance.
While Variant 2 emphasized preserving free access to WUNC's content, Variant 1's focus on perks and thank-you gifts appeared to resonate more with website visitors, leading to slightly higher donation rates. This suggests that offering tangible rewards and emphasizing membership benefits may be more effective at driving immediate contributions than messaging centered around maintaining free access.
Note: Although Variant 2 reportedly generated more recurring donations, this data is not validated and cannot be factored into the conclusions drawn from this experiment. Further research may be required to evaluate long-term donor retention strategies.

experiments.title: How a Slide-Up performs against an Exit-Intent during a Holiday Appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up with gift array for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because the experience is less intrusive than the pop-up, and the in-line array pre-fills the donor’s selected amount on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we conducted a re-test of the Slide-Up with gift array vs the standard Exit Intent pop-up. The goal was to determine whether there was a significant impact upon donations and revenue during a high urgency appeal – where motivation is significantly higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-up with gift array did not perform as well as the standard exit intent pop-up in terms of donations during the high urgency appeal. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 55.0%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

However, this test did help troubleshoot an earlier discrepancy between the slide-up and exit intent where traffic targeting was unfairly impacting results. In reality, when distributed to traffic properly 50/50, the two onsites performed similarly.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock on a donation page in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to the donation page will increase the donation conversion rate because it creates urgency by highlighting the time remaining until the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/01/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including a countdown clock on the donation for the final 4 days of the campaign.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the donation page: the control version without a countdown clock and the treatment version with a countdown clock. Their hypothesis was that the countdown clock would create a sense of urgency, encouraging more users to make a donation before the deadline (When the countdown clock ran out).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a countdown clock on the donation page did not have the intended impact of increasing donation conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group, which had the countdown clock, experienced a 6.6% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the effectiveness of different strategies to create urgency or incentivize donations, such as limited-time offers or matching donation challenges, to see what resonates best with donors. Conducting further A/B tests with a larger sample size and varying durations may provide more conclusive insights for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page where people can make a gift in order to become a member of Heritage. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from on-site buttons and popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the gift amount displayed saw a 0.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control version, but at a 3.92% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing different variations of the submit button language and design to see what impact they have on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run experiments for a longer duration and with more traffic to ensure reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a quiz affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a quiz will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 10/15/2024
The Heritage Foundation has an offer where people can take a quiz to test their knowledge on Marxism and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight the Marxist movement in America today. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that decreasing the gift array amounts from $50, $100, and $250 in the control group to $25, $50, and $100 in the treatment group did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, but at a 3.6% level of confidence, this result is invalid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before drawing any conclusions or making changes based on the results. This can be helped by increasing traffic to the test page. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the messaging, imagery, or targeting strategies used in the donation process. Conducting further experiments with different variables and analyzing the results with a higher level of confidence will help in making informed decisions to optimize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does customizing a gift array based on a donor’s giving history impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that customizing the gift array for donors will achieve a higher revenue per donor because the amount is specific to their giving history.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s year-end efforts, we decided to test the gift array on our donation page.
The control gift array for everyone is $25, $50, $100, $200
We used the donor’s HPC (highest previous contribution) or largest gift as the base for the treatment. Then we multiplied the amount by 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.
To execute this experiment
This organization uses HubSpot as its email marketing platform and Anedot as a donation platform, but this experiment is possible with other platforms.
First, we created new calculated contact properties within HubSpot, which took the largest gift amount and multiplied it by 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3. We also had the contact property round up to the nearest dollar so the donor would not see random amounts with cents.
Second, we reviewed Anedot’s documentation on how to pass the custom amounts.
This test can still be conducted for organizations that do not have these platforms.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the custom amounts and upload them to your email marketing platform as new fields. Then consult your donation platform documentation to see if/how to pass custom amounts.
If your donation platform does not allow custom amounts, you could break this test into buckets based on HPC. For instance, $1-50, $51-100, $101-150, $151-200, etc, or in whatever buckets best fit your organization. Then, you can AB test by sending half of the bucket to the control and half to a different donation page with a gift array that best fits that bucket’s range.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that customizing the gift array for donors based on their giving history can potentially lead to an increase in revenue per donor. In this particular case, the treatment with customized gift amounts based on the donor's highest previous contribution saw a 40.6% increase in revenue for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 79.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We will be conducting further analysis on this test to see how much more individuals gave based on the gift array.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal thermometer to a year-end donation form impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/30/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For their year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether using a donor goal thermometer on the year-end donation form will impact donor conversions. In order to hit the $320,000 goal, Avila needed 500 donors to step up and make a donation. In this experiment, the control had no thermometer; the treatment automatically updated based on gifts to that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors resulted in a 19.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. While the treatment group did outperform the control group, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.0%, falling below the typical threshold of 95% for valid results.

Moving forward, it is important to note that these results should not be used for future decision-making as they do not meet the standard threshold for statistical significance. However, the experiment does provide some insights into the potential impact of using a donor thermometer on donor conversions.

For future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the use of a donor thermometer but with adjustments to the implementation or design in order to potentially achieve statistically significant results. Additionally, considering a longer duration for the experiment and a larger sample size may also help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How adding additional value prop on a donation page impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2024 - 11/11/2024
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding some additional value prop, especially around becoming a sustainer, impacted donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors showed a positive impact on donor conversions, with a noticeable increase in Transactions for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 27.6% is below the recommended threshold of 95%, indicating that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical power to generate reliable results. Additionally, clear and measurable key performance indicators should be established to accurately assess the impact of any changes or interventions. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and variability in donor behavior.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit from adding value prop to donation forms, further experimentation with a stronger design and methodology is needed to draw meaningful conclusions and make informed decisions for future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover affects donations during a Giving Tuesday campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a homepage takeover will increase donor conversion rate by reducing friction for motivated traffic during the Giving Tuesday campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/04/2024
For The Heritage Foundation’s Giving Tuesday campaign, they wanted to test the impact of a homepage takeover that would direct visitors to their homepage on Giving Tuesday to the campaign’s donation page with a link to go to back to their intended destination.
We believed that redirecting visitors to the donation page would reduce friction for highly motivated traffic that came to give a special gift and in turn increase donor conversions and revenue.
This experiment ran for one day. Each variant received 50% of traffic from mostly organic, direct sources to The Heritage Foundation’s home page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a homepage takeover to direct visitors to a donation page on Giving Tuesday can potentially increase donor conversions. We saw a 19.6% increase in transactions for all traffic during the one-day experiment. However, with a statistical confidence level of 56.7%, these results are inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

These results may indicate that site visitors may not have been highly motivated to navigate to the Heritage homepage in order to give a gift for the Giving Tuesday campaign.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar homepage takeover experiment over a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level. This can be accomplished by running another test over the last few days of the year during the calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How referencing the new presidential administration in donation page copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating instant donation page copy to reference the new presidential administration for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because visitors will have increased context on the renewed opportunity for Heritage’s policy solutions to be implemented with the new administration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2024 - 11/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign where visitors could sign up to receive an eBook version of their Founders Almanac book. After signing up to receive the free eBook, visitors were redirected to a donation page where they could choose to give a gift.
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, and in response to the changing landscape of American policy following the presidential election, they wanted to test whether updated page copy referencing the incoming Trump administration and the work that Heritage can do because of it leads to this increase.
The control page contained copy that referenced the radical Left. The treatment page took the opportunity to take on a more hopeful tone of restoring the Founding Father’s vision that is more attainable with the newly elected administration.
The test ran for one week and each variant received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads and email prospecting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that updating instant donation page copy to reference the new presidential administration for eBook downloaders resulted in an increase in Transactions by 18.6%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.5%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

These results suggest that more timely copy referencing the new administration may not be as motivating a factor as focusing on the Left's destructive policies.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any potential fluctuations or outliers. It is also essential to carefully analyze the data and results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on them.

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout on the tributes donation array impacts conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic impact array for users on the tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it encourages interaction with the array and clearly outlines the impact of a donor’s gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that a dynamic impact array that automatically updated impact language based on the user’s gift selection would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamic impact array tested on the tribute donation page led to a 3.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 31.7%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is large enough to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more diverse data and account for any external factors that may impact the outcome.
While the initial results of the dynamic impact array seem promising, it is important to validate these findings with a more rigorous experiment before implementing any changes based on this data. Further testing with a larger sample size and a longer duration will provide more reliable insights into the impact of the dynamic impact array on donor conversion rate and revenue.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for high urgency campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running their yearly high-urgency President’s Challenge campaign. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was ultimately inconclusive. We learned that adding the donor's selected gift amount to the donation form's submit button may have a positive impact on donor conversion rate for high urgency campaigns. The treatment version, which included the gift amount in the submit button, saw a 1.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 12.5%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This means that we cannot confidently say that the increase in transactions was solely due to the change in the submit button language.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of including the gift amount in the submit button across multiple campaigns and audience segments to see if the results are consistent.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing is needed to confirm the impact of this change on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results cannot be referenced as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for eBook campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate. because  the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2024 - 11/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running ads for their Election Fraud eBook offer, where visitors fill out a form to receive a copy of the eBook and then are redirected to a donation page. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for the eBook campaign did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 7.4%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
Therefore, we should not make any conclusive decisions based on the results of this experiment. It is important to recognize that sometimes variations in performance may occur due to random chance rather than a true impact of the changes made. In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used and that statistical significance is reached before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other variations or factors that could potentially impact donor conversion rates to gain a better understanding of what drives donor behavior.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have signed up to receive an eBook will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 09/12/2024
An organization has an offer where people sign up to receive an eBook and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the amounts on the gift array may have a positive impact on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had lower gift amount options of $10, $25, and $50, saw a 9.4% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 61.6% is below the threshold of 95%, meaning that these results are invalid and may not be reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, it could be useful to test different variations of gift amounts to see which combination yields the best results in terms of donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment suggest that lowering the gift array amounts can potentially increase donor conversion rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How simplified value prop on a premium donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop for users on a premium donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it introduces them to the impact of their gift regarding the premiums specifically, rather than a more general “org-centered” value prop.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/23/2024
On Reasons to Believe’s premium donation pages, we noticed that multiple pages had an old value prop (one now replaced in newer copy) that outlined a series of bullet points demonstrating the impact of a gift. This language tended to be longer, more focused on how the org is impacted and the full mission of RTB, versus the newer premium specific value prop which simply talked about the impact of a donor’s gift regarding the premium specifically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that simplifying the value proposition on a premium donation page can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with a more focused and specific value prop regarding the impact of the donor's gift on premiums saw a 61.7% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 67.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to conduct similar tests with a larger sample size to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the value proposition to see if there are specific language or messaging that resonates best with donors on premium donation pages. By continuously testing and iterating on different approaches, we can gather more robust data and make more informed decisions to optimize donation conversions.

experiments.title: How more applicable tab titles on a donation form + an action oriented statement impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donation form tab title from recurring to monthly and implementing action-orienting statements for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the word “monthly” will better resonate with their audience and the action-oriented statement prompts the visitor to take said action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2024 - 11/21/2024
The Navigators run a monthly Scripture Memory Text challenge. After participants sign up to join the challenge, they are asked for a gift.
We noticed that The Navigators tend to use the word “monthly” rather than “recurring” however their donation form says “recurring.”
We thought that this may be causing unnecessary friction on the donation page, and we saw it as an opportunity to also test into action statements for the donation form tab titles.
Control: One-time Gift & Recurring Gift
V1: One-time Gift & Monthly Gift
V2: Make a One-time Gift & Make a Monthly Gift
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the donation form tab title from "recurring" to "monthly" did not have a positive impact on donor conversion metrics for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, with a 21.6% statistical level of confidence.

These results suggest that the original tab title of "recurring" may have resonated better with the audience, despite the preference for using the word "monthly" in other communication from The Navigators. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that these results cannot be considered statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully consider the language used in communication and donation forms, taking into account the preferences and expectations of the target audience. Additionally, it is advisable to conduct further A/B testing with a larger sample size and potentially try different variations of the tab titles to determine the most effective messaging for donor conversion.

experiments.title: How will increasing the readability on the donation page affect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the readability for the main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will give a better user expereience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2024 - 09/03/2024
The statement ‘many donors that make up the bulk of the program are over the age of 65’ reign true.
As we think about being user friendly and making the experience easier for users, it’s important to look at the demographic of those who frequent the donation pages.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we increased the readability of the donation page. For this we increased the font size, increased the margins, increased the line spacing, etc..
Here is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that increasing the readability of the donation page did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 95%, making these results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

Therefore, it is important to be cautious when implementing changes based on assumptions without conducting thorough testing. Future experimentation should continue to focus on user experience and readability but should be done so with careful monitoring and analysis of results before making any significant changes. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor demographics and preferences to tailor the donation page for the specific audience.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.

The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: Will having a guided reading experience improve transactions on a donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a guided reading expereince for the campaign donation page will achieve a higher number of conversions because the donation page will explain what the campaign proves to solve and how the donor can help in a easy to digest way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/01/2024
Each year this organization runs a a campaign that drives traffic to a main campaign donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we added a guided reading experience to the donation page that listed out the problem, the solution, and what you can do to help would increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a guided reading experience to the campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 64.3%. This indicates that the guided reading experience likely helped to better explain the problem, the solution, and how donors can help in a more easily digestible way, leading to higher conversions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of guided reading experiences on donation pages to further validate the initial results. It may also be beneficial to explore different formats or variations of the guided reading experience to see if there is an optimal design that maximizes conversions. Additionally, conducting further tests over longer periods of time with a larger sample size would provide more robust and reliable results to confidently conclude the effectiveness of the guided reading experience in driving donations.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles  for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because  the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.

Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.

While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.
The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course will increase the donation amount because people won’t feel marketed to, but realize they have the power to make the decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/28/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible. To this aim, they offer free online courses. All their courses are funded by generous donors. After a person signs up for the Revelation course they are re-directed to an instant donation page. This page thanks them for signing up and then asks then to donate.
In an attempt to increase the donor amount on this page, they wanted to test whether donor autonomous language would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course can potentially increase the donation amount. While the treatment did see a 6.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing conclusions. It may be beneficial to repeat the experiment with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the donation page to further test the impact of donor autonomous language. Additionally, it could be useful to explore other factors that may influence donation amounts, such as the design of the donation page or the timing of the donation request. By continuing to test and analyze different variables, Dallas Theological Seminary can gather more reliable data to inform their decision-making process and optimize their donation strategy.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to a campaign donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for the Fall Share campaign donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because the need is reinforced on the page in a more eye catching way when somebody lands on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign each year. This campaign draws in a ton of traffic from ads, organic, and on-air listeners.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if adding a stickybar at the top of the campaign giving page would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a humanized sticky bar to the Fall Share campaign donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

However, we did see an in the average gift when the sticky bar was present. The control saw a $293 average gift while the treatment saw a $345 average gift!

Although we might not have increased overall transactions, by having the sticky bar present on the page we did see an increase in generosity. More testing needs to be done with this before it can be rolled out.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to an acquisition donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy on the donation page for the pledge will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because there will be clarity as to what the page is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2024 - 09/09/2024
Care net is a well-known organization that has multiple offers out in the market. One of these offerings is a pledge.
The offer is set up similar to any other, a user clicks on an ad, goes to the landing page, submits the form and then is redirected to a donation page. However, we were seeing a ton of people drop off once they hit the donation page. Could this be because they don’t want to give or is it because the page is not clear in what it is?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on the Pledge donation page are:

1. Changing the copy on the donation page can have a significant impact on donor conversion rates.

2. The treatment group experienced a 22.2% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

3. The statistical confidence level of 60.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be reliable.

4. The hypothesis that clarity in the donation page copy will lead to higher conversion rates was supported by the results.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

1. Continue testing variations in copy and messaging on donation pages to optimize for clarity and donor conversion.

2. Ensure that experiments run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future hypotheses and testing strategies for donation pages across different campaigns and offerings.

4. Consider additional factors such as design, layout, and call-to-action language to further improve donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment are promising, it is important to conduct future experiments with a higher level of statistical confidence to make informed decisions for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversions.

experiments.title: How adding an image to a premium offer impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the image of the large bundle for email subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is visual reenforcement of what all is included in the bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/24/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a new book. Some of the emails also mention an upgrade to purchase a larger bundle of resources.
For this test, we decided to test adding an image of the large bundle
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding an image of the large bundle for email subscribers resulted in a 6.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 16.1%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity.

3. Therefore, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.

Specific to the PS, we saw a 120% increase in clicks to the PS link for the large bundle, however control and treatment each only received 1 large bundle donation.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is high enough to draw meaningful conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to continue testing the impact of visual elements, such as images, in email campaigns to see if they consistently lead to higher donation rates.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the donation page copy for Avila’s audience will achieve an increase in overall donations because they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/17/2024 - 09/02/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
They are currently running an offer, Root Sin. We decided to run a test changing the copy on the donation page. Recently, we updated their main donation page with more value proposition; we decided to add that to the Root Sin page to see if this would have a positive effect on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved adding a value proposition to the donation page copy, led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic by 36.2%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 24.3%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. It is also crucial to consider the timing and duration of the experiment to account for any external factors that could influence the outcomes. Additionally, further testing and iterations may be necessary to determine the impact of adding a value proposition to the donation page copy for Avila's audience accurately.
Overall, while this particular experiment did not yield conclusive results, the learnings can be used to inform future experimentation strategies and help optimize the donation process for Avila Foundation.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field to a donation form impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a phone number field to the donation page for donation page vistors will achieve a similar amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 08/09/2024
We wanted to start sending a thank you voicemail to donors after they donated, but to be able to do this we needed to start collecting phone numbers on the donation page.
