Before rolling out this change to the donation page, we wanted to make sure it wouldn’t impact the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a phone number field to the donation page resulted in a 7.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to adhere to a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an experiment. This will help to avoid making decisions that may not actually have a meaningful impact on the desired outcome.

experiments.title: How adding an icon to the donation page showing the number of gifts made so far impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in gifts and revenue to the tribute widget because it makes this tab more visible, highlights its importance, and drives more users to the widget..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 09/13/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile, it includes copy “Join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of [name].
Since migration to CB2, CaringBridge has seen low performance on this widget. We hypothesized that by driving more traffic to the tab, it would increase donor conversion rate and revenue. On the old site, this Tributes tab was it’s own designated page and the button to navigate to it included a badge icon with how many tributes were on the profile. We created two treatments, one that’s a static badge icon and another where the same badge icon animates and counts up to the total number of tributes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab on site profiles significantly increased Revenue - Tribute from the widget by 106.7%. Additionally, Tribute Tab clicks experienced a substantial increase of 9,5881%. These results were statistically significant with confidence levels of 95.6% and 100% respectively.

Moving forward, it is clear that showcasing the importance of the Tribute tab by making it more visible and engaging users with a badge icon can lead to a significant boost in donations and revenue. This insight should be taken into consideration for future experimentation and site optimizations. By continuing to test and iterate on features that drive engagement with the Tribute widget, CaringBridge can further enhance user experience and ultimately increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will Adding ACH as the Preferred Method of Payment Decrease Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by showing ACH as the preferred of payment for donors coming the monthly giving page will achieve longer donor retention in the long run because banking information expires with less frequency over credits cards. An added benefit is the reduction in credit card processing fees as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2024 - 09/23/2024
An organization worked on identifying ways to retain their monthly partners long-term. Because bank account information vs. debit/credit card information tends to change or expire less often than credit cards, will we retain our donors longer? Simultaneously, we also need to see if showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page hurts our current donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page resulted in a 6.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.2%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. This shows us an important finding. Including ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly donation page DOES NOT hurt donor conversion and can be considered for monthly giving pages as the preferred method of payment as it will help retain monthly partners longer.

experiments.title: How adding copy about giving monthly on a donation page impact recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy about why somebody should become a monthly donor for people who take the survey will achieve more recurring donations because it explains the value of becoming a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 05/08/2024
Care Net has a survey we were running in Facebook. The instant donation page for this offer didn’t have any language around why a donor should give monthly instead of once. We wanted to test how adding language about giving month would impact the recurring gifts made on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment version, which added copy about why someone should become a monthly donor, resulted in a 32.1% decrease in Recurring Donations for All Traffic with a 33.1% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the language used in donation appeals and how it may impact donor behavior. Further testing with different messaging strategies and potentially adjusting the copy to better communicate the value of becoming a monthly donor may help in identifying the most effective approach for increasing recurring donations.

experiments.title: How a $25 callout on the Non-Tribute donation age impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $25 callout  for the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because it reinforces the impact of the user’s gift in a tangible way — powering a user’s site..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 09/06/2024
For CaringBridge’s non-tribute donation page (main donate) we hypothesized that including a $25 callout over the gift array, leveraging language that’s been used successfully in other locations/widgets on the site, would improve overall revenue and transactions on main donate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a $25 callout for the non-tribute donation page resulted in a 3.0% increase in revenue for all traffic but with a statistical level of confidence of only 54%.
It is likely that this callout is not making a significant impact either way, because in both the control and treatment, the average gift is already above the $25 callout.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiments hould not be be considered valid. It is essential to ensure that any future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the hypothesis and experiment design to potentially achieve more significant and reliable results. Testing different variations, such as adjusting the language or positioning of the callout, could also help to optimize future experiments.

experiments.title: How mentioning reach on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by pointing out the reach due to the gift size for potential fiscal year end donors will achieve higher conversion rate because they know the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2023 - 10/02/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
In an effort to increase donor conversions for fiscal year end donations we decided to test calling out how many people would be reached with the gospel based on their gift size.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pointing out the reach due to the gift size for potential donors can potentially increase conversion rates. The treatment version, which included language highlighting the impact of the gift on reaching people with the gospel, saw a 15.1% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered completely valid.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of highlighting the reach of donations on conversion rates, but with a larger sample size and a longer duration. Additionally, it may be helpful to refine the messaging and design of the treatment version to further enhance its impact. Overall, this experiment provides a promising indication that emphasizing the impact of donations can positively influence donor conversions.

A break down on conversions

Only 24% of donations and 12% of revenue came from nondonors, implying that existing donors are more motivated to give during a FYE campaign.

Existing donors had an average gift of $84.38 while nondonors had an average gift of $41.26. 

Emails 1 and 5 (the final day) emails received the most donations of all the emails. Accounting for 55% of total revenue and 52% of gifts.

Total gifts: 328

Total revenue: $25,189.00

While the treatment for this test did not win out in a valid manner. This was the first full FYE campaign conducted by PTV and based on the results, we consider this a success.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline  for August Scripture Memory donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the visitor will be less likely to perceive the donation page as a confirmation page and understand there is another step to take.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2024 - 08/20/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a monthly text campaign to memorize scripture. Each month, participants are sent new methods to memorize a new verse. These have been really successful campaigns, but we wanted to continue optimizing the funnel process to see further growth. This test ran on the donation page directly after signing up to participate in the campaign.
For their August campaign, we decided on testing a continuation headline which removes the “Thank you” out of the headline. Our hypothesis was that removing this phrase and adding a “would you consider” would increase donor conversion metrics because the donation page visitor would not perceive the donation page as a confirmation page.
Control Headline – “YOU’RE IN! Thank you for joining us for our Scripture Memory Challenge!”
Treatment Headline – “You’re in! Now, would you consider making a gift so even more believers can experience this Scripture memory challenge?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included a continuation headline on the donation page without the "Thank you" phrase, showed a positive impact on donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in an 11.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 55.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As well as a 23.3% lift in overall revenue at an 86% level of confidence.
Although the results cannot be deemed statistically significant, it is still valuable to consider the potential impact of the continuation headline on donor conversion as well as the positive direction both of these metrics were heading.  In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the headline or explore other elements on the donation page that could enhance donor conversion rates. Additionally, increasing the sample size and running the experiment for a longer duration may help achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

experiments.title: How adding project-specific value proposition language to a donation page affects donor conversion rate (Pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a donation page and making it a single column experience for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 10/13/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift.
This is the second iteration of this experiment because we noticed that desktop experienced a large decrease in conversions. So, we hypothesized that we could use the same language but change the layout of the page so that it was a single-column experience so that the user could read through all the value prop language before making a decision
experiments.keyLearnings: Our first results showed a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. However our secondary hypothesis did not prove to be true. This experiment resulted in a 7% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 46% - meaning there is not statistical difference in the treatment. Upon closer examination it seems as if the donation form inadvertently got changed from the control's donation form. The header of the form changed and the gift designation selection was removed. These additional variables may have also impacted the results of our experiment.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How changing the picture of a premium resources impacted donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the picture on a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 07/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  Every quarter they publish the Hoover Digest, which is a collection of essays from 30 of their scholars. This offer is one of their best performing acquisition offers. We’ve tested into a premium test for the content offer where we ask donors for a gift of $100 or more to get all 4 editions of the Digest in the mail for the next year.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the image of the Digest itself to something that featured the current cover would make an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the picture on a premium offer for content downloaders led to a 29.6% increase in revenue for all traffic. This implies that the new image featuring the current cover of the Hoover Digest had a positive impact on donor conversions.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 71.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered entirely reliable and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of using updated images or visuals in premium offers to see if they consistently lead to higher conversion rates. It is also essential to ensure that experiments are run for longer durations to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, considering other elements such as copywriting, call-to-action buttons, and layout design in future experiments could provide further insights into what drives donor conversions effectively.

experiments.title: How switching the order in which information is presented affects conversion rate

rows.name: Switch order of the Main Donation Page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by switching the order in which information is presented for our donors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2021 - 10/24/2021
We tested this on FamilyLife’s main donation page. We noticed that when filling out your information, the last step to complete the donation process is adding your card information. We suspected that this could potentially cause friction due to all the internal decisions we are already making before hitting “make my gift”. Could be not having your card ready to fill out that information or perhaps your willingness to make that “commitment” decreases as you fill in other information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 57.3% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the impact of a gift impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including this statement of value proposition for visitors will achieve an increase in the number of transactions because the visitor will be reminded about the impact that their gift could make within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2023 - 11/27/2023
In an effort to increase the number of donations for ACI, we wanted to test adding additional value proposition within the donation form. We tested this on a multitude of pages with varying content and offers ranging from the homepage donation page, pro-life pledge donation page, education survey donation page, and a few others. With this being said, each different topic had a different value proposition statement that was relevant. Here are the statements that we used:
Pro-life: Your donation today will help fight for the rights of unborn children across the nation.
Education: Your ongoing support will help educate thousands of children on the true principles of America’s founding throughout the year.
General: Your recurring monthly gift will defend our nation’s guiding principles of life, liberty, community, and faith 365 days a year.
Little Patriots: The entirety of your gift will directly support our efforts to provide free curricula and cartoons that teach true American history through our Little Patriots program.
This statement was shown under the tabbed options of “One-time” or “Monthly,” and it was shown regardless of which tab the visitor had selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition statement within the donation form had a slightly positive impact on the conversion rate for transactions at 4.9%.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 67.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the impact of value proposition statements on donation conversions, using larger sample sizes and longer experiment durations. Additionally, it would be valuable to determine whether the recurring specific statements were more or less beneficial in the future.

By conducting more rigorous experiments and increasing the statistical level of confidence, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights on how to optimize the donation form and increase the number of donations for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to the main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators historically had very little copy on their primary donation page. As they had set an initiative to generate more “where most needed” gifts (as opposed to staff gifts), they knew they might need to convince donors who arrived on the page to make a gift. However, they did not want to do this at the expense of staff giving. They developed a value proposition that would encapsulate the mission of the organization and placed it on the page. During the month of December they ran an experiment to see how the additional value proposition affected project giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though there was a slight increase in project giving (that was not statistically valid), the added copy to the page produced across-the-board decreases in overall giving, which led them to end the experiment. This showed that the additional copy might have confused staff donors, even if it did motivate more people to make one-time gifts.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 21.0% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.
Desktop visitors had a 4.9% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 7.4% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 7.1% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Direct Traffic visitors had an 11.8% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 12.8% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.7% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using a video on a high urgency campaign donation page impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the transcript of a video on a donation page for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/17/2022
Reasons to Believe launched their fiscal year end campaign and in their launch email they were leading their email recipients to a donation page with a video. Past experiments with other organizations have shown that a video on a donation page can actually add friction to the giving experience. The reason being that many people are unlikely to watch through a whole video and they’re more likely to read through some copy.
For this email we sent half of their list to a donation page with the video (the control) and the other half of the list to a donation page with a transcript of the content of the video (the treatment). Our hypothesis is that more people would convert on the donation page with the transcript of the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 56% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%. Something to note is that on the email itself, RTB used different copy on the CTA button. The control had "Watch my video" and the treatment had "Read my note." When looking at the click thru rate between each button we saw a valid 28% decrease in clicks with a level of confidence of 99%. In a sense, 2 variables were tested in this experiment: the call to action and the donation page itself.

It's likely that RTB's audience is conditioned to watch videos for their content and throughout their high urgency campaigns. However it seems as more traffic was pushed to the video donation page because of the different CTA. Watching a video may sound more appealing than to read something.

It's recommended that this is retested in a channel that gets more traffic and that has the same CTA button, like a sticky bar. This will isolate one variable and get us more definitive results.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary). On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary). On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation page for Giving Tuesday affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clarity on the donation page will increase donor conversion rate because people have a clear understanding to what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/04/2023
For Giving Tuesday this year, we wanted to test additional clarity on the donation page to see how IMB’s donor base would react.
The control offers an emotional appeal that focuses on the history of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering and emphasizes the tradition of the offering. On the other hand, the treatment page focuses on a time sensitive appeal by mentioning Giving Tuesday specifically, and lays out a more straightforward and urgent need for financial support.
We believe that the added clarity will allow for greater comprehension of the donation page leading to an increase in performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity on the donation page did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control page with a drop of 6.3% with 35% LOC.

Based on this outcome, it is necessary to retest this theory before implementing it. However, the current results suggest that IMB's audience does not respond positively to urgent time-bound appeals, but rather they prefer the emotional appeal and tradition of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering, as it seems to resonate more with the donor base.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that could potentially increase donor conversion rate, such as different messaging or design elements on the donation page. It is also important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is above 95% in order to make valid conclusions from the experiment results.

experiments.title: How loss aversion language on a donation page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the emphasis on their contribution will create a stronger sense of urgency and connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 02/02/2024
In an effort to increase our conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar donation page, we wanted to test the inclusion of loss aversion language to see how their audience would respond.
Our hypothesis was that by leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion, we believe that potential donors will be more motivated to take action. Specifically, we hypothesize that framing the donation as an opportunity to prevent missed chances and emphasizing the tangible impact of their contribution will create a stronger sense of urgency and connection. This heightened emotional response is expected to increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion did not lead to the desired increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which included loss aversion language, actually saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group as well as an even larger decrease in revenue and average gift.

While this experiment did not validate, it was trending enough in a negative direction that we decided to turn it off. It can be concluded that IMB's audience did not respond well to the loss aversion language.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies or messaging approaches to increase donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be helpful to conduct further research and analysis to understand why the loss aversion language did not have the expected effect. This will allow for more informed decision making and potential adjustments to the messaging strategy in future experiments.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a high-urgency donation page through a slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a slideout for Eagle Lake 2 donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/26/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 during June and July to raise money for their Eagle Lake camps.
We have seen success running social proof-based tests for other clients, so we were anxious to run it for The Navigators.
For this test, the treatment version displayed a pop-out of a statement that read “FirstName from State just donated $GiftAmount”
All the information displayed was data pulled from donors to the campaign. It is also worth noting, that these contacts were pulled manually due to our lack of ability to install a pixel to track conversions on their donation pages.
Our hypothesis was that the pop-out would increase the amount of social proof experienced by the visitor on the page which would then lead to an increase in our donor conversion metrics by increasing a sense of urgency and need to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding social proof in the form of a slideout for the Eagle Lake 2 donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 34.4%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid, and it would be unwise to make any decisions based on them. However, we can assume that the addition of social proof may have a negative consequence in terms of donor acquisition metrics. Therefore, we will need to test this concept again to come to a clear conclusion.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result. Additionally, it might be beneficial to conduct qualitative research to understand why the  slideout did not have the desired effect. This could provide valuable insights that can be used to design more effective experiments in the future.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up can effect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
We have previously conducted this experiment and wanted to see if we used a different image if it affects donor acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage increased transactions for all traffic by 25.6%. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.1%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
This experiment did result in a positive outcome when compared to previous experiments, indicating that using this image may be the way to go moving forward.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration or increasing the number of participants. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variables, such as using different images or messages in the pop-up, to determine their impact on donor acquisition rates.
Overall, while the initial results of the experiment were promising, it is important to prioritize statistical rigor in order to draw accurate conclusions and make informed decisions for future strategies aimed at increasing donor support for WUNC.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase sponsorship conversion by prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/03/2021
Save the Children has thousands of children that people can choose to sponsor at any given time. As users navigate throughout the site, they can sort and filter on certain variables to find the child they want to sponsor: location, gender, age, and by birth month. Many people used the search widgets to filter through children, because the number of children available was overwhelming—even after filtering through multiple searches. In an effort to make the decision process easier for an end user, we hypothesized that prioritizing a single child at the top of the page may increase conversion. To test into this, we developed a treatment that pulled out a single child, that remained static, that used personal language and information of that child. The variation said, “Sponsor Me!” and gave users the ability to quickly add that child to their cart to begin the sponsorship. All sponsorship cart functionality of the variation remained consistent with the control, the variation simply just visually highlighted one child at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in conversion from the treatment variation. Additionally, in the initial weeks of the experiment running, the results were, in fact, positively valid. As the experiment continued to run, the results remained positive, but loss the strength of its validity. That said, this experiment reveals something interesting about the sponsors: while we might think that they want more options to choose, they actually are more likely to choose a child when we choose the child for them. The is a result of removing friction to the decision process.

One other result of this experiment is that we also saw a directional lift in overall purchases from this page. Yes, sponsorship conversion is the primary goal of this page, but it is important to ensure that any tactic we would apply to the page to impact that metric doesn't negatively impact the overall conversion.

Because we initially saw valid results with this experiment, which then turned to directional, we believe that this tactic can improve sponsorship conversion, but the principle may need to be strengthened through the value proposition of the prioritized child. This is the next phase of testing for this feature in an effort to validate the directional results.

experiments.title: Will a “Give” button have a higher donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing donate to give for the sticky button will achieve a higher donor conversion because give could be more cohesive with the messaging during year end.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the Donate sticky button on the side of the web pages to say “Give” would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the sticky button from "Donate" to "Give" did not achieve a higher donor conversion. In fact, it resulted in a decrease in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How reducing organizational references and using donor-centric language impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language for course subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is stronger appeal to give based on value and belief alignment with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2024 - 07/19/2024
When evaluating a donation page that appeared for course subscribers, we wondered if the language was too centered around the organization and not focused on the page viewer. We hypothesized we could convince more potential donors to say “yes” to the ask if we treated the donation page like a conversation and used the messaging to align with the page visitors’ beliefs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor-centric language for course subscribers can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion. The treatment group in the A/B experiment saw a 12.8% increase in one-time donations for all traffic, at 43% level of confidence. Results of past tests of this concept suggest that there is a stronger appeal to give when the messaging aligns with the page visitors' beliefs and values, but this test did not validate.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue exploring the use of donor-centric language and messaging that focuses on the values and beliefs of the potential donors. Additionally, incorporating feedback from donors and continuously iterating on the messaging based on donor responses could also help in optimizing donation pages for increased conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding emphasis on recurring giving impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing recurring giving for donation page vistors will achieve more recurring gifts because it encourages donors to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/18/2024
Luther Seminary was running their Fiscal Year End campaign. While writing copy for this campaign, we decided we wanted to test how emphasizing recurring giving on their FYE donation page giving impacted recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing recurring giving on the donation page led to an increase in recurring donations for Luther Seminary. Despite the positive outcome, the results are not statistically significant with only a 43.3% level of confidence. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. This will help in making informed decisions based on reliable data.

experiments.title: How Adding the Impact of the Individual Donor Makes Affects Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the impact that one donor makes for the FYE emails will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the solution is whittled down where the one donor is the hero.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For the test, we wanted to see if adding a paragraph saying the impact that the reader will make increases the motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a paragraph explaining the impact that one donor makes can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 40.7% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that donors may be more motivated to give when they understand the direct impact of their contribution. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 78.2%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of highlighting the donor's role as the hero in the narrative. It may also be helpful to explore other ways of emphasizing the donor's impact, such as highlighting specific success stories or showcasing the tangible outcomes of donations. Additionally, ensuring that future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence will be crucial in making informed decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How shortening copy on a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for instant donation page visitors will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because the donor will be able to see the donation form right away, which takes away friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 07/05/2024
ACI has a survey that has been running in acquisition for a while. It was preforming well, but we were wanting to refresh the copy. We decided to test the donation page, because the copy on it was long. We tested shorter copy, so it all could fit on the page without the user having to scroll. That way, when the user arrives on the page, they can see the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the copy on the instant donation page did not lead to an increase in conversion rate for one-time donations. The treatment group actually experienced a 6.3% decrease in conversion rate compared to the control group.

Since the statistical confidence level of 37.0% is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered reliable or valid for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variables or elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. By continuing to experiment and gather data, we can make more informed decisions on how to optimize the donation page for better conversion rates.

experiments.title: How an embed form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an embedded donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an embedded form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the embedded form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an embedded donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

In terms of revenue, the control produced $3,555 in revenue and the treatment produced $1930. Therefore the control had an average gift of $148 and the treatment produced an average gift of $77. This produced a 44.8% drop in revenue at a 65.3% level of confidence, also considered invalid.

Due to the lack of validity in results, these results indicate that the embedded made little to no difference in the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience.

In future experimentation, we plan to continue testing ways to optimize IMB's donation process and increase our donor conversion metrics for the organization.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson for donation page vistors will achieve more transactions because there is social proof that what ACI does is approved by Trump.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2024 - 05/15/2024
ACI’s Dr. Carson has a relationship with former president Trump. Since ACI’s audience is mainly conservatives, we wanted to try using a quote from Trump about Dr. Carson on a few of ACI’s instant donation pages. We created a sticky bar that had the quote and a photo of Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson on the donation page did not result in an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only 38.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide valid and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the audience and their preferences when incorporating endorsements or quotes from public figures. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies or elements to understand what resonates best with ACI's conservative audience. A larger sample size and further testing could provide more conclusive insights for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How a embedded form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an IDonate embed donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an IDonate embed form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the IDonate embed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an IDonate embed donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

These results indicate that the IDonate embed made little to no difference on the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience. It is important to note that the revenue is not accurate in the above graph due to the fact that revenue from the IDonate Embed does not flow into Winston.

In future experimentation, we plan to continue testing ways to optimize IMB's donation process and increase our donor conversion metrics for the organization.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form tab affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s monthly gift tab and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2024 - 06/11/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
Wondering whether this concept would appeal to donors on another direct appeal donation page, they also tested this flag image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button, which led to a decrease in donations at a near-valid level of confidence.
As a follow-up experiment on the direct appeal donation page, they wanted to test whether the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, namely recurring donations.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's monthly gift tab and submit button did not lead to a valid increase in donations, specifically recurring donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 40.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of 78.8% is below the valid threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable.

The treatment also resulted in just a 0.17% increase in recurring donations at a 68.3% level of confidence. With such a small sample size, these results cannot be relied upon to inform future action.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a statistically valid result. Additionally, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as flag icons on donor behavior. Other ideas for follow-up testing include testing a different icon than an American flag or testing this concept on a donation page following a content offer.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount to a main donation page form submit button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for main donation page donors will achieve an increase in donation rate because increased clarity and donor autonomy will persuade donors to follow through with making their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase donation rate on their main donation page, The Heritage Foundation wondered whether adding the donor’s gift amount in the donation form submit button would increase clarity and donor autonomy and lead to this increase.
The control form submit button read “MAKE MY GIFT” while the treatment button read “MAKE MY GIFT OF $(amount)”.
Each variant in this A/B experiment received 50% of traffic which ran for 24 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button on the main donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.0%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Future experimentation should include running this test on a donation page that receives more traffic, such as a content offer.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the submit button text or other elements on the donation page to identify what truly resonates with donors and leads to an increase in donations.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small amount of people donate after requesting an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a free ebook offer for “Guide to the Constitution” for several years. Due to how long this offer has been in the marketplace, we’ve seen ICR slowly decline.
Because of this, we wanted to test highlighting the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take download the ebook are willing to take the next step and donate.” They’ve ran this experiment for other offers and have seen valid lifts in ICR.
This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the fact that only a small minority of people donate for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 32% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is only 66%, therefore these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing different messaging and strategies to determine what resonates best with potential donors. It is possible that highlighting the low donation rate may not have had the intended effect on the target audience. Future experiments could explore different ways to appeal to the ideal donor and emphasize the impact of their contribution, rather than focusing on the low donation rate. By continuing to test and iterate, The Heritage Foundation can refine its approach to maximize conversions and ultimately increase donations.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to an instant donation page through a FOMO slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a free ebook offer through multiple paid advertising channels.
They had previously tested a FOMO slideout on their ‘Renew Membership’ donation page but did not see any valid results in terms of donor conversion. They wanted to test how this concept would perform on an acquisition donation page.
The FOMO slideout pulled the most recent donations from that donation page and slid out every 10 seconds showing a different donor’s first name and state saying they just donated to The Heritage Foundation.
This test was ran for 20 days and traffic was split 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 2.3%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to detect any significant differences between the control and treatment groups. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the targeting of the paid advertising channels. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the FOMO slideout on different types of pages or with different messaging to see if it has a different impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline affect donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for ebook downloaders will increase the donor conversion rate because we’re providing clarity on their next steps and sparking curiosity by capitalizing on the visitor’s heightened attention and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously seen lifts in ICR when removing ‘thank you’ from the headline and instead, clarifying on what the users next steps are. Because of this, we wanted to test this concept on their Guide to the Constitution donation page. This instant donation page follows a free ebook download landing page.
Traffic was split 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a continuation headline for ebook downloaders may have a positive impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment version, which clarified the next steps for the user and prompted them to consider donating, saw a 2.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical confidence level of 6.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test this concept with a larger sample size or a longer duration to determine if the trend towards increased donor conversions holds true. It is important to always prioritize statistically significant results with a high level of confidence when making decisions based on experimentation outcomes.

experiments.title: How Adding More Value Prop to a Campaign Donation Page Affects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the donaton page for the See Life campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion because the value of what is on the page will increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2024 - 05/15/2024
Focus on the Family runs a SeeLife campaign each year that receives a ton of traffic to its campaign donation page. The original campaign donation page was very short and did not have a ton of value proposition on it. We wondered if adding more value proposition, and length to the test could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more value proposition to the donation page for the See Life campaign did not result in a higher donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 76.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the donation page with varying levels of value proposition to see what resonates best with donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donor conversion, such as the overall user experience, the donation process, and the clarity of messaging on the page. By conducting thorough and valid experiments, Focus on the Family can gather meaningful insights to optimize their donation pages and ultimately increase donor conversion in the future.

experiments.title: How reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on donation pages impacted revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our video series acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the smaller button will provide less of a competition for the donor’s attention as they’re making their gift, and this means that the friction will be reduced.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 06/26/2024
The team at Focus on the Family (FOTF) has been testing various elements of their donation pages to determine which factors could improve donor conversion and revenue. One element that we wanted to test was the Express Checkout button that appears in the donation form below the gift array. Users who click this button can choose to donate through Google Pay and the idea is that this makes the donation process easier and thus increases donor conversion. However, we wondered if this button could serve as a distraction during the donation process and actually negatively affect donor conversion.
FOTF first tested removing the Express Checkout button on their main donation page and the results were very flat. They then tested whether reducing the Express Checkout button’s size would drive a different outcome. This test found that the smaller button size led to a 21.3% increase in transactions, with a 95.7% level of confidence.
The purpose of this experiment is to see if that lift in transactions will occur when we test the smaller button on FOTF’s video series acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were completely flat; we didn't see any significant changes in revenue or transactions between the control and treatment. This means that, even though we didn't see any statistically-significant improvement on these metrics, the smaller button didn't hurt our donor conversion or average gift.

Because the smaller button saw a positive lift on Focus on the Family's other donation pages -- and did not harm their acquisition donation page conversion -- it's recommended that the smaller button treatment be rolled out on all of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How Adding a Premium to the Winter Digest Affects ICR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium  for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate  because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 05/14/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The user gets a free digital version but this test upsells them on 4 physical versions for $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version, which offered physical copies of the digest for a $100 donation, resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 13.8% compared to the control version. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.
Therefore, based on these findings, it cannot be conclusively stated that the addition of a premium for donation page viewers led to a significant impact on conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the offer and possibly testing different variations to see if there is a more effective way to encourage donations without negatively impacting transaction rates. It is important to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficiently long period with a large enough sample size to obtain statistically significant results that can be used for making informed decisions.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for survey takers will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 05/14/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Voter Fraud Survey offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that clarifying the language in the tab titles of a donation form can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates, both for one-time donations and recurring donations. The treatment version, which used clearer language ("Make a One-Time Gift", "Make a Monthly Gift"), saw a slight 0.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 2.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% to consider the results valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Interestingly, the same treatment did lead to an increase in email conversion rate on a donation page following an ebook offer. This suggests that the treatment tab titles may resonate more strongly with people downloading an ebook as opposed to people who have taken a survey.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of language clarity in survey donation forms on conversion rates, ensuring that statistical significance is achieved to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the design, layout, and call-to-action language in donation forms could also be valuable for optimizing conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the Digest Premium Offer Affected ICR on the Trickle Down Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2024 - 05/17/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The NextAfter team tried this test on the Hoover Digest Donation Page, and it performed well, so it was implemented on other offers to test if it would improve ICR on more than just the Hoover Digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a premium offer on the donation page can potentially lead to an increase in transactions for the Hoover Institution. In this case, the treatment group experienced a 34.8% increase in transactions compared to the control group. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 74.0% is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, it cannot be deemed as a conclusive result. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing the inclusion of premium offers on the donation page to see if the trend of increased transactions holds true with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other elements such as different types of premiums or messaging strategies to further optimize the donation page and ultimately improve conversion rates for the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How the Digest Premium Offer Affected ICR on the Dueling Populism Offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2024 - 05/17/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The NextAfter team tried this test on the Hoover Digest Donation Page, and it performed well, so it was implemented on other offers to test if it would improve ICR on more than just the Hoover Digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a premium to the donation page for viewers may not necessarily increase the instant conversion rate. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 60.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

Therefore, based on these results, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this particular experiment. It is important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and possibly different variations to truly understand the impact of including a premium on donation page viewers.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies for increasing conversion rates, while ensuring that the sample size is sufficient and the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. It is also essential to consider other factors that may influence donor behavior and tailor experiments accordingly.

experiments.title: How a multi-step form impact donation conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a single step form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversions because previous research suggests that well-optimized single step form out performs a multi-step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2024
In this experiment, George W Bush Presidential Center had just migrated to the iDonate platform. Their previous donation page used a long-form, single page donation form. When migrating to the iDonate platform, they opted to roll-out a more streamlined multi-step donation form—but they wanted to test the impact of the multi-step widget versus a single step form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that in this specific case, the single step form for main donation page visitors did not achieve an increase in conversions as hypothesized. But with a level of confidence of 52%, the likelihood is that there is no significant difference in performance between the control and the treatment.

Bush Center can move forward knowing the moving to a multi-step for is unlikely to harm results. In fact, there's a chance that it may be the more effective treatment.

With low conversion volume on the donation form, future experimentation should be focused on driving more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Letter Treatment on a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized treatment of the donation page that looks like a letter from a Practice Manager for users on the Mother’s Day campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it humanizes and personalizes the appeal to come from a real person who is working at the centers impacted. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, we tested a humanized experience in various channels of the campaign. For the donation page, we hypothesized that updating the intro copy and end of the donation page to look like a letter from Amy, a Practice Manager, would help humanize the experience, personalize the appeal, and as a result improve donor conversion. It’s important to note that aside from the intro and sign-off, no other copy was changed on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The humanized treatment in the form of a letter from a Practice Manager on the donation page for the Mother's Day campaign resulted in a 27.8% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. While this humanized treatment showed promise in increasing donor conversion, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the results. Other metrics to observe are revenue and recurring.

Revenue saw slight improvements in average gift but below an 85% level of significance:

Control: $1,442 from 26 Gifts | $55 AVG Gift

Treatment $1,669 from 27 Gifts | $61 AVG Gift

Recurring:

Control: 716 Samples | 2 Recurring Donations | .3% Conversion

Treatment 655 Samples | 4 Recurring Donations | .6% Conv Rat | 118% Relative Difference | 63.9% Significance

experiments.title: How clarity around the problem and expanded value prop on main donate impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increase clarity around the problem and expanding value prop copy to specify how RTB is the solution for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because users will not only have better clarity around the problem RTB solves but also the impact a gift makes towards solving it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/26/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s main donation page, we believe there was an opportunity to leverage the value prop language we’d seen perform well on acquisition donation page and in campaigns. We hypothesized that adding more clarity around what the problem is and better illustrating how a gift to RTB helps solve it would improve conversion rate. A few specific callouts are the more action-oriented headline versus “Support Reasons to Believe.
