The inclusion of details about what types of questions RTB answers, whose writing the resources, and finally what’s really at stake when these questions go unanswered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing clarity around the problem and expanding the value proposition copy to specify how Reasons to Believe addresses the users' needs can significantly impact donor conversion rates and revenue generated. While donor conversion rate remained similar between control and treatment, the latter did show a substantial 103.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.5%, below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing variations of the value proposition copy and messaging to determine the most effective way to communicate the impact of donations to Reasons to Believe. Additionally, conducting experiments with larger sample sizes or running the test for a longer period may help increase the statistical confidence level of the results. Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that focusing on clearly articulating the problem and how RTB provides a solution can have a positive impact on donor conversions.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the titles of a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for previous members will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a membership renewal page where previous members of the organization can renew their membership either through a monthly gift or a one-time gift.
They wanted to test adding clarity to the titles of the donation form to see if it would increase the recurring conversion rate and one-time donor conversion rate.
The control monthly tab is labeled “Join Leaders Club” and the one-time tab is labeled “Single Gift.” For the treatment, we clarified on the monthly tab that they could “Make a monthly donation” and for the one-time tab they could “Make a one-time donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding clarity to the titles of the donation form tabs did not have an effect on donor conversion rates for one-time or monthly giving. We saw that clarifying the monthly tab to "Make a monthly donation" led to an 11.8% increase in recurring transactions but with only a 19% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 5.4% lift in one-time giving, but with only a 13% level of confidence.

This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the donation form titles to further optimize conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results. By continuously testing and refining different elements of the donation process, the organization can work towards maximizing donor conversion rates and ultimately increasing support for its cause.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because   they will be presented with the opportunity to know what impact a monthly gift can have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page on their website where people can join the organization as a member by making a one-time or recurring donation, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through new memberships.
On the donation page, users have the option to make a one-time or monthly gift, but if the user chooses one-time and makes a gift under $100, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Make my gift” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what impact a monthly gift will have.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift had no effect on conversion rates for monthly or one-time giving. The treatment group saw a 32% decrease in Transactions - Monthly, but this decrease is only based on 5 recurring transactions and only had a 33% level of confidence. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.
Additionally, we saw a 15% lift in one-time giving but with only a 38% level of confidence.
The key takeaways are that the recurring interrupter will most likely not hurt one-time or monthly giving conversion rates, but we do not have enough data to know if it will produce a lift of any kind.
In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the donation interrupter with a larger sample size and over a longer period to determine its true impact on recurring donor conversion rates. Additionally, considering other factors such as user experience, messaging, and timing of the interrupter could provide valuable insights for optimizing donation processes and increasing donor engagement.

experiments.title: How adding a headline impacts conversions pt2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headling for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because there is additional clarity in the offer. because there is additional clarity in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month, PTV promotes a different book with a gift of a generous amount.
For April, we decided to test adding a headline to the donation page. We have seen with other organizations that a headline can drive clarity and, therefore, drive results as it presents clear, brief, easy-to-read information.
In a previous experiment we saw a decrease in performance however we think that the headline was not appealing to the PTV audience. Therefore we are retesting this hypothesis but with a different headline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a headline to the donation page can potentially increase transactions, as evidenced by the 5.9% increase in donations for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 44.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid or reliable for making future decisions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the outcome, such as the appeal and relevance of the headline to the audience. Future experimentation should focus on refining the headline to better resonate with the PTV audience and conducting the test with a larger sample size to increase the statistical power of the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of headlines to determine which one is most effective in driving donations.

experiments.title: How more concise copy on a content offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the story from the donation page  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate  because we’ve streamlined the message to focus on The Navigators’ mission and purpose. Without the narrative, potential donors can swiftly grasp crucial information without distraction, reducing the risk of donor fatigue and ensuring a more prominent and compelling call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2024 - 05/06/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion on a content offer funnel, we decided to run a copy test on the donation page.
The control donation page features a story about the founder of The Navigators. While the story is relevant to the organization, we felt that it added unnecessary friction to the page because it was irrelevant to the offer.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the story from the donation page will increase the donor conversion rate because we’ve streamlined the message to focus on The Navigators’ mission and purpose. Without the narrative, potential donors can swiftly grasp crucial information without distraction, reducing the risk of donor fatigue and ensuring a more prominent and compelling call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the founder story from the donation page led to a 34.2% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control version with the story performed better. However, with a statistical confidence level of 85%, these results are not considered valid or reliable for decision-making.
While these results were not considered valid, they do suggest that The Navigators audience prefers receiving the backstory and the meaning of the organization even within content offer funnels which is opposite of what we initially hypothesized.
Due to the fact that these results were trending in a negative direction, we chose to end the test and keep the all traffic going to the control.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the impact of storytelling and personal connections in donation pages, as these elements can sometimes resonate strongly with potential donors and influence their decision to contribute. Further testing could explore how to incorporate storytelling effectively while still maintaining a clear and compelling call to action.

experiments.title: How using shorter, more conversational language on a content offer instant donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter and more conversational language on an instant offer donation page will increase donor conversion by removing friction, particularly for mobile audiences.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2024 - 04/04/2024
Focus on the Family uses dozens of free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For the last year, their offer instant donation pages have all used the same template sharing “5 things you should know.” Reviewing this design, their team felt it could have been more conversational and shorter, especially on mobile devices.
We created a shorter, “3 things you should know” template used VWO to split donation page traffic for their popular Kingdom Mindset offer. The test ran a full month.
Our hope was to at least find no negative impacts from the treatment as it was preferred by Focus’ team and ours. Ideally, we want to see if we could validate the increase.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During the first three weeks, we experienced a significant increase in donor conversion, hovering around an 80% or greater confidence level. However, in the final week of the test, that confidence diminished.
When we examined the results, we saw that every visitor segment and device category preferred the new version to the control, but again, at a lower than 95% Confidence level. Mobile audiences saw the largest increase in donor conversion at 25% over control but there were only 5 donations in the mobile segment.
The Focus team decided to continue testing the new, shorter version and leave behind the longer one since they did not experience a negative result during the test and overall preferred the way the new template looked on mobile devices. We will continue to test and refine this new template.

experiments.title: How Changing the Headlines on GOAA/SOAA Pages to be focused on the value of the content vs the organization impacted donor transaction rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headlines to be more value focused for the GOAA/SOAA pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because traffic coming to these pages are more focused on receiving the premium being offered rather than the organizational value prop.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2024
Focus on the Family have GOAA/SOAA (gift of any amount, sustainer of any amount) pages where you can make a donation of any amount and receive the premium that is being offered. These pages are often called out on their broadcast for specific days. Usually the headline is focused around the organization. However, we wondered if we could change the focus of the headline to be focused on the value that they would be receiving.
Because these pages only draw traffic for a day or two we had to string multiple days together to be able to test. Each day offers a different premium, therefore no two days had the same headlines.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the headlines on the GOAA/SOAA donation pages to be more value-focused did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the value-focused headlines, saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.1%.

experiments.title: Will Adding Personalization to a Donation Page Headline Increase Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding someone’s first name on the instant donate redirect for those that signed up for Romans in Action will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will receive a personalized giving page upon submitting their first name for the resource.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2024 - 04/18/2024
The Next After Team was looking to find opportunities that helped donor conversion on KCBI’s acquisition offers — particularly on our higher-performing offers at the time of the design of experiments we produced. We decided to run this test on an eBook Bible study called Romans in Action. The sign-up rate for the resource was healthy but we saw there was an opportunity to grow donor conversion on the instant donate landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding someone's first name on the instant donate redirect for those that signed up for Romans in Action had a positive impact on donor conversion, resulting in a 11.8% increase in completed transactions for all traffic. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being 86.7%, these results cannot be considered valid.

We are going to take this test and try it on newer KCBI content offer that has more traffic and will most likely see a valid result. We're viewing this test as a clue to continue testing on an additional test idea that may result in a "new normal" for KCBI's acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How reinforcing a pledge agreement impacted conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the agreement to the pledge on the instant donation page will cause an increase in donor conversion rate because of its emphasis on personal agreement, and detailed actions to combat cancel culture. The explicit call for agreement and clear steps for support may enhance engagement, making the treatment more effective in motivating individuals to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2024 - 03/25/2024
When looking to improve conversion on an instant donation page behind a pledge, we wondered if including additional emphasis on personal agreement and detailed actions to combat cancel culture would lead to higher participation on the donation page. We developed a treatment that reminded visitors of their shared commitment in the same space as asking them to make a gift to combat the issue they stood against. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for six weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included reinforcing the agreement to the pledge on the instant donation page with detailed actions to combat cancel culture, did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group. We believe this may be due to additional friction because of the length of the copy.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 22.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.

It may be beneficial in future tests to consider revisiting the design and messaging of the treatment to see if any adjustments can be made to improve its effectiveness in driving donor conversions. Additionally, testing different variables or approaches in future experiments can help in understanding what resonates best with donors and ultimately leads to higher conversion rates.

experiments.title: Is there a difference in conversion when asking for $10 or $25 gift from nondonors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for donations for $25 or more for nondonor subscribers will achieve a higher average gift because the based level giving has been raised.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2024 - 02/23/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV primarily raises funds through donations made for books written by Dr. Robert Jeffress. For Easter this year, PTV decided to create a premium offer specific to Easter and to support the mission of PTV, spread the Good News of Jesus Christ to a lost and hurting people, confront an ungodly culture with God’s truth, and equip the saints to apply scripture to their everyday lives.
Last year, PTV tested into asking nondonors for a gift of $10 or more and saw great results.
With this offer, we decided to test a higher gift ask of $25
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the donation ask from $10 to $25 for nondonor subscribers resulted in a 0.7% increase in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 4.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

Donations and average gift
Control: 15 gifts, $21.33 average gift
 	Treatment: 14 gifts, $21.43 average gift

experiments.title: Adding a Callout Above the Monthly Gift Frequency Button on the Campaign Giving Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages will increase the donor conversion rate because of the social proof and added exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 03/26/2024
By adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages, will this element help add additional direction to those motivated to give and who will have already landed on the campaign giving page? By adding a donor countdown animation within the message, we hope to add an additional layer of “encouragement” to give during the giving process by showing donors can be part of the answer and help reach an impactful goal through their gift by “becoming one of” the donors needed. We believe adding a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button will insight more donors to get involved and make a monthly donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button showed a 9.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 23% level of confidence. This test overall was pretty flat. In the future, we would like to rerun this test on a campaign-giving page for a longer amount of time to see if we can reach validity.

experiments.title: Removing Express Checkout on the Sustainer Campaign Giving Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing express checkout for HUC donation pages will achieve a higher percentage of conversion because there will be less decision friction once a donor hits the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 03/12/2024
On previous tests for FOTF, we’ve seen varying degrees of success when testing the removal or decrease in size of the Express Checkout method of payment on FOTF-giving pages. Would removing the Express Checkout on the Sustainer campaign giving pages also yield the same results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Removing the Express Checkout for the Sustainer campaign giving pages resulted in a significant decrease in Transaction - Recurring by 60.1%.
2. There was a 61.2% statistical level of confidence in the result for Transaction - Recurring, indicating that the control group performed better.
3. The Transactions - One-Time experienced an 861% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and Transactions experienced an 840% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Therefore, it is not recommended to make decisions solely based on these results. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to reconsider the hypothesis and perhaps conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to draw more conclusive findings.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated tabbed form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because there is additional clarity in the process by adding frequency choice before the gift array choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 03/18/2024
When sending pledge signers to an instant donation page, we wondered if emphasizing the monthly gift option by creating a tabbed form experience would increase recurring donor conversion. We split traffic between the two versions and ran for the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the animated tabbed form for the instant donation page resulted in a 50.3% increase in recurring donation for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As a result, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to determine the most effective ways to increase recurring donor conversion. It may be beneficial to explore other design and messaging variations in future experiments to see if they have a significant impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How adding a banner to a donation page with a button to accelerate the process impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to begin the donation for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be reduced friction present for visitors who are already motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 03/04/2024
For highly motivated traffic coming to a main donation page from the homepage donate button in the top right corner, we hypothesized that giving them a chance to skip over the copy on the donation form and go straight to the gift frequency and gift array selections without having to read the donation page would reduce friction in the process that we know they initiated by arriving on this page. We had tested into having shorter copy on this page to help get the form above the fold, but wondered how else we could escalate the process and reduce the work involved for visitors here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to begin the donation process for main donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 6.7% increase in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 72.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results may not be reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore ways to reduce friction for motivated visitors on the donation page. This could include streamlining the donation process, providing clearer calls to action, or optimizing the layout of the page to make it easier for visitors to navigate and complete their donation. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to improve donor conversion rates, while also ensuring that the results are statistically valid before implementing any changes permanently.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Pop-Up on a Main Donation Page Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly popup with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the popup reinforces the impact of a smaller amount than their original gift made on a consistent basis. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2024 - 01/12/2024
To increase monthly gifts on CaringBridge’s main donation page, we hypothesized a pop-up that presents the user with an opportunity to turn their original one-time amount into a lowered monthly gift would increase recurring donations. This pop-up would appear when the user submitted their one-time donation. We hypothesized that by making this conversion easy, suggesting a lower amount than what the user was originally going to donate, and reinforcing the impact of that smaller amount on a consistent basis it would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which involved presenting a monthly pop-up with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation, resulted in a 25.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level of 77.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Our hypothesis for why this popup not only didn't increase monthly donations but in fact decreased donations overall is due to the way CaringBridge's form is preset. Monthly is already pre-selected as the default giving option, so to submit a one-time gift a user would have to intentionally click it, fill out the form, and press submit. Targeting that audience, who already has a specific motivation for making a one-time donation, with another popup likely made the experience more disruptive rather than presenting them with something they hadn't already considered.

In future testing, we will focus our efforts on that initial action — selecting one-time rather than monthly — at the top of the form so that we are meeting the donor at the point of consideration rather than at the end where they've already made a decision.

experiments.title: How using continuity in the headline of a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a continuity headline for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will provide more clarity on the next steps for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2024 - 03/14/2024
The Navigators ran an acquisition offer this spring offering a set of Easter cards. In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate at the end of the funnel, we wanted to text additional continuity between the landing page to the donation page. The idea behind the test is that our donation pages may be perceived as confirmation pages when a visitor is working their way through a funnel. By adding a “continuation headline” we are able to better communicate the next steps to the visitor, therefore leading to the potential increase in donor conversion rate. We have seen success with other clients using this technique, so we wanted to see how The Navigators audience reacted to it. Our hypothesis was that the additional continuity would increase donor conversion rates.
Control:
Headline: Help us bring the disciple-makers to The Great Commission
Treatment:
Headline: Your Easter cards will be sent to you soon. Before you go, would you consider something?
Subheadline: Will you help us bring the disciple-makers to The Great Commission?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a continutation headline for potential donors can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this specific case, the treatment group saw a 14.0% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence of 44.5% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results not statistically significant and not suitable for future decision-making.
However, due to the slight increase, it would be important to retest this concept for The Navigators. It is important to note that we were unable to let this test continue to run and potentially validate due to a lack of inventory of the offer. Therefore, we may be able to get valid results on a test that can run for a longer period or on a more evergreen offer like an ebook.
While transactions did not validate for this test, it is encouraging to see a relative lift that may indicate their audience responds well to a continuation headline.

experiments.title: How adding an image of a thank-you gift impacted donations during a one-day fund drive.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/08/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day. This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding an image of the gift on the donation page made a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors resulted in a 13.6% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 33.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and improve the reliability of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments conducted.

experiments.title: How adding a “Most Popular Gift” call out on the gift array impacts one-time donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a “Most Popular Gift” call out for donation page gift arrays will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is.. because  it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2024 - 02/23/2024
Luther Seminary has seen a steady amount of traffic on their donation page. Conversions have been consistent, but the average gift has been lower. We wanted to see if we could get more conversions and also increase the number of gifts because we increase clarity of what the most popular option is for most page visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Most Popular Gift" call out to the donation page gift arrays led to a 22.0% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 77.3% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and account for any potential fluctuations.
Overall, the concept of highlighting a "Most Popular Gift" option shows promise in potentially increasing donor conversions and gift amounts. It could be worth conducting further experiments with adjustments to ensure reliable and conclusive results that can guide decision-making for optimizing donation pages in the future.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition on a donation page impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the value propostion for Education CYE main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2023 - 12/11/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to test if shortening the value proposition on the Education Donation Page to focus on one area instead of being very broad, could increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: By shortening the value proposition to only be positioned for year end, we where able to increase the donor conversion rate by 46.2% at a 75% level of confidence. While the result is not valid, it gives us a good baseline to expand on this test.

experiments.title: How does a humanized sticky bar on a donation page impact conversion rate for a direct appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the continuity from the email to the donation page will increase the personal connection and credibility of the sender.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/11/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal, we ran a 50/50 split in traffic to the donation page, half of which would see the normal donation page, the other half would see a humanized sticky bar featuring the the email sender on the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that individuals who saw the humanized sticky bar of Scott’s face, the email sender, would be more inclined to give a gift because it creates a more personal experience and increases the credibility of the sender.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with the sticky bar actually saw a decrease in transactions of 14.9% at a 38.2% level of confidence, compared to the control group as well as a slight decrease in revenue at 0.13% with a 46.3% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to design experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the appeal, the messaging, or the overall design of the donation page. It is also important to note that because there was no CTA on this sticky bar our results may be invalid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing the impact of humanized elements in donation appeals, but also to explore different variables and combinations to determine what resonates best with donors. We could potentially try a different email sender to see if a different person from the organization resonates with The Navigators audience more effectively.

experiments.title: How providing form validation impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding validation that a field is completed correctly for web donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is visual confirmation of correct information.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2024 - 02/12/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
We decided to test and see if by adding validation on each form field would generate a higher conversion rate due to the confidence of completing the form correctly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding validation to fields for web donors may potentially increase the conversion rate. While the treatment group did see a 0.1% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is adequate before drawing any conclusions. This can be done by increasing the sample size or running the experiment for a longer period of time. Additionally, it would be beneficial to evaluate other factors that may impact conversion rates, such as the user experience, website design, and donation process flow. By considering these factors and conducting experiments with sufficient statistical power, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights for improving conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: How a headline on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headline for donation page viewers will achieve a higher conversion rate because their is clarity on how their gift will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2023 - 03/30/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio station. For their main donation page, we decided to test adding a headline to the page to drive clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the donation page of WUNC, a nonprofit North Carolina Public Radio station, aimed to test the impact of a headline on the donation page. The hypothesis was that adding a headline would clarify how the gift would be used and increase the conversion rate. The experiment ran for 42 days and showed that the treatment had a 4.0% increase in revenue for all traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 57.5%, it cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

However, there were some significant findings from the experiment that should be taken into account for future experiments. Tablet visitors saw a 5922% increase in revenue, with a 98% level of confidence. On the other hand, organic search visitors experienced a 570% decrease in revenue, with a 96% level of confidence.

The key learning from this experiment is that adding a headline to the donation page can have a positive impact on revenue. However, more research is needed to determine the specific language and message that resonates with different visitor segments. It is also important to note that statistical significance is crucial in making data-driven decisions and cannot be ignored. Future experiments should aim for a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How an animated donate button impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with an animated donation button in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/19/2021
With the high volume of traffic to the Save the Children website, we wanted to discover new ways to increase traffic to the donation page from the donate button in the primary navigation. To do this, we hypothesized that drawing more attention to the button might increase the number of people who would see the button and ultimately click and give. We set up a treatment version of the donate button that was animated. The animation was subtle with a motion going across the button coming from a heart icon. We tested this button against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated donation button ran for three weeks. While the goal was to increase donor conversion from the animated donate button - which we weren't able to do with valid results, we did see a significant lift in clicks from it (11%). Essentially, more people clicked to the page but only a minimal amount more donated.

When analyzing the donor conversion, we saw a higher conversion rate from site visitors if they landed on the donation page and saw the animated button versus landing on the homepage.

We also saw the greatest increase in conversion from mobile users. This is most likely due to the button being much more visible on the small device.

After running this experiment and seeing that it was likely not going to validate, we came away with two questions:
Will the subtle animation be a novelty feature that will fade with time?
 	Can we present the subtle animation to people when they are at a point in their experience when they are more likely to convert? This question leads us to our next experiment for this feature where we will trigger the animated donate button at a higher motivation point in their conversion pathway so we can not only get more people to the donation page, but get more people to give.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How a headline with more clarity and impact bullet points affected donation page performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of value proposition and impact for visitors will achieve an increase in performance on the donation page because visitors would have a more tangible understanding of the impact of their donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/06/2023
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on our Holy Lands funnel for IMB, we decided to run a 3-way split test. We ran the control page vs. a new headline, vs the new headline + impact bullet points.
Variation 1:
New headline: Reach hurting people in the Holy Land with the transformative power of the gospel
Variation 2:
New headline: Reach hurting people in the Holy Land with the transformative power of the gospel
Impact bullet points:
$10, you’ll purchase a Bible for one person in their local language.
$30, you’ll pay for one person to receive one hour of trauma counseling.
$100, you’ll pay for a hosted training event in trauma healing leadership.
$200, you’ll pay for one month of media outreach.
We thought that the headline would offer additional clarity and value proposition as well as the impact bullet points which detailed. out exactly what each gift amount would result in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning of this experiment is that the new headline had a negative impact of 47.2% on the number of transactions while the new headline and impact bullets had a very minimal increase on the number of transactions.
While these results are not what we hypothesized, neither of the variations validated, so we can not rule out the effectiveness of the headline change or addition of the impact bullets.
We did notice that the impact bullets were based on these amounts $10, $30, $100, $200, but the gift array on this donation page had these amounts pre-selected: $100,$250,$500,$1,000. It would be interesting to run this test again focusing only on the impact bullets, but match up the impact bullet amounts with the gift array. We hypothesize that the stark difference between the options given and the impact bullets could have caused additional friction.
An important note: revenue is not pulling into Winston
However, VWO is reporting that Variation 1 raised $5,125 in revenue and Variation 2 raised $1,055. Based on these results, we did make the decision to roll out the new headline to all traffic.
It is crucial that we retest the use of a clear but impact based headline as well as impact bullets.

experiments.title: How positioning the donor as the hero impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clarity on the donation page for donors and positioning them as the hero will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because they will have a clear understanding as to what their gift will do and how they will make an impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/30/2023
During The Navigator’s Giving Tuesday fundraising campaign, we wan a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. This copy test aimed to frame the donor as the hero to see how it impact the donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that making the impact of donation clear in the donation page copy would resonate more with potential donors, therefore, influencing them to complete their donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity on the donation page and positioning the donor as the hero can potentially increase donor conversion rate. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 32.2%. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. This can be done by increasing the sample size or conducting more iterations of the experiment.
Overall, while the results of this experiment are inconclusive, it does provide some indication that providing clarity and positioning the donor as the hero may be effective strategies. These strategies can be further tested and refined in future experiments to determine their impact on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How clearly stating the problem and positioning the organization as the solution affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment with its emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasis on the threat to democracy, will lead to a higher donor conversion rate because it’s focusing on the problem and how Heritage is the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey through paid acquisition channels to get the public’s opinion on voter fraud. Although the offer receives a lot of traffic and receives names for very cheap, they wanted to test on the instant donation page to see if we can decrease our cost per donor and increase our conversion rate.
They believe that the combination of emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasis on the threat to democracy, along with positioning Heritage as the solution, will make the treatment more compelling and result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the control page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which aimed to increase the donor conversion rate by using emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasizing the threat to democracy, did not lead to a higher conversion rate compared to the control page.

The treatment saw a decrease of 22.1% in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Based on this, it is clear that the treatment did not have the intended impact and may not resonate with the target audience. In future experimentation, it would be important to explore other strategies or messaging that can effectively increase the conversion rate.

It is also important to note that the experiment ran for 26 days, which is a significant duration. This indicates that the results are reliable in terms of the data collected. However, as the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, it is necessary to conduct further experiments to validate the findings and make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary).  On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.
Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on a primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 12/12/2023
We previously tested how a tabbed donation form affected recurring donations on a public policy organization’s main donation page. In this experiment, the tabbed donation form produced an 11% decrease in recurring gifts (with a 25% level of confidence) and a 25.4% decrease in overall donations (94.4% level of confidence), which disproved our hypothesis that a tabbed donation form would achieve higher recurring donation rates than a monthly giving checkbox by making the monthly giving option and its impact more visible.
In an effort to learn whether a tabbed donation form on this organization’s main donation page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate, we retested this idea. The control in this experiment presents a monthly giving option as a checkbox below the gift array, while the treatment has two tabs for either a Single Gift or Monthly Gift with a value proposition callout box above the monthly gift tab. 50% of traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we observed that the tabbed donation form on the main donation page led to an insignificant 2.2% increase in overall donor conversion rates, but its 11.3% level of confidence is not high enough to consider these results valid. Therefore, we cannot confidently conclude that the tabbed donation form is more effective in increasing conversions, but we also cannot conclude that it is less effective.
Additionally, the treatment saw a 99.5% increase in recurring donations, but at an invalid 58.4% level of confidence.
These results, along with data of tabbed donation forms leading to an increase in donations in the organization's acquisition offers funnels, may indicate that people visiting their main donation page may have high donor motivation and be less influenced or even more distracted by the tabbed design of the form. The results also may have been impacted by an additional variable—the control not having the value proposition callout box found in the treatment's experience.

experiments.title: How using language around the impact of the matching gift affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language around the impact of the matching gift rather than simply stating it will be doubled  for ACI’s housefile will achieve an increase in conversion rate  because it provides more clarity and transparency, addressing potential concerns and building trust. because  it provides more clarity and transparency, addressing potential concerns and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2023
ACI is running their CYE campaign. They have a $100,000 matching grant for this campaign, so we wondered how using language around the impact of the matching gift rather than just saying their gift will have double the impact would affect donor conversion rate. We ran this test in an email to their full housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language around the impact of a matching gift instead of simply stating that the gift will be doubled did not have an effect on donor conversion rate.
Because CYE is already a motivating time to give, we believe that the added context and clarity did not increase motivation to give because it was already so high.
In future experimentation, it would be wise to consider alternative approaches to communicate the impact of a matching gift. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies, such as emphasizing the urgency or importance of the campaign or highlighting specific examples of how the matching gift will be used to make a difference.

experiments.title: How removing a premium and specific ask amount from a donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the premium of a pledge coin  for visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will remove unnecessary friction from the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2023 - 01/04/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate for ACI, we wanted to test removing the premium of their 2023 Pledge Coin which required a $100 donation. We felt that the high ask amount and the premium was causing unnecessary friction for the user, causing them to give lower amounts or not at all.
Therefore, our hypothesis was that be removing the premium all together, potential donors would have a clearer idea of what was being asked of them due to the clarity now being provided.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the premium of the pledge coin for visitors resulted in a 11.9% increase in the number of transactions. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment saw a negative impact in revenue at a 51.7% drop with a 16.4% level of confidence, also not considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct experiments for a longer duration to collect more data and increase the statistical confidence level. While you should not conduct future decision making off of these results, we can observe how donors perceived this change. That is, more people were willing to give when there was no premium, but the average gift fell significantly. Therefore, it would be crucial that you consider these results from a strategic standpoint of focusing on donors or donations.
Overall, this experiment highlights the potential of removing unnecessary friction in the donation process to improve donor conversion. It is recommended to continue exploring different strategies to simplify the donation experience and increase revenue for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because they will be forced to think of the impact and what they receive in return to convert the one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
MSLF is running a petition through paid Facebook advertising, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through acquisition efforts.
Once users finish the petition, they proceed to an instant donation page where they’re asked to make a gift to support this case and the work MSLF is doing. There are options to make a one-time or monthly gift on the donation form, but if the user chose one-time, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Donate” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what they will receive if they make a monthly donation and become a member to MSLF.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift can significantly increase the conversion rate for recurring donations.
The treatment group experienced a 154.5% increase in recurring donation conversion rate, indicating that the interrupter was effective in prompting users to consider the impact of their donation and what they would receive in return as a member of the organization. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.1%, which falls below the recommended confidence level of 85%.
In addition, the experiment showed that the one-time donation rate decreased by 326% with a 97% level of confidence. This means that the interrupter influenced users to opt out of giving all together instead of making a one-time gift or recurring gift.
Future experimentation can involve refining the design and messaging of the interrupter to maximize its impact. It is also important to consider the potential impact of the interrupter on user experience and whether it could potentially deter users from making any donation at all. A/B testing with different variations of the interrupter can help in identifying the most effective approach.
Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential of using a donation interrupter to increase recurring donations. However, further experimentation and validation are needed to make informed decisions and implement effective strategies.

experiments.title: How a larger impact number affects donor conversion on the main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a larger impact number for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will increase their perception of Heritage’s impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2023 - 11/06/2023
The Heritage Foundation had long used their 500k member base as a leg of their value proposition to show their broad support. However, their marketing team’s outreach initiatives meant that they actually reached a lot more people each year than they had members.
Donors responded very favorably to this “larger” metric, but they wanted to see if that proved out in increased fundraising results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that by showing a larger impact number for main donation page visitors, the conversion rate can be increased. This is supported by the fact that the treatment version, which presented the larger number of 10 million members, advocates, and concerned Americans reached per day, resulted in a 7.7% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 34.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Based on this, it is recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact of showing a larger number on the conversion rate. The future experimentation should involve a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations in the language used to present the impact number to see if different phrasing or positioning can further improve the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How lowering a gift array on an instant donation page for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower gift array aligns better with the perceived value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
IMB is running an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads to sign a note of encouragement for missionaries who are traveling and out in the field. They wanted to increase the donor conversion rate on the instant donation page of the acquisition offer so we decided to lower the gift array because this is a low value offer and they aren’t receiving in return like an ebook funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the lower gift array saw a significant decrease in donations of 49.3% compared to the control version.
Based on these results, it is evident that the perceived value of the offer did not align with the lower gift array. This suggests that donors may be more willing to donate higher amounts when presented with a wider range of options. Or it suggests that users see this content offer as high value and makes them want to give more to support more missionaries.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the perceived value of the offer and ensure that the gift array aligns with this value. It may be beneficial to test different gift array options to find the optimal balance between a wide range of options and the perceived value.

experiments.title: How using a more concise donation page in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and being more concise for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because repeated information on a donation page is added friction to the donation process which can deter people from giving.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
This ministry organization ran a high-urgency campaign to their full housefile with the goal of acquiring more recurring donors. The original donation page re-explained most points that the emails had already described, so we asked ourselves the questions:
Are subscribers still needing this much information by the time they get to the donation page? Or are they just ready to give, and we are getting in their way by proposing more hypothetical questions?
That’s why we proposed a second, short, to-the-point donation page that focuses on clarity and is more concise.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a more concise and clear donation page may lead to a higher donor conversion rate. The treatment, which focused on eliminating repeated information and reducing friction in the donation process, saw a 0.9% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 4.8%, which is below the recommended 85%.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of clarity and conciseness on donor conversion rates. The results of this experiment suggest that potential donors may prefer a streamlined and simplified donation process without excessive information repetition. Further testing with larger sample sizes and higher statistical power could help validate these findings and provide more reliable insights for decision making.
Additionally, it would also be valuable to explore other potential factors that could impact donor conversion rates, such as the design and layout of the donation page, messaging and storytelling techniques, and ease of use. By combining multiple variables and testing different combinations, a deeper understanding of the most effective strategies for acquiring recurring donors can be gained.

experiments.title: How a headline on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headline on the main donation form for all traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is a clear call to action and reason for the donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2023 - 11/17/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station.
In an effort to convert donors, we decided to test adding a headline to WUNC’s main donation page during their Fall Drive. All traffic from email, advertising and web point to this page.
We did experience a delay in getting the experiment live due to technical issues, so we did miss the first 4 days of the drive which historically have the most traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a headline to the main donation form for all traffic did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, due to technical issues and missing the first 4 days of the drive which historically have the most traffic, the results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that technical issues are resolved promptly to avoid missing significant portions of the experiment. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct experiments for a longer duration to gather more accurate and comprehensive data.
Based on the inconclusive results of this experiment, it would be wise to test other variations and elements on the main donation page to identify factors that may lead to a higher conversion rate. It is essential to continue experimenting and iterating on the donation page to optimize its effectiveness in converting donors.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the book impacted revenue for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for nondonors will achieve a higher revenue impact because of the visual appeal of the book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book for non-donors did not achieve a higher revenue impact. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to further explore the reasons behind the decrease in revenue for the treatment group. This could involve conducting qualitative research or gathering feedback from participants to better understand their preferences and reactions to the image of the book.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment suggest that including an image of the book may not be the most effective strategy for increasing revenue. It would be beneficial to consider other potential variables or elements to test in future experiments in order to optimize revenue impact.

experiments.title: How making the donor the hero and using a empathetic, conversational tone on a content offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the donor the hero and using a conversational, empathetic tone on a content offer donation page will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2023 - 09/22/2023
Focus on the Family uses many free video series offers to acquire subscribers and donors. When reviewing their donation page template, we found that the copy was both long and impersonal. Its tone also talked a lot about the organization and less about the donor’s impact. It included phrases like, “we’ve done the math” that could potentially make visitors feel like Focus was the expert and their thoughts weren’t as valuable.
Users also mentioned that the template lacked a more authentic and conversational tone. We decided to redesign the donation page to be more impact-driven and conversational, hypothesizing that this would increase the perceived value of the offer while also making it easier to read.
We chose to run this on several offers. This experiment is for their Bringing Biblical Truth to a Broken Culture offer. You can review the results of a similar experiment on their Navigating Adolescence offer here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a month, we found no statistically significant impact on donor conversion from the treatment for this offer. We did however see an increase in donor conversion on the Navigating Adolescence offer we tested during the same time frame at a higher, though not quite valid level of confidence When looking at recurring donor conversion in the Broken Culture offer, we found a 34% increase but only at a 72% level of confidence so this wasn't valid. We will continue to test donation page layouts and language on a per-offer basis to see which designs will increase donor conversion.
Additional data can be found below:
One-Time Transactions: treatment led to a -1.6% decrease in donor conversion w/ a 11.9% LOCRecurring Transactions: treatment had a 37.9% increase in donor conversion w/ a 44.9% LOCVWO captured approximately $1,300 more revenue from the control than the treatment.

experiments.title: How concise copy and additional clarity impacted donor conversions for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points for visitors will achieve an increase in clarity and comprehension.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2024 - 01/16/2024
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar campaign, we decided to run a copy test to condense some of the copy. Our hypothesis was that the more concise the copy, the higher level of comprehension for the potential donor therefore, leading them to the donation form quicker.
We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points will lead to increased clarity and comprehension. As a result, we believe the treatment version will outperform the control version in terms of donor understanding and response, leading to a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points in the copy had a very slight lift in the number of transactions. While the treatment version of the campaign showed a 1.1% increase in transactions, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.1%, falling below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.
Furthermore, we saw a a drop of 35.3% in revenue between the control and treatment at a 12.1% level of confidence. While this did not validate, it is important to consider in future decision making that IMB's donors give less when they are given less value proposition, and more concise copy may lead to a lower average gift.
In future experimentation, it is important to carry out the A/B test for a longer duration to gather a larger sample size and increase the statistical power. This will help establish a more reliable and accurate conclusion about the effectiveness of the treatment version. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine and optimize the treatment version based on the insights gained from this experiment. Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further iterations and testing to drive improved results.

experiments.title: How added value of a premium impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding more information about the premium for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is clarity around what they will receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2023 - 09/15/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV promotes various premiums each month, in an effort to increase donor conversion we decided to test adding additional copy about the offer on the donation page to drive response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding more information about the premium on the donation page led to a slight increase (2.7%) in transactions compared to the control group. While this increase is statistically significant (27.6% level of confidence), it falls below the recommended threshold (85%) for valid results.
Therefore, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision making. It is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or make changes to the experiment setup to achieve a higher level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to focus on increasing the sample size to improve statistical significance. Additionally, evaluating other factors that could potentially impact donor conversion, such as the clarity of the premium offer, could provide valuable insights for future optimization efforts.
Overall, this experiment highlights the need for rigorous statistical analysis and a larger sample size to obtain reliable results for decision making in the future.

experiments.title: How removing the main menu navigation bar from a donation page effected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the main navigation menu from the page for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because this would reduce decision friction so the donor motivation would not break by having too many decisions to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/20/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client offers different ministries that a donor can choose to give towards. In this experiment, this donation page contributes to providing biblical resources to international prisoners. We were interested in testing decision friction on the donation page. We believed that removing the main navigation menu at the top of the page could reduce decision friction. Decision friction gives the donor too many options to consider which breaks the train of thought they had for giving. This is something we wanted to reduce and could potentially lead to a higher conversion rate.
Between the control and treatment donation page, the only difference was that the control page had the menu navigation whereas the treatment page did not. This test ran as a split traffic 50/50 A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the main navigation menu from the donation page did not lead to an increase in conversions. In fact, the treatment page saw a decrease in donations compared to the control page. However, the statistical confidence level of 23.3% is below the acceptable threshold of 85%, so these results did not validate.

Based on these findings, the presence of the menu may not have been a significant source of decision friction for donors, and removing it may have actually hindered their experience on the page.
