For future experimentation, we could focus on other elements of the donation page that could be optimized to increase conversions. This could include testing different calls-to-action or simplicity the donation page copy to enhance the persuasiveness of the page. It is important to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and stricter statistical confidence thresholds to obtain valid and reliable results.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button color on a homepage affected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the donate button color in the menu for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because people will have less friction finding where to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 07/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to test changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu for this prison ministry client. The control color of the donate button is part of the color palette that this brand has. Our idea was to find a color that is different and would make the word “Donate” a lot more obvious to someone looking at the site. If the donate button stands out more, we believed that it would increase the conversion rate since the donor can see the button quicker and have less friction finding the donation button.
For this experiment, the only difference is that the control page has the original color donate button and the treatment has a new color. We split this test 50/50 as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu resulted in a 29.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.9%, which is not validated since it is below the 95% standard level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test a more radical design difference for the donate button to identify the most effective option. This could include shape, size, button animations, and font. By adding a more drastic design difference to the donate button, this could draw more donors which could increase the conversion rate. A/B testing should continue to be conducted, comparing different design elements or variations to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown impacts donor conversion on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s Spring Recurring Campaign, they rely on a daily donor countdown in sticky bars and website calls to action to increase urgency and drive traffic to their donation page. We hypothesized that reinforcing this countdown on the page itself would add urgency and increase donor conversion.
The message would indicate how many new monthly donors were still needed before midnight that night.  When the number was reached, it would change to say, “We still need new monthly partners before 11:59pm to be here for you and other families in need.”
We split traffic evenly between both versions of the page and ran it for almost three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page did not increase donor conversion during the high urgency campaign. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in FamilyLife - Recurring Donations compared to the control group.
While we could not validate this decrease, it was a sharp enough decline in conversion that we ended the experiment early so we wouldn't harm donor conversion during the critical campaign. It is possible that the message was more of a distraction than provided additional urgency to the page.

experiments.title: How does personalizing copy for new names impact donor conversion rates during a match for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing the way someone came onto the file for new names will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/04/2023
Pathway to Victory (PTV) is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
Part of the overall strategy for PTV is to acquire new names onto their file, both from new donors as well as people that sign up for some of their acquisition offers. New names tend to have a very low instant conversion rate; for this match campaign, we wanted to test whether adding language in the body of the email that referenced how they came onto the file would impact conversion rates.
This test ran across 4 different emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that referencing the way someone came onto the file for new names may have an impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which included language referencing how they raised their hand to seek spiritual help, saw an 8.8% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of the results is only 27.8%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experiments, placement of the copy, as well referencing the name of the offer they signed up for, will be an interesting test.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the value proposition of a donation with an impact statement on the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving will increase donations by helping to overcome hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the impact of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving can potentially increase donations and help overcome hesitancy at the point of completing a transaction. Although the treatment showed a 19.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence of 72.3% is below the threshold of 85% needed for the results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving. The results of this experiment provide promising insights that can be further explored and tested with larger sample sizes to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, it is important to evaluate different variations of the statement to identify the most effective messaging to drive donations.

experiments.title: How updating value proposition on acquisition donation pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net’s mission will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
In the last few months, there have been some major social movements in the pro-life space, including the overturning of Roe v. Wade and the federal government announcing that they will be developing a Federal Reproductive Health Care Hotline. The hotline in particular is direct competition to one of Care Net’s ministries: the Pregnancy Decision Line. We wanted to see how updating the language to center around this particular issue would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net's mission resulted in a decrease in donor conversion and revenue. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on these learnings, it is important for Care Net to conduct further experimentation to validate the impact of updating the value proposition. The organization should explore other strategies and hypotheses to understand the factors contributing to the decrease in donor conversion. This can involve testing different messaging, design elements, or targeting strategies.

Future experimentation should be conducted for a longer duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical significance.

experiments.title: How an animated lock on a donation page affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security on the donation page for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor. because  there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge already had an existing image of a security lock on their tribute donation pages.  We wanted to reinforce the perception of security even more by drawing attention to this part of the giving form. We made the lock an “animated” lock where it opened/closed every few seconds and we additional copy to remind the prospective donor that they are making a secure donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4.9% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 71%.
Reinforcing the perception of security on the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. An animated lock and additional copy reminding the prospective donor about the secure donation process can help reduce anxiety and increase donations. It's likely that because the security lock already existed on the page, security was already sufficiently highlighted for the end user.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a campaign donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for users who are familiar with this organization will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because Jake is a familiar face to these subscribers which will increase the humanization of the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/01/2023
This ministry client was running a campaign for their fiscal year-end. We’ve seen humanized sticky bars increase donor conversion for other organizations when used for high-urgency campaigns, so we decided to test this on their main campaign donation page which received traffic from their housefile list and Facebook ads.
The sticky bar included a message from Jake Hennes, someone who the file is very familiar with and who they receive cultivation emails from often. We wanted the urgent, important message to come from someone they recognize. Jake is also the one who sent a majority of the campaign emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a humanized sticky bar featuring a familiar face can significantly impact donor conversion rates, especially for users who are already familiar with the organization.

2. The treatment resulted in a 25.6% increase in donations for all traffic, suggesting that the humanization of the donation ask through the use of a familiar face resonates positively with donors.

3. Female visitors, in particular, showed a significant increase in donations, with a whopping 956% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This suggests that the humanized sticky bar was especially effective in engaging and persuading female donors.

4. While the results of the experiment showed promising improvements in donor conversion rates, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, but due to the volume of gifts and difference in donor conversion, we believe this concept should be used in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2023 - 06/26/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings:
"Start a Page" resulted in a 27% decrease in site creation rate with a level of confidence of 75% while the "Start a Journal" treatment resulted in a 14% increase in site creation with a 40% statistical level of confidence, indicating a positive impact.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%.
Surprisingly, we did see a softness in response when we used "page." It's likely that some of the response is offer-specific but we did see a more positive response to using "journal." Which could indicate that journal lowers the barrier to entry for some reason.
It's worth to continue testing into this by reducing anxiety for the user by using supporting copy to highlight how easy it is to create a site.

experiments.title: How a Radical Redesign Impacted Staff Donation Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that re-positioning the donation page and better highlighting one-time vs monthly gifts for staff donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because moving the gift array to the bottom allows the user to read the copy and see the value first before being given an opportunity to give. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 04/06/2023
The Navigators’ saw tremendous success with a radical redesign of their instant donation pages that implemented a tabbed form and moved the gift array to the bottom of the page instead of the top. We hypothesized that creating the same treatment for Staff Donation Pages would result in a similar lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, we saw neither a valid increase nor a decrease in this experiment. The relative difference in conversion rate was only 2.8% with a low level of confidence at 27.7%. A learning here is that simply because a treatment worked on one page doesn't mean that it will generate the same results on another. The minimal difference also urges consideration on how this treatment was being applied on a very different page to an audience with potentially very difference motivations than in the original test. For Staff Donation Pages, users are more likely to already know in advance who they'd want to support. It's possible that because they are coming to the page with an intentional motivation of who they are or are not giving to, this treatment did very little to affect this audience's decision.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline prompting readers to read on impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a continuation headline  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will learn that there is more to their journey and will realize their opportunity to make a gift and why a gift is needed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/02/2023
DTS’ James course offer is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. When user’s sign up for the James online course, they are redirected to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to give. We want to see if we can get more people to give after signing up for the course.
We’ve built a donation page headline test that applies a continuation headline. The control donation page headline says “Thank you” and “Congratulations”, implying that their journey has ended and that nothing else if needed of them.
We want to change this implication to rather encourage users to continue on. That’s why we’ve built a treatment headline that reads as follows:
Before you go…
Your course material for week 1 is headed to your inbox – we’re excited for you to get started! But before you go, would you consider one more thing?
We believe this headline will encourage readers to read on, enabling them to engage with the value proposition copy and to see that donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings: After being able to run for only 8 days, the treatment saw a 2.9% decrease in donations with only a 4.3% level of confidence. It led to a more notable 40.9% decrease in revenue (77.1% level of confidence). As we were constrained by the sunset of the testing platform that this experiment was being run on, we will have to test this experiment once more on a new platform to give it more runway and to determine more trustworthy data outcomes.

experiments.title: How an animated lock impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated lock  for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lock is eye catching and increases perceived security.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
In an effort to increase donations, and therefore revenue, we decided to test an animated lock on the donation page for the America is a Christian Nation donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the A/B experiment conducted on the America is a Christian Nation donation pages, the key learnings are as follows:
1. Animated lock impact: The treatment group, which had an animated lock on the donation page, showed a 2.5% increase in revenue for all traffic. This indicates that the animated lock had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the statistical level of confidence of 58.5% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, rendering these results invalid.
2. Audience segmentation: When the results were further analyzed based on visitor types, returning visitors showed a significant 720% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence, and other visitors had a 355% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the impact of the animated lock was more pronounced for repeat visitors and other visitor segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that there is potential for using an animated lock on the donation pages to increase donor conversion rates. However, to draw stronger conclusions and make informed decisions for future experimentation, the statistical validity of the results needs to improve by achieving a higher level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials in the donation page value proposition impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials through the value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the issue will feel closer to home for donation page visitors and they’ll be more inspired to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis instant donation page is producing a a low conversion rate. We wondered if enhancing the value proposition messaging through evidentials would better engage donation page visitors, as the messaging would feel closer to home and they’d therefore want to make a donation to help improve the border crisis situation.
The control uses more general value proposition while the treatment highlights specific threats to Texas citizens and examples of the harm the border crisis is causing to these citizens.
Let’s see which approach appeals more to our audience in terms of urging them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, the treatment resulted in a 45.2% decrease in donations with a 78.6% level of confidence. Potential donors did not respond to the treatment evidential-infused value proposition copy like we expected them to. Perhaps they already feel they have a strong grasp of the problem and therefore were not moved by the evidentials. In this case, we would want to hone in on the solution side of the formula more so than the problem side.
The treatment also produced a 29.5% decrease in revenue with a 73.6% level of confidence.
Returning visitors produced an 88% decrease in donations to the treatment with a valid 97.6% level of confidence - 1 donation to the treatment compared to 7 to the control. This provides further proof that our returning visitors already have a strong grasp of the problem.
Donors may care more about how their donation would make a difference in resolving the issues caused by the border crisis. Especially on this donation page where they're being asked to make a monthly donation, it's likely even more important to stress the reasons to give.
It would be interesting to run a test where we isolate the reasons to give between the control variant and the treatment variant to see how that impacts giving. In this instance, the treatment reasons to give focus more on stopping the bad guys (cartels) while the control reasons to focus more on implementing better policy. It would be interesting to isolate these to see which motivates our audience more to make donations.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is producing a low instant donor conversion rate after users sign the petition. In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their CEO’s picture and a message directing the reader’s attention to the donation opportunity below and reminding them of the impact that they can make. We believe this message coming from a person and prominent figure in the organization will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar with the picture of a prominent member of the organization and a personal message from that person can significantly increase the donor conversion rate - the power of personal touch. The treatment version of the donation page, which included the humanized sticky bar, saw a 164.8% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence is only 63.5% which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. This experiment had to end after 15 days, preventing the necessary data to reach validation. It also recorded only 4 total donations between the two variants, which is below the recommended 50 transactions to reach confident results.

experiments.title: How urgency-focused value proposition copy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that urgency value proposition language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because visitors will feel a sense of urgency to give and make an impact that they otherwise wouldn’t.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/23/2023
DTS is amid a high-urgency fiscal year-end campaign. As our org is pushing a large number of users to our fiscal year-end donation pages, we want to test into urgency-focused donation page copy.
As part of our FYE campaign, we launched a free content offer where users can sign up to receive the eBook, and once they do, they’re redirected an instant donation page. Our existing donation page deploys standard value proposition messaging through problem/solution copy and reasons to give; however, it does not include important FYE messaging including urgency language, the campaign deadline, campaign goal, or matching gift challenge associated with the campaign. The only urgent element it includes is a countdown clock, but it does not explain what the clock is counting down to.
We want to test urgency-focused messaging that does include these elements and mentions, and that grabs the user’s attention immediately, alerting them of this urgent opportunity to make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The urgency-focused language did not produce the results that we expected. After running for 17 days, the treatment resulted in a 9.6% decrease in donations with a 40.4% level of confidence. It also produced a 4% decrease in revenue with a 56.7% level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that urgency-focused language does not always lead to more donations. It can provide the urgency, the flash, and the untraditional that could inspire someone to give. Though, in this case, I wonder if the supporting value proposition copy was not as strong as the control. The control includes strong problem/solution copy and highlights specifically how a donor's gift will be used, while the treatment includes more general value proposition copy that never really explains the problem(s) that a donor's gift might help solve and how the gift would be used to solve them.
For a future iteration, it would be wise to blend the value proposition copy from the control with the urgency elements used in the treatment. This could be the formula for increase donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How social proof vs. credibility impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of credibility for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will build trust in the value proposition and give the donor more reasons to believe our claims.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/22/2023
In a previous test, we saw that adding a quote from a current volunteer board member produced a lift in donations. We wondered if adding a statement of credibility using the same format would make any difference in conversion. We pulled a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization and put it in the same placement on the page as an element of social proof right before the donation form and split traffic 50/50, sending half of visitors to the control with Ken’s quote about donating and the other half to the treatment with the Forbes magazine call-out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to determine if adding a statement of credibility before the donation form would increase donor conversion. The treatment included a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization, while the control had a quote from a volunteer board member. The experiment ran for 28 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 53.8% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% and thus cannot be considered valid.

From this experiment, we can learn that adding a statement of credibility may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion. It is essential to conduct further experimentation to confirm the validity of the initial results. What we saw was the audience was more motivated by a donor-centric quote from a supporter regarding their experience with giving to the organization than they were by the organizational-centric quote from a review that gave an exclusive reason to support the organization. One reason for this could be the difference in the positioning of the language in the quotes "Let's go get 'em" which insinuates there is work left to be done versus the treatment that claims the organization has the "largest communication presence" which could lead donors to believe that the organization does not need their help or support since they are already doing the most/best with their existing resources and are already in a position to be successful without the donor making a gift to help.

experiments.title: How adding donor autonomy to a headline impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity and autonomy in the headline will increase instant conversion rate because people will be more likely to read the rest of the page when they are offered autonomy to help something as opposed to a command to join something.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 06/09/2023
When looking to improve donor conversion on an instant donation page, we wondered if the headline to “join the fight to eliminate election fraud” was too vague, too much mental work, and too commanding of a call to action. We hypothesized the headline needed additional specificity, clarity and autonomy to drive donor engagement. We developed a treatment that phrased the next step as an option or a choice rather than a direct instruction. We included “Here’s how” to drive curiosity from the reader and keep the communication in motion and guide the reader’s attention to continue down the page. We split traffic between the control “Your answers have been submitted! Now join the fight to eliminate election fraud in the U.S.” and the treatment “Your answers have been submitted–Now you can help eliminate voter fraud in the U.S. Here’s how:”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to test whether changing the headline's language to "you can help eliminate voter fraud, here's how" would increase the instant conversion rate compared to the control headline. Upon review, the treatment resulted in a 75% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the level of confidence was 75%, and the total number of donations evaluated was below 50, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.
Because we did not reach the needed sample size during the experiment's duration we will launch the next iteration of this test that will maintain the headline change in the treatment while also including additional focus in the body copy on what specific solutions to voter fraud issues that the donor’s gift would directly support.

experiments.title: How a donation exit intent popup on an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an exit intent popup prompting a return to the donation page will increase donations because potential donors will be brought back in by the ask and simplified value prop, and ultimately convert.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/17/2023
TPPF’s monthly donor conversion funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In an effort to increase conversion, we’ve implemented an exit intent popup that appears when someone tries to exit the page.
The popup will hit donation page visitors with one more ask with condensed value proposition copy in hopes that it will convince them to return to the donation page and complete a donation.
This approach could be effective in a number of ways:
It’s unconventional and it literally “pops out” to users
The popup will catch all users that try to exit, but could be especially impactful to the users that would bounce from the page immediately without reading the value proposition copy and realizing the donation ask
Perhaps the exit intent popup will help make the mind up of anyone that is on the fence about donating
Prompting visitors to second guess their decision to leave the page could lead some to return and convert – but will it be enough to produce a valid lift in conversion?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not produce the results we had hoped for. In fact, it only produced eight conversions in total and they were split between the control and the treatment (exit intent popup). Ultimately, the treatment had a 1.9% lift with only a 2.12% level of confidence.
This approach is worth testing again, but on donation funnel that has ample traffic and overall conversions. We simply don't have enough data from this experiment to prove any effectiveness or lack of effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a relevant image on an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will appreciate the image and gain inspiration from it leading them to make a gift to receive the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
In an effort to boost instant donor conversion rate through ACI’s Red, White, and Blue direct ask funnel, we want to test adding a relevant image to the donation page body section. The image is of Dr. Ben Carson reading the Red, White, and Blue: Our Flag Matters to Me and You book that he wrote to an audience of American children. We selected this image because it encompasses his image and likeness, his influence and care towards educating and engaging the American youth, and the book he is reading is the book someone can make a donation to receive on this donation page.
We believe that implementing this warm, inspiring, and relatable image as potential donors read through the value proposition will encourage more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of the image within the value proposition copy did not provide a boost in donor conversion like we hypothesized it would. In fact, the treatment led to a 14% drop in donor conversion with a 68.2% level of confidence. We saw a steeper 22% decrease in revenue with a nearly valid 91% level of confidence.
This is our second attempt at adding an image of Dr. Carson and the Red, White, and Blue book and both attempts led to a decrease in donations. It seems our audience does not respond well to images, or perhaps disruptions, as they are reading the value proposition. They may arrive at the page already motivated and the image could simply get in their way as they're working their way to the donation form. They may arrive at the page and are turned off by the image, as they may associate images with ads or blogs, or something that may turn them off when they're online.
It may be time to transition away from image implementation and try experimenting with other elements of the page.

experiments.title: How shorter, more congruent donation page copy impacted donations for a direct ask funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter, more congruent value prop copy will result in an increase in donations because the donor experience will be better-connected and remain on topic as they transition from the direct ask ad to the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
TPPF’s CAF-Focused funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In our analysis, we see that roughly 75% of our traffic for this funnel is coming from mobile and the mobile bounce rate is 95%. In evaluating the user experience, we hypothesize that the donation page copy is too lengthy – especially for mobile users – and that there is a disconnect between the direct ask Facebook ad creative and the instant donation page value proposition copy.
The Facebook ads are generating high click-through rates we believe primarily from the issue-specific yes/no ad graphic in which we ask users if they agree or disagree with a question that is relevant to the opportunity we’re providing to them. The ad copy also speaks specifically about one issue but then speaks much more generally on the donation page.
We need to find a way to maintain the momentum from the ad level to get more people to convert at the donation level.
Shorter copy should better engage our mobile audience and more congruent copy between the ad and the landing page should hone the value proposition copy and better motivate page visitors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment variant with the shorter copy and honed value proposition copy did produce more donations than the control, however, the difference was only one donation. The experiment produced only 5 conversions total: 2 to the control and 3 to the treatment. The 35.3% conversion lift from the treatment variant had only a 26.2% level of confidence.

Simply put, the experiment did not reach the conversion sample to validate one way or the other after running for 30 days. Thus, we cannot make any valid conclusions. We'll keep a look out for other opportunities to launch this type of test.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a donor’s gift before a call to action, affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treating the pushdown headline to first answer “why” for users on the homepage will achieve a higher conversion rate because leading with “what” a donation accomplishes for the user and “why” will be more compelling than the command in the control.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Navigators created a pushdown/sticky bar for their Eagle Lake campaign. This asset targeted the main homepage, and the button would take users to the instant donation page for the campaign. At launch, the pushdown was underperforming compared to its effectiveness on past campaigns—we hypothesized that this was due to copy itself lacking value prop. In the control, the main headline starts with a call to give even before the user has been given any reasons as to why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to improve the underperforming pushdown/sticky bar for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign by changing the copy of the headline to address the "why" of giving. The treatment resulted in a directional 13.9% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold for validity.

Though not a valid result, we did see that the treatment captured

Ultimately, Navigator's audience seemed to respond similarly to both control and treatment versions of the copy. Of course, the next steps could be testing into new copy treatments but the question is whether this is the most likely angle to move the needle for Navigators' audience given the subdued reaction here. Instead, we'd like to turn our focus away from testing into copy and instead determine whether a visual change, like a goal thermometer, is what will prove most effective in communicating value.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline + congruent value prop messaging impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a continuation headline and more congruent reasons to give consistent with the theme of the funnel will achieve an increase in donations among donation page visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/04/2023
ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to similar offers. Click through rate at the ad level and petition sign up rate are very healthy, but we’re struggling to get donors to convert once they reach the instant donation page.
Reviewing the current donation page, two things stood out:
1) The headline comes off as conclusive and does not imply that there is another action the petition signer can take.
2) The reasons to give in the last paragraph are not on theme with the petition subject and therefore could be creating a disconnect that turns the page visitor away.
We developed a treatment that implements a continuation headline and closing value proposition copy (reasons to give) that align with the action they just took (signing a petition to stop judicial weaponization).
Let’s see how the treatment performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results were not what we anticipated. The treatment resulted in a 6.6% decrease in donations with a 20.1% level of confidence. Revenue to the treatment resulted in an 18% decrease with a 67.6% level of confidence. Ultimately, neither variant moved the needle.
Interestingly, there was a notable difference between mobile and desktop visitors:
Mobile: Treatment produced a 95.1% increase in donations with an 85.7% LoC
Desktop: Treatment produced a 42.5% decrease in donations with an 88.2% LoC
These outcomes came close to validating, however, it's difficult to put a finger on what led to these vastly different user responses given that the copy length stayed roughly the same and there was little difference in the actual visibility of the page.
We'll keep these results in mind and see if we can test this similar approach for other offers. If we do, we'll monitor to see if results are consistent or different to the ones we've recorded here.

experiments.title: How increased perceived security impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding additional copy for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion because there is perceived higher security.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 06/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
To increase donations and revenue, we decided to test increasing the perceived security of the donation form for PTV’s 18 Minutes with Jesus book
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we only saw a 4% increase with a 53% confidence level.
Due to the inconclusive results, we are going to test other methods of security such as an animated lock.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2023 - 05/29/2023
TPPF’s Pocket Constitution Booklet was getting a ton of traction as an individual content offer. We want to find out if taking that momentum by applying it as a premium as part of a new content offer funnel would produce a lift in donations for the new content offer (School Choice Survey).
We added an image of the Pocket Constitution to the School Choice Survey instant donation page, along with the following language at the end of the value proposition copy to clarify the premium opportunity:
“And if you make a donation of $10 or more today, we will send you a FREE Pocket Constitution for your home or classroom as a reminder of your commitment to better education in the Lone Star State.”
Let’s find out if the added premium offer increases donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test the hypothesis that adding a premium to an instant donation page would increase donations. The treatment included an image of a Pocket Constitution Booklet and an offer to send the booklet to anyone who made a donation of $10 or more on the School Choice Survey instant donation page.

The experiment ran for 32 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 17.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a 22.2% statistical level of confidence.

While the treatment did not produce a bump in donations like we had hoped, it did produce a large bump in revenue through a 645.8% lift with a nearly valid 84.8% level of confidence.

The average gift among donors to the treatment was $90 compared to an average gift of just $10 among those who donated to the control variant.

It seems the extra incentive provided by the Pocket Constitution Booklet premium made people want to give more, even though the minimum donation to receive the premium was $10.

This speaks to the "what do I get in return" mindset many people have. By getting an actual item in return for making a gift, it seems donation page visitors were willing to give more.

The nearly valid increase in revenue gives us the confidence to roll out the treatment, connecting all funnel traffic to the premium opportunity.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion

rows.name: Campaign Focused Donation Page - With Thermometer

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the goal thermometer on a donation page leverages psychological factors such as visual progress, social proof, competitiveness, accountability, and psychological commitment and engages potential donors on an emotional level and provides them with a compelling reason to contribute to the cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/26/2023
Luther Seminary is running its bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a goal thermometer to the donation page would increase donor conversion.
We added the thermometer to the treatment page from the start of the campaign and showed it to 50% of traffic. Users came to this donation page through email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that adding a goal thermometer to a donation page for Working Preacher subscribers resulted in a 4.6% increase in donations with a 42.1% level of confidence. While the experiment suggests that adding a goal thermometer may be effective in increasing donations, further testing is needed to confirm this.

We believe this did not validate as an increase in donor conversion because the Working Preacher subscribers are already comfortable and emotionally driven to support Working Preacher. Therefore they did not need the extra push that the thermometer provided.

experiments.title: How personalizing a donation ask with a signature impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing a donation ask to come from a well-known authority figure and adding his signature at the end of the ask will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will feel called upon directly by the authority figure to make an impact, and the signature will provide authenticity to the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2023 - 05/29/2023
For ACI’s Religious Liberty Survey funnel, we want to test a personalization approach for the offer instant donation page in which we will frame the value proposition to come from the organization’s leading authority figure. To execute this approach, we’ve:
updated the headline to call attention to the following message from the authority figure 
placed the message in quotations to show that it is being spoken/written from the authority figure
added the authority figure’s signature after the message to “authenticate” it
Our hypothesis is that this combination of personalization tactics will resonate more strongly with donation page visitors and lead to an increase in donations.
The control maintains value proposition language that comes from the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which presented a donation ask from ACI Founder Dr. Ben Carson with his signature added post-message, resulted in an 11.7% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.8%, below the required 95% for the results to be considered valid.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a well-known authority figure's signature to a donation ask can potentially increase donations, but further testing is necessary to validate the results.
In reviewing the treatment, we wonder if the "special offer" text next to Dr. Carson's signature on desktop and below his signature on mobile takes away from the personalization by introducing marketing copy between the message and the donation form. As a supplement test, we may want to experiment with removing or relocating the "special offer" text to where it does not disrupt the momentum the potential donor has going from the value prop to the donation form.

experiments.title: How using support-focused language impacted gifts on an instant donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that focuses on how a donor can directly support an issue on a donation page will achieve an increase in revenue because people are more motivated to give if they feel that their individual support is needed and important.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 04/30/2023
In an effort to improve the instant donation rate of a survey offer, we tested how a more conversational tone and donor-focused language would affect donations.
The original donation page focused on what policy reforms the organization was promoting to impact fiscal reform. It also mentioned that a donor’s gift would help introduce over 50 specific policy recommendations that would affect the donor. However, we saw an opportunity to focus more on “you” language and putting the donor’s support at the center of the proposed solution. We believed that prospective donors would be more motivated to give if they felt that their support was integral to solving the problems outlined in the survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language that focuses on how a donor can directly support an issue on a donation page may not be effective in increasing donations. The treatment saw a very slight decrease in donations compared to the control, although the results are not statistically valid. The treatment saw a larger 55% decrease in average gift.

Reasons for this decrease could be:
the treatment's removal of a bulleted list in favor of only paragraphs, which can cause people to move on from without reading
 	the treatment's removal of specific policy recommendations could have led the viewer to not understand what actual, tangible solutions their donation would support

experiments.title: How staging a donation ask to come from an authority figure impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing a donation ask to come from a well-known authority figure for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will feel called upon by the authority figure to make an impact, and this version of a donation ask will hold more value and emphasis. because  potential donors will feel called upon by the authority figure to make an impact, and this version of a donation ask will hold more value and emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2023 - 05/04/2023
For ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition, we want to test two different copy angles to see which one produces the most donations. The control is our conventional approach, consisting value proposition speaking from the organization to the end-user. The treatment take a first person approach, presenting the donation ask from ACI Founder Dr. Ben Carson.
Leveraging Dr. Carson’s name and likeness has worked in many previous tests, so we want to try it for this offer funnel and donation ask.
We want to determine if a message communicated from Dr. Carson will resonate more with potential donors and lead to more donations. Will appreciate the personal message? Will they take action based on the message of “will you join with me?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that framing a donation ask to come from an authority figure may have a positive impact on donations. The treatment featuring Dr. Ben Carson as the messenger resulted in a 2.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence was only 25.5%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
The very slight life in donations does not lead us to confidently believe that the treatment is the optimal option moving forward.
Revenue resulted in a 5.1% increase with a higher but still invalid 66.5% level of confidence.
For a future test, we may consider emphasizing Dr. Carson in a more unique way, such as through a humanized sticky bar at the top of the page. This would enable users to hear from him even sooner.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because seeing the authority figure in action and in a natural, relatable manner will make them want to support the organization and receive the premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 04/29/2023
ACI’s Red, White, and Blue book instant donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. This is a direct ask funnel where users go directly from an ad to the donation page, where they can receive this book when making a donation of a certain amount. We wondered if implementing an image of the author of the book and the face of the ACI organization – Dr. Ben Carson – would help improve donor conversion rate. The book is written for children and the image shows Dr. Carson signing the book for a young kid. Will the relevance of the image and the inclusion of Dr. Carson make potential donors appreciate the opportunity more and convince them to make a donation to receive the book?
Let’s find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment (instant donation page WITH the image) resulted in a 7.5% decrease in donations with a 42.8% level of confidence.
Revenue did however produce a 33.7% increase with a 90.5% level of confidence. Additionally, new visitors produced an 86.4% increase in donations with a 78.% LOC compared to returning visitors producing a 5% decrease in donations.
The overall results tell us that there is potential with this concept. Overall donations did not increase like we expected, but revenue did and so did donations among new visitors. Potential deterrents could be the treatment page layout: The layout includes two images, both of Dr. Carson. The images split up the page copy and could really disrupt the flow of value proposition copy. We may want to try using only one image on the page or right aligning the book image in the main body to avoid disrupting the reader's flow.
And perhaps this specific image is not our most effective. We may want try implementing a different image but with the same intention. It may be beneficial to test different variations of images to see which resonates best with potential donors.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Do different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase downstream conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
Although there was no difference in clickthrough rate, treatment 3 (with the emotional appeal) produced a 67% increase in revenue through a combination of an increase in conversion rate and a simultaneous increase in average gift. This shows that while the initial metric was not affected, those who chose to click on the third treatment had a much higher motivation to give, which resulted in more gifts at larger sizes.
Treatment 3 offered something that none of the other treatments did—a way to show love and support for the journal author. While the other three treatments showed ways that the user could support the site and connected the site’s impact with the author, the third treatment gave them a direct connection to the person they care for, and proposed a donation as the way to show love and support. This produced an opportunity to give that no other treatment could match.
This experiment prompts more testing around this language throughout the site, as well as testing around how to increase clickthrough rate on the donate box through accentuated placement and visual UI.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.

The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They tested three different treatments to see if they could produce a lift in clickthrough rate:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
 	The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
 	The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
none of these experiments produced a significant lift in clickthrough rate

experiments.title: How offering a premium versus a direct missional gift impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a missional gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 04/23/2023
Pathway to Victory is the ministry of First Baptist Dallas, and fundraises through a premium-focused program.
As part of that program, they have a new name acquisition program where people are asked to provide an email address and contact information in exchange for a digital version of a resource offer.
In this experiment, we were testing whether asking people for a gift after that initial sign-up with a missional ask had a better conversion rate than with a resource-based ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a missional gift for potential donors might lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 19.8% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 39.6%, which is not high enough to be considered valid.
Since this is an ongoing acquisition effort, we will let this experiment run until for a bit longer; we will also test this approach on different acquisition offers to see whether it is the actual premium being offered that is helping skew results, or if it is truly missional giving that motivates new names to donate to Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How communal language on a sticky bar affects child sponsor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. These sticky bars were placed on non-sponsorship pages, linking the child sponsorship page. Both sticky bars counted down the children left who needed sponsors. The control sticky bar had language focused on the urgency and the countdown of days. And the test had communal language around the reader being part of the mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were entirely inconclusive due to small sample size and no difference in sponsorships. Further testing on different audiences, such as their Restorative Justice page, where there is higher traffic and a more community feel would be an interesting iteration.

experiments.title: How does a stickybar with a countdown affect sponsorship acquisition during a campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: A countdown that increases urgency and expands on the value prop will increase sponsorships acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. The control had no sticky bar. The test counted down both days left to acquire sponsors and the children left who needed sponsors. It was placed on sponsorship pages to increase motivation and reinforce value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate due to sample size that was too low and not enough of a disparity between control and treatment. Our hypothesis is that this did not validate for two reasons:
The countdown clock did not create enough urgency or movement.
 	The high motivation during calendar year end typically decreases the effectiveness of a test, because those receiving the control are typically more motivated to give.
Organic Search visitors had a 481.1% increase in child sponsored with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 10.5% increase in child sponsored with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language on a sticky bar affects child sponsor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. These sticky bars were placed on non-sponsorship pages, linking the child sponsorship page. Both sticky bars counted down the children left who needed sponsors. The control sticky bar had language focused on the urgency and the countdown of days. And the test had communal language around the reader being part of the mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were entirely inconclusive due to small sample size and no difference in sponsorships. Further testing on different audiences, such as their Restorative Justice page, where there is higher traffic and a more community feel would be an interesting iteration.

experiments.title: Will adding a sponsorship countdown slider increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out on the main website to determine if adding a countdown clock vs. our original it would increase the conversion rate with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.

• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).

• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).

• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.

experiments.title: How adding statements of credibility to a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility language to the instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain belief in our organization and trust us to make a true impact with their contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 04/13/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis survey offer funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. We’ve run several tests on this page, including a recent test in which we added evidentials to the copy in order to make the problem more tangible and understandable. This test produced a drop in donor conversion rate. The result made us confident that our audience already has a strong understanding of the problem, and that the issue with conversion rate is elsewhere on the page.
So for this experiment, we’re testing adding credibility language with the hypothesis that it will convince our audience that we are capable of solving or improving the problems we face at the border. Perhaps the current donation page does not do a good enough job of separating TPPF from other organizations and selling us as the organization most capable of helping in this critical area.
Our audience understands the problem we’re trying to help solve, but do they trust us and believe that we can solve it?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding credibility language to the instant donation page for potential donors has a significant impact on increasing donations. The treatment group saw a 162.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a 76.6% level of confidence. We saw even greater results in revenue with the treatment producing a 614.2% increase in revenue for all traffic with a valid 96.5% level of statistical confidence. The average gift to the treatment was $20.40 compared to just $7.50 to the control.
Returning visitors converted four times through the treatment compared to zero through the control. This tells us that people who have experience with the organization and have engaged with it in the past appreciate the language separating us from other organizations.
Based on these findings, it is recommended that future experiments focus on improving the credibility and trustworthiness of the organization in order to increase donor conversions. Some future tests could include adding social proof, testimonials, and other forms of credibility language to the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations – attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will have an improved understanding of the impact their gift would have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 03/31/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
It’s important to note that we ran a previous expanded value proposition test on this main donation page roughly a month ago and it produced a 28% drop in donation conversion rate with a 85.9% level of confidence. The focus of that test was to bring attention to the “reasons why” someone should give. We laid out five reasons via bullet points but the approach was deemed unsuccessful per our audience’s response. You can view the experiment write up at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/pRdg
Our hypothesis stemming from our previous experiment’s results was that that because someone who clicks a “make a donation” button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to “stay out of their way” by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
For this experiment, we eliminated the “reasons why” someone should give – due to the high motivation someone has when clicking “make a donation,” we also hypothesized that they already have at least one reason to give in mind and therefore do not need more reasons presented to them. Here we cut down the copy from our previous test while still expanding the value proposition copy on the current control variant, this time focusing on a broader message of how their gift would help our nation today.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 30 days, this experiment produced flat results. The treatment resulted in a 0.27% decrease in donations with a less than 1% level of confidence. The drop was not as extensive as our previous donation page value proposition experiment, which further supports our hypothesis that the previous experiment's extensive copy was not well-perceived by our potential donors. However, the less extensive, but still enhanced value proposition we used for the treatment in this experiment still did not produce positive results.
Returning visitors saw a 73% increase in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence, while new visitors saw a 34.5% decrease in donations with an 83.3% level of confidence. It's difficult to explain the negative results of new visitors as that's the segment we would expect would benefit from expanded value prop and learning more about our cause.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page may not always be effective in increasing donations. It is important to consider that visitors who click on the "make a donation" button are already highly motivated to give, and adding more copy may create unnecessary friction.
The control variant continues to stand. For a next main donation page test, we may want to consider taking the existing language from our control and expanding specifically on it. The control mentions "America's founding principles" both in the headline and the main copy. Perhaps that phrase has great importance, clarity, and appeal to our audience, and instead of focusing on copy on something else like core pillars, we should use America's founding principles to expand on within our messaging.

experiments.title: How an exit-intent popup affects overall donation rates on a donation page during a recurring-giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing an exit-intent popup with a one-time ask for traffic attempting to exit a recurring-giving donation page will achieve higher overall donation rates by presenting a lower-commitment offer to leverage the reject and retreat principle of persuasion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 03/17/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on acquiring new monthly donors, we wanted to know how an exit-intent popup would affect overall donation rates.
As the name applies, the exit-intent popup would populate only as a prospect attempted to leave the donation page without completing a donation. The specifics of the popup were designed to leverage the persuasion principle of reject and retreat. This principle is based on the idea of reciprocity and states that when someone is presented with a perceived large commitment and rejects the offer, they may be more likely to accept a subsequent offer if it has a perceived lower level of associated commitment—due to the fact that the prospect will be motivated to reciprocate the retreat by accepting the smaller offer.
In this case, the exit-intent popup would provide prospects who are attempting to leave the page without becoming a recurring donor the opportunity to still make an impact by instead giving a one-time gift of the same amount.
For this experiment, 50% of donation page traffic was shown the exit-intent popup while the other 50% did not see anything when they closed the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment showing the exit-intent popup did produce a slight lift in overall donations, we are unable to draw any definitive conclusions about this experiment based on how the results were tracked.

Because the experiment session "fired" based on page load (rather than popup load) we are unable to determine whether it was the popup that persuaded more people to give or if these results occurred merely by chance. We were also only able to achieve a 39% level of confidence in these results. This does not mean that the tactic itself is invalid, just that in order to retest we will want to make sure the experiment "fires" only once someone see the popup and attempt to obtain a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How asking existing donors for a recurring donation on an instant donation page affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition to a recurring donation ask on an instant donation page for existing donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because these people are donors and have shown that they care about this organization in the past.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 04/01/2023
Luther Seminary is wanting to increase the number of recurring donors they have. In order to try and get existing donors to convert to recurring donors, we decided to target their existing donors with Facebook ads that advertised their free content offers. They typically offer study guides or online courses.
We decided that 50% of traffic would see the regular instant donation page that includes value proposition around funding the next resource, and the other 50% of traffic would see an instant donation page with value proposition centered around becoming a monthly donor with the recurring tab preselected.
We made sure to only target donors by setting the audience targeting to include traffic when the utm_content contains “donors” (the name of the asset).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring ask instant donation page saw a 3.8% lift in recurring conversion with a 69.2% level of confidence. But this is only factoring in one gift.

We did see a 74.4% decrease in overall giving with a 97.6% level of confidence for one-time donations.

Due to this experiment running for over a month, we decided to turn it off. The traffic to this experiment was very low because this ad was in a multi-offer campaign specifically targeting existing donors.

Although we did not see a valid increase or decrease in recurring giving, we do believe the recurring ask negatively affected giving all together. We would like to test this experiment again for an offer that is receiving more traffic.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving process from 2 pages to 1 page impacts donor conversion rate (pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experience to one step  for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because reducing steps to a process can reduce friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/09/2023
In a previous experiment, the Navigators successfully increased donor conversion rate by reducing the number of steps in the giving process from 3 to 2. Our hypothesis was that we would reduce even more friction in the giving process by consolidating the experience into a single page. Unfortunately, the organization’s current giving platform was not able to accommodate a single step giving experience. Therefore, we used a completely different platform for this experiment’s treatment. We kept the control as-is in its current platform and the treatment on a new platform as a single-page experience.
In a previous iteration of this experiment we saw a valid 55% decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that the user may have experienced a break in branding congruity. So, we rebuilt this experiment on another offer and made the donation page look more like the original donation page in terms of colors and aesthetics.
We only ran this experiment in one ebook offer funnel and most of the traffic was driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 73%, indicating that the results are not valid.
Though slightly better in results from our previous experiment, we're still seeing a decrease in donor conversion rate which was not expected.
Additionally, we thought that the control might be more successful in converting returning donors because there is a "log-in" feature in the original platform that may be a convenient way for pre-existing donors to give. However, when looking at new vs. returning donors in each segment, there is virtually no impact in conversion rate among each segment.
Ultimately, we believe that there are too many new variables introduced in a completely new platform that could've impacted giving. It may not be that reducing the experience down to 1 step is reducing conversion rate. Other features like not being able to personalize the first name, or including Anedot express giving/Google pay, are features we did not initially account for.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2023 - 03/31/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (showing the thermometer) resulted in a 7.8% increase in donations but with a level of confidence of only 34.8%. The treatment produced 64 donations to the control's 54. Revenue saw a 9% increase through the treatment with a level of confidence of 65.4%.
