experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift, increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.
When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.
When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.
It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.
Because of this, and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: How communal language on a primary donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying communal language for main donation page visitors  will achieve increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge had tested “communal” copy that reduced transactional language and attempted to build a relationship with the user first to great success on a series of popups throughout their site.
They wanted to test this copy on their main donation page during the holiday season. They changed the question-based headline to a complimentary headline thanking the donor for their consideration, and put the power to give in the user’s hands with “if it’s not too much to ask”. They also used language that had worked on the popup that removed the Board of Directors from a position of power and put them in a position of service.
experiments.keyLearnings: All in all, the communal language failed to have much of an impact at all in the time that it ran. Motivation is very high for this page, so it can be difficult to affect conversion rate. On further observation, the headline may be confusing for visitors, and that might need to be reworked.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift on a child’s future and emotional health will impact sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: That showing what the child’s life is and what life will be after the donors gifts, with all the emotions and impact of that gift, will increase child sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their child specific page, the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor this particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the increased traffic to these pages, there was no discernible difference in sponsorship. A 52% level of confidence is a veritable coin flip either way.
Our hypothesis is that copy alone is not enough to impact conversion one way or the other. Our next step is to test tactical page design test to keep folks on the child sponsorship page and make this the complete decision point. With the current design, users are presented the option to leave the page at several different points. In fact, twenty percent of the gifts that were made on the control were people who left this page to give a donation to the main PFI donation page.
Revenue did experience a 27.9% decrease with a 93% level of confidence. This is because more people were likely to give a one-time gift on the control.

experiments.title: How personalizing the donation page with the reader’s first name impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that speaking to someone using their first name for visitors to the donation page will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2021 - 04/09/2021
Focus had a new offer running around Biblical truth in a broken world. It had a strong conversion rate and a lot of traffic. So we tested a personalization by passing the person’s first name from the landing page to the donation page and adding it to the header. Instead of just saying “Congratulations!” it said “Congratulations, John!” This has worked well in the past for other organizations and we wanted to test it for the Focus audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the opposite of expected. Using the reader's first name actually decreased donations by 36% and revenue by 37% with a directional decrease. This level of decrease held true across almost all devices and demographics.
Our hypothesis is that it caused more friction by the surprise of seeing their own name. The other possibility is that it made the Congratulations more noticeable and led them to believe their work on the page was complete and they did a good job and could now leave the page. We are now testing a form design on this page rather than a personalization, to see if making the choices in giving more clear and the process easier will increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual those segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.

When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.

When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.

It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.

Because of this and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: LWF – Email Subject Line Test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the best results for LWF?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/15/2021
Testing subject lines and the preheader. The results on opens and clicks were very similar, however the treatment had a higher conversion rate when it came to donations. While not above the 95% confidence level it does give us something to build upon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that was tested may have done a better job of attracting people that wanted to get the series as it framed the content as what a person would get out of the book when they read it.

experiments.title: How using communal language in a sticky bar impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language for website visitors will achieve increased traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2021
To highlight a Challenge Grant with an upcoming deadline, we added a sticky bar across Buckner’s website to draw additional clicks over to the donation page. For the control we used time-focused language to build urgency but we wanted to test incorporating communal language and remind visitors of their friendship with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the test for two weeks ahead of the Challenge Grant deadline but we did not receive enough traffic to validate the experiment and only reached a 55% level of confidence so we can't say for sure if this had any impact on donation page traffic. We'll look for additional opportunities to test into using communal language on housefile appeals where we have an established connection with the audience.

experiments.title: How does focusing on a specific ministry over the full suite of programs affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing in on one ministry that the donor will be supporting rather than all of the organizations outreach will allow for a deeper explanation of value prop as well as a simpler ask, and that will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2021 - 03/29/2021
Prison Fellowship International launched its first content offer, Where Love & Justice Meet. Since this was a new initiative for them and they didn’t have many learnings from the market yet, we wanted to test which of their ministries were more likely to convert a donor. They have a mix of programs—prison bible studies, sponsorships for children of prisoners, and restorative justice projects. So to find out what converts a donor better, we did a 50/50 redirect split toward two different donation pages—The control, a donation page that showed how the reader’s donation would support their many prison ministries and 2. The Treatment, a page solely focused on the ministry and impact of a their prison bible study called The Prisoners Journey. The goal was to see which inspired more gifts. Because the copy on the donation page for The Prisoner’s Journey bible study was geared toward a monthly recurring donation ask, we made that form a tabbed donation form to highlight the recurring giving option, which was another variation in the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a full month of testing, the results are inconclusive. Neither donation page outperformed the other in donor conversion at any level that was valid. Our hypothesis is that both pages had equally strong value prop and simply delving into one ministry is not enough to move a needle on an acquisition offer.
Another key metric we were aiming for was acquiring recurring donors. The TPJ page acquired three monthly donors in that time span and the general ministries donation page acquired 2, so no meaningful findings there either.
Our next step is to focus on the page specific to the Prisoners Journey and learn how we can use their Lamplighter's program for monthly donors to TPJ as a greater incentive to give a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How adding a donors first name to the instant donation page can increase conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the first name for those that visit the page will achieve an increase in both donations and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2021 - 05/01/2021
In this experiment, we added a donor’s first name to the instant donation page once they downloaded the eBook it said “Thank you, First name” while the control just said, “Thank you, friend”. This experiment was run specifically on Facebook and we had a 50/50 split on who saw the control vs the treatment.
The main metric we were looking to track on this experiment was the donor conversion rate. Previously with Hoover we ran this experiment and saw a lift but we didn’t launch it across the board so we wanted to test it again as they had also recently changed their donation platform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had to stop this experiment early so it did not receive the necessary traffic it needed to validate (67%LoC) but with it seeing a 12% increase in conversion rate and a 43% increase in revenue (99% LoC) we will continue to test this across multiple Hoover offers.
People enjoy seeing their name and it adds that personal aspect and helps reinforce that they completed the signup.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 43.8% increase with a 99% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had an 8.3% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 14.3% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 322.3% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How longer value proposition on an acquisition donation page increases revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Expanding value proposition on an acquisition donation page will increase revenue by increasing the perceived value of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights Institute had been running an offer for an eBook on Equal Rights for months. And the average gift was not as high as we believed it could be. So we decided to test including more copy on the donation page around the need for their gift and the impact it would make. The hypothesis was that this would lead them to give more.
This test ran for two months, longer than typically suggested, because we continued to see fluctuations in the results and wanted to give it a long time horizon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not result in valid findings. When looking at the different demographics. We found that it increased revenue with new visitors by 45% at a 87% LOC, and decreased the revenue from returning visitors by 24% at a LOC of 77%. The findings from these two segments of users negated the other when you view this test through all traffic.

Our hypothesis is that this test showed a directional lift in revenue for new visitors because it told them more about an organization that they are new to, and it taught them more about the value of the eBook they just received. But overall, this test showed that this copy alone was not enough to increase revenue significantly for a sustainable amount of time.

Despite the extra time, these findings were still not strong enough to roll out this test. We will be testing other avenues for increasing revenue.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement of a premium affects donor conversion for a postal chase donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visual reinforcement of a premium for postal chase visitors will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/22/2021
The Navigators were sending Easter cards as a premium offer to their direct mail file during the Easter season. They created a donation page for this campaign to receive traffic from the direct mail campaign and also as a landing point for Facebook ads which were shown to that audience to drive additional giving.
The donation page was very short and to the point. On reviewing it, the team wondered if adding a picture of the Easter cards (to reinforce what was sent in the mail) would increase conversion. They put an image of the card on the page, and added an additional paragraph of incentive-focused copy to remind them of the free premium they received.
They split the traffic to see which treatment converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment didn't validate due to low overall traffic, it reveals more tactics that can be used to directionally increase conversion on these pages.
While the same audience is targeted with both campaigns, we must remember that there is a time-delay between the direct mail piece arriving and seeing the digital ad, and must overcompensate to remind them of the piece they received in the mail (which could be one of many). When this reinforcement is done properly,

experiments.title: How increasing clarity that a content offer is free affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making it clear a content offer is free, while tying that to value proposition, will not decrease giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2021 - 05/20/2021
Focus on the Family had been receiving complaints that people didn’t know the video series was free and they believed they needed to make a donation to get the series. We tested one iteration of a donation page that increased the use of the word free in conjunction with the video series. That test decreased donations by 20%.
For the second iteration, we focused more on clarity and addressing the reader specifically, rather than increasing the reference to the series being free. You can see the enhanced test language below. This was a 50/50 A/B test that ran for four weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: This iteration of the test didn't decrease donations by any level of confidence while it increased clarity that the reader did not have to donate to receive the video series. We will be rolling this language out to their other instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How prioritizing personalization on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing the first name of the connection for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2021 - 06/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute donation page is where the majority of their conversions happen. As people are reading journal pages, they are able to give to support the site of their friend.
This page had long had a “cause and effect” value proposition that had not been challenged in some time. In reviewing this copy, they noticed that the organization name came before the “familiar” name of the friend whose journal they were reading.
The team came up with two treatments—one that just reversed the order of the “familiar” and organization names, and then another that also made a more direct ask and included language about a “vital connecting point” to increase the strength of the case for support.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither treatment moved the needle. Both treatments had slight decreases in conversion rate that weren't going to validate, and slight increases in revenue (through larger average gifts) that weren't going to validate either.
With a 34-35% donor conversion rate, it might prove more fruitful to get more motivated traffic from upstream to funnel into this page.

experiments.title: How making gift type the first decision point affects donor acquisition on premium offers
experiments.researchQuestion: Clarifying gift type at the beginning of the form will not harm donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2021 - 05/09/2021
FamilyLife found great success using tabbed donation forms on their main donation page. But before rolling this experience out to all of their donation pages, we wanted to ensure that this user experience did not not harm donor acquisition on acquisition offers. So we tested this on one of their most high performing premium offers, A Quiet Place. This was a 50/50 AB split test using an Optimize redirect. So half of the traffic saw the original format and the other half of traffic saw the tabbed form.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we were hoping, the tabbed form did not harm new donor conversion in any significant way. Neither revenue nor donations saw a valid decrease in this test. The next step is to roll this page out to their microsite.

experiments.title: Does the signer on a Dear Reader impact donation rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific signer on a dear reader for blog pages will achieve higher donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2020 - 12/31/2020
During KCBI’s calendar year end campaign, we added Dear Reader notes at the end of their blog posts asking for a year end donation. Originally the design was to have the note signed from “The KCBI Team”. But since the majority of their blogs are by one radio personality, we wondered if having her be the signer instead would increase donations. So we tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment or even show a directional lift. However, we did find that Dear Readers are a valuable source of revenue and that further experimentation is needed.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition and removing elements of friction impact donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How the value proposition copy on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the value prop copy treatment that we validated on the Genesis course stand on its own in the Revelation course, or does it only work when coupled with the removal of the premium offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2019 - 04/06/2019
DTS validated a copy test on the Genesis donation page that increased donations by 248% (experiment id: 10582), but also included the removal of a high-dollar premium offer from the page. They wanted to test the separation of the copy from the premium removal, so they tested the same copy on the Revelation course donation page, while still including the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not reach statistical validity and only impacted conversion rate slightly. We are going to re-run this test with the removal of the premium to see if the same concept that worked on the Genesis course works here.

experiments.title: How personalization on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalization on an instant donation page after a content offer increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 10/02/2020
As part of a giving day campaign, DTS was offering a content offer and wanted to test using the donor’s first name in the headline to see if this would drive conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach validity with this experiment, when evaluating the results and drilling down even further by each segment, we noticed that while overall conversion rate decreased on the page, we saw an 89.4% lift in average gift—specifically with the returning visitors segment—with an 88% level of confidence. Across all traffic we saw a 40.8% lift in average gift with an 82% level of confidence. These findings in the directional lift in average gift will be used for future testing and will be useful for developing what language we present on donation pages to returning visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring donor ticker affected a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the live total number of donors acquired towards the overall goal increase conversion rate on a donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2020 - 09/24/2020
With a specific goal set for a high urgency campaign, we wanted to test if adding a ticker to the donation page in a sticky bar to count the number of monthly donors would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the minimum level of confidence needed for validity of the experiment and saw a slight decrease in the number of transactions, we did notice that the average gift increased on the treatment, which is something we would want to test further to evaluate. We also hypothesized that the results could have been negatively influenced since the campaign met its monthly donor goal ahead of schedule, there may have been a reduced sense of need or urgency when visitors arrived on the page and saw the goal had already been met before they decided to contribute. For future tests, we want to launch the sticky bar earlier in the campaign to see how it affects motivation and we also want to test replacing the text-only version sticky bar with additional visuals like a progress bar or thermometer to further add clarity to the progress of the goal and what is being counted.

experiments.title: How adding an “About Us” section to the landing page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of an “about us” section to tell the organization’s history and beliefs to the landing page impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2020 - 10/03/2020
As a part of promoting a new content offer, The Fund for American Studies wondered whether telling more about their organization’s history would help boost email signups, but most importantly, whether or not it would increase the donor conversion rate after someone had signed up to request the resource they were offering.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 61% level of confidence, we observed a -23.7% decrease in donor conversion rate. This was subsequently driving a similar decrease in revenue, as well.
We did observe roughly a directional increase of +8.5% in email signup rates, but we decided to turn this off and focus experimentation elsewhere.
Further experimentation may be required, as we did notice an uptick in revenue from returning visitors with the treatment, but decreases in new visitors. This may be driven by new visitors largely being motivated by the offer, instead of the organization. Perhaps adding this description to the donation page may be a better place within the funnel for experimentation next.

experiments.title: How a dynamic donor countdown on a sticky bar impacts conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation at the end of a campaign by adding a sticky bar to the donation page to reinforce the value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2020 - 08/04/2020
In the final week of Focus on the Family’s summer campaign, they wanted to see if they would increase donor conversion on the donation page by adding a sticky bar to the top of the page that reinforce the need. On this sticky bar was also a dynamic countdown of how many donors were needed that day (this changed every day). They ran this test for a week against the control that didn’t have the sticky bar on it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in giving when the sticky bar was present on the page. The sticky bar increased motivation of the prospective donor by reminding them of the need and creating urgency around the goal of the number of donors needed to meet the need. This tactic has proven to work well for this organization and could be implemented in future campaigns without having a negative impact on giving.

experiments.title: How a different offer affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will lead to more emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
In this experiment, we had a list of attendees from an event we sponsored. We want more people to sign up for our own event, but ultimately, we just want to get them to opt-in to communication so we can develop a relationship.
So we wondered…is it more effective to send this list an email about our conference and ask them to register for a free ticket? Or should offer them something of immediate value like a research study?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page offer increased conversion by 72%, although it only reached a 78% level of confidence. Although this experiment didn’t reach the level of confidence we would like to see, it’s an indicator that NIO Summit might not be the best initial email acquisition point for an email prospecting campaign.
The donation page offer did increase clicks by 58% and reached statistical validity.

experiments.title: How bringing clarity to a homepage redirect experience impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by bringing clarity to a homepage redirect?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2020 - 07/13/2020
During Live Action’s high urgency campaign, we ran a homepage redirect where we redirected traffic that came to the homepage to the donation page for the campaign. We were curious what would happen if we brought more clarity to the experience and helped to orientate people with what was going on once they saw the donation page, so we tested language on the page that simply said, “Pardon the interruption but we wanted to be sure you saw this”. Our hypothesis was that people may be more likely to read what we were wanting them to see and do if we communicated that to them.
experiments.keyLearnings: Regardless of audience, or device type, there was no difference in donor conversion. Because this test was run on the very last day of the high urgency campaign, the motivation of donors was already high resulting in no difference in conversion between the control and treatment.

experiments.title: How removing a video affects email appeal revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the video from the landing page (and thumbnail from the email) increase donation revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/04/2017
We saw a number of different factors and potential learnings at play this test.
First off, eliminating the softer CTA (“Watch Video”) in the email reduced the open-to-click rate by 34.9% in the non-video treatment.  However, there wasn’t a corresponding reduction in donations—likely suggesting that the larger audience clicking the control was less motivated to give and more motivated to watch the video.
At first glance when reviewing overall giving, we saw a 2.1% increase in conversions for the non-video treatment . . . but at a 28.8% decrease in revenue. Neither met our 95% confidence threshold, although the revenue decrease had a confidence of 90.9%.  For this reason, we scrutinized the data more closely to ensure that we didn’t miss a learning.
After reviewing the individual transactions from both variants, we found that the gift amounts had significant outliers due to $,1,000+ donations.  Knowing when to filter data is a difficult challenge of testing and optimization—but since we have repeatedly seen that higher-value gifts are less likely to be motivated by an individual element of a broad-base ask, we typically exclude these from revenue validation.  After making this adjustment, we see the smaller difference noted in the results table.
Here are a few thoughts on this result, and why it requires additional testing to validate:
Response sizes are too smallDue to a low conversion rate among both target segments, there isn’t enough data to back up the normal distribution of gift amount due to its existing variability.  This is especially true of a non-donor segment that was tested with similar results, where conversion rate was just 0.007%.  Further testing, including during higher response windows, may improve the actionability of results.
The video wasn’t the only thing testedWhen removing the video thumbnail from the email, it was replaced with a different image, which was also rotated.  The additional call to action button was also removed instead of being modified. Copy was added in that area, but from a different testimonial changing the value being conveyed.On the landing page, the same additional testimonial copy was used, but was added much later in the copy compared to the video.  When testing video as a medium, the core value proposition of the video should be represented in text—if not, you’re also testing between different value proposition or the removal of some of the value proposition presented completely.
There were significantly more outliers in the control segmentWhile excluding $1,000+ gifts from both segments made a difference, it’s difficult to tell what the normal distribution of gifts is and whether segmentation played a factor in the results.  Typically the only ways to answer these questions are to measure historical distributions to be able identify abnormal ones, or to have a larger number of results to help balance any small number of outliers.
Due to the significant impact we’ve seen when replacing video on appeal pages, we would recommend further testing to ensure a clear and applicable learning for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The team at Focus on the Family had seen experiments from other NextAfter partners suggesting that video on donation forms could actually be harming conversion.  With an appeal featuring video set to go out, they decided to test into this effect to see whether their audience would respond the same.  This particular test was against a segment of existing donors, although a non-donor segment was also tested.

experiments.title: Will smart content on a general donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will referring to visitors by their first name and featuring their past giving history on the donation page result in greater donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2020 - 04/26/2020
We have been exploring a new donation page solution provided by DepositFix. These pages are built within Hubspot, so we can use Hubspot’s smart content on the donation page to reference a visitors name, past giving, etc. We decided to test a radical redesign of our general donation page featuring the visitor’s name and the amount of their last donation vs our current “static” donation page provided by RaiseDonors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
According to Hubspot, no one converted on the smart variations of the Deposit Fix page. The donations we acquired were on the standard/non-smart version which was seen by visitors not in Hubspot. Our hypothesis is that people were turned off when they saw their prior gift/first name on the deposit fix page leading to few donations.
We think the reason that the smart content on the page worked when coming from an email campaign is because donors had just read a personalized email, so then seeing a personalized donation page “made sense” in a way seeing a personalized page while just browsing the public site didn’t. We will continue to test these two solutions to see what more we can learn.

experiments.title: Will more text on a Facebook video ad lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more copy lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPR news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. They were running a campaign and wanted to focus on securing more monthly gifts and hoped a Facebook ad could drive more people to click and go on to make a donation. They’ve seen other Facebook advertising experiments where longer copy has led to increased donations and decided to test it for themselves. In each design, there is a video that was clickable but the main call to action and hope for the ad was to get people to click Donate Now and then visit the donation page where they could continue on to complete a recurring donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither the control or the treatment led to any donations and while the treatment had a slight increase in clicks, it was not significant. When looking at total unique clicks, the shorter ad (control) showed a statistically significant increase but the bulk of those were video views so, if the goal was to get more people to watch a video, it appears shorter copy is more effective. It should also be noted that the difference between the control and treatment was perhaps not great enough to prove the hypothesis. Also, taking a donor from Facebook ad to a recurring gift can be challenging.
So moving forward some ideas that require more testing and exploration are:
Shorter ads with videos where the goal is video views or just brand awarenessClearly longer ads where the goal is donations or clicks to a donation pageA short vs. long test with just an image and not a video that can be clickedDonation focused ads asking for a smaller one-time donation as opposed to a recurring gift

experiments.title: How the kind of offer impacts the kind of donor acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer would be the best acquisition opportunity on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/01/2019
Through a series of tests, Focus on the Family discovered that segmented GOAA offers – based off of what people are consuming on the Focus website – results in the best conversion from article pages. That said, we were curious if the GOAA offer was the best opportunity to present on the article pages. To test this, we proposed testing the free video series content offer that also had a donation ask at the end of the name acquisition funnel against the GOAA offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment only ran for ten days and we saw no difference in donor conversion between the two offers. As we dug into the data a bit more, we learned a couple of valuable insights…
Our primary metric is donations and revenue, but what is not communicated in these results is that the video series also allowed us to acquire 472 emails addresses in addition to the donations and revenue. These emails also provide an opportunity for downstream donor conversion which isn’t measured from this experiment.
While donor conversion was not impacted by the kind of offer, revenue was. The average gift on the video series offer was significantly higher than the GOAA offer. In fact, revenue increased by over 600% with the video series offer. What this tells us is that we are able to acquire donors that have a higher value to the organization through the video series, and we are able to acquire “purchasers” through the GOAA offers. People viewed the GOAA offers as transactional, whereas the video series as an investment in the ministry and lives of other families like theirs.
The video series is the best method to acquire donors for the organization on the article pages. They provide the opportunity to not only acquire high value donors for the organization, but capture emails as well that could convert to donor downstream.

experiments.title: How the addition of a dynamic sticky bar during a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation with the addition of a sticky bar on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2019 - 01/02/2020
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations from their primary donation page by including a sticky bar at the top of the page that reminded people how many donations they needed to reach their goal. The sticky bar was dynamic and would count down as donations were being made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The sticky bar had no difference on donor conversion. These results were slightly surprising as we have seen this tactic help improve donor conversion for other organizations. That said, this experiment was performed during the highest giving season and donor motivation in a year. Our hypothesis is that we didn’t see a difference in conversion between the control and the treatment because donor motivation was already high. We’d like to test this same tactic when motivation is more neutral to see if we can create urgency during normal giving seasons.

experiments.title: How simpliying donation page copy flow affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version of the donation page copy and format resonates with more donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2018 - 01/10/2019
Hoover institution was running their Dueling Populisms offer with their classic control instant donation page, with bulleted gift impact statements. They wanted to test a version that removed the bullet points and rearranged some of the original copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment did not validate, we did see a decrease in donation conversion from the treatment. This could be because the bulleted version lays out the reasons for giving in a more clear and concise manner.

experiments.title: How a “most popular” pre-selected value in the gift array did not hurt conversion or revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a visual treatment using that increases the visibility of the pre-selected value increase the average gift from instant donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2019 - 11/04/2019
In an attempt to increase the average gift for The Fund for American Studies’ acquisition campaign, we decided to experiment with a “most popular” treatment on the $50 amount in the gift array to see if it would increase the average gift amount from instant donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not see a validated increase in either donor conversion rate or revenue, we observed a directional lift in both metrics.
The key learning with this experiment was that after running for a month, this treatment DID NOT show, at any point, a decrease in either one of these metrics, which was enough for us to consider that the lifts we achieved were directional, and that there would not be risk in rolling this treatment out as the “new control” version of the donation page.

experiments.title: How using a brand name in donor acquisition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will using a brand name in donor acquisition affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2019 - 08/04/2019
CaringBridge had long acquired many donors through their “tribute giving“ pages, which allow people to make a gift in honor of a friend or loved one using CaringBridge. The language on the donation page let the donor know that their gift made “this site” possible. The CaringBridge customer care team hypothesized that using the name “CaringBridge” instead of “this site” would increase clarity and decrease refund rate. The development team was open to this, but wanted to make sure that using the brand name didn’t negatively affect conversion. They developed a page with the same creative, but using “CaringBridge” in place of “this site”.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the brand name performed no differently than the “this site” language. This was a positive development as it cleared the way to test this downstream to increase clarity and reduce refund rate.

experiments.title: How a dropdown on a donate button affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a drop down navigation option for one-time and recurring gifts increase overall (or recurring) giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2019 - 07/20/2019
For Caringbridge, the primary navigation has always had a prominent donation button in the top right corner. This button took all potential donors to a generic donation page that offered both one-time and recurring giving options.
There was a hypothesis that since a recurring donor and a one-time donor are two distinct segments, it may make sense to split this audience at the navigation level. Doing this would allow for targeted donation pages for each segment which may help increase the relevance and perceived value of the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months with over 10 million visitors, we found that the drop down navigation offer had no statistically significant impact on overall giving or the likelihood for individuals to give a recurring gift. This could potentially suggest that we're bringing this split too high up into the conversion funnel. Instead of the navigation, it may make more sense to emphasize this segmentation split (with associated value proposition) on the donation page itself. Similar experiments have been run using tabs on donation forms with success. That would be a natural next step.

experiments.title: How “impact language” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “impact language” that describes the outcome of the gift increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2019 - 08/09/2019
CaringBridge’s main donation page had always been difficult to test, since it had a relatively low volume of traffic and a relatively high conversion rate. However, they didn’t want settle for “good enough”, so they kept testing. The control copy asked “What does CaringBridge mean to you” and had a highly personal appeal that proposed various emotions that might be brought to the surface when one uses CaringBridge. They wanted to test “impact language” that talked about what CaringBridge does instead of what it means to the individual viewer. They drafted new copy that very simply told the viewer what would happen when they gave—loved ones would be connected.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the treatment failed to produce any lift, most likely due to the high motivation of donors, which makes it difficult to increase motivation through copy. The next test on this page will be a recurring donation ask popup that seeks to increase lifetime donor value rather than conversion rate.

experiments.title: How clarifying the steps of a donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will modifying the form headers of the donation form improve donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/16/2019
The monthly recurring donor page for Focus on the Family had held a relatively low conversion rate when compared to other donation pages. After running more than five experiments, we had not been able to statistically improve overall results. After testing several value propositions, advertising to the page, and even adding additional functionality (live chat), we decided to shift focus to the form itself.
Based upon learnings we had seen with similar organizations, we decided to try modifying the form headers to add clarity on the purpose of each section. We also numbered the headlines to help move the user down the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than a month, we found that the numbered form headers improved donor conversion by 27%. The additional clarity of the headlines helped reduce the friction associated with the form. There is also potential that the mention of the form security in the final section helped to reduce anxiety as well.
The potential impact of this experiment can extend beyond just the Friend of Focus form. It would be our recommendation to test these changes on the main donation form and all “gift of any amount” forms.

experiments.title: How more direct copy affected donations on a landing page

rows.name: Variant 1 -- copy test, more direct copy

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct headline that tells people what they just did (and more direct body copy) increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2019 - 08/26/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation sent chase emails to their file for a direct mail letter about ending the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare). They then versioned those chase emails to send to the rest of their file. They wanted to test the donation page copy to see if a more direct headline and explanation of what TPPF is doing to combat Obamacare would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: This copy experiment led to a 27.1% decrease in donations. However, there are several factors in the data that make these experiment results inconclusive. The sample size is too small, there is only a one gift difference, and the level of confidence is too low to come close to validating. Given this information, the experiment results cannot be used for future communications to the TPPF donor file.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a premium on the donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2019 - 07/11/2019
Focus on the Family was running a paid acquisition campaign through Facebook promoting a video series on intimacy in marriage. In a previous experiment for a different video series about parenting, they found that adding a premium offer on the donation page increased donor conversion. They wanted to test this approach with this marriage video series as well to see if they’d get the same/similar results. To test this, they added a premium to the donation page that people would see after they signed up for the free video series. The premium was the “5 Love Languages” book. They also added some copy about the premium to communicate the value of it to the end reader.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional lift of 18%. The results of this experiment are in line with what we saw from the previous experiment with the parenting video series. We would propose rolling this approach out to future acquisition efforts. The premium adds incentive to convert more new names into donors. What is important to note is that the content of the premium offer reinforced the content of the video series they just signed up for. The premium becomes the next logical step in the conversion process for people looking to improve their marriage.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the donation page impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the greatest impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 01/02/2017
Neither of the value propositions showed a statistically significant improvement over the other. With such a low instant donor conversion, is showing that the visitors were not highly motivated to give which makes finding the right language more difficult. The email offer is still receiving emails for a decent cost so we will try testing a new value proposition to improve donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: When launching the free eBook offer for The Jesus Agenda, we wanted to determine the right value proposition to use for the instant donor conversion opportunity. The initial thought was that the new subscribers may be motivated enough to make a gift to get the full version of the book. However, we also wanted to see if explaining the various aspects of Buckner’s ministry would increase the perceived value of the contribution enough to lift giving.

experiments.title: How presenting segmented offers based on a person’s engagement on marriage articles impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering people targeted premium offers increase donor conversion on marriage articles?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 03/26/2019
Focus on the Family has done significant testing within the articles on their website. From testing the kind of offer to the placement of the offers, they have optimized and continue to optimize the conversion opportunities on the articles pages for the significant volume of traffic. Most recently they learned that presenting people a premium/book at the end of the articles, that readers could receive by giving a gift of any amount, had the most significant impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this learning, they hypothesized that presenting people with a targeted offer based on their engagement on the Focus on the Family website might increase donor conversion. Focus identified the top premium offers based on what is most requested and the kind of content that people read and consume from their site and offered premiums that closely aligned with those topics. This approach was tested against offering people one single premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we’ve continued to monitor and dig into the results of this experiment, we have discovered an interesting finding that is misrepresenting the results and learnings. A significant portion of the traffic on the article pages are either first time visitors to the Focus website, or this is the first article they’ve consumed from Focus. This audience has not been receiving targeted content offers based on cookies. Because of this, 69% of the donations from the treatment results have been receiving the single content offer/premium.
After discovering this and learning what was happening, we have paused the current experiment and relaunching it to target that audience with targeted content offers based on the current article they are reading. This small nuance to the experiment is critical in knowing whether a targeted content offer based on the kind of content people consume helps increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: Impact of changing the design of a banner ad and directing it to a monthly donation page

rows.name: Dear Reader to pledge to monthly gift

experiments.researchQuestion:
We wanted to learn two things:
Would changing the design of the ad so it was conversational and matched the look/feel of the site decrease visual friction for visitors
Would directing people to a monthly donation page increase donor revenue on the ads.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2019
For several years, we have been running banner ads on a popular pro-life website to try to drive traffic to become donors. Our most successful ads have been ads for our pledges, the most recent being one against infanticide. We wondered if making our ad match the look and feel of the site, rather than have its own design, would decrease friction for visitors. Additionally, we wanted to see how well conversational language would work in the ad. Our final desire was to see how well directing people to a monthly donation page vs a one-time donation would impact the revenue generated from the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for a month. Both ads had great engagement, however the ad on the sidebar got far more clicks than the dear reader variation. We also, as expected, got far more donations on the sidebar ad as it led to a one-time donation page. We brought in 19 donations and raised $633 on that ad. However, through the dear reader variation ad, we brought in 4 new monthly donors and raised $195.70 in their first months gifts. This will result in $2380 over the first year of their giving, 369.7% higher revenue than the control ad. 

Moreover, while the sidebar ad had far more clicks than our variation, it had a lower conversion rate to donors, even with it using a one-time donation page. .27% of clicks on the ad became one-time donors while .31% of clicks on the dear reader ad became monthly donors. While we didn’t have enough samples to validate this portion of the test, it is encouraging to see that we got higher donor conversion on the dear reader variation.

Our next test will be to change the sidebar ad so it drives traffic from a pledge to a monthly donation page. This will let us see which ad brings in more monthly donors.

experiments.title: Dear Reader footer ad vs a mid-page pledge ad (500% increase)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will bring in more monthly donors, the dear reader footer ad or the pledge ad?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/13/2019
In a prior test, we found that we could significantly increase the revenue from our Life News ads by creating a footer ad that looked like part of the site, using “dear reader” language on the ad, and linking it to a pledge and then to a monthly donation page. (the results of that test are here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2423) The “dear reader” ad had higher conversion and higher revenue than the pledge ad, but the pledge got more clicks. We wondered if we would bring in more monthly donors on the pledge due to its higher volume of clicks if we changed it so the pledge ad linked to the same monthly donor campaign as the dear reader one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the dear reader maintained a higher click to donation rate (16.67%), the pledge ad had 627% more traffic. This caused us to bring in 500% more monthly donors on the pledge ad than the “dear reader” footer ad. We also had a 76% higher email acquisition rate on traffic to the pledge ad vs the dear reader.
We aren’t sure how much of these results is due to the higher placement on the page of the pledge ad. We will likely test a “dear reader” version of this mid-page placement in the future.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page treatment impacts acquisition revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall revenue to the acquisition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2019 - 08/03/2019
After running for nearly six months, we had not been able to achieve a statistically significant lift to the donation page used with Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer. After multiple iterative approaches, we decided to test a radical redesign of the page. We combined multiple factors based upon positive results seen with similar organizations.
The down side to this approach is that it makes it difficult to isolate exactly what helped move the needle if a lift is achieved. However, the overall goal was to lift revenue and if that could be achieved then knowing the exact reason for the lift became less important.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a statistical level of significance, we did see an increased average gift from the treatment version. Initial results saw the treatment also have a higher conversion rate but this balanced out after a few weeks of running. This is a good reminder of why it is so critical to ensure there is both a significant amount of time and total conversions before making a call on any experiment.
Our current hypothesis for the increase in average gift is that the treatment makes a targeted ask of $50 while the control version asks for $25. However, given all of the other factors that changed, we will need to isolate this factor to prove it out in the future.

experiments.title: How consistency between the call-to-action and the body copy affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will consistent messaging of the value prop increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2019 - 07/21/2019
The Leadership Institute’s campus newsite CampusReform.org is proven to be major point of interest for donors and prospective donors. The Leadership Institute wanted to run a test to optimize the Campus Reform donation page. They wondered: will consistency between the call-to-action and the body copy using the word “expose” and a concentrated message of the value prop increase donations? They hypothesized that it would.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version of the donation page with concentrated copy led to a 42.5% increase in conversion rate. This experiment did not validate due to the small sample size and number of conversions. It appears that people prefer a more concise message that is consistent throughout the page. The Leadership Institute decided to roll out the winner of this experiment.

experiments.title: How the experience on a donation confirmation page impacts subsequent gifts and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase the number of subsequent gifts and overall revenue by improving the experience on the donation confirmation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2019 - 05/15/2019
A lot of focus gets put on the optimization and improvement of the actual donation process and little attention is put on the experience after a person makes a gift. We hypothesized that improving this experience and making the donor feel valued, celebrated, and affirmed in their decision to make a gift to Focus on the Family we might be able to impact and improve subsequent giving and overall revenue from the donor in the future. To do this, we developed a treatment experience on the donation confirmation page that thanked the donor by name, celebrated the donor in the copy we used, reinforced the value proposition of their gift by sharing two testimonials, and even had confetti explode on the page as soon as the donor landed on the page. We tested this approach for over six months to really assess the impact that we might be able to have on giving over a longer period of time. Half of the audience during those six months experienced the control confirmation page and the other half experienced the “Super, Happy, Thankful” treatment experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To analyze the winner, we looked at donors based upon the initial treatment they saw and removed any recurring donations since they were automated. We then looked at the likelihood of a donor to make a subsequent gift, the number of subsequent gifts, and their change in revenue (did their subsequent giving increase). In all of those situations, there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment.
Even though we didn’t see an impact on future gifts and revenue, the treatment did a better job in thanking and celebrating the donor – which is important. Maybe the one joyful, celebratory experience on the donation confirmation page wasn’t enough to impact giving, but could we create that same experience in a series of emails every single month for the donor and have an impact on giving? We’d like to test it.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up presented at the time of a donation being processed impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the recurring gift pop-up increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2019 - 06/21/2019
Back to the Bible wanted to increase the number of recurring donors coming from their primary donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that presented a pop-up at the moment a gift was being processed with the opportunity to convert their one-time gift into a recurring donation. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional increase in recurring gift conversion of 38% with the recurring gift pop-up. What this tells us is that people are willing to make a recurring gift if we clearly communicate the even greater impact they can have through their gift and give them the opportunity to do it. When analyzing all data associated with this experiment we also found that it did not significantly impact the likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an offer in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an image of an offer in an email affect click rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2019
Hillsdale College recently launched a new online course titled “The Second World Wars.” They are now offering this course as a DVD boxed set and are offering it as a premium offer to their housefile. They wanted to know whether or not adding an image of the DVD boxed set to the email would have any effect on click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 4% lift in the treatment version with the image of the DVD. The confidence level of this experiment was not at or above the 98th percentile, so this experiment shows no change in results. Even with a larger sample size, this test is not likely to validate. This is because there is such a small difference in click rate that a larger sample size will not determine any valid conclusion. This image test can be run instead on the landing page where people actually convert. We hypothesize that people like to see visuals with text as it helps them better understand what is being asked of them/offered to them.

experiments.title: How linking one line of text instead of two affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will having only one linked line of text in email copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2019 - 07/10/2019
Hillsdale College ran a fiscal year end campaign. In this campaign, they versioned emails to lifecycle audiences: lapsed donors, active donors, and nondonors. In their email to active donors, they wanted to know if linking one line of text to a donation page instead of two or more lines would affect click rate. They decided to test this on their third email appeal in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 5.8% increase in clickthrough rate in the treatment with only one linked line of text to a donation page. However, this is not a large difference and the level of confidence is insufficient to conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: How deploying a coaster premium affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of a premium incentivize more people to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2019 - 06/28/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation ran a campaign to fund a legal case to defend John Yearwood against the federal government. They had a coaster premium emblazoned with Texas’ famous motto “Come and Take It” that they had used in direct mail, and wanted to test it on the donation page to see if it would increase conversion. They created a simple treatment that added an image of the coaster to the page and split traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than two months, the coaster premium showed a directional decline in donations, though it wasn’t statistically valid and did not reach a valid sample size. Since this wasn’t able to move the needle, TPPF decided to go back to the drawing board and find a new test to deliver a radical result.

experiments.title: How positioning the printed guide impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion:
Would using the appeal of the ability to take notes in a physical book improve donor conversion rate?
5/16/2019 Note: As it stands currently, there is only a 1% difference in Stickybar clickthrough rate, but the donor conversion rate for the “take notes in a printed guide” message is proving to increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/23/2019
While continuing experimentation around what sticky bar message generates the most donors, we wanted to test the donation page headline (from the control) against a new message. We assumed that one motivation to receive the printed guide might be simply because the person like the benefits of a physical book. So, we played with the message to lean into that assumption.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a successful micro-donation acquisition campaign to raise money to feed Holocaust survivors. However, they wanted to see if they could increase conversion or revenue through additional landing page testing. They created a treatment that centered the value proposition at the top, included a picture of a Holocaust survivor, put the copy on a white background for more legible reading, and then reoriented the gift array. Previously, the gift array offered $3 and $90, with a pre-filled $30 ask in the open field. The treatment page offered the $3 gift, along with a $45 gift and a $90 gift. The hypothesis was that the landing page elements would increase conversion and the bracketing might increase average gift. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 67% increase in revenue that had a valid level of confidence, though with the small overall sample size of donors, this was not fully valid. This showed that the page elements did not, in fact, increase conversion, but that the bracketing did increase the average gift enough to validate the separation between the control and the treatment.

experiments.title: How a personal donation ask affects conversion on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value proposition in letter form from a known figure of the organization on a donation page increase donor conversion more than a traditional stepped approach with a premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2019 - 05/11/2019
Heartlight Ministries was offering a free online course called Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, and wanted to test a previous approach on the donation page that involved a letter from Mark instead of the original stepped donation approach with a premium. This letter from Mark was a much more conversational approach, that told the story of Heartlight and explained that the true goal of the ministry was to equip so many parents, that they didn’t need to run the boarding school anymore.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment option increased donations by 94%. Even though it wasn’t fully valid, this tells us that the message of Heartlight and Mark’s heart for the ministry came through in this copy enough to move people to want to give more than a traditional stepped approach, or the option to get a premium for a donation. This messaging angle, which we refer to as “close the camp”, is a powerful angle that emotionally connects with a potential donor. It is also written in Mark’s voice, which makes a strong impression on donors who heard from Mark in the Facebook ad, in the housefile email, and throughout the course, so they know him and trust him when he says these things.

experiments.title: How Faith-Driven Copy Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page more focused on the religious value proposition of “C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2017 - 08/31/2017
Due to lower traffic volumes, this test did not validate. Further testing will be necessary to provide an answer to this research question. However, Hillsdale can still consider the results that they found. Though there wasn’t a statistically significant difference, Hillsdale did notice that the faith-focused copy was approaching a valid decrease in donor conversion.
This decrease in donor conversion may suggest that the treatment page copy did not increase the clarity of the course’s value proposition. It may also indicate that visitors felt an increase in tension if they did not align with the overtly religious value proposition. New tests are being developed to provide a clearer answer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After noticing new room for improvement, Hillsdale College launched a series of new donation page treatments. “C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” is one of their most popular online courses. After reviewing their donation page, they hypothesized that emphasizing why they offer this course from a religious viewpoint would help clarify the course’s value proposition for visitors. They created a treatment and launched a test to find out.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a main donation page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding third-party validation and more context about the organization in the copy increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) is testing their donation pages to increase conversion rates. They wondered if talking more about Texas and how TPPF can help Texans and the rest of America be more free would make a bigger impact on donations. They decided to take a radical redesign approach to their main donation page. They added a header with a call-to-action, more copy that explained what TPPF does and who they help, removed links that took people to a different page, and added an image and quote of a famous Texan for third-party validation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results from this experiment were surprising. The radical redesign caused a directional 38.4% decrease in revenue, but with no statistical validity. The original donation page had a better conversion rate, even with the less-inclusive copy and the links to another site. However, because the confidence level is not at or above 95%, this experiment is not statistically valid. TPPF should continue to run experiments on their main donation page, but overall conversions combined will make it difficult to validate a result.

experiments.title: How adding third-party validation to a donation page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a quote and image of a famed Texan increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) ran a petition in support of John Yearwood, a veteran who is working with TPPF to defend his land from the government. After the petition, petition signers have an opportunity to make a donation in support of TPPF and people like John Yearwood. TPPF wanted to run an experiment on the donation page where they tested the effect of third-party validation on conversion rates. They decided to use a quote and image of former Texas governor Rick Perry to see if it would lift conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The conversion rates for each of the two donation page versions during the one month that this experiment ran were the virtually the same, which meant that the photo alone had no impact on conversion rate. This means that TPPF must make bigger changes to impact conversion in this funnel.

experiments.title: How removing a high-dollar premium from a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing a high-dollar premium from a donation page increase instant donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 05/03/2019
DTS was running a test on their Revelation donation page that utilized copy that had just increased donations on the Genesis course in a previous experiment, but also removed a high-dollar premium that they hypothesized was leading to fewer instant donations, because they thought it was possible that people didn’t want to give at the level necessary to get the book ($75).
experiments.keyLearnings: This test didn't validate, but showed a slight trend towards a lower instant conversion rate with the treatment. These results are likely to be better analyzed in future testing if the same copy is used on both control and treatment, but that the premium offer's placement on the page is an isolated variable.

experiments.title: How an updated value proposition impacted conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on children instead of the whole family and that incorporates scripture increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2019 - 05/02/2019
The text used on Buckner’s main donation page was created over three years ago and used messaging and tactics that we had long ago tested out of in our acquisition efforts. In an effort to boost the conversion to the page, we decided to test out an updated value proposition that incorporated scripture and focused more on the tangible impact to the children that Buckner serves instead of the holistic care being provided to all individuals.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant change in donor conversion from the treatment version of the donation page.
