It is possible that we did not identify the right value proposition for this audience. However, it is our hypothesis that it is likely the form itself that is causing a donor conversion rate lower than we would prefer. The next step in testing this hypothesis is setting up a new experiment that reduces the friction and anxiety associated with the form.

experiments.title: How using a primary issue on the main donation page increased donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can moving a “defeat cronyism” -focused message into a single column layout outperform the two-column, generic message of “restore economic freedom” message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2019 - 04/21/2019
We wanted to experiment with improving the results of the primary donation form. In reviewing the “control” experience, we theorized that we could lift performance by addressing two issues:
Creating a more “issue-focused” message (defeating cronyism), and
Moving to a single column layout (instead of a two-column layout).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment ran for more than 3-months and we only achieved a 47.9% level of confidence, we did see a +15.0% increase in donor conversion rate.
We did notice that the treatment also saw a +60% increase in revenue (with a 71.1% level of confidence), as well.
Although neither treatment officially validated, we believe this information is directional enough (having run for 3-months) to move forward with the treatment donation page. It would appear that the primary issue the organization is largely focused upon solving (ending cronyism) is more inspirational, driving a higher conversion rate and average gift/revenue than the control message.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a premium added to a landing page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2018 - 06/01/2018
Compassion International wanted to test the impact of a premium on sponsorship conversion. They had a partnership with Chip and Joanna Gaines that allowed them to offer a book as a premium as a way to thank the donor for their sponsorship.
They added this premium to the sponsorship pages specifically around their remarketing campaigns driven from Facebook and launched an A/B test to determine the impact for conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the premium offer to the donation page produced a slight lift (8%) with no statistical confidence. This lack of variance seemed to indicate that by the time the prospect had reached the page, they had already made up their mind to give and weren’t affected by the premium.

The Compassion team decided to launch another test after this that moved the premium offer upstream in the communication chain to see if the premium would attract more traffic from the Facebook ad itself, and would result in a higher downstream conversion rate.

experiments.title: How matching gift language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will added matching grant language affect donor conversion after an online course signup?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/23/2017
Hillsdale College had received a matching grant of $400,000 to double gifts from their online courses. They wondered what the impact of adding this language to their online course donation pages would be—their hypothesis is that it would increase giving by giving donors an incentive to be generous.
They created treatments across three course donation pages that included the extra language for the matching grant and launched an experiment to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The matching gift language produced a slight, though statistically invalid decrease in conversion. A meta-analysis of Hillsdale’s online course pages showed that it was critical to not “break the spell” of excitement after signing up for a course.
Though matching gift language might work in other areas of fundraising, it might cause the prospect to think too much about the donation, thus breaking the spell and reducing conversion.

experiments.title: How Donation Page copy test between pro-life vs. fiscal angle on defunding Planned Parenthood
experiments.researchQuestion:
Does including the term “pro-life,” mentioning that we work with “pro-life organizations” and that we are for stopping all abortions resonate with our donors and increase gifts and revenue?
OR
Does only focusing on goal of stripping federal taxpayer dollars from going to Planned Parenthood increase gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 -
We have some limited knowledge that those who want to defund Planned Parenthood don’t necessarily identify themselves as pro-life. They are fiscally conservative and not necessarily social conservative. We want to drill into this and see how much of our base identifies this way. This will help guide messaging moving forward- helping with what terms to use and what terms to avoid.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects revenue on a donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page will increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2016 - 03/08/2016
In response to Hillary Clinton’s plans to repeal the Hyde Amendment, SBA List created a petition in support of the Hyde Amendment. Traffic was pushed to the petition page through email and Facebook advertising. Once someone signed the petition they were taken to a thank you page with a donation ask.
SBA List wanted to do a radical redesign of the donation page to try and increase revenue. The control was a page layout they had been using previously. For the treatment, they tested a one column approach with a new headline and body copy that reinforced and clarified the value proposition. They also presented fewer giving arrays and a new call-to-action on the button. They did an A/B split test to determine which page would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings: They were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the test. Though the initial data they were able to get did show the one column treatment performing better because of a higher average gift, it is not enough data to come to a conclusion with those results. A similar test will be done for a future campaign with a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How creating unique landing pages for every email in a campaign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will creating unique landing pages to tie to each email theme increase the donors to a money bomb campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2016 - 04/12/2016
During a SBA List money bomb, we wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations by creating unique landing pages for each email theme. Each email focused on a specific theme taken from their all-encompassing general value proposition. Besides wanting to increase the number of donors through reinforcing the message and theme from email to landing page, SBA List also wanted to see which theme of their value proposition, donors would respond to most. We tested these unique landing pages against a general, all-encompassing value proposition landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the test, initial results showed that unique landing pages for each email theme did not help increase donations. The general value proposition landing page was able to convert donors at a higher rate. These initial findings tell us that SBA List donors aren’t more motivated by one theme over another and instead are motivated by all that SBA List does. These learnings were implemented for the remainder of the campaign.

experiments.title: How the addition of value proposition copy affected donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition copy increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2016 - 05/19/2016
Susan B. Anthony list wanted to try and increase donations on their main donation page. In efforts to do this, they developed a treatment of the existing page. The existing page had minimal value proposition copy on it. For the treatment, they rewrote the existing copy, added value proposition language and a new headline and call-to-action that reinforced that value proposition. They split the traffic coming to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren’t able to validate the results for this test, the initial results tell us that the addition of the value proposition copy to the donation page actually caused a decrease in donations by 12.2%. The value proposition copy may have caused friction for those people who were already motivated enough to give once they came to the donation page.
The addition of the value proposition copy caused a decrease in donations for this test, but actually improved the second gift ask test we did on this same donation page. You can see that test here.

experiments.title: How two different value propositions on a donation page affected donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would motivate more donors to instantly convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 05/19/2016
In efforts to acquire names and email addresses, Susan B. Anthony List offered a free eBook called “Shutting Down Planned Parenthood”. After someone would request their free eBook they were taken to a confirmation page that had an immediate donation ask on it. SBA List wanted to test if they could improve instant donor conversion by testing two different value propositions. The value proposition for the control focused on getting the eBook in to the hands of more people and the treatment focused on advancing the pro-life movement and shutting down Planned Parenthood
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this test, the initial results told us that more people were motivated to donate to help put this resource into the hands of more people than they were to help advance the pro-life movement and shut down Planned Parenthood. The treatments value proposition may have seemed too big of a task for one person to do and accomplish through their donation. This could be why more people were motivated to convert with the control because it was more of a tangible thing that they could help do.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party endorsements impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd party endorsements improve the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2016 - 08/12/2016
On the main donation page for Susan B. Anthony List, we wanted to see if we could lift the donor conversion rate on the page. We decided to use some of the 3rd party endorsements that had been given to the organization by well known political figures.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we did not see any level of statistically significant difference between the two treatments. After further analysis, we realized that the treatment did not zero in on the 3rd party endorsements like we had intended. The headline modified the value proposition slightly, there were minor design tweaks, and the button copy was changed. Due to all of these factors, we are not able to say exactly what led to the insignificant result.

experiments.title: How the donation button design impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2016 - 08/12/2016
To optimize the donation funnel for the Susan B. Anthony List organization, we did an analysis to discover the biggest opportunities. What we found was that there were not enough organic visitors getting to the donation page to do any testing in that area. We decided to move our efforts forward in the funnel to try to drive more people to the page.
We had the hypothesis that if we made the donation page easier to find and put it into “first person” language, we could drive more visitors to the donation page. We decided to create an experiment to test the idea.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a nearly identical click through rate and donor conversion rate when compared to the control. The big take away from this experiment is that the button was not hard to find in the first place and that we did not add any significant value with the change in copy. Without those two factors influencing traffic, the treatment did nothing that would impact visitors making it to the donation page.

experiments.title: How quotes made along side the donation form affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd party quotes lower anxiety enough to improve upon the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 06/29/2015
During their a campaign to help make up for their financial shortfall, Jews for Jesus wanted to find a way to increase the conversion rate for the campaign landing page. A previous experiment had yielded a 252.6% lift by simplifying the donation form. It had reduced friction by removing key elements of the donation form which provided the lift.
For this new experiment, we placed quotes in support of Jews for Jesus from well known figures in the Christian community next to the donation form. By adding the quotes, we hoped to add 3rd party credibility which would, in turn, lower the potential donors’ anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, the 3rd party quotes did not have any statistically significant impact on the conversion rate of the page. It would seem that by the time donors made it down to the donation form, they had been significantly convinced of the credibility of Jews for Jesus that they did not need any additional information.

experiments.title: How high visitor motivation affects donation page experiments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will produce the greatest conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2015 - 07/20/2015
In an effort to lift the conversion rate and average gift of their primary donation page, Jews for Jesus launched a radical redesign of their existing design. The goal was to boost the value communicated on the page with additional copy, lower anxiety with quotes from 3rd party sources, and modify the donation form to reduce friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for nearly two months, there was no statistically significant difference between the two treatments when analyzing both conversion rate and revenue.
After examining the results in greater detail, we saw that both treatments had a conversion rate of ~65%; this was information not available before the launch of the experiment. A conversion rate that is this high is rare for an organization the size of Jews for Jesus. This would seem to indicate that only the most highly motivated donors are making it to the donation page.
Based upon these observations, the next logical step in optimization is to look earlier in the donation funnel to find ways to drive more visitors to the page.

experiments.title: How a quick donate button at the top of a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the “Quick Donate” button increase the number of donor conversions to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2015 - 07/01/2015
Through prior testing, we had learned that the Jews for Jesus visitors were highly motivated donors. As their shortfall campaign was coming to the end, we wanted to lower the barriers to these donors making a gift.  We decided to create an experiment that would place a “Make My Gift Now” button at the top of the page to jump the visitor down to the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite 28% of visitors clicking on the “Make My Gift Now” button having it didn’t significantly improve the overall conversion rate of the page.  However, it did seem to lower the average gift which may be attributed to roughly 1 out of 4 visitors skipping over the value proposition copy when they jumped down to the donation form.
By skipping over this copy, they did not read the reasons why their gift was needed which may have impacted how much they gave.  Again, this was not statistically proved and would require further experiments to validate the hypothesis.

experiments.title: How adding a login option back to the donation form affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will lead to the most revenue and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 10/26/2015
After two previous unsuccessful attempts to lift the revenue coming into Jews for Jesus primary donation page, we decided to take a step back and try to get a better understanding of the donors. The page converts at a high rate (~60% to ~70%) which means we are dealing with a highly motivated audience. It also means these visitors are likely existing donors which means they may not need as much convincing and may find value in elements that would cause friction with new donors.
After examining our previous treatment, we realized that by removing the login feature, we may have inadvertently made it harder for existing donors to give. On a donation page visited by primarily nondonors, this feature would have caused friction in the process but for existing donors, it would make it easier.  We decided to relaunch the previous value proposition test but add the Login feature back into the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our previous tests had decreased the conversion rate of the page. With this latest test, we were not able to statistically increase or decrease the results. What this means is that the login feature was impacting the donors’ likelihood to give.  It also means we have not yet discovered a value proposition that would prompt existing donors to give more generously.
In addition to our efforts to drive more traffic to this page, our next experiment will test a new value proposition in order to find a message that resonates better with the Jews for Jesus audience.

experiments.title: How a radical treatment to the primary donation page affects average gift size
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will produce the greatest lift to donor conversion rate or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2015 - 12/04/2015
On the Jews for Jesus main donation page, the default language is a simple headline with a call to action. We had the hypothesis that if we reduced the friction associated with the form, added language that expanded upon the primary value proposition, and inserted quotes from well known figures in the Christian movement, we could improve the already high average gift value.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment produced no significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue per visitor. It would seem that we have still not found a stronger way to communicate Jews for Jesus’s primary value proposition to their already highly motivated donors.

experiments.title: How adding a homepage overlay affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which homepage overlay treatment will produce the greatest lift to donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2015 - 01/04/2016
Jews for Jesus added a homepage overlay to their website during a year-end giving campaign. The overlay would pop-up once you came to the site to help drive traffic to the donation page. The hypothesis was that by removing the value proposition copy and adding a button, we could increase the donor conversion rate with this highly motivated audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment saw a slight increase in conversion rate, but the experiment didn’t validate so there was no real significance to that increase. However, it is worth pointing out that both treatments received a 7% conversion rate. This validates that the tactic (a homepage popup) is a good vehicle for driving donor motivated traffic to the right page. We just need to find if there are other options that would increase the perceived value of the gift.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the action affects donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Does making a specific dollar amount ask in an email lead to an increase in the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/14/2015
As part of a follow up to a group of petition signers, the Media Research Center wanted to find what email call to action would lead to the greatest number of gifts.  The standard approach had always been to make a gift request with a link and compliment it with a Donate button.
We had a hypothesis that if we added specificity to the call to action and specifically told them what we would like them to give, we could get more constituents to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach statistical validity but the results would directionally indicate that adding the “Quick Donate” button motivated a greater proportion of the audience to give a gift. When we add specificity to an action, we are able to remove a portion of the decision making the donor has to do.
We will need to do further testing to validate this approach in the future.
Additionally, when examining the actual gifts made as a result of clicking the button, we found that even if donors clicked a certain amount in the email, approximately 50% ended up giving more on the actual donation form.  This indicates that the value proposition on the page was strong enough to motivate a larger gift.

experiments.title: How the copy on the page affects conversion of a highly motivated audience
experiments.researchQuestion: Will recognizing and thanking the visitors for their previous action increase the donations and revenue to the subsequent page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2015 - 09/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a second landing page and given the opportunity to make a gift. The copy on the page continued the messaging on the initial petition page and targeted the outrage the visitors would be feeling at the lack of media coverage for the Planned Parenthood videos.
After reviewing this copy, we realized that what the follow on page never did is acknowledge the visitor’s prior actions, specifically the signing of the petition. We decided to create a new treatment that would first thank the visitors for signing the petition and then lead in to what else they can do to help solve the problem (i.e. give a gift).
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running the experiment, the new treatment produced no statistically significant change in conversion or revenue. It is our hypothesis is that this lack of change goes back to a previous experiment where we learned the motivation of the visitors to this page was extremely high. The copy on the page was not the reason the majority of donors gave so any changes made would not have any significant impact.

experiments.title: How a change in tone of the landing page affects the performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will yield the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find the best possible messaging tone to drive an increase in donations and revenue. A previous experiment using member benefits had not produced any significant lift so we decided to take a more radical approach at test the tone used on the page.
Our hypothesis was that landing page that more clearly laid out the problem and emphasized how the potential donor (instead of the organization) could help solve it would convert better. We also removed the 3rd party credibility indicators since, after further consideration, those may have confused the message.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither landing page was able to produce a significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue. This would suggest that the tone of the message was not influencing the campaign as much as the core message itself. For future campaigns, it would be best to take a more radical approach and test the entire messaging platform before getting to the final days of the campaign.

experiments.title: EOY Countdown Timer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a sense of urgency increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2015 - 01/07/2016
We wanted to test having a countdown timer on the landing page about two weeks before the end of the year to see if that would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We learned that the placement of the timer may or may not have had an impact on the number of donations. However, the average gift amount was $14 higher per visitor.
I recommend testing this element again next year, but placing it closer to the body of the copy rather than next to the amount they are choosing.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the donation form radical redesign increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2016 - 05/23/2016
Jewish Voice Ministries was testing out a radical redesign of their standard donation page. They wanted to validate the impact of multiple factors on the conversion practice. For this experiment, they increased the amount of value proposition copy, moved to a two-step form, and switched to a two column layout to move the donation form closer to the top.
experiments.keyLearnings: After over a month of running, this experiment did not reach a high enough level of statistical validity. When analyzing the treatment, it is difficult to determine what led to this result given the variety of factors tested.

experiments.title: How positive or negative tone in donation page copy impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a positive or negative tone on a donation page motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2017 - 06/21/2017
Live Action was running a high-urgency campaign centered around the topic of cultivating a culture of life. They wanted to test whether a positive tone that focused on donor impact (“You can help create a culture of life”) or a negative tone that focused on media silencing of the pro-life voice (“Our voice is being silenced”) would motivate more people to give.
The elements tested were headline and body copy. All other elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though this experiment didn’t validate, there was still a 12.7% decrease in conversion rate on the negatively-toned variant. This is due to a couple of things:
People like to know that their gift will make a positive and direct impact. Positive and uplifting language empowers the reader and makes them feel like their gift is making a difference.
The negative-tone test was focused on things happening to Live Action (being silenced by the media), which was not as directly affective to the donor as the positive tone test- changing their culture. People aren’t as likely to feel they can really change that situation.

experiments.title: How mentioning a specific giving amount in the call-to-action of an email impacts click-rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2017 - 06/05/2017
Live Action sends monthly email appeals to their email file. In an effort to get more people clicking through to their donation page, they wanted to test if putting a specific donation amount in the call-to-action would help increase conversion. The control didn’t mention a specific giving amount and the treatment mention a specific giving amount of $50. All of the other copy in the email remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the treatment email decreased click rate by 10%. We will need to do more testing around this idea to know exactly the impact a specific giving amount has on click-through, but we could hypothesize from this initial data that mentioning a specific giving amount to people presents a point of friction in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How an extended value proposition on a main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an extended value proposition increase donor conversion on a main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 12/21/2017
Live Action wanted to re-test an extended value proposition on their main donation page. Earlier tests had revealed that extended value proposition decreased conversion, but due to technical restrictions and errors on the donation platform, these results proved to be faulty and not reliable. Because of this, they ran a new test on the main donation page, testing a new headline and body copy that had a longer explanation of the value proposition of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, as there was not enough traffic during the two months the test ran to get statistically sound data on a winning treatment. We recommend testing this approach in the future during a high-urgency campaign that will guarantee more traffic to the page in order to secure a valid learning on this experiment.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which countdown clock would increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2015 - 01/28/2016
As part of their year-end campaign, Online for Life wanted to find a way to boost the conversion rate on their donation page.
In a previous test, we had learned that the addition of the “Every 26 seconds” clock on the donation page had increased donor conversion rate. Now, we wanted to it against a treatment with a timer that counted down to the end of the year.
Our hypothesis was that people would be more motivated to give based on the urgency of the year-end. We ran a split A/B test. The control donation page had the”Every 26 seconds” clock and the treatment was the donation page with the year-end countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn’t reach a large enough sample size to validate the test. We were wrong in our hypothesis, but did learn that the “Every 26 seconds” clock had a greater sense of urgency and supported and reinforced the value proposition creating a stronger message. The strong value proposition not only convinced people to give a gift, but to give a higher gift amount as well. That is reflective in the increase of average gift.
Taking these learnings, the “Every 26 seconds” clock will be tested again on general donation pages.

experiments.title: How multivariate copy changes on a primary donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing one set of copy variations against the control for primary donation page visitors will achieve a valid learning.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/16/2024
A public policy organization previously launched a multivariate test on their primary donation page in which 36 copy variations were tested in order to find the optimal combination of those changes. These variations included adding a “Your voice in Washington” subheadline, editing copy within a bulleted list describing what the organization does using the donor’s gift, adding “We fight for YOU” copy above the donation form, and more.
After running the test for 11 days, the organization ended the test and implemented changes on the donation page based on the combination of copy variations within the variant that led to the most revenue or donations during that time.
In an effort to solidify the learning on whether or not these copy edits led to a valid increase in revenue or donations, we created a new test with one control, the new donation page based on copy edits as a result of the multivariate test, and one variant, the original donation page copy.
We believe that by testing only one set of copy edits against the original copy, we will be able to reach a higher sample size for the variant and the test will be more likely to lead to a valid learning.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reverting to the original donation page copy led to a 21.3% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 79.3%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.
This means that the results of this experiment should not be considered valid or used for future decision-making. It is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or longer duration to obtain more reliable and conclusive results.
Future experimentation should focus on testing one variation of copy edits to identify individual changes' effect on donation page revenue or transactions.

experiments.title: How year-end specific language on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would year-end specific copy on the primary donation page increase donor conversion during year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 01/06/2019
During Back to the Bible’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the primary donation page if we used year-end specific language that communicated the overall value proposition of the organization instead of focusing on just one aspect of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment during the campaign. We hypothesize that this is because the motivation of donors during year-end is higher than other times of the year. We see that people are more motivated during the month of December than any other time of year to give and it is more difficult to positively or negatively impact that motivation.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates from The Daily Signal visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We had two questions:
How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates?
Which message would convert the most donors (stand with either (a) the president, or (b) Heritage)?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
On The Daily Signal’s year-end fundraising page in 2018, we installed an exit popup with two messages, where one was focused on “Stand with the President” while the other was “Stand with Heritage”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we gleaned some insight from the experiment.
Exit Popup Tactic Observations:
It’s also worth noting that the landing page secured a total of 27 transactions without firing the “exit popup”, and that the “exit popup” regardless of messaging variant secured an additional 42 gifts combined between the two message types. That means that the exit popup seemingly secured an additional 55% more gifts from donation page viewers who were attempting to leave the page. Subsequently, the revenue from the “exit popup audience” drove an incremental 58% in additional revenue to the audience that did not see the popup.
This supports the idea that the exit popup does indeed work to secure additional transactions and revenue.
Messaging Observations:
Although the “Stand with Trump” message actually had a 14% higher click rate on the affirmative button (40 / 350 = 11.4% CTR) compared to the “Stand with Heritage” message (which was 38 / 391 = 9.7% CTR).
However, we actually saw that the “Stand with Heritage” message drove an increase of 8.4% for donor conversions (5.9% vs. 5.4%).
Finally, The “Stand with Heritage” also had a 12.6% higher average gift value ($45.81 vs. $40.67) compared to the “Stand with Trump” message.
Final Conclusion:
With only 21% of the required sample size collected during this experiment, we propose continuing to test which messages work best to drive donor conversions, but that it would certainly appear that installing an exit popup on donation pages can have a profound impact as a tactic to convert donors and drive incremental revenue that otherwise would leave the donation page without interruption.

experiments.title: How a higher gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with higher values affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2019 - 03/14/2019
CaringBridge had used a giving infrastructure that had separate mobile and desktop donation pages. As a result, certain optimizations had not been rolled out to both experiences, resulting in slight differences between the two pages. In previous tests, CaringBridge had “unified” the copy on the page by testing out of all negative impact.
However, the mobile version still had another gift array that started at $25, unlike the others that started at $50. As their final “unification” test, CaringBridge wanted to test the $50 array that was found everywhere else to make sure there was no impact on revenue.
The control had an array of $25, $50, $100, $250. They launched a treatment with the array found on the other pages: $50, $100, $250, Other.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than 700 transactions, the new gift array had no significant difference in conversions, which gave CaringBridge the all-clear to launch the unified experience. This process, though it took time, is the proof-positive way to launch new experiences. CaringBridge could have easily just launched the same copy and donation array everywhere, but there could have been serious ramifications in revenue. This series of unification tests shows that there was no risk at all.

experiments.title: How a mid-dollar premium impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a $50 premium increase overall revenue to the page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
The instant donation page for Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had seen a significant increase in conversion with the implementation of Purchase targeting on Facebook and the switch to a Shoes for Orphan Souls as the donation ask. However, with the change to Shoes, we saw our average gift hovering around $25 (the cost to provide a pair of shoes.) In an effort to increase this average gift, we decided to run a test of offering a premium for a gift of $50 or more. Our belief is that by positioning this $50 ask as the first number they see, we would be able to increase overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we saw that the two pages had nearly identical conversion rates and the premium offer had a slightly higher average gift. However, this average gift was something that began trailing off over time for the treatment. Our hypothesis is that the premium (a calendar) became less relevant the further we got into the year. With the offer being less relevant, people started giving $10 gifts instead of the $50 which brought the average down to a similar level as the control.
A big takeaway from this experiment is that we were able to lift average gift when the premium is both relevant and timely without negatively impacting overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding gift handles to a landing page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a suggested giving amount increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2019 - 03/02/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. They wanted to know if donations would increase on the landing page with the addition of a brief explanation of how many pocket Constitutions could be sent to children with a certain donation amount. They thought that if they suggested a giving amount next to how many pocket Constitutions could be sent with that amount, it would help the donor make a decision that they would otherwise have to make on their own. They hypothesized that telling people the impact a gift amount would make would inspire people to give generously, thus increasing donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Hillsdale team saw a 7.2% drop in conversion with the gift handle treatment. This was after sending all traffic from the second email of the campaign to the treatment landing page, which suggests that there would be an even larger decrease in conversion had the traffic been split evenly in that second email. After the test had run for three days, the Hillsdale team decided to end the test in favor of launching a new test against the control: the addition of a photo in the landing page. This new experiment can be found here.

experiments.title: How shorter value proposition on a donation page during year-end impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a shorter value proposition increase donor conversion for email traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/04/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the donation page for the people coming to it from email. To do this, we developed a shorter value proposition that didn’t remove the strength of the message, but just made the copy/content shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this experiment, we did start to see a lift in conversion. Our hypothesis is that this traffic has already read and understands (through their email) the value proposition of the ask – they don’t need as much convincing and motivation to convert. The longer copy of the control may have even acted as friction in the giving process for these people.

experiments.title: How an email acquisition offer performs on a ‘Case’ page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the number of CTAs to a single action and utilizing the Statement of Belief as the offer increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 07/17/2018
In a recent data analysis, we discovered that ADF’s Case Pages see a significant spike in traffic when a case makes national news. Knowing that the results of the Jack Phillips case and NIFLA would be announced in the coming weeks, we decided to see if we could capitalize on that increase in traffic.
To do that, we created a treatment that added the already highly performing Statement of Belief to the Jack Phillips and NIFLA case pages. It replaced the existing call to actions which were buttons at the bottom for both a newsletter and a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant increase in the number of emails acquired when using the Statement of Belief. Our hypothesis is that the Statement of Belief was not the right offer to best match the motivations of those coming from organic search. Since, for many of them, they were just learning about Jack Phillips and NIFLA for the first time, they were likely not yet ready to sign on to a statement of belief.

experiments.title: How using exclusivity on a recurring gift page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donor conversion increase by appealing to potential recurring donors through exclusivity?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 02/26/2019
There has been significant testing done on Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page. The majority of this testing has been around strengthening the value proposition. We hypothesized that increasing the exclusivity on the page might increase donor conversion. To do this, we created a treatment that was more conversational and tried to appeal to recurring donors as a select, exclusive group of people, “the few”, that have been chosen or stepped up to give on an ongoing basis.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment ran for over a month and we weren’t able to validate the results. The treatment had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. When we analyze the experiment, we see that the donor conversion is really low on the page. What this tells us is that we would need a more radical version of the page to impact conversion, or look at testing higher in the funnel to get MORE people to the recurring donor page in an effort to increase recurring donors from this page.

experiments.title: How the framing of the transition on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does framing the transition into the donation ask differently in the headline + body copy affect donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2018 - 08/03/2018
For Dallas Theological Seminary, we wanted to see if we can reframe the donation page that follows the Genesis online course into a broader value proposition. This copy approach leads in more quickly into the donation ask and value proposition than the control, which focuses more on the next steps for the actual course, rather than the big picture value of these online courses to believers everywhere.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results were not statistically valid, but we did see a trend towards a 25% lift in donations with the treatment. Further testing necessary to validate this learning.

experiments.title: How a more emotional appeal impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more emotionally charged message about partnering with the president convert donors at a higher rate than an organizationally-focused message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2019 - 01/31/2019
As a part of the annual Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff campaign, we wanted to experiment with a more emotional message to stand as a partner with the president that has worked well in converting new donors from The Daily Signal against the more organizationally-focused, core control message that has previously been used.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As with previous experiments with the landing page language within The Heritage Foundation’s annual “Kickoff Campaign”, we were unable to substantiate a lift over the control language.
The key here is that the motivation is so high for members to go online and renew their membership that emotionally charged language cannot further lift their intent to renew, which is being programmed within the email language used to drive them online to give.
We ended this experiment early so that we could move on to experiment with tactics on the donation page and capitalize upon this unique high-traffic, high-donation period of time by identifying and/or capitalizing upon new opportunities.

experiments.title: How adding a security lock to a donation page affects donor conversion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the appearance of increased security on the donation form increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/26/2018
Within our research library, we have validated several experiments with nominal increases in donor conversion rates when increasing the appearance of security at the point of the donation form where the donor will need to enter credit card information. Considering year-end efforts would drive a higher likelihood of traffic through the donation page, we decided to experiment with this treatment type for HAFA.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 15.3% confidence score, we did not fully validate the experiment. Further experimentation is required to fully validate whether the appearance of increased security on the donation form improves donor conversion rates.
We decided to pause the experiment to pivot towards different experimentation opportunities during this high-traffic time of the year. We did see a slight increase (+2.5% in donor conversion rates), and just assumed the treatment would improve conversion rates (like in the other experiments for other organizations), and rolled this treatment out as the new control without validating/substantiating what kind of lift it would produce.

experiments.title: How presenting a gift of any amount instead of a direct ask impacts donor conversion on mobile devices.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion on mobile devices during year-end by presenting a gift of any amount over a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the homepage of mobile devices if we presented people with a relevant premium offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct year-end donation ask. We featured a devotional book for the treatment that included an image of the book and the appeal. When people clicked through to the donation page, there was an appeal for year-end for a gift of any amount to get the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate and we saw little to no difference in donor conversion with the treatment. This is due to the motivation of donors during this point in the campaign. The experiment was launched the week prior to the end of the campaign when motivation is much higher. We propose testing this approach earlier in the campaign and even during the normal giving season to see the impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How different colored CTA buttons affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the color of the CampusWire CTA button affect the number of people who click on the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2018 - 09/07/2018
The Leadership Institute’s Campus Reform daily email, CampusWire, is sent from a template with bullet point lists of news stories. This email is a direct copy of what is sent each morning at 5am to major news outlets across the country. At the end of each email is a CTA button which leads to a donation page. They wanted to test different background colors on the CTA button because they didn’t want to deviate too far from the original template. They decided to test an orange button against the original blue button, and looked for the number of clicks on the CTA button itself rather than click through rate on the email, since there are several links in each email. The images below are just one of many emails that were sent during the experiment time frame. They hypothesized that the colors would make no real difference.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a .03% and .04% conversion rate on the CTA buttons, no real winner can be determined from this experiment when measuring only clicks. However, when comparing the number of clicks to the click through rate for each of the emails, the blue button did receive 35% more clicks with a 95% confidence rate. See the file “Test for CTA color on CampusWire stats” for details (https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nFI5H606QoeCdA3Kql90NftVcw02kIFXW4dWpzJvOqA/edit#gid=1283200889). Going forward, the Leadership Institute will use a blue background on the CTA for CampusWire.

experiments.title: How validating shared beliefs affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a section to validate shared beliefs affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a campaign to raise support for their Center for the American Future, which defended citizens against government overreach. They were running a petition of support for John Yearwood, a rancher who faced a threat from the government if he disturbed the habitat of a rare spider—one which he had never seen!
They ran an experiment on the petition page to see if adding a section that asked the reader to confirm their shared values—namely that Texas landowners should be able to use their land as they please, that the federal government should let property owners conserve their own land, and that the government should follow the 10th Amendment—would increase email acquisition. Previous testing had implied that these checkboxes could have an impact on email acquisition (more people would sign the petition) and donations (more people would give after signing). They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the shared values checkboxes had no effect on email acquisition. However, the downstream data must be examined further to understand how this is affecting follow-on conversion.

experiments.title: How adding background information in the copy affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a storyline and more information about what Mr. John Yearwood is up against increase giving?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2018
The Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a petition on behalf of a Texas citizen who was under threat of legal action from the federal government. After the petition, signers were given the opportunity to make a donation to this man’s legal fund. Donor conversion was below their benchmark, so they decided to run an experiment. The control copy assumes the reader knows the backstory of Mr. Yearwood. They hypothesized that because this case was not well known, that potential donors might need more  background information, including more on the challenges facing Mr. Yearwood. They created a treatment that ended up being quite long and included a thermometer to show progress and social proof. They launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 11 donations to the control and 12 donations to the treatment, they found that there was no difference in conversion between the two pages. While the sample size is not large enough to validate this experiment, current results indicate that even with more traffic, it is likely that there still won't be significant difference in donations between the two experiments.
This prompted them to run another experiment that heightened urgency to see if they could move the needle on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How body copy length on a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Are more people moved to give by the promise of future offers/resources, or by the overall mission of the organization?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2018 - 07/30/2018
Hoover has been running the Dueling Populisms content offer for some time, and wanted to test 2 things: a shortened mission-focused value proposition, and a more offer-centric rather than mission-centric value proposition on their donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but both versions of the shorter copy increased donor conversion rate. This validates the fact that a shortened, concise, and strong value proposition, whether it's mission-focused or offer-focused, is most effective for Hoover's audience. More testing is necessary to determine whether the mission-or-offer-focused value prop works better.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the donation page can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Could we increase donor conversion by expanding the value proposition and telling them the premium they would get with their gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/31/2018
On the Boys Town website, they have a “You can help” page that get significant traffic and tells people the different ways they can help the organization. One of the primary ways is to make a gift. During their year-end campaign, they wanted to test this donation feature by linking people to two different pages. The control page was a donation page that didn’t have a lot of value proposition copy on it and featured a banner with the primary message. This approach is more traditional to what Boys Town has done on their primary donation page. The treatment page did have extensive value proposition copy on it and featured the premium gift as well. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to impact donor conversion with this experiment. We hypothesize that because of the time of year that this experiment ran – year-end – motivation of donors was already high. It is difficult to impact donor conversion (positively or negatively) when motivation to give to an organization is already high. We propose testing this again when motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How presenting a gift of any amount instead of a direct ask impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would presenting people with a content offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct donation ask impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the homepage if we presented people with a relevant premium offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct year-end donation ask. We featured a devotional book for the treatment that included an image of the book and the appeal. When people clicked through to the donation page, there was an appeal for year-end for a gift of any amount to get the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: The GOAA offer had little to no impact on donor conversion. This can be attributed to the motivation of users during the time the experiment was launched. We propose testing this approach again earlier in the campaign when donor motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
CaringBridge’s donation process had always had a separate page for mobile devices because of some legacy technology. Instead of loading a responsive page, the CaringBridge site used redirects to send mobile users to a separate page entirely. Over time, this page had not kept up with all the optimizations that had been implemented on the desktop page. CaringBridge decided to test the two pages against each other to see the cost and benefit of rolling both pages into one responsive page, which would keep them from having to keep two pages updated and in sync.
The first major difference in the two pages was the gift array. This was tested independently, and had no impact on conversion or revenue. The next major change was the value proposition and layout of the form. The mobile page had always had a shorter value proposition because it was assumed that they had less “real estate” on the page to hold such copy. The team decided to test a mobile version of the exact same donation page that desktop users saw to see the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no change—and even a slight directional increase with the new copy. This gave CaringBridge the confidence to revert to a single, mobile-optimized donation page for their tribute funnel. Going forward, they will monitor desktop and mobile results independently to see how future experiments affect conversion on each.

experiments.title: How problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: How will problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2018 - 12/30/2018
Hillsdale College was trying to increase conversion for their donation pages during their year-end campaign. They had previously seen that problem-centric language, or explaining the challenge they were trying to solve, had increased conversion in a fiscal-year-end campaign. However, they were unsure how that would affect results at a very different time of year, so they decided to test it.
They created a page that started with the problem, rather than positioning Hillsdale as the solution and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The problem-centric language produced an 8.4% decrease in conversion, though it didn’t reach statistical validity. However, due to the time-sensitive nature of the year-end ask, they decided to continue using the control as not to lose any revenue during peak times.
Previous testing has shown that problem-centric language can increase conversion, but a calendar year-end campaign might not be the best time to try it.

experiments.title: The Impact of Making Two CTAs in one Appeal Pt. 1
experiments.researchQuestion: Would appealing to donors to “cut the cord” by cutting their entertainment bill (and its support of pro-choice programming) in favor of becoming a monthly donor increase the number of donations we received? What would the impact of making two CTAs in the same appeal be on donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2018
For months, Care Net has been brainstorming ways to increase the number of monthly donors, Caring Partners, that we have on file. We proposed testing our traditional appeal language against a new “Cut the Cord” campaign. This appeal would argue that money spent on entertainment providers like Netflix, Amazon Prime, Cable often supports pro-choice initiatives. By “cutting” some of our expenses on such entertainment and redirecting the money to Care Net’s work, lives would be saved and pro-choice orgs would lose funding. A win-win. We hoped that a pro-life person’s ire about pro-choice funding and programming would help motivate them to “cutting the cord” and saving lives.
The drawback to this approach would be that it would be essentially making two calls to actions: Cut expenses on entertainment and become a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the Cut the Cord email had the benefit of a new sender and a higher CTR, the donation rate was significantly lower. The results seemed to confirm that two CTAS in the appeal suppressed donor response as they had to agree to cutting back on something they were already doing and beginning something they weren’t yet doing. This was too much friction. However, we wanted to be sure. The variation had a 33% higher CTR. We wondered if we had too much copy on the donation page for the variation and that people dropped off who were prepared to make a gift. We decided to run an additional test to see if shorter copy on the donation page would cause Cut the Cord to perform better.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition to the offer influenced donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the application of Construal Level Theory increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2018 - 12/21/2018
For the instant donor conversion page on Buckner’s Advent Devotional, we wanted to test out a theory in conversion optimization known as Construal Level Theory. The theory states that an individual would be more likely to take an action that went against their own personal self interests if they felt it benefited a small group that they were a part of. In this situation, we were asking them to give a donation because they were part of a select group of people that wanted to protect vulnerable children this Christmas season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, we simply ran out of time before we could observe any significant influence of the treatment. We did not have enough visitors to the two variants to be able to measure the impact. Since this offer was only applicable during the Advent season, we had to turn if off two weeks into the experiment.
This is a theory worth retesting in the future when we have an offer that will see significant traffic volume or one that is being sent out to the email file.

experiments.title: How personalization and value proposition affect recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does personalization (and the presence of value proposition copy in the first place) affect conversion for recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2018
CaringBridge had recently reintroduced the recurring gift option in their tribute page donation process. While a previous test had showed that a single checkbox with value proposition copy beat a binary (monthly/one-time) option, they wanted to test the impact of personalizing the copy to the subject of the journal. While they were doing that, they wanted to also test a version that had no additional value proposition copy to establish a baseline conversion rate, since their first test was a radical redesign. They designed two treatments—one with personalized copy and one with just “Make this a monthly recurring donation” and launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there weren't enough conversions to validate any of the treatments, the final treatment with no value proposition copy showed a directional 40% decrease in conversion, with a growing level of confidence. This implies (though not statistically valid) that any amount of value proposition copy is better than no value proposition copy. There was no difference at all in conversion by adding personalization (which goes against the grain of previous testing), but also shows that we must work harder at this ask to increase results, as it is a secondary value proposition—asking the donor to "lift" a little more (with a monthly donation) when they are already making a lift as it is (with a single donation).

experiments.title: How the donation page a pop-up links to impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the donation microsite impact donor conversion for the homepage pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2018 - 08/23/2018
On the Boys Town homepage, they had a pop-up appear once you had been on the site for five seconds. This pop-up links to a specific donation page for the value proposition of the pop-up. After just launching a new donation microsite and seeing that the donation microsite increased donor conversion by 21% on the primary donation page, we wanted to test the donation microsite against the original donation page for this homepage pop-up. Each donation page had identical copy on it, it was strictly just testing the donation experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The kind of donation page we sent people to from the pop-up had little to no impact on donor conversion. People coming from the pop-up were motivated enough to not be impacted by the donation experience. We would like to test this again with using just the banner image at the top of the page since we now know that that version of the donation microsite increases donor conversion most.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of the suggested gift amount impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2018 - 08/16/2018
Boys Town offers parents an eBook on how to teach your child to have integrity. This was a brand new offer and they wanted to use a suggested giving amount on the instant donation page for the ask. They hypothesized that by telling people the impact of the suggested gift amount, they’d might be able to increase donor conversion. The control only had a suggested gift amount and the treatment had a suggested gift amount and the specific impact it would have, “Your $50 gift will help us reach 2,000 more people with parenting resources.”
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for over three weeks and we weren’t able to reach validity. This is due to the really low donor conversion on the page. More radical experiments will need to be done to impact donor motivation and conversion.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a shortened value proposition impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2018 - 09/13/2018
In a previous test for the primary donation page, we found that an expanded value proposition didn’t convert as many people into donors as a version of the page that had an banner image at the top with no additional value proposition. Since we have seen in hundreds of other experiments that value proposition can increase donor conversion and average gift, we wanted to continue to test this approach. We hypothesized that by shortening the value proposition we might be able to increase donor conversion since a large majority of the traffic coming to this page is mobile.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for three weeks, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. That said, we did see a directional decrease with the treatment. This tells us that the banner image is still the best performing version of the primary donation page. The additional value proposition copy might actually be creating friction in the donation process because the people coming to the page are on mobile and are already highly motivated to give. Additional testing around the value proposition should be done to gain a better understanding of the donor’s motivation.

experiments.title: How a more organizational-centric value proposition impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Equip more parents like you with this resource
Invest in the lives of forgotten kids

experiments.researchQuestion: How would a different value proposition impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2018 - 10/08/2018
Boys Town offers an eBook on integrity. They promote the eBook through paid Facebook ads. It has a high appeal and they have been able to acquire new names really well with it. With the significant amount of people getting this eBook, that also means that they get a lot of traffic to the instant donation page that follow the eBook offer. The instant donor conversion on this page was low and they hypothesized that testing a different value proposition might increase donor conversion. They developed a treatment that focused more on the mission of Boys Town. It validated the parent reading the copy that they are a good parent for wanting to walk alongside their child and help them with their character. But then it talked about the kids at Boys Town who have been forgotten and aren’t as fortunate to have parents or loved ones who care about them. We tested this more organization-centric value proposition against the control that asked for people to make a gift so that other parents could be equipped with the resource that they just downloaded.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over a month, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. The motivation of donors on this page is extremely low and the kind of value proposition didn’t impact donor conversion. We will need to do a very radical redesign of this page to impact donor conversion.

experiments.title: How simplifying and streamlining the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and creating an ordered “next steps” list on the instant donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2018 - 08/23/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an email acquisition campaign around the upcoming Supreme Court nominations. After visitors signed an open letter to the Senate, they were presented with an instant donor conversion page. We had seen relatively low donor conversion so we wanted to test out a bit of a radical change to the donation page.
Based upon experiments with similar organizations, we had found success in creating an ordered “next steps” list that walked visitors through what to expect and why a donation to AFP was so important. We decided to implement this and then remove some of the details of the full “plan” to reduce the friction associated with reading the whole page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two pages. After further analysis of the two value propositions, one aspect of the pages that remained the same was the focus on the “product-level” value proposition of the efforts behind the Kavanaugh confirmation hearings. It may be worth testing a value proposition that is not so narrowly focused and, instead, emphasizes the overall efforts of the organization. This will be the next step in our efforts to optimize this campaign.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition on a pledge donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would expanding the value proposition increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 03/15/2018
Focus on the Family has a separate donation page from their primary page specifically for recurring giving. When looking at the copy on this page, we hypothesized that the copy led with a more transactional nature and that the messaging could be strengthened to better communicate the impact a person could have by becoming a recurring donor. We developed a treatment that reinforced the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for a month, we weren’t able to validate the results. This tells us that the expanded value proposition had no impact on conversion. Conversion rate on this page is very low. We will need to do a more radical redesign and address multiple friction points on the page if we want to decrease anxiety and increase motivation of people on the page.

experiments.title: How addressing a person’s fear to become a recurring donor impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would addressing a person’s potential fear of not being able to cancel their recurring gift increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 05/09/2018
On Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page, we hypothesized that people may have a fear of commitment when it came to becoming a recurring donor. To address this potential concern, we added copy to the page that clearly communicated to people that they could cancel their recurring gift at any time.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had hypothesized that addressing a person’s fear in becoming a recurring donor could dramatically impact donor conversion. What we found was that addressing this element of fear had no impact on conversion. This tells us that there is a different element of friction causing the low conversion rates. We propose potentially discovering this through an exit-intent survey asking users why they didn’t become a recurring donor.

experiments.title: How a product-level value proposition performs on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a product-level value proposition, focused on the Open Letter, increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/15/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an open letter to the US Senate in regards to the upcoming confirmation hearings for Judge Brett Kavanaugh. After every signature, visitors were taken to a donation page for the option of making an instant donation. Given the emphasis put on this acquisition effort, we wanted to identify the optimal value proposition that would lead to the best overall donor conversion.
With this in mind, we wanted to test two different value propositions. The control focused more about the overall goal of the campaign and all of the effort that was going to go into getting the Senate to confirm Judge Kavanaugh. The treatment version focused more on the Open Letter. The hypothesis was that since the visitor had just taken part in the Open Letter, they may be more likely to support a value proposition that centered around it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, after running for over a week, we did not see a significant improvement in the donor conversion. Given the time-sensitive nature of this campaign, we decided to turn off this campaign and move on to another hypothesis.
While we did not determine, with statistical significance, whether the product-level value proposition would outperform something that was a bit broader, it was unlikely that the product-level value proposition would outperform the control. Based upon that, we decided to move to the next hypothesis.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal affects donor conversion rate in a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the mention of a campaign goal in a high urgency campaign motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running their fiscal year end high urgency campaign and wanted to test whether the inclusion of a campaign goal motivated more people to click through to the donation page and give. They ran this on each email of the campaign , with copy in the emails being largely the same other than the inclusion or exclusion of the goal amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but we saw a directional decrease of 11% in donations with the removal of the campaign goal. This shows us that it’s likely more people will give towards a goal rather than an open-ended ask.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a redesigned donation page that removes header content increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running a high-urgency fiscal year end campaign and wanted to test whether the format of the donation page was causing friction for people coming to the page and giving them decision anxiety. They proposed a radical redesign that removed all other links from the header and top of the page and streamlined the form and body copy. The copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but we did see a directional lift of 6% with the redesigned page. This concept would be good to test with additional campaigns along with other elements of the page to produce a valid lift.

experiments.title: How value proposition copy affects clicks on a donate button in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the additional value proposition copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/19/2018
Leadership Institute sent a daily email called CampusWire, updating their audience about developments through their campus network.
