At the bottom of the email, they had a simple button that asked the reader to support CampusReform. While this did produce donors, they wondered if adding value proposition to the button would increase clickthrough rate and thus increase donation page traffic. They crafted a treatment and sent it to half of the file for several weeks to see if it would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After several weeks, there was no statistical difference between the two treatments. Clickthrough rate overall was low, since this is at the bottom of an email designed to get people to click through to the site, but it showed that the additional value proposition was not significantly increasing motivation—which suggests that readers were already motivated to give just through the content.
This experiment deserves further review to see if donation page conversion improved. It also suggests that fundraising might increase if this value proposition was broken out into its own email, away from the context.

experiments.title: How pre-selecting the monthly recurring gift option impacts recurring donor conversion.

rows.name: Added Call to Action + Recurring Gift Default (1)

experiments.researchQuestion: Would pre-selecting the monthly gift option increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/02/2018
FamilyLife launched a recurring gift campaign. On the donation page they pushed people to, donors were given two options for their gift – a one-time gift, or a monthly gift. The primary goal of this campaign was to get more recurring donors. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase recurring donors if we get rid of the one-time gift check box and pre-select the monthly gift option.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did get a directional 33.7% lift in recurring donor conversion. This experiment ran during a high urgency campaign and what we found was that the increase in recurring donor conversion was much higher at the beginning of the campaign

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a premium added to a landing page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2018 - 06/01/2018
Compassion International wanted to test the impact of a premium on sponsorship conversion. They had a partnership with Chip and Joanna Gaines that allowed them to offer a book as a premium as a way to thank the donor for their sponsorship.
They added this premium to the sponsorship pages specifically around their remarketing campaigns driven from Facebook and launched an A/B test to determine the impact for conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the premium offer to the donation page produced a slight lift (8%) with no statistical confidence. This lack of variance seemed to indicate that by the time the prospect had reached the page, they had already made up their mind to give and weren’t affected by the premium.

The Compassion team decided to launch another test after this that moved the premium offer upstream in the communication chain to see if the premium would attract more traffic from the Facebook ad itself, and would result in a higher downstream conversion rate.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects conversion on an “instant” donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will optimizing the value proposition on the donation page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/22/2018
Leadership Institute offered a free online video course, Conservatism 101 that they promoted through Facebook, hoping to use it to acquire prospects and turn them into donors. After users signed up, they were presented with a thank you page that asked for a donation. While this page had a conversion rate above 1%, which was the low side of the benchmark, they believed they could improve it through testing and optimization. They created a second donation page that took a more emotional tone.
The initial page went from the thank you message directly into tactical copy about how the course was delivered and why it was created. They reduced this “tactical” copy on the page in order to move more quickly into “why” the course was created, and the context of how it is currently taught on college campuses. The new copy tried to cast a vision for the course, and made a direct ask (in the form of a question) to fund it, finishing with a quantified ask of how $50 will help. Then, it preemptively thanked the donor before they made their gift. They launched an A/B test to determine which page delivered a better conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The second page produced a 97.5% lift, though the results were not statistically valid because of reduced media spend and low sample size. However, since a 75% level of confidence was reached, they felt the risk was mitigated to roll this out as the winner, increase the media spend, and develop a new treatment on top of this learning.

experiments.title: How adding an impact-focused value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/30/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Despite our best efforts, this experiment didn’t move the needle enough to be statistically valid. Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we should continue to test people’s motivation for providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well.

experiments.title: How the messaging approach for the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will targeting the visitors’ fear of loss increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2018 - 05/21/2018
The NIFLA statement of belief had received significant attention in our optimization efforts, especially at the top of the funnel. However, we had not yet done much testing in the final step of the process which was the donation page. This offer was going to be sent to the existing subscriber base so, with the potential influx of visitors, we decided to set up a test on this final page.
After reviewing the existing page, we had a hypothesis that we could improve the results by altering the delivery of the value proposition. Instead of focusing on the work being done, we shifted our message to focus on what could happen if we were to fail. Our hypothesis was that by focusing on people’s fear of loss, we could increase the urgency of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we did not see a statistically significant difference between the two variants. This would suggest the two tactics were not radically different enough to increase the urgency of the appeal. For future testing, it would be best to test a new tactic for increasing the perceived urgency of the appeal.

experiments.title: How an impact-focused value proposition on a donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move people to give more generously?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 06/02/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we did see a directional lift of 85% in average gift amount, which shows us that people are motivated by providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well. We should continue testing this type of value proposition in future messaging to see if this continues to be the case.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the Plugged In donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an expanded and strengthened value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/22/2018
On Focus on the Family’s Plugged In donation page, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on that page by adding additional value proposition for why a person should give and what their donation would help accomplish. We developed a treatment that not only strengthened the value proposition, but also connected Plugged In with the parent brand, Focus on the Family. We included Plugged In as an evidential for why a person should support Focus on the Family.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for three weeks, we saw no significant difference in conversion with the treatment. In looking at the data from this experiment, a clear insight from it is that the conversion rate on the page is extremely low. The low conversion rate points to low motivation of the end user. Not even strengthening the value proposition were we able to impact the motivation of the end user. The next approach in testing this page needs to be focused on a more radical treatment to better understand the motivation and kind of people visiting this page.

experiments.title: How adding security indicators on a giving form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we be able to increase donor conversion by increasing perceived security on donation pages.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2018 - 04/11/2018
We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on FamilyLife’s donation pages if we increased the perceived security near the credit card section of the donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that added a blue box around the credit card information, added an image of a lock in this same section of the form and said, “Your information is secured”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over two months, we weren't able to validate it, but we did see directional lift in donor conversion of 7%. What this tells us is that there is a mental cost to people when making a donation and that when we can relieve some of that friction - in this case by visually telling them that their information is secure - that we can decrease friction and increase conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and removing friction on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing friction and adding value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2018 - 04/13/2018
The Missionary Oblates wanted to increase donations on their Mother’s Day premium gift pages. They wanted to test if streamlining the page by removing additional ways to leave the page (a back button) as well as adding value proposition to the copy would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased donor conversion rate by 32%. The motivation of people coming to this page is relatively high and it seems that the added value prop copy actually added friction to the conversion process rather than motivate more people to convert. Although this experiment wasn’t statistically valid, it would be helpful to try testing this again with only one changed variable, so we can nail down the exact variable that decreased conversion.

experiments.title: How membership language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will membership language affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2018 - 04/09/2018
Illinois Policy Institute wanted to launch a membership program to better sell the benefits of a donating and increase retention. They were curious whether this membership language would present a stronger value proposition than the straightforward donation ask. They split traffic at the “donate” button and sent half the traffic to the donation page and half to the membership page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for a month, there was no statistical difference on

experiments.title: How shortening the funnel on the Dear Reader impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a link to the donation page in the mid-article teaser increase overall donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2018
The Daily Signal has been running an inline donation ask for over a year now. Through a series of iterations, we had found that moving the article to the end of the article was the ideal positioning. However, we had always kept a mid-article teaser that would link to the bottom of the article where the ask was found. It would hint about the need to take action. Given the high volume of traffic that clicked on the link, we decided to test if sending people directly to the donation page would increase our overall donor conversion. Our hypothesis was that, even if we sent a far lower motivated traffic, the overall increase would net out an increase in donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Including a link and a direct call to action in the mid-article teaser was able to triple the amount of traffic getting to the donation page but it had no statistically significant impact on donor conversion. It turns out that despite the significant increase in traffic, the visitors had such a low motivation that it was unable to move the overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How modifying the donation pathway impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will linking to the donation widget increase overall donor conversion compared to linking to a separate donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2018 - 03/02/2018
Since the launch of their homepage donation widget (found in the middle of the homepage), Alliance Defending Freedom had seen a significant lift in the amount of revenue that was coming from visitors landing on the homepage. Historically, they had also included a donate link in the banner at the top of the homepage but his button linked to a separate landing page.
We had the hypothesis that linking to the widget may actually cause less friction than linking to a separate page. We decided to test this with one of the monthly appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: At the conclusion of this experiment, we saw minimal difference between the overall donations to the two variants. When digging into the details, we found that we were able to increase donations to the widget by 72% but this just shifted donations away from the normal donation page; it did not result in net new donations. This just proved that the jump did not eliminate friction within the donation process; it just provides an additional opportunity for giving.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition on the primary donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding value proposition to the primary donation page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2018 - 02/27/2018
In an effort to increase donor conversion on the Focus on the Family primary donation page, we hypothesized that adding value proposition to it would increase donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we developed a value proposition for the page that communicated why a person should give to Focus and what a person’s gift would go towards. We then tested this treatment page against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for four weeks, we turned it off because we were seeing minimal impact at a low level of confidence. Because the main donation page has high motivation of people, the additional value proposition isn’t impacting conversion.
When we looked at conversion based on specific devices, we noticed on mobile that the updated value proposition had a negative conversion. We would hypothesize this is due to the additional length that came with the copy on the treatment.
We propose continuing to test the value proposition on the primary donation page, but decreasing the length of the copy on the treatment. This would help to validate the hypothesis on copy length and the impact a strong value proposition can have.

experiments.title: How expanding a sub-brand’s value proposition to include the broader ministry brand impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase conversion by expanding the value proposition on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 02/20/2018
On the Plugged In donation page we hypothesized that expanding the value proposition might help increase conversion on the page. We proposed testing two versions of the value proposition on the donation page; the current Plugged In donation page focuses on that particular sub-brand and the impact the program has. As a treatment, we developed a message around the broader Focus on the Family brand and value proposition. We then included language about Plugged In in that treatment copy but only as evidentials for how Focus is accomplishing the broader mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we weren’t able reach a significant level of confidence with the results (20%). We had trouble validating this experiment, not due to the sample size, but because the conversion rate on the page is so low. This tell us that the people coming to this page are not coming to give or are ready to give. No amount of value proposition will help increase the conversion rate on this specific page when we are asking for a gift. Instead, we need to be testing something higher in the conversion funnel. We propose testing a content offer on this page that then leads into a soft donation ask.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a donation page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a premium to a donation page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2017 - 12/12/2017
NBCF was applying learnings from their previous premium tests and wanted to test a more focused experiment on the premium’s perceived effect on instant donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although this experiment did not validate, the 102% increase in donor conversion was high enough to implement this across all content offers.

experiments.title: How asking for a recurring gift vs. a single gift affects conversion in a “Dear Reader” experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for a recurring donation affect conversion in a Dear Reader experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/25/2018
Through previous testing, Care Net found that by inserting a “Dear Reader” feature at the end of their blog posts, they were able to increase the number of people to their donation page. During the month of January, Care Net made an asserted effort to acquire more recurring donors. To support this effort, they tested adding a recurring focused ask in the “Dear Reader” feature instead of a one-time donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: You can see in the data that we weren’t able to validate the experiment in the short time that we ran it. We needed to turn off this experiment earlier than planned resulting in a small sample size. Because the Dear Reader feature gets more people to the donation page, we recommend testing this recurring gift focus again because of the potential revenue impact it could have.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn’t significantly impact conversion – positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.
You can see this fluctuation in the graph below where the lines cross paths multiple times.
This learning is valuable to know because of the cost associated with having the exit-intent pop-up on the site. Even though the test didn’t validate, we would recommend removing the exit-intent pop-up all together from the primary donation page – both during high urgency campaigns like year-end and during regular times of the year when motivation is neutralized.

experiments.title: How putting a countdown clock in the body copy of an email appeal impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a countdown clock increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2017 - 01/31/2018
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase motivation of their email file to click through to their donation page towards the end of the campaign by increasing urgency in the email appeal. To do this, they inserted an animated countdown clock in the body of their email copy and tested this against the control version that didn’t have the clock. The clock counted down to the end of the year and informed the end reader that their time was running out to make their year-end donation. All other copy remained the same. They split their file in half and tested this approach
experiments.keyLearnings: White we weren’t able to validate the experiment, there was a definite directional lift with the treatment of 54%. By adding a countdown clock to the copy, we were able to increase urgency to the message and motivate more people to click-through to the donation page. This tactic will be implemented moving forward for high-urgency campaigns in an effort to not only get significantly more people to click through to the donation page, but potentially give to their organization.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2017 - 10/23/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How year-end messaging and urgency indicators affect donation page revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will specific year-end messaging and urgency indicators affect donation page revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Compassion International wanted to run a test on their main donation page at the end of year. Typically, people came to this page to make year-end gifts, with traffic and revenue ramping up sharply in December. However, the copy on the page remained the same all year, with nothing special done to reflect the year-end deadline.
They wanted to test copy and urgency indicators to see if they could positively affect motivation and generate more revenue from the page during the last half of December.
They crafted a treatment page with a new headline: “Make your year end donation today”. They added copy that referenced the December 31st deadline, added a story to show the impact of the donation, and added a countdown clock to visually show urgency in the sidebar.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The year-end treatment page showed a directional lift in revenue (14.1% increase), but it was unable to achieve statistical validity. This outlines the difficulty of testing at a time of the year where there is already high motivation. The treatment page might produce a lift, but because conversion is higher than normal on the control, there wasn’t enough separation to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How instrumentation error affects a donation button test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donate page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2015 - 11/18/2015
The Hillsdale online courses donation page currently averages a conversion rate of ~40%. While it is exciting to have this high of a conversion rate, what this implied to us is that we are only sending the most highly motivated visitors to the page.  As a result, we wanted to move our testing to earlier in the donor funnel to see if we could drive an increased volume of traffic to the donation page.
We decided to run a test on the donation button that is displayed across the online courses website. The current button uses “Donate” as the call to action and the color blends into the background a bit. We had the hypothesis that we could improve the click rate by making the button stand out more and changing the CTA to be a bit softer of language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test are invalid due to instrumentation error. It would appear that the VWO snippet was removed from many of the pages and blocked from several others.

experiments.title: How personalizing the donation page headline affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the headline to include the potential donor’s name increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2016 - 08/02/2016
In an experiment with another NextAfter partner, we had found that personalizing the headline of a donation page increased the likelihood to give by 20%. With Con101 being the biggest source of acquisition for Hillsdale, we wanted to know if the same change would have the same level of impact for them. We set up a test where the only change on the page was customizing the headline to include the potential donor’s first name.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 2 months of running the test, we did not see any significant lift to revenue or conversion rate when we personalized the donation page headline. This is another good example that there is no such thing as “best practices” in the nonprofit world. What works for one organization is not necessarily applicable to everyone. This is why we must test each and every hypothesis to ensure and changes have the intended impact.

experiments.title: How donation page design affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the new landing page design affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/24/2016
Hillsdale College had recently redesigned their main website. They wanted to apply the new design, which was simpler and used new fonts, to the donation page. But they understood the importance of testing this first, and set up a test between the control design and the new design. The content on the pages stayed the same, which meant that this experiment was really to validate that there was no difference between the two.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically valid movement either way. In this case, it was a good thing — it meant that the new design didn’t affect donor conversion either way. Though we always like to see the big green arrow with the win, this is a critically important step that many organizations miss when they choose to launch a new design without validating the impact.

experiments.title: How an optional phone number field impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the optional phone number field increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2017 - 02/08/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for ways to continue to improve their donation page. One of the factors that potentially hindered donor conversion is the friction associated with filling out the donation form. In an effort to reduce that friction, we decided to see if removing the phone number field would increase the likelihood of a visitor to make a gift. This field was already optional but the hypothesis was that by simply having this field as an option, it created mental friction in the mind of the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two weeks, we were able to determine that the phone number field did not add a significant level of friction to the donation process. This is actually great news for the college since they are starting up a phone program and collecting donor phone numbers is now becoming a priority. It is good to know that donor conversion does not suffer by simply having it available.

experiments.title: How a declarative value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a declarative value proposition produce an increase in donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2017 - 05/03/2017
Hillsdale College’s popular “Constitution 101” course had a donation page that had been through many iterative tests to optimize conversion. The control version (at the time) proposed a question to the reader:
We are often asked: Why does Hillsdale offer this course for free?
The copy then answered this question through three bulleted blurbs. Hillsdale’s team wondered if the rhetorical question-and-answer method might not properly motivate visitors to read through the copy. They developed a treatment that declared why Hillsdale taught the course for free, rather than proposing the question and answering it.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a slight lift, but was very far from statistical validity. This showed that there wasn’t any discernible difference at all between the two versions, and prompted more radical testing. It also might indicate that the motivation of the donor was high enough that the different styles of copy didn’t affect their decision to give one way or the other.

experiments.title: How an empty thermometer affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an empty thermometer affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2016 - 12/15/2016
Hillsdale College started their calendar year-end campaign with the intent to launch a thermometer on their donation page once they achieved a critical mass of donations toward their goal. Previous testing had shown that the ideal time to introduce a thermometer to maximize conversion was when the goal was at least halfway met. However, the team wondered if putting the empty thermometer on the page would disincentivize visitors from giving.
They launched a treatment with the empty thermometer and closely monitored the results to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The page with the empty thermometer failed to reach statistical validity, which showed that it did not disincentivize donors from giving. However, Hillsdale chose to remove the empty thermometer from the page to maximize the “reveal” of the thermometer once significant progress had been made toward the goal. This result may have been clouded by the fact that these donors were the first to respond to the campaign, and tended to have the highest motivation.

experiments.title: How Premium-Focused Copy Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a premium-focused “Constitution 101” donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2017 - 08/31/2017
Hillsdale College has a premium offer for their most popular online course, “Constitution 101.” Everyone who donates  $100 or more receives a free copy of The U.S. Constitution: A Reader. They wondered if donor conversion would increase if they focused more heavily on The U.S. Constitution: A Reader and it’s benefits to the student. They created and launched a treatment donation page that focused more on the premium to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale found no statistical difference from their control and treatment donation pages for this test. However, it appears the more premium-focused treatment page was trending towards a decrease in donor conversion. Though this is not statistically valid, we can consider a few reasons why this may be the case.
It’s possible that visitors to this page experienced a decrease in motivation due to the transactional nature of the treatment page. Though the value proposition of Hillsdale College is still present, the treatment page focuses heavily on the content and benefits of the The U.S. Constitution: A Reader instead of the actual course itself. This may have muddied the true value proposition of Hillsdale College and their free online courses. However, further testing is required to validate that hypothesis.

experiments.title: How premium-specific donation page copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing more specifically on The U.S. Constitution: A Reader increase donor conversion on the ‘Constitution 101 Thank You!’ page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for a new way to enhance the experience of those who sign up to take ‘Constitution 101,’ while increasing donor conversion on the ‘Constitution 101’ donation page.They know that the The U.S. Constitution: A Reader is a helpful guide to students looking to get the most out of this course. They wondered: Can we increase donor conversion by adding more specific language about the benefits of The U.S. Constitution: A Reader. They created a treatment and launched a test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Hillsdale College found that this treatment version did not significantly increase or decrease donor conversion on the ‘Constitution 101’ donation page. It’s apparent that this change in language was not dramatic enough to change the actions of the page visitor. Even though this test wasn’t statistically valid, we can still look at the trend of the test results for clues. We see that the donation page copy that focused more heavily on the premium offer, rather than the value proposition of Hillsdale College, was actually experiencing a lower conversion rate. This is interesting to remember as we develop more tests with donation pages that contain a premium offer.

experiments.title: How simplifying a donation page and removing designations affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will removing designation options and reducing visual friction affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2017 - 12/21/2017
Though Compassion International’s fundraising focus centered around child sponsorships, they do have a general fund that is collected on their donation page. This page offered donors the opportunity to contribute to a wealth of funds—from HIV to education to clean water.
Their team wondered how all of these designation options affected conversion. Did they present too much decision friction? Did they cause donors to think too much about their gift size?
They wanted to test a single donation ask with one place to enter a gift. Their hypothesis was that this would increase conversion by reducing decision friction. At the same time, they wanted to test the impact of reducing visual friction by moving the copy from being overlaid on the image to a white background, reducing the size of the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistical difference between the control and the treatment. This tells us two important facts—removing the list of gift designations didn’t increase giving by reducing decision friction, but it also didn’t decrease giving by offering fewer options to give. Thus, the amount of giving options was neither the problem, nor the solution.
This indicates that the primary factor affecting conversion on this page might not be friction. Instead, this indicates that the value proposition is the element which should be tested and strengthened. This page’s conversion rate of nearly 50% shows that users are quite motivated when they show up. If we can successfully increase the force of the value proposition, then we have a chance to increase that conversion rate.

experiments.title: How visually highlighting security impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will visually highlighting the security of the credit card section reduce anxiety enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2017 - 02/08/2017
Based upon experiment results from similar organizations, we decided to launch an experiment across the primary donation forms for Heritage to see if visually reinforcing the security of the credit card form was able to lift donor conversion. The hypothesis was that by visually separating the form from the rest of the page, we could reduce the anxiety associate with entering a credit card online.
experiments.keyLearnings: This change did not seem to have any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a gift. It may be possible to increase the visibility and the language around the security but, given this experiment result, it does not seem like it would be significant lift even with those changes. Given the current schedule of experiments, we feel that we would be better spent testing new technologies and radical changes than iterating on this concept.

experiments.title: How a homepage donation ask impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition and additional calls to action to the homepage increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2017 - 03/16/2017
At the launch of the new Heritage.org redesign, we wanted to find a way to add emphasis to the Donate and Renew buttons that were commonly found in the navigation. The belief was that the navigation would be too hard to find which would result in a reduction in donors coming from the homepage.
To alleviate this potential drop in traffic, we created a treatment that overlaid a donation ask with value proposition copy on the primary banner. The goal was to create an easier to find pathway for visitors to give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The additional callout and buttons were able to drive 20% more traffic to the various donation pages. However, the increased traffic did not statistically increase the overall donor conversion on those pages.

experiments.title: How the membership offer impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a membership offer increase donor conversion on those that just subscribed to The Agenda?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2017 - 11/22/2017
In a recent meta-analysis, we discovered that when it comes to new donor acquisition, the membership offer generally outperforms a straight donation ask. We had recently seen success promoting a subscription to The Agenda but it was using a direct ask on the confirmation page. We decided to see if a membership offer would be right for this audience too.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant level of improvement with the membership offer. While there was a slight improvement in performance, the conversion rate and traffic levels were too low to statistically validate. This speaks to the low donor motivation of this audience and the need to shift efforts to boosting traffic by optimizing the earlier steps in the funnel.

experiments.title: How a Facebook headline affects direct-ask donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the headline ‘Donate now’ or ‘Become a member’ drive more donor conversions on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 12/12/2017
The Heritage Foundation noticed an opportunity on Facebook to retarget visitors who viewed their online donation pages. They created to direct ask Facebook ads that invited anyone who visited the page but did not complete the form to return and support the Heritage Foundation. In the first ad, they simply asked all visitors (including members who visited the page) to ‘Donate now.’ In the second ad, the asked all non-member visitors to join the Heritage Foundation by ‘Becoming a member today.’ They launched a test to find out which approach would be more effective at converting donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: They found that that the results were inconclusive. Due to a low overall donor conversion rate, The Heritage Foundation decided to pause this campaign and assess the results. The first ad did receive 1 donation, but this was not enough to validate this approach over the other. Using the urgency of the calendar year, They decided to launch and run a similar test that is directly tied to their CYE theme.

experiments.title: How a membership offer impacted instant donor conversion on a premium donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing membership language on an instant donor conversion page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2016 - 06/10/2016
On the instant donor conversion page for the email acquisition offer “Guide to the Constitution,” we wanted to test whether the membership language was creating significant enough friction to the process to be negatively impacting conversion. We decided to test removing this language to see what kind of impact it would have on the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for one month, we did not see a significant difference from removing the membership benefits language. In this situation, it is our hypothesis that the value added by the membership was equal to the friction associated with the additional copy. In future tests, we should try testing a shorter version of the membership information to see if it has the same impact.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock added to an email appeal impacts click through
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of a countdown clock in an email help get more people to click through to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 12/31/2016
In the final days of a year-end campaign, urgency plays a key role in people making gifts to an organization. On the second to last day of NBCF’s year-end campaign, they hypothesized that they could increase the urgency of the ask by adding a countdown clock to their emails. They developed a treatment that had a countdown clock added to the top of the email copy and test this against their control that didn’t have the countdown clock on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Because NBCF’s email file is still in it’s infancy, there was low engagement and we weren’t able to validate the results. From the data that we were able to collect, the addition of the countdown clock received less clicks than the control that didn’t have the countdown clock.
We recommend testing this approach again once the email file has been cultivated more and there is a large enough sample size.

experiments.title: How showing impact of a gift on a donation page affects revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the layout of the donation page affect donor conversion, and revenue per donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2017 - 12/05/2017
Buckner International wanted to increase instant donations on their 7 Scriptures acquisition offer, and wanted to test how redesigning the donation page would affect donor conversion- and also if people would give bigger gifts if they more clearly understood the impact.
The elements tested were the design of the confirmation of offer download and how it flowed into the body copy, as well as a rewritten headline and some body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment did not validate at a level of confidence of 95%, we saw a trend towards higher donor conversion and higher revenue per visitor with the redesigned page. This is likely due to a more user-friendly format as well as a clearer value proposition in the headline and body copy on the page.

experiments.title: How ordering and numbering the content on the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the simplified donation page acquire more donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2017 - 04/09/2017
The Missionary Oblates developed an Easter Novena as an acquisition offer. Once a person had signed up for this content offer, they were taken to a donation page. The Missionary Oblates hypothesized that they might be able to increase donations on this page if they simplified the content. They developed a treatment that took the same content as the control and ordered/numbered it on the page. They split the traffic coming to this donation page and tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the test didn’t quite validate, the initial results showed that by simplifying the content on the donation page, we were able to increase donations by 52%. What we can learn from this is that we are able to not only simplify the content on a donation page by ordering/numbering it, but we were able to mentally walk a person through the steps that we wanted them to take on the donation page resulting in more people converting as donors!

experiments.title: How a stepped approach on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a stepped approach on a donation page increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2017 - 06/07/2017
Though this experiment did not validate, there was a 26% lift in conversion rate from the stepped approach treatment copy. The stepped approach allows the Oblates to walk the viewer through the value proposition and why they should give to their mission, which increased clarity in what their gift will accomplish.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates launched a 9-day Novena offer for Mother’s Day, and wanted to increase conversion on the donation page. They ran a test to see if a stepped approach performed better than paragraphs in the body copy of the page.

experiments.title: How navigation impact donor conversion rate on landing pages
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing navigational elements increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2017 - 07/18/2017
With the launch of the Oblates Celebrating Mercy online course, we wanted to test out some to the more tactical elements of their donation page. Specifically, we had the hypothesis that the navigational elements that were a part of the standard template were causing significant friction to the donation process. We decided to test removing the elements to see if that increased instant donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month an a half of running, we found that removing the navigation did not have any statistically significant impact on donor conversion. When digging deeper into these results, one of the elements that jumped out at us was the overall low instant donor conversion. Based upon the low rate for both the control and treatment, it would seem that we needed to shift our focus away from reducing friction and begin testing into the right value proposition.

experiments.title: How user experience improvements impacted donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an updated user experience increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2017 - 09/26/2017
In an effort to improve the instant donor conversion on the Missionary Oblate’s Celebrating Mercy online course, we wanted to see if we could improve the overall user experience for the donation page. This involved making it mobile-friendly, removing the navigation on the page, and making the text easier to read and follow.
To validate the impact of this change, we set up a test of this new experience vs. the standard store checkout page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At first glance, it would appear the changes had no statistically significant impact on conversion. However, when we started analyzing the data, we realized this test may have been skewed by some of the technical difficulties experienced during the time period. There was an issue with Facebook that prevented ads from being run for a month and a technical issue that crashed the website for a week.
With the significance of these outside factors, we decided to relaunch this test to see if they had any impact on conversion.

experiments.title: How evidentials of success impacted subscriber click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shorter copy increase the subscriber click through rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2016 - 01/12/2017
For the first email in their year end campaign, the Illinois Policy Institute wanted to determine what depth of explanation yielded the highest level of engagement from their subscribers. They set up two different emails, a short and a long version. The long version was nearly identical to the short with the exception of including bullet points in the copy with evidentials of their accomplishments in 2016.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the shorter version did see a 23% increase in the number of clicked, it was not a statistically significant lift. With such a low level of confidence (77%) it would be dangerous to try to make too many assumptions about the impact and results of this experiment.
This test does go a long way to proving that we need to create a more holistic reporting loop so that we can tie donation results back to an individual email. It would be a pretty safe assumption that a shorter email will increase the number of people clicking through to the web page but the behavior on that page is what is important to monitor. If the longer email sends less subscribers with a higher motivation, that may have a better impact on donation results.
For future appeals, it is recommended to utilize UTM tracking to allow attribution back to the individual email.

experiments.title: How the call-to-action of the button impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call to action increase the motivation and subsequent donor conversion of website visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 04/14/2017
A previous analysis of the Dear Reader experiment found that we were able to drive a significant volume of donor traffic to the donation page but were seeing an 8% conversion of those visitors. Given the low conversion of the traffic, we developed a hypothesis that the call-to-action “Support Illinois Policy Institute” may not be clear enough. We decided to test the language on the button using a more direct “Donate Today” call-to-action. Our hypothesis is that this direct statement would receive less overall traffic but more donor motivated traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 2 weeks, we had not yet reach statistical validity. Based upon the progress of the test, we expected it would take another full month before we would know with certainty the impact of the experiment. We decided to move on to another experiment that we felt could have a higher potential impact and roll out the “Donate Today” language for future iterations.

experiments.title: How a campaign-focused ad impacted donor conversion during a money bomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a specific campaign with highlighted deadline increase conversion rate over the general value proposition offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2017 - 07/03/2017
The short time frame of the experiment with minimal difference in conversion rate did not allow us to reach statistical validity for the experiment. When analyzing the data, we did discover a spike in giving on June 30th for both the control and treatment. It would seem that the campaign had an impact on giving even if visitors didn’t see the campaign-specific treatment. These visitors were likely exposed to the messaging through email which increased their motivation when they came to the site.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the June money bomb campaign, we wanted to see if we could utilize the Dear Reader treatment that is shown on all article pages to drive additional donations for the organization. The hypothesis was that the additional urgency associated with the campaign would increase the likely conversion of the visitors.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page redesign can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a mission-focused page improve donor conversion against a premium-focused page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2016 - 03/28/2016
Illinois policy Institute was promoting their free e-book about Mike Madigan to several Facebook audiences, and then asking for a donation on the following page. They had a premium-focused page that was not producing donors, and wanted to test a more mission-focused page. They create a treatment that removed the premium (in case that was “getting in the way of the donation”) and focused on the value and the efforts that they are undertaking to remove Mike Madigan from office.
They set up an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The mission focused page failed to produce a donor as well, which fosters a few possible conclusions:
The offer might not create enough perceived value to solicit a donation.
 	The audience might not have enough time to digest the offer before making the decision to make a gift.
 	The offer might be targeted at a less philanthropic audience.

experiments.title: How refining the value proposition affected the follow-on HAFA gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will produce the best conversion rate for follow-on Heritage Action gifts?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2015 - 11/18/2015
The follow-on ask for Heritage Action that occurred on Heritage forms had become one of the largest sources of gifts for HAFA. In an effort to continue to improve upon the success of the page, we wanted to see if we could refine the stated value proposition. We set up a test across all donation forms so that we could test it with all completed transactions in order to reach validation quicker.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment with a refined value proposition was not able to significantly impact the conversion rate one way or another. With an average conversion rate of ~24%, it would appear that we are going to have to take a more radical approach in order to improve the results.

experiments.title: How a targeted HAFA secondary ask impacted donor response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a HAFA value proposition used in the secondary ask that is targeted to the topic of the specific campaign lift secondary asks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2016 - 11/10/2016
After a gift is made to The Heritage Foundation, the donor is immediately prompted with the opportunity to make a donation to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. Historically, we have used the same copy for the secondary ask on every donation page.
As part of the SCOTUS Money Bomb campaign, we had the opportunity to test a secondary ask for Heritage Action for America that was targeted to the theme of the campaign. Our goal was to determine if it is worth the time and effort to craft a unique value proposition for the HAFA ask for each campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant impact on donor conversion for the targeted secondary ask. It would appear that there was a slight improvement but it would have required significantly more donors to the Heritage campaign before this could be validated. In short, it would seem that it is not worth the time to develop targeted secondary asks for individual campaigns.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for a year-end donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign impacts conversion on the year-end donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
During the Focus on the Family year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able increase conversion by testing their current year-end donation page with a radical redesign. The radical redesign included removing the video on the page, adding a countdown clock on the page, increasing value proposition and added a giving thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate this experiment but initial data shows us that there was no real significant difference between the control and the treatment. With such a low level of confidence we can hypothesize a couple of take aways and next steps:
The more we learn about the Focus on the Family audience, the more we are realizing that this audience has a very different motivation and we must take a different testing approach.
Normally, we can dramatically impact conversion with radical redesigns. But with the Focus on the Family audience, we want to learn more specifically about what impacts donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we need to isolate specific variables in our testing instead of doing radical redesigns.

experiments.title: How adding an incentive to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an incentive to the donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/16/2017
FamilyLife was running a name acquisition offer that included a summer reading list for dads. They wanted to test if adding an incentive to the donation form would increase donor conversion- in this case, one of the books mentioned in the offer. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment didn’t validate, we saw a trend towards a significant increase in donor conversion if the test had continued to run. This could be due to the perceived value of the incentive, and/or because it increased the value of the offer itself by giving readers a headstart on the summer reading list.

experiments.title: How the type of ask affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a direct ask or a soft ask in the call to action produce better donor conversion in a high urgency campaign email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 08/25/2017
FamilyLife was running a high urgency campaign and wanted to test a direct ask vs. a softer ask in the call to action of email #3 to see which motivated more people to click through to the donation page and give.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a trend towards higher donations from the soft ask version of the email. This is likely due to higher clickthrough rate which led to higher overall donations, but the same conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a headline on a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused headline on a banner increase donations more than a match-focused headline on a banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that led with value prop headline first instead of match language would increase donations more than a match-focused headline on banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, likely because there was not enough difference in the variables tested against the control version to statistically affect conversion rate. The timing of the test was also during the end of their year-end campaign, when many people were already highly motivated to give once they got to the website.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts donor conversion during year-end.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign motivate more people to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2018 - 01/27/2018
For FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, they had a special matching gift they offered to people in an effort to motivate more people to give during this critical fundraising campaign. The donation page was focused on this matching gift and encouraged people to give before December 31 to take advantage of this incentive. We hypothesized that while people will be motivated by this incentive, they might be more motivated if we increased urgency and reminded them of what their gift will be used for. We developed a radical redesign of the donation page that led with a FamilyLife value proposition, increased urgency with a countdown clock and also included the match incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a directional lift in conversion of 10%. An incentive like a match is great, but we are able to motivate more people to convert if we can also remind them of what their gift is going towards and increase urgency visually. People don’t give to give, they give to have an impact and we must communicate what that impact is.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.

experiments.title: How the cost of a premium affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a premium boost the overall conversion and revenue of a donation landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 06/24/2015
As part of the a name acquisition campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary was giving away a free eBook in exchange for a visitor’s email address.  After the successful email acquisition, visitors were sent to a contextualized donation page.  In an effort to boost conversion on this follow up donation ask, we wanted to see if offering a relevant premium (a hardcover book on a similar topic) would boost conversion.
As a result, we created two landing pages, with one that had a premium offer for a gift of any amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page with the premium offer did not have any statistically significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue. Since this book had an associated cost to the seminary, it didn’t make sense to continue offering the premium to donors.
For many organizations, premium offers will often have a positive impact on donor conversion and average gift but there are times where, as in this case, the impact was negligible. This is why it is so critical to test any changes to a donation form to ensure the impact with worth the cost.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition delivers a higher donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 10/26/2015
DTS launched a resource center for biblical perspectives on today’s issues. The goal of this resource center was name and donor acquisition. Immediately after the signup page was a donation ask. DTS wanted to test messaging on the donation page to determine which value proposition was stronger: an ask for an investment in a student or an ask centered around teaching biblical truth.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for several months, with no clear winner. We determined that for this resource, there was no clearly superior value proposition. This resulted in the launch of a new, more radical test.

experiments.title: How suggested giving amounts on a donor acquisition page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving type would increase donor conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2015 - 01/28/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary offered an a free online course. The goal of the free online course was for email acquisition and donor acquisition. Immediately following the course sign-up page was a donation ask.
On the donation page, a premium was offered with an open giving field. We wanted to test that page with a treatment that had suggested giving amounts next to the premium. Our hypothesis was that by adding suggested giving amounts to the donation form we would show the value of the premium and be able to increase donor conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for a couple of months, with no signifiant results. We determined that for this donation page we would need to do a more radical test that included suggested giving amounts as one of the variables.

experiments.title: How the length of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 06/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course studying Genesis. After a new user signed up, they were presented with an opportunity to make a donation supporting the course and receive a companion book.
The value proposition on the page laid out a clear reason to support the course, but the team hypothesized that two changes might increase conversion. They changed the headline from “Thank you” to “Your enrollment in Genesis is confirmed” to entice more people to read the copy. Then, they shortened the body copy to attempt to increase the force of the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no valid lift or decrease in the conversion rate for the treatment. This tells us that DTS donors did not prefer one treatment over another, and that we must launch more radical experiments to increase the force of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.
The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They wanted to test different messages to see if they could radically increase clickthrough rate, and with that increase in traffic, increase conversion rate and revenue.
They developed three treatments:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
Since CaringBridge has large amounts of traffic, they launched a 4-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After several weeks of testing, none of the copy variants produced any significant lift over the control. While this was disappointing, a further review of the data found that one of the variants produced a significant increase in revenue after the click. This showed that the value propositions might not produce a result immediately, but might inspire a higher motivation to give.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a donate button affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donate button inside the secure box increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/15/2017
CaringBridge had performed extensive testing on their “tribute” donation form, which produced the majority of their online fundraising revenue. One experiment was able to increase the conversion rate on the page by increasing perceived security, adding a box around the credit card information. However, this box left the donate button outside of the box. Their team wondered if this was leaving conversions on the table due to the button being perceived as “insecure”.
They launched a version of the page that moved the donate button into the “secure box” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test failed to produce a valid result in either direction. While the secure box produced a lift, simply moving the donate button inside the box was not enough of a difference-maker to affect conversion positively or negatively.

experiments.title: How direct copy affects donation page conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does more direct copy increase the force of the value proposition and result in more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s primary donation page had proven to show better results with a broader value proposition, as opposed to the highly personalized value proposition presented on their tribute donation pages.
In a copy review of their tribute donation page, they wondered if the question posed in the headline: “What does CaringBridge mean to you?” added cognitive friction as people tried to answer this question for themselves, instead of continuing to ingest the value proposition.
They created a treatment that instantly offered an opportunity to the reader—”You make CaringBridge better.” The treatment copy removed questions and made statements, telling them that if they valued CaringBridge, their donation was vital.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not produce a statistically significant result either way, which showed that more radical changes need to be made to the page to deliver a result. Since this page has a high donor conversion rate and low traffic, it might be difficult to prove a winning treatment due to higher visitor motivation…but we will continue testing.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on mobile devices affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an open field on a donation form affect donor conversion rate on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2017 - 06/15/2017
CaringBridge had seen a significant lift in donor conversion on one of their donation pages when they removed the gift array and simply offered an “open field” that allowed donors to pick their own donation amount. However, their donation page setup split out desktop pages and mobile pages, so they wanted to test the exact same premise on their mobile pages.
They removed the array on a treatment page and left an open field, then launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though the experiment didn’t validate, CaringBridge saw quite the opposite result on mobile devices—a 10.5% decrease in conversion when the open field was presented. This could be due to the fact that the buttons become larger on mobile devices and are much easier to use on a small screen. It could also be related to the situational difference between desktop (likely seated at a desk) and mobile (out and about, with many more possible distractions).

experiments.title: How emotion-driven copy affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive copy increase donation revenue on a tribute donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2017 - 08/31/2017
CaringBridge had previously run an experiment on the journal pages of their site that produced a big lift  in revenue when “emotive” language was shown. They wanted to run an additional test to see if the same sort of emotive language would produce an increase in revenue on the tribute donation page. Since the the winning copy from the previous experiment wouldn’t deploy for a few weeks, they had a chance to test the copy on the tribute page independently. They hypothesized that if they could also get a lift on the donation page, then they might be able to have a compound lift from the two areas of improvement.
They created a treatment donation page that utilized the “love and support” language and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page failed to produce any significant difference from the control donation page. This shows that while the emotive language might be a great motivator to start the gift process, it doesn’t have a significant effect at finishing the donation process. It also may show a relationship between the emotive language in the donate box and the standard control language on the donation page—these two copy variations may be working in tandem to produce a higher revenue total.

experiments.title: How using clarity in the call-to-action can impact traffic to a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving button copy would increase traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2017 - 10/11/2017
Care Net wanted to increase the volume of traffic going to their primary donation page. One of the ways to try and do this is to test the kind of copy that was being used on the “donate” button of their homepage. Their current donate button currently used value proposition focused language, “Save Babies Now”. They hypothesized that a treatment version with language that more directly reflected where the button would take them once they clicked on it might increase traffic to the donation page and ultimately have a positive impact on donations and revenue. They treatment button copy said “Support Care Net”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy had a slight increase in conversion, but the results weren’t statistically valid. The experiment ran for over a month and would need a more radical treatment with a larger sample size to validate.
Even though the test didn’t validate, we can hypothesize that with the slight increase in conversion from the button that said “Support Care Net”, the copy presented more clarity with the end-users action. They knew exactly where they would be taken if they clicked on the button. By saying “Save Babies Now” in the control, the copy may have been too vague and created a higher level of friction in the mind of the end-user – where would they be taken if they clicked on that button? What if they weren’t ready to “save a baby now”?
When we test copy in a call-to-action, it is good to keep in mind that clarity trumps persuasion.

experiments.title: How an offer with low appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will receive the highest amount of revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2016 - 08/19/2016
During Buckner’s Shoes for Orphan’s Souls campaign, we ran a series of experiments with the goal of boosting donor conversion. One of the experiments was to run Facebook ads asking directly for a gift to the campaign. The normal flow for Facebook acquisition is to offer a premium in exchange for an email address followed by a donation ask. However, with this specific campaign, we thought a gift may be an appealing enough offer that the initial email acquisition was not necessary.
To make sure we got the most out of this campaign, we created two landing page treatments to test for the offer. The goal was to ensure we were going with the right message for the audience. The control included some additional language around Buckner International and the broader mission while the treatment focused specifically on the Shoes aspect of the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment spoke more towards the general appeal of the offer than the individual results of the pages. After spending $200 on ads, we did not net a single donor. As a result, we shut down the offer before we could achieve a valid sample size and result. This concept, “general Buckner value prop” vs. “Shoes specific value prop” is a concept worth testing again next August.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does added value proposition copy on a donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2017 - 07/10/2017
Though this experiment did not validate, they experienced a 10% lift in donations from the added treatment copy. This could be due to a more readable format (a bulleted list) for people to make a decision to donate, or perceived added value of their donation to the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International released a new offer giving parents a series of prayers to pray over their children. They wanted to test if adding additional value proposition and a bulleted list of what Buckner does to the donation page would increase donations. The form and other elements remained the same.

experiments.title: How transcribing a video on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the transcription of a video convert more donors than a video alone?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running a video ad on Facebook targeting their donors and housefile, with a video of Dr. Reyes explaining the impact that their gifts had in 2017 on children and families that Buckner served, and inviting them to make a year-end gift.
The video linked to a landing page with the video on it and the opportunity to make a gift. They wanted to test whether transcribing Dr. Reyes’ message in the video on the page would have a greater impact than just re-watching the video.
Other elements of the donation page, including the form, remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The motivation of visitors to the page and traffic to the page was too low to make any definitive assessment of which was the better treatment. This makes sense given the reason they clicked to the page (either to watch more of the video or for more information).
